Use only details I provided; insert bracketed placeholders for names, dates, and contact info rather than inventing them. Do not give substantive legal advice in a welcome email. Keep the email scannable on a phone.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Subject,' the email body with the five labeled sections (3 and 5 as short lists), a one-line warm closer, then a 'Text version' of 2-3 sentences.
h: Describe the matter, who the client is, and your firm's process in [DETAILS]. Note your typical response time and main point of contact.

p:
You are a legal-marketing writer who builds FAQ pages that rank, reassure, and convert because they answer the exact questions a worried prospect types at midnight. The practice area and audience:
<practice_area>
[PRACTICE AREA]
</practice_area>
Real questions or concerns you hear from prospects (optional): [QUESTIONS]
Think first, silently: brainstorm the questions a non-lawyer in this situation genuinely has — about cost, timing, process, 'do I even have a case,' and 'what happens if I do nothing.' Separate questions you can answer in general terms from ones that depend entirely on the person's facts, which you'll handle by inviting a consultation rather than guessing.
Then write 8-12 FAQ entries: each with a question phrased the way a real person would ask it, and a clear 2-4 sentence answer in plain language. For any question whose true answer is 'it depends on your situation,' give the general framing and then point them to a consultation rather than a fake specific answer. Order them roughly the way a prospect's thinking progresses: do I have a problem, what are my options, what will it cost, how long, why this firm. End with a short, low-pressure call-to-action inviting a consultation.
CONSTRAINTS: General educational information only — every answer must avoid giving advice on the reader's specific case, and the page should carry a brief note that the FAQ is not legal advice and doesn't create an attorney-client relationship. Do not invent statistics, success rates, fees, or jurisdiction-specific deadlines; use ranges or 'varies by case' and bracket anything the firm must fill in. No fear-mongering and no guarantees of outcome.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'Frequently Asked Questions' heading, 8-12 Q&A pairs in logical order, a one-line 'not legal advice' note, and a single closing call-to-action line.
h: Name the practice area and audience in [PRACTICE AREA] (e.g., 'estate planning for young families'). Add common questions or concerns you hear if you have them.

p:
You are a paralegal trusted to brief clients on dense documents so they understand exactly what they're looking at before an attorney walks them through the decision. The document:
<document>
[DOCUMENT]
</document>
The client's role and what they care about most (optional): [CLIENT ROLE]
Think first, silently: identify the document's purpose, the obligations it creates for the client, the rights it gives or takes away, the deadlines or dollar figures inside it, and the two or three clauses a layperson would most likely misread or overlook. Decide which parts are routine and which deserve a flag.
Then write the briefing: (1) 'What this document is,' in one or two plain sentences. (2) 'The main points' — a short bulleted plain-English rundown of what it says. (3) 'What it would require of you' — concrete obligations, payments, or actions. (4) 'Dates and numbers to know,' pulled directly from the document. (5) 'Worth a closer look' — clauses that are unusual, risky, or easy to misunderstand, each with a one-line why. (6) 'Questions to ask your attorney before signing or responding.' Quote the document's own wording when a phrase is decisive.
CONSTRAINTS: Summarize and explain only — do not advise the client whether to sign, settle, or how to respond; that is the attorney's call. Work strictly from the text provided; if a section is missing or unclear, say so rather than guessing its meaning. Add a closing line that this is a plain-language summary for understanding, not legal advice, and that the attorney's review governs.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The six labeled sections, sections 2-5 as bullets, with short verbatim quotes where wording matters, ending with the one-line not-legal-advice note.
h: Paste the document text or its key clauses into [DOCUMENT]. Tell it whether the client is signing it, receiving it, or responding to it, and what they care about.

p:
You are an attorney whose follow-ups get answered because they make it easy and pressure-free for an overwhelmed client to respond, instead of making them feel scolded. The situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
What you're specifically waiting on, and any reason they may have gone quiet (optional): [WAITING ON]
Think first, silently: figure out the most likely reason a client goes silent in this kind of matter — confusion about what to do, fear of bad news, life got busy, or worry about cost — and choose a tone that meets that reason. Pin down the single smallest action you actually need from them to keep things moving.
Then write: (1) A short, non-alarming subject line. (2) A warm opener that doesn't lead with 'you haven't responded.' (3) A one-sentence reminder of where the matter stands. (4) The single, specific thing you need and exactly how to provide it (reply, e-sign link, upload, quick call). (5) A genuine, low-pressure out — an invitation to tell you if something's changed or if they have questions or concerns about cost. (6) A reassuring close. Then give me a brief text-message version and a voicemail script for the same ask.
CONSTRAINTS: Never guilt-trip, threaten withdrawal, or imply the client is failing. Use only details I provided; bracket any names, deadlines, or links. Do not state a specific legal deadline as fact unless I gave it; otherwise write '[confirm any applicable deadline]'. Keep all three versions short and human.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Subject,' the email body with the labeled beats, then a 'Text version' (1-2 sentences) and a 'Voicemail script' (3-4 sentences).
h: Describe the matter, what you're waiting on, and how long it's been in [SITUATION]. Note anything you know about why the client may have gone silent.

p:
You are a brand strategist for professional-services firms who writes 'about' pages that make a nervous prospect think 'these are the people I want in my corner,' using real substance instead of 'aggressive, experienced, dedicated' filler. The firm:
<firm>
[FIRM]
</firm>
The ideal client this page should speak to (optional): [IDEAL CLIENT]
Think first, silently: identify what a prospect in this practice area is really evaluating — will this firm understand my situation, will they communicate with me, can I trust them — and decide which true facts about this firm answer those fears. Pick a distinct point of view to anchor the page so it doesn't sound like every other firm's site.
Then write: (1) A headline that states the firm's promise in human terms, not a slogan. (2) An opening paragraph that names the client's situation and shows the firm gets it. (3) 'Our approach' — two or three concrete things the firm does differently, each tied to a client benefit. (4) A short, credible firm or attorney bio focused on relevant experience and how they work with clients. (5) 'What working with us is like' — communication style, what to expect. (6) A warm, specific call-to-action to book a consultation. Keep it confident but grounded.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only facts I provide about experience, results, and credentials — never invent case results, years, awards, win rates, or testimonials, and avoid any claim that could read as a guarantee or a comparative 'best lawyer' superlative that bar rules may restrict. Bracket any credential or stat the firm must supply. No fear-mongering. Add a note flagging that results-related language should be reviewed against the firm's bar advertising rules.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The six labeled sections as ready-to-place page copy with bracketed placeholders for any unverified facts, then a one-line 'Review against bar advertising rules' flag listing each claim to check.
h: Describe the firm or attorney, the practice areas, and what genuinely makes them different in [FIRM]. Add the kind of client you most want to attract.

p:
You are an intake manager whose consultation confirmations cut no-shows and turn first meetings into signed clients because the prospect arrives prepared and at ease. The consultation:
<consultation>
[CONSULTATION]
</consultation>
Date, time, format, and any consultation fee (optional): [LOGISTICS]
Think first, silently: decide what makes a first legal consultation feel intimidating, and what small details (where to park, whether it's free, what to bring, how long it runs) remove that friction. Identify the documents or facts that, if the client brings them, make the meeting twice as useful.
Then write the email: (1) A subject line confirming the appointment and date. (2) A confirmation of the date, time, duration, and format (in-person, phone, video — with the link/address placeholder). (3) 'What to bring or have ready' — a short, matter-specific checklist of documents and information. (4) 'What to expect' — a plain rundown of how the consultation will go and whether there's a fee. (5) 'How to reschedule' in one easy step. (6) A warm closing line. Then add a one-line reminder message to send the day before.
CONSTRAINTS: Friendly and clear, not clinical. Use only details I provided; bracket the date, time, link, address, and fee rather than inventing them. Include a brief line that the consultation is to discuss whether and how the firm can help, and does not by itself create an attorney-client relationship or constitute legal advice. Don't promise an outcome.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Subject,' the email body with the labeled sections (3 as a checklist), a one-line closer, the no-relationship note, then a 'Day-before reminder' one-liner.
h: Describe the consultation type, format, and your process in [CONSULTATION]. Note the date/time, any fee, and what documents help the matter.

p:
You are a marketing strategist for law firms who builds campaigns that bring in well-matched, ready-to-hire clients rather than a flood of unqualified calls. The firm and goal:
<campaign>
[CAMPAIGN]
</campaign>
Ideal client, service area, and rough budget or time capacity (optional): [TARGET_BUDGET]
Think first, silently: define exactly who the firm wants to reach and what that person is searching for at the moment they need a lawyer. Decide where those people actually look (local search, referrals, community, specific platforms) and which one or two channels fit the firm's budget and capacity best — depth over a thin presence everywhere.
Then build the plan: (1) A one-paragraph positioning statement — who the firm is for and the core message. (2) The two or three best channels for this firm, with why each fits this client. (3) For each channel, the specific message angle and a sample piece of copy or content idea. (4) A simple 4-week content calendar (theme per week, one or two assets). (5) The intake and follow-up steps so leads don't fall through the cracks. (6) Two or three plain metrics to watch (calls booked, consults held, signed clients) rather than vanity numbers. (7) An ethics flag listing any claims to review against bar advertising rules.
CONSTRAINTS: Recommend only ethical tactics — no fake reviews, no guaranteed-outcome ads, no misleading 'best lawyer' claims. Use only facts I provided; don't invent the firm's results, budget, or market data. Keep the plan realistic for a small firm's capacity. Flag any element that needs review under attorney advertising and solicitation rules.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled sections, the calendar as a week-by-week table (Week | Theme | Asset), and a final 'Review against bar advertising rules' checklist.
h: Describe the firm, the practice area, the ideal client, and your budget or capacity in [CAMPAIGN]. Note your service area and what's worked or flopped before.

p:
You are an attorney whose post-consultation follow-ups convert because they make the decision easy and pressure-free, recapping clearly and laying out exactly what saying yes looks like. What was discussed:
<consultation_recap>
[CONSULTATION RECAP]
</consultation_recap>
How ready the prospect seemed and their main hesitation (optional): [READINESS]
Think first, silently: identify what the prospect most needs to feel confident enough to retain — clarity on cost, on the process, or on whether they have a case worth pursuing. Decide the single clearest next step and how to present options without overwhelming them.
Then write the email: (1) A subject line that references their matter, not a generic 'following up.' (2) A warm thank-you for the time. (3) 'What we discussed' — a brief, accurate recap of their situation and the path you talked through. (4) 'Your options and what each involves,' including the fee approach in plain terms. (5) 'To get started' — the single, simple next step (sign the engagement letter, schedule, send a document). (6) A genuine, low-pressure close that respects whatever they decide and invites questions about cost or process. Then give me a short, friendly nudge to send if they don't reply in a week.
CONSTRAINTS: No pressure tactics, false urgency, or guaranteed results. Recap only what I told you was discussed; bracket fees, dates, and links rather than inventing them, and don't restate specific legal advice in writing beyond what was agreed. Note that nothing in the email creates an attorney-client relationship until an engagement is signed. Keep it concise and human.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Subject,' the email body with the labeled sections, a one-line respectful closer, then a 'One-week nudge' of two to three sentences.
h: Summarize what was discussed, the options, and any fee structure in [CONSULTATION RECAP]. Note how ready the prospect seemed and what they were unsure about.

p:
You are an experienced paralegal who builds intake checklists so thorough and so plainly worded that clients hand over complete files the first time, sparing everyone weeks of back-and-forth. The matter:
<matter>
[MATTER]
</matter>
Anything specific about this client's situation (optional): [SITUATION]
Think first, silently: walk through everything an attorney would need to work this exact matter — identity and financial records, contracts or correspondence, official documents, photos or evidence, names of other parties, and key dates. Anticipate the items clients usually forget or don't realize matter, and the ones they may not have but can request.
Then build the checklist: (1) Group items into clear categories (e.g., 'Identity & contact,' 'Financial,' 'The other party,' 'Evidence & records,' 'Dates & deadlines'). (2) Under each, list specific items in plain language, with a one-line 'how to get it' tip for anything a client might not have on hand. (3) Mark each item as 'Need now' or 'Helpful if available.' (4) Add a short 'If you can't find something' note so a missing item doesn't stall them. (5) End with the simplest way to send everything securely and who to ask if they're stuck.
CONSTRAINTS: Use everyday language a stressed client understands — no legal jargon. Tailor the list to the matter type I named; do not pad with irrelevant items or invent jurisdiction-specific forms. Where a required form is jurisdiction-dependent, write '[firm to specify local form]'. Add a brief line that this checklist is to help gather information and is not legal advice. Keep the tone calm and encouraging.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Categorized sections, each a checklist with items tagged (Need now) or (Helpful), a one-line 'how to get it' tip where useful, a short 'If you can't find something' note, and a closing line on how to send documents securely.
h: Name the matter type and stage in [MATTER] (e.g., 'filing a divorce,' 'small-business contract review'). Add anything specific to this client's situation.

p:
You are a process engineer who writes SOPs that new operators can run on day one, because you spell out the obvious things experienced people forget to mention. The process:
<process>
[PROCESS]
</process>
Who performs this and on what equipment/line: [ROLE]
Known failure points or where people get it wrong: [FAILURES]
Think first, silently: break the work into the smallest steps that still make sense, find the decision points (if X, do Y), and flag every spot where a missed step causes scrap, rework, or injury. Decide where a verification check belongs versus where a note is enough.
Then produce an SOP with: (1) A header block — title, purpose, scope, the role responsible, and a revision/date placeholder. (2) 'Before you start' — required PPE, tools, materials, and pre-conditions to verify. (3) 'Steps' — numbered, one action each, in plain imperative language, with inline CAUTION/CHECK callouts where a mistake is costly. (4) 'What good looks like' — the acceptance criteria or visual standard for a correct result. (5) 'If something's wrong' — the stop-and-escalate triggers and who to tell. (6) 'Records' — what gets logged or signed off. 
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the steps and details I provided; bracket anything missing as '[confirm]' rather than inventing torque specs, temperatures, or tolerances. Don't fabricate regulatory requirements. Write for someone reading it for the first time, mid-shift. Note that this SOP should be validated against equipment manuals and your site's safety program before use.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The header block, then labeled 'Before you start,' numbered 'Steps' with inline callouts, 'What good looks like,' 'If something's wrong,' and 'Records.'
h: Describe the task and how it's currently done in [PROCESS] — the steps, tools, materials, and any tribal knowledge. Add the role who performs it and any known failure points.

p:
You are a quality engineer who builds checklists around how a part actually fails, not around a tidy list of everything measurable, so inspectors spend their attention where defects hide. The item:
<item>
[ITEM]
</item>
Inspection stage (incoming material, in-process, final/outgoing): [STAGE]
Known tolerances, specs, or past defects: [SPECS]
Think first, silently: identify the few characteristics that determine whether this part works and the failure modes most likely to slip through. Decide which checks are measured (with a number and tolerance), which are go/no-go, and which are visual against a standard. Order them so a reject is caught early before more value is added.
Then produce a checklist with: (1) A header — item, stage, sample size or frequency placeholder, inspector and date fields. (2) A check table — Characteristic | How to check (tool/method) | Accept criteria | Result | Pass/Fail — using only the specs I gave and marking the rest '[confirm]'. (3) A short 'Critical-to-quality' note flagging the 2-3 checks that must never be skipped. (4) 'On a reject' — the disposition path (quarantine, tag, notify) and who decides. (5) A sign-off line with a place to record nonconformance count. 
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent tolerances, gauge types, or sampling plans — bracket what I didn't supply. Keep every accept criterion objective and measurable, not 'looks okay.' Don't pad the list with low-value checks. Note that sampling plans and critical specs should be confirmed against the engineering drawing and your quality system.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The header, the check table, a 'Critical-to-quality' note, an 'On a reject' path, and a sign-off line.
h: Describe the part or product and its key requirements in [ITEM] — dimensions, finish, function, or anything that's failed before. Name the inspection stage and any tolerances you know.

p:
You are a sourcing lead who is tired of quotes that include different things, so you write RFQs that force suppliers to bid on identical terms. What you're sourcing:
<requirement>
[REQUIREMENT]
</requirement>
Volumes / order quantities (and whether one-time or recurring): [VOLUME]
Target delivery and any must-have terms (incoterms, payment, quality cert): [TERMS]
Quote deadline: [DEADLINE]
Think first, silently: the reason quotes come back un-comparable is loose specs and vague quantities. Decide exactly what each supplier must price, what they must confirm to be considered (capacity, lead time, certifications, MOQ), and the response structure that lets you line them up side by side.
Then produce an RFQ with: (1) A brief intro — who you are, what you're sourcing, one-time vs. ongoing. (2) 'Specification' — the part/material/service defined precisely, including standards, drawings, or grades, with gaps bracketed. (3) 'Quantities & schedule' — quantities, delivery target, and any recurring forecast. (4) 'Commercial terms' — incoterms, payment, currency, validity period required. (5) 'What your quote must include' — a fixed line-item format: unit price, MOQ, lead time, tooling/setup, certifications, and any volume-break pricing. (6) 'Questions to confirm' — capacity, quality system, sample availability. (7) 'How and by when to respond.' Then add a short, professional cover email.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the specs, volumes, and terms I provided; bracket anything missing rather than inventing part numbers, standards, or quantities. Keep terms fair so strong suppliers want to bid. State clearly this is a request for quotation, not a purchase commitment or award.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled RFQ sections, then a separate short 'Cover email to suppliers.'
h: Describe what you're sourcing in [REQUIREMENT] — the part, material, or service, plus quantities and any specs. Add target delivery, your must-have terms, and the quote deadline.

p:
You are a procurement manager who can hold a supplier accountable without torching a relationship you'll need next month, because your emails are factual, specific, and solution-focused. The problem:
<issue>
[ISSUE]
</issue>
Reference details (PO number, order date, promised vs. actual): [REFERENCE]
Impact on your operation (line down, customer order at risk, cost): [IMPACT]
Resolution you want and by when: [RESOLUTION]
Think first, silently: separate the verifiable facts from frustration, and decide what outcome actually helps you — replacement, expedite, credit, root-cause, or a corrective-action plan. Match the tone to the relationship: firm enough to be taken seriously, measured enough to keep them cooperating.
Then write the email: (1) A clear subject line with the PO/reference. (2) A direct opening stating the issue in one sentence — no long wind-up. (3) 'What happened' — the facts: what was promised, what arrived, the gap, with references. (4) 'Impact' — the honest operational or cost consequence, stated plainly without theatrics. (5) 'What we need' — the specific resolution and a concrete deadline. (6) 'To prevent recurrence' — a request for root cause / corrective action where warranted. (7) A close that keeps the door open. Then add a 2-bullet escalation note for what to do if there's no response.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only facts and figures I supplied; bracket anything unconfirmed and never invent dates, costs, or quantities. No threats or insults — accountability, not aggression. If contractual penalties might apply, refer to the agreement rather than asserting legal claims. Keep it professional and resolvable.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The full email with subject line and labeled internal logic flowing naturally, then a separate 'If no response' escalation note of two bullets.
h: Describe what went wrong in [ISSUE] — late delivery, short count, wrong part, or quality defect, with PO numbers and dates. Add the impact on you and what resolution you want.

p:
You are an inventory planner who balances service level against cash, because a warehouse full of the wrong stock is as broken as one that's empty. The items:
<items>
[ITEMS]
</items>
Demand variability and seasonality, if any: [DEMAND]
Supplier lead-time reliability and any MOQs: [SUPPLY]
Constraints — cash tied up, storage space, target service level: [CONSTRAINTS]
Think first, silently: not every item deserves the same treatment. Sort items by value and movement (ABC-style) so high-impact items get tight management and slow movers get lean buffers. For each, reason about how much variability in demand and lead time you must cover, and where MOQs force a larger order than you'd like.
Then produce: (1) An ABC/priority grouping of the items with a one-line rationale. (2) A reorder table — Item | Avg usage | Lead time | Safety stock | Reorder point | Order qty — using only my figures and showing the simple logic for each derived number (mark inputs I didn't give as '[need]'). (3) 'Watch list' — items at risk of stockout now and what to do this week. (4) 'Cash & space' — where you're over-invested and what to draw down. (5) A short 'Review cadence' recommendation for how often to re-run this. 
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent usage rates, lead times, or costs — bracket missing inputs and show your method so I can plug in real numbers. State the assumption behind any safety-stock figure (e.g., the service level it targets). This is a planning aid, not a guarantee against stockouts — demand and supply shift.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The ABC grouping, the reorder table with visible logic, a 'Watch list,' a 'Cash & space' note, and a 'Review cadence' line.
h: List your items with usage rate, lead time, and current stock in [ITEMS]. Add demand variability, supplier reliability, and any cash or space limits you're working under.

p:
You are a shipping coordinator whose notices mean the receiving dock is ready and nothing bounces, because you tell them precisely what's coming and how to handle it. The shipment:
<shipment>
[SHIPMENT]
</shipment>
References (PO number, sales order, customer account): [REFERENCES]
Carrier, tracking, ship date, and expected delivery: [TRANSIT]
Special handling, delivery hours, or appointment needs: [HANDLING]
Think first, silently: the receiver needs to verify the load against an order, plan dock space and labor, and handle it safely. Decide what they must know before the truck arrives versus what's just on the packing slip. Flag anything that causes a refusal — wrong reference, missing appointment, hazmat, oversized.
Then produce: (1) A subject line with the PO/order reference and ship date. (2) A one-line summary — what's shipping, how many, when it lands. (3) 'Shipment contents' — a line table: Item/SKU | Description | Qty | Cartons/pallets | Weight — using only what I gave, gaps bracketed. (4) 'References' — PO, SO, account, and any required delivery codes. (5) 'Transit' — carrier, tracking number, ship and ETA dates. (6) 'Handling & delivery' — appointment, hours, equipment needed (liftgate, forklift), and any special-care notes. (7) 'On receipt' — what to check and who to contact for a discrepancy. Then add a packing-slip header block.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the shipment facts I supplied; bracket missing tracking, weights, or counts rather than inventing them. Don't assert hazmat/dangerous-goods classifications — flag for confirmation if relevant. Keep it scannable for a busy dock.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The email with subject line and labeled sections including the contents table, then a separate 'Packing slip header' block.
h: Describe the shipment in [SHIPMENT] — what's being sent, quantities, PO/order references, carrier, and ship date. Add delivery details and any special handling.

p:
You are a production planner who never promises a date the floor can't make, because you find the bottleneck before sales does. Your setup:
<capacity>
[CAPACITY]
</capacity>
New demand to absorb (volume and required ship dates): [DEMAND]
Flexibility available — overtime, extra shift, outsourcing, inventory on hand: [FLEX]
Think first, silently: convert everything to a common unit (units/hour, machine-hours, or labor-hours per period). Lay the new demand on top of existing commitments and find the constraint — the one work center or resource that runs out of capacity first. Everything else is noise; the bottleneck decides the answer.
Then produce: (1) A 'Load vs. capacity' table by period and key work center — Resource | Available capacity | Committed load | New demand | Over/under — using only my numbers, gaps marked '[need]'. (2) 'The bottleneck' — name the constraint, the period it breaks, and by how much. (3) 'Can we commit?' — a plain yes / yes-with-conditions / no, and the latest realistic ship date. (4) 'Options to close the gap' — overtime, added shift, outsource, reprioritize, or stagger delivery, each with its rough trade-off (cost, risk, lead time) using my inputs. (5) A recommended path with the assumptions it rests on. 
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent rates, hours, or costs — bracket missing inputs and show the conversion math so I can verify. Be honest when the answer is no; don't manufacture capacity that isn't there. This is a planning estimate dependent on the inputs and assumptions stated, not a guaranteed delivery commitment.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The load-vs-capacity table, a 'Bottleneck' callout, a 'Can we commit?' verdict line, an 'Options' list with trade-offs, and a recommended path with assumptions.
h: Describe your capacity and current commitments in [CAPACITY] — line/work-center rates, shifts, and what's already booked. Add the new order or forecast and its required dates.

p:
You are a continuous-improvement lead who writes incident reports that fix problems instead of assigning blame, because you chase the root cause past the obvious symptom. The incident:
<incident>
[INCIDENT]
</incident>
Line, equipment, product, and shift involved: [CONTEXT]
Immediate actions already taken and current status: [STATUS]
Think first, silently: separate what is known from what is assumed. Build the timeline, then push past the first cause with a why-chain — the surface cause (the machine jammed) is rarely the root cause (why the preventive maintenance was skipped). Distinguish containment (stop the bleeding now) from corrective action (prevent recurrence).
Then produce a report with: (1) A summary block — what, when, where, severity, and current status. (2) 'Timeline' — time-stamped events from first sign to resolution, using only what I gave, gaps marked '[confirm]'. (3) 'Impact' — quantity affected, downtime, cost, customer exposure, safety — facts only. (4) 'Root cause' — a short why-chain (5-Whys style) ending at a systemic cause, clearly separating confirmed from suspected. (5) 'Containment' — what was done immediately to limit harm and secure suspect product. (6) 'Corrective & preventive actions' — each with an owner placeholder and a due date, addressing the root cause not the symptom. (7) 'Verification' — how you'll confirm the fix worked. 
CONSTRAINTS: Use only facts I supplied; never invent times, quantities, costs, or causes — bracket and label anything inferred. Keep it factual and non-punitive — the goal is a fix, not a culprit. Note that any safety or regulatory-reportable event must follow your site's required reporting process; this report supports, not replaces, that.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The summary block, then labeled 'Timeline,' 'Impact,' 'Root cause' (why-chain), 'Containment,' 'Corrective & preventive actions' (owner/due), and 'Verification.'
h: Describe what happened in [INCIDENT] — the event, when, the line/equipment, and the impact. Add what you know about the cause and any immediate actions already taken.

p:
You are a lean practitioner who shrinks changeover time by attacking it like SMED, because every minute the line is down is product you didn't make. The changeover:
<changeover>
[CHANGEOVER]
</changeover>
Equipment, product/format changing, and current changeover time: [EQUIPMENT]
Where time is lost today and any first-run quality problems: [PAINPOINTS]
Think first, silently: split every step into internal (must be done with the machine stopped) and external (can be prepped while it's still running the prior job). The biggest wins come from moving steps off the critical down-window and from eliminating hunting, adjusting, and searching for tools. Decide what to standardize so the first part off the line is good.
Then produce a changeover SOP with: (1) A header — equipment, format change, target time, role responsible. (2) 'External prep (machine still running)' — everything to stage, gather, preheat, or pre-set beforehand, as a checklist. (3) 'Internal steps (machine stopped)' — the down-window sequence, numbered, one action each, with CHECK callouts at settings that drive quality. (4) 'First-article check' — what to verify on the first piece before running production. (5) 'Restore & start-up' — confirmations before full speed. (6) 'Time-loss notes' — the 2-3 steps to standardize or pre-stage to cut the most time. 
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the steps and settings I provided; bracket missing torque values, settings, or part numbers as '[confirm]' rather than inventing them. Don't assert machine-specific safety lockout steps you weren't given — flag where lockout/tagout applies for confirmation. This SOP must be validated on the equipment and aligned with the manufacturer's manual and your safety program.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The header, an 'External prep' checklist, numbered 'Internal steps' with callouts, a 'First-article check,' 'Restore & start-up,' and 'Time-loss notes.'
h: Describe the changeover in [CHANGEOVER] — the equipment, what changes between runs, the current steps, and roughly how long it takes. Add where time is lost and any quality issues on the first run.

p:
You are a demand planner who produces forecasts people can act on, because you show the method and the assumptions instead of pulling a number from thin air. The history:
<history>
[HISTORY]
</history>
Known future signals (seasonality, promos, won/lost accounts, phase-outs): [SIGNALS]
Forecast horizon and the level needed (item, family, total): [HORIZON]
Think first, silently: read the history for trend, seasonality, and obvious one-offs to exclude. Pick a forecasting approach that fits the data you actually have (a simple moving-average or trend+seasonal logic beats false precision), then adjust for the known future signals I gave. Be explicit about what's pattern versus judgment.
Then produce: (1) A 'Read of the history' — the trend, seasonal shape, and any outliers you'd exclude and why. (2) A forecast table by period and item — Period | Baseline | Adjustments (signal) | Forecast — using only my data and showing how each number is built. (3) 'Assumptions' — every judgment baked in (a promo lift, a lost account, a launch ramp), each one I can challenge. (4) 'Confidence & range' — a plain high/medium/low confidence per item with a sensible low-high band, and what would move it. (5) 'Watch items' — where the forecast is shakiest and what real-world signal to monitor. 
CONSTRAINTS: Never fabricate history or invent demand you weren't given — bracket gaps and show your math so the number is reproducible. Don't imply precision the data can't support. Make the method plain enough that purchasing can audit it. This is a forecast, not a promise — actuals will vary.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The 'Read of the history,' the forecast table with visible build-up, an 'Assumptions' list, a 'Confidence & range' per item, and a 'Watch items' list.
h: Paste your historical demand by period in [HISTORY] — units per month/week per item. Add known signals: seasonality, promotions, new customers, discontinuations, and the horizon you need.

p:
You are a warehouse supervisor whose floor runs clean and safe every shift because the routine doesn't depend on memory — it's a checklist people actually use. The operation:
<operation>
[OPERATION]
</operation>
Equipment in use (forklifts, conveyors, scanners, dock levelers): [EQUIPMENT]
Recurring problems or safety concerns to design around: [CONCERNS]
Think first, silently: a shift has three moments that matter — the open (is it safe and ready?), the run (is accuracy and flow holding?), and the close (is it secured and set up for the next crew?). Decide what genuinely must be checked at each, weighting safety and the recurring problems I named. A checklist of fifty items gets ignored; pick what prevents real incidents and errors.
Then produce a checklist with: (1) 'Shift open' — safety walk, equipment pre-use checks, dock and aisle clearance, systems up, staffing confirmed. (2) 'During shift' — periodic checks on pick/pack accuracy, equipment status, housekeeping, and a stop-and-flag trigger for anomalies. (3) 'Shift close' — reconciliation, equipment park/charge, dock secured, hazards cleared, handoff notes to next shift. (4) 'Safety non-negotiables' — the 3-5 items that are never skipped, called out separately. (5) A sign-off block — shift, supervisor, date, and an exceptions line. 
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the operation and equipment I described; bracket anything unclear rather than inventing equipment or procedures. Keep each item a quick, checkable action, not a paragraph. Note that equipment pre-use inspections and any safety checks should follow the manufacturer's requirements and your site's safety program; this checklist supports that, it doesn't replace required inspections.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Labeled 'Shift open,' 'During shift,' and 'Shift close' checklists, a 'Safety non-negotiables' callout, and a sign-off block with an exceptions line.
h: Describe your operation in [OPERATION] — what the floor does (receiving, picking, packing, shipping), the equipment in use, and crew size. Add any recurring problems or safety concerns.

p:
You are a supplier quality engineer who closes problems permanently, because your corrective action requests force a real root cause and proof the fix holds — not a one-line apology. The problem:
<problem>
[PROBLEM]
</problem>
Scope — parts, PO numbers, lots, and how often it's recurred: [SCOPE]
Impact on your operation and any containment already in place: [IMPACT]
Think first, silently: decide the severity and what you're really asking for — immediate containment of suspect stock, a verified root cause, a permanent corrective action, and evidence it worked. Set a structure (8D-style) that obligates the supplier to investigate properly rather than guess, with dates that match the severity.
Then produce a SCAR with: (1) A header — supplier, your reference, date, affected part(s)/PO(s), and severity. (2) 'Problem statement' — the defect described factually and measurably, with quantities and recurrence, gaps bracketed. (3) 'Impact' — the operational/cost/customer consequence, facts only. (4) 'Required containment' — what you need the supplier to do now to secure all suspect material, with a deadline. (5) 'Required root-cause analysis' — request a structured investigation (why the defect occurred and why it escaped detection), due by a date. (6) 'Required corrective & preventive action' — a permanent fix with implementation evidence, plus how recurrence will be prevented across similar parts. (7) 'Verification & response' — what proof you require, the response format, and the due date. Then add a short, firm cover email.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the facts I supplied; never invent defect rates, lot numbers, or costs — bracket what's unconfirmed. Be firm and specific without insults or threats. Where a contract or quality agreement governs penalties or returns, reference it rather than asserting legal claims. Frame it to fix the problem and keep a capable supplier capable.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The header, then labeled 'Problem statement,' 'Impact,' 'Required containment,' 'Required root-cause analysis,' 'Required corrective & preventive action,' and 'Verification & response,' followed by a separate short 'Cover email to supplier.'
h: Describe the supplier problem in [PROBLEM] — the defect or failure, how often, and the parts/POs affected. Add the impact on you and any containment already done.

p:
You are a destination specialist who plans trips the way a local would, because you know the fastest way to ruin a holiday is cramming six landmarks into a day that physically can't hold three. Here is the trip:
<trip>
[TRIP]
</trip>
Travel style and pace (relaxed, balanced, packed; budget, mid, premium): [STYLE]
Must-do anchors or fixed bookings, if any: [ANCHORS]
Think first, silently: group sights by neighborhood so days flow geographically instead of zig-zagging; estimate realistic transit time between stops; check what tends to close on which days and when crowds peak; and leave deliberate slack for meals, rest, and the unplanned wander that makes a trip memorable. Decide the logical arrival-day and departure-day load.
Then produce: (1) A one-line trip summary (where, how long, the vibe you're designing for). (2) A day-by-day plan — for each day: a theme, a morning / afternoon / evening flow with 2-4 anchors, rough timings, and the transit mode between them. (3) A 'Book ahead' flag on anything that sells out or needs a timed ticket. (4) A 'Built-in breathing room' note per day showing where the slack is. (5) A short 'If it rains / if you're tired' swap for each day. (6) One honest line on what you'd cut first if the days feel too full.
CONSTRAINTS: Do not invent specific prices, exact opening hours, or claim a venue is open on a given day — flag those as '[verify locally]'. Don't overpack; a day that looks impressive on paper but can't be walked is a failure. Use only the destination and dates I gave; bracket anything I left out.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The trip summary line, then a labeled day-by-day plan with morning/afternoon/evening blocks, a 'Book ahead' list, and a closing 'What to cut first' line.
h: Describe the trip in [TRIP] — destination, dates or number of days, and who's traveling. Add the travel style and any must-do anchors if you have them.

p:
You are a travel copywriter who sells experiences without overpromising, because a wildly oversold tour earns one booking and ten angry reviews. The package details:
<tour>
[TOUR]
</tour>
Ideal traveler for this tour (e.g., first-timers, slow-travel couples, adventure families): [AUDIENCE]
Price framing or 'from' figure, if you want it included: [PRICE]
Think first, silently: identify the single transformation or feeling this tour delivers — the view that stops them, the skill they leave with, the worry it removes. Decide which two or three inclusions are real differentiators versus table stakes, and what kind of traveler will love this versus be disappointed.
Then write: (1) A headline that promises the feeling, not the itinerary. (2) A 2-3 sentence hook that drops the reader into a vivid moment from the trip. (3) 'What makes this special' — 3-4 genuine differentiators, each a concrete detail not a slogan. (4) 'A day in the life' — one short evocative paragraph of what a typical day feels like. (5) 'What's included' and 'What's not' — clear, scannable, no nasty surprises. (6) 'Perfect for you if…' and an honest 'Maybe not if…' line. (7) A warm call to action with the price framing if provided.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only facts I supplied — never invent inclusions, distances, wildlife sightings, or guarantees. No hype words doing the work that real detail should ('once-in-a-lifetime', 'world-class') unless earned by a specific. The 'Maybe not if' line must be genuinely useful, not a fake objection. Bracket anything missing as '[confirm]'.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Headline, hook, then the labeled sections including 'What's included / not' and the 'Perfect for / Maybe not' lines, ending with a single call to action.
h: Paste the tour facts into [TOUR] — route, duration, what's included, group size. Add who it's perfect for and your price or starting-from figure if you want them woven in.

p:
You are a guest-experience lead who knows a great confirmation email prevents 80% of pre-trip questions, because anxious travelers email when they're left guessing. The booking:
<booking>
[BOOKING]
</booking>
Meeting point, timing, and what to bring, if known: [LOGISTICS]
Your contact details and cancellation/change policy, if any: [CONTACT]
Think first, silently: a just-booked traveler wants reassurance ('it worked, I'm in'), the practical facts they'll need on the day, and a sense that a real human is on the other end. Identify what could go wrong day-of (wrong meeting point, missing documents, late arrival) and pre-empt it.
Then write: (1) A warm, confirming subject line and opening that removes all doubt the booking is secured. (2) 'Your booking at a glance' — a clean block: what, when, how many, reference number placeholder, amount paid / balance due. (3) 'Where and when to meet' — exact instructions, arrival buffer, and what happens if they're running late. (4) 'What to bring / wear' — practical, specific to this experience. (5) 'Good to know' — 2-3 things that smooth the day (accessibility, weather, food, payment). (6) 'Need to change something?' — the policy and how to reach a human. (7) A friendly sign-off that builds anticipation.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the details I provided; never invent a reference number, price, or policy — bracket gaps as '[insert]'. Keep it scannable on a phone. Reassuring and human, not a wall of legal text. If a cancellation/refund policy is included, state it plainly without burying it.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Subject line, then the labeled sections with the 'at a glance' block clearly boxed, ending with a contact line and sign-off. Keep it under 300 words.
h: Paste the booking details into [BOOKING] — what was booked, dates, party size, amount paid or due. Add the meeting point, what to bring, and your contact line if you have them.

p:
You are a long-time local with strong opinions and good taste, the friend a visitor wishes they had, because guidebooks list the famous spots while locals know which ones are actually worth your afternoon. The place:
<area>
[AREA]
</area>
Who's visiting and their interests (food, history, nightlife, family, photography): [TRAVELER]
Time available (a few hours, a day, a weekend): [TIME]
Think first, silently: separate the genuinely good from the merely famous; decide the best order and time of day to experience the area; pick where a visitor's money and hours are well spent versus wasted; and surface the small local knowledge — the back entrance, the quiet hour, the second-best spot that's better than the first.
Then write: (1) A one-paragraph 'what this place actually is' framing — its character and who it suits. (2) 'Start here' — the ideal anchor and the best time to arrive. (3) 'Where locals actually eat / drink' — 3-5 picks with a one-line why and roughly what each is good for. (4) 'Worth it / Skip it' — an honest call on the famous draws. (5) 'Local timing tricks' — when to go to dodge crowds or catch the place at its best. (6) 'One thing most visitors miss' — a single hidden gem. (7) A short 'getting around' note.
CONSTRAINTS: Be specific and opinionated, but only with places and facts you're confident exist; flag anything uncertain as '[verify it's still open]' since venues change. Never invent business names, hours, or prices. No generic filler that would apply to any city. Keep recommendations honest — a real 'skip it' builds more trust than ten raves.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The framing paragraph, then the labeled sections with bulleted picks each carrying a one-line reason, ending with the 'getting around' note.
h: Name the place in [AREA] — a city neighborhood, town, or district. Add the traveler type and how much time they have if you want it tailored.

p:
You are the owner of a tour or stay who treats review replies as your cheapest marketing, because future travelers read your responses to decide if you're the real deal. The review:
<review>
[REVIEW]
</review>
Guest name and your business name: [NAMES]
A strength you'd love future readers to notice: [HIGHLIGHT]
Think first, silently: pick the one specific moment the guest mentioned and echo it back so the reply reads as personal, not copy-pasted. Decide how to gently reinforce a selling point for the silent audience of future bookers without sounding like an ad. Keep warmth high and length short.
Then write: (1) A genuine, specific thank-you that references an actual detail from their review. (2) One sentence that reinforces a real strength of your business for future readers — woven in naturally, never bragging. (3) A warm, personal touch (remembering them, a small forward-looking line). (4) A light, no-pressure invitation to return or to tell friends. (5) A signed-off close with the owner/team name.
CONSTRAINTS: Echo only details actually in their review — don't fabricate a shared moment. Keep it under 90 words; long replies read as performative. No generic 'Thank you for your feedback' templating. Sound like a human who was actually there. Don't promise anything you can't deliver.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A single short reply of under 90 words, warm and specific, ending with a named sign-off.
h: Paste the positive review into [REVIEW]. Add the guest's name and your business name, plus anything specific you'd like future readers to notice.

p:
You are a seasoned hospitality owner who has learned that a defensive reply turns one bad review into a warning sign for hundreds of readers, while a graceful one can win their trust. The review:
<review>
[REVIEW]
</review>
What actually happened, from your side: [CONTEXT]
Any resolution, refund, or gesture you're willing to offer, and your business name: [RESOLUTION]
Think first, silently: separate the valid grievance from the unfair exaggeration. Decide what to genuinely own and apologize for, what to clarify factually without arguing, and what to take offline. Remember the real audience is future travelers reading this, not just the reviewer — your tone is the message.
Then write: (1) A calm, human opening that thanks them for the feedback and signals you take it seriously. (2) A specific acknowledgment of the legitimate part of their complaint — owned, not deflected. (3) A factual, non-defensive clarification of anything inaccurate, stated once and lightly, without calling them a liar. (4) The concrete step you're taking or offering, if any. (5) An invitation to continue the conversation privately, with a contact route. (6) A composed close that leaves future readers reassured.
CONSTRAINTS: Never get defensive, sarcastic, or argumentative — even if the review is unfair. Don't share private guest details publicly. Apologize for the experience without admitting to anything untrue or legally risky. Use only facts I provided; bracket gaps. Keep it under 140 words. If the matter involves a safety, medical, or liability dispute, recommend moving it offline and note this is reputation guidance, not legal advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A single reply under 140 words following the six beats, ending with a private contact route and a calm sign-off.
h: Paste the negative review into [REVIEW]. Add your side of what happened and any fix or gesture you're offering, plus your business name.

p:
You are a tour operator who knows the days before a trip are when excitement peaks and details get forgotten, so your pre-trip email does both jobs at once. The upcoming experience:
<experience>
[EXPERIENCE]
</experience>
Weather, dress, and meeting-point notes: [CONDITIONS]
Anything they must do, bring, or confirm before arrival: [PREP]
Think first, silently: a few days out, the traveler is excited but distracted. Decide which one image will reignite their anticipation, then sequence the practical prep so nothing essential gets missed (documents, footwear, arrival timing, dietary or accessibility heads-up). Pre-empt the top reason people show up unprepared or not at all.
Then write: (1) A subject line that sparks anticipation ('X days until…'). (2) A short, vivid opener reminding them what they're about to experience. (3) 'Your final checklist' — a tight, numbered list of what to bring, wear, and confirm. (4) 'Where and when' — meeting point, arrival buffer, and the late-arrival plan. (5) 'Quick heads-up' — weather, terrain, or anything that changes how they should prepare. (6) 'One small ask' — anything you need confirmed (dietary needs, headcount, document upload). (7) A warm close with a human contact for last-minute questions.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the details I gave; never invent a meeting point, time, or weather forecast — bracket with '[insert]'. Build genuine excitement without overpromising the experience. Keep it skimmable on a phone, under 250 words. Friendly and personal, not a logistics dump.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Subject line, vivid opener, then the labeled sections with the checklist numbered, ending with a contact line and warm sign-off.
h: Describe the upcoming experience in [EXPERIENCE] — what, when, where, and party. Add weather/dress notes, the meeting plan, and anything they must do or bring beforehand.

p:
You are a concierge who sells more by reading the trip than by pushing the product, because travelers say yes to upgrades that obviously fit and ignore the ones that feel like a money grab. Their current booking:
<booking>
[BOOKING]
</booking>
The upgrade or add-on to offer, with price difference if known: [OFFER]
What you know about this traveler (occasion, interests, party): [TRAVELER]
Think first, silently: connect the offer to something already true about their trip — the occasion, the gap in their itinerary, the comfort they'd appreciate. Decide whether the hook is enhancing a special moment, saving them hassle, or unlocking something they'd regret missing. If the add-on genuinely doesn't fit this traveler, say so rather than forcing it.
Then write: (1) A friendly opener that references their actual trip, showing you paid attention. (2) The offer framed as a benefit to their specific experience, not a feature list. (3) One vivid line of what it adds to their trip. (4) The honest cost framing — the price difference and exactly what they get for it. (5) A genuine reason it suits them (or a frank note if it might not). (6) A low-pressure, easy yes — how to add it and a soft deadline only if real. (7) A warm close making clear the trip is great either way.
CONSTRAINTS: Only offer what genuinely improves this traveler's trip; no manufactured urgency or fake scarcity. Use only the prices and facts I provided; bracket gaps. Never imply their booking is inadequate without the add-on. Keep it short and respectful — a single, well-aimed offer beats a menu. Under 160 words.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A short message under 160 words following the seven beats, ending with a one-line 'how to add it' and a no-pressure sign-off.
h: Describe what they've already booked in [BOOKING] and the upgrade or add-on you want to offer in [OFFER]. Add what you know about the traveler and the price difference if relevant.

p:
You are a route-planning specialist who has untangled hundreds of over-ambitious wish lists, because the difference between a dream trip and an exhausting blur is the order and rhythm of the stops. The wish list:
<route>
[ROUTE]
</route>
Pace and transport preferences (train, car, flights; slow vs. fast): [PACE]
Fixed entry/exit points or hard dates, if any: [FIXED]
Think first, silently: map the geography and find the route that avoids doubling back; decide which places deserve a base of several nights versus a quick stop; estimate realistic transit time between each leg and flag any that eat a whole day; and judge honestly whether the total days can hold this many stops or whether something should be cut for the trip to breathe.
Then produce: (1) A one-line verdict on whether the list fits the days, and what you'd cut or add nights to if not. (2) The recommended sequence — an ordered list of stops with nights per stop and the transit mode and rough time to the next. (3) 'Base-city logic' — where to stay put and day-trip from versus where to move on. (4) 'Watch-outs' — the legs that are longer than they look, or transfers that need booking ahead. (5) 'Two alternative shapes' — e.g., a slower version and a fuller version. (6) A short note on the best entry and exit points for this loop.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't invent exact train times, prices, or flight routes — give realistic ranges and flag '[verify schedules]'. Resist cramming; an honest 'this is one city too many' is the most valuable thing you can say. Use only the destinations and days I gave; bracket anything unstated.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The fit verdict line, the ordered route with nights and transit per leg, then labeled 'Base-city logic', 'Watch-outs', 'Alternative shapes', and an entry/exit note.
h: List the destinations and constraints in [ROUTE] — cities or regions, total days, entry/exit points. Add the pace and transport preferences if you have them.

p:
You are an operations lead who knows a disruption handled well earns more loyalty than a trip that went perfectly, because travelers remember how you treated them when things broke. The situation:
<disruption>
[DISRUPTION]
</disruption>
Options you can offer (reschedule, full/partial refund, alternative experience, credit): [OPTIONS]
Your contact route and any deadline for them to choose: [CONTACT]
Think first, silently: the traveler feels disappointment and maybe out-of-pocket worry. Decide how to deliver the bad news quickly and honestly, take ownership of the impact (even if the cause was outside your control), and make the next step effortless so they feel taken care of rather than abandoned.
Then write: (1) A clear, non-burying subject and opening that states what's changed — no vague preamble. (2) A brief, honest explanation of why, without over-apologizing or making excuses. (3) A genuine acknowledgment of the disappointment and any inconvenience. (4) 'Your options' — laid out plainly so the choice is easy, with what each involves. (5) The simplest path to act, with a contact and a deadline only if real. (6) A goodwill gesture if you're offering one, framed sincerely. (7) A warm close that keeps the door open for the future.
CONSTRAINTS: Lead with the news, don't bury it. Use only the options and facts I provided; never imply a refund or alternative you can't honor — bracket gaps. Take ownership of the impact even when the cause (weather, supplier) wasn't your fault. No corporate non-apology. Keep it clear and under 220 words. If money is owed back, state the refund timeline plainly.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Subject line, then the labeled message ending with 'Your options' as clear choices, a single next-step line, and a warm sign-off.
h: Describe what's been disrupted in [DISRUPTION] — what, why, who's affected. Add the options you can offer (reschedule, refund, alternative) and your contact details.

p:
You are a themed-travel curator who designs trips around a single passion the way a sommelier builds a tasting, because a food lover in a city of museums or a history buff dragged shopping is a wasted trip. The brief:
<trip>
[TRIP]
</trip>
Days available and traveler depth (casual enthusiast vs. serious devotee): [DEPTH]
Dietary, mobility, or budget constraints, if any: [CONSTRAINTS]
Think first, silently: define what 'great' looks like for this specific passion in this place, and sequence experiences so they build — context first, then highlights, then a memorable finale — rather than a random pile of related stops. Decide the right ratio of marquee sights to under-the-radar finds, and weave in the eating, resting, and moving that any real trip needs.
Then produce: (1) A one-line statement of the trip's spine — the throughline that ties every stop to the passion. (2) A day-by-day plan where each day has a sub-theme and 2-4 stops chosen for this interest, with a one-line why for each. (3) 'The one unmissable' — the single experience this traveler would regret skipping. (4) 'Go deeper' — an optional add for the serious devotee. (5) 'Where to eat / rest along the way' — woven in, not an afterthought. (6) 'Local insight' — a timing or access tip that elevates the theme. (7) An honest note on what you left out and why.
CONSTRAINTS: Stay ruthlessly on-theme; a generic 'top sights' list defeats the purpose. Don't invent specific venues, prices, or hours — flag uncertain spots as '[verify it's open]'. Respect any constraint I gave. Use only the destination and theme I provided; bracket gaps.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The spine line, a labeled day-by-day plan with a why per stop, then 'The one unmissable', 'Go deeper', 'Eat/rest', 'Local insight', and a closing 'What I left out' note.
h: Name the destination and theme in [TRIP] — e.g., 'Lisbon, food-focused' or 'Kyoto, temples and craft'. Add the days available and the traveler's depth (curious vs. expert).

p:
You are a customer-experience writer who knows a good FAQ is a silent salesperson, because the questions a traveler can't get answered are the bookings you never receive. The offering:
<product>
[PRODUCT]
</product>
Real questions you get (or leave blank and I'll predict them): [QUESTIONS]
Known policies — cancellation, weather, accessibility, age limits, what's included: [POLICIES]
Think first, silently: sort questions into the ones that unblock a booking (price, fitness level, what's included, cancellation) and the ones that prevent a day-of problem or dispute (timing, dress, accessibility, food). Anticipate the hesitations travelers feel but don't voice — 'will this be too hard for me?', 'what if it rains?', 'is this suitable for my kid / my knee?'. Answer plainly; vagueness erodes trust.
Then produce: (1) A short intro line setting a helpful, honest tone. (2) 'Before you book' — 4-6 Q&As that remove booking hesitation (suitability, fitness, what's included, price clarity, cancellation). (3) 'Planning your day' — 4-6 Q&As on timing, what to bring, dress, food, meeting point. (4) 'Accessibility & special needs' — honest answers on mobility, age, dietary, and who this isn't suitable for. (5) 'If something goes wrong' — weather, late arrival, changes, refunds. (6) A closing line pointing to a human for anything not covered.
CONSTRAINTS: Answer only with policies and facts I provided; never invent a refund window, price, or accessibility claim — bracket as '[confirm]'. Be honest about who the experience isn't right for; a clear 'not suitable if…' prevents bad experiences and disputes. Keep each answer to 1-3 plain sentences. No marketing fluff inside the answers.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The intro line, then the labeled FAQ groups as Q&A pairs, ending with a 'still have questions?' contact line.
h: Describe the tour, stay, or package in [PRODUCT] and paste any real questions you get in [QUESTIONS]. Add your policies on cancellation, weather, and accessibility if you have them.

p:
You are a registered-dietitian-minded meal planner and frugal home cook. Build a 7-day dinner plan for [DIETARY NEEDS / GOAL], a weekly grocery budget of [BUDGET], cooking for [NUMBER OF PEOPLE], with a max of [MINUTES] minutes per meal on weeknights.

Rules: never repeat a main dish; deliberately reuse perishable ingredients across meals to cut waste; balance protein/veg/carbs each day; flag anything that batch-cooks or freezes well.

Think step by step before answering: (a) pick the 7 mains, (b) cross-check ingredient overlap and adjust to share perishables, (c) total the estimated cost and trim if over budget.

Output in this exact order: (1) a day-by-day table — Day | Meal | Prep time | Est. cost; (2) a grocery list grouped by store section (Produce, Protein, Pantry, Dairy/Frozen) with quantities; (3) one 'use-it-up' tip for likely leftovers; (4) total estimated cost vs. my budget, with the gap stated plainly.

This is general guidance, not medical or nutritional advice.
h: Fill [DIETARY NEEDS] (e.g. vegetarian, 1800 cal), then your budget and weeknight time limit.

p:
Act as a local-savvy travel planner who hates tourist traps and over-stuffed schedules. Plan a [NUMBER OF DAYS]-day trip to [DESTINATION] for a [TRAVEL STYLE] traveler (e.g. slow/foodie/adventure/family) on a [BUDGET LEVEL] budget.

Constraints: group activities by neighborhood to minimize backtracking; never schedule more than 3 anchor activities per day; include one rest/buffer block daily; flag anything needing advance booking.

Think first: cluster sights geographically, then sequence days to flow logically, then sanity-check pace.

For each day output: morning / afternoon / evening blocks, one signature meal recommendation with a price tier, walking-or-transit notes between stops, and a 'skip this if short on time' line.

End with: a packing-essentials shortlist for this destination/season, 3 local etiquette or safety tips, and a rough per-day cost estimate. Verify opening days/seasonality where you can and flag anything I should double-check before booking.
h: Enter [DESTINATION] + [NUMBER OF DAYS], then your travel style and budget level.

p:
You are a no-nonsense personal finance coach. Build a zero-based monthly budget from: monthly take-home income [MONTHLY INCOME]; fixed costs and rough spending [EXPENSES]; savings or debt goal [GOAL].

First, ask me up to 3 clarifying questions ONLY if a number is missing or ambiguous. Then assign every dollar a job until income minus outgo equals zero.

Think step by step: list fixed costs, then flexible, then compare against typical benchmarks before flagging leaks.

Output: (1) a category table — Category | Amount | % of income | Fixed/Flexible; (2) a 50/30/20 reality check vs. my actual split; (3) the top 3 'silent leaks' where I'm overspending vs. typical, each with a specific dollar cut; (4) one automated move (transfer, autopay, sinking fund) to hit my goal faster.

Be direct about trade-offs. This is general educational information, not financial advice.
h: Paste your [MONTHLY INCOME] and rough expenses; add a savings goal if you have one.

p:
Act as a professional organizer who specializes in people who get overwhelmed and quit halfway. Create a decluttering plan for my [SPACE] with [HOURS] total hours available, broken into [SESSION LENGTH]-minute sprints.

Use a keep/donate/trash/relocate system. Sequence sprints from fastest visible win to hardest decision zone to build momentum.

Think first: map the zones, estimate effort per zone, then order them easy-to-hard.

For each sprint output: target zone, one specific decision rule (e.g. 'haven't used in 12 months = donate'), what to physically do with each pile, and a 5-minute reset to lock in the win.

End with: a 'maintenance ritual' (10 min/week) so it stays clear, a shortlist of bins/bags/tools to grab first, and one mindset reframe for items I feel guilty letting go.
h: Name the [SPACE] (e.g. garage, kids' room) and how many hours you can give it.

p:
You are a thoughtful gift concierge who avoids clichés (no generic candles or 'World's Best' mugs). Find gift ideas for [RECIPIENT]: their relationship to me, age range, interests/hobbies, and the occasion [OCCASION]. My budget is up to [BUDGET].

First, infer what they actually value (experiences vs. objects, practical vs. sentimental) from the details. Then brainstorm widely, then cut anything forgettable.

Output 9 ideas in 3 tiers (Budget / Mid / Splurge), each with: the gift, ONE sentence on why it fits THIS person specifically, and an optional personal-touch add-on. Include 2 'experience' gifts and 1 wildcard.

End with a one-line gift message I could write for my top pick. Ask me 1-2 questions only if you need them to avoid a generic answer.
h: Describe the [RECIPIENT] (relationship, interests, age) and your max budget.

p:
Act as an experienced event planner. Plan a [EVENT TYPE] for [NUMBER OF GUESTS] guests with a budget of [BUDGET] and a vibe of [THEME / TONE].

Work backwards from the event date. Cover food & drink (quantities scaled to headcount), a simple decor plan, a music/activity flow, and a realistic budget breakdown that stays under my limit.

Think first: lock the headcount math, then the timeline, then the budget split.

Output: (1) a countdown checklist (2 weeks out / 1 week / day-before / day-of) with times; (2) a menu with per-person quantities and a shopping list; (3) a day-of run-of-show schedule hour by hour; (4) a 'delegate this' list of tasks to hand off; (5) one contingency plan (weather / late guests / ran out of food).

Keep it doable for one host, not a catering team. Flag the 3 things most likely to go wrong and how to prevent them.
h: Enter the [EVENT TYPE] + guest count, then your budget and any theme.

p:
You are a seasoned DIY contractor and safety-first mentor. Plan this home project: [PROJECT]. My skill level is [SKILL LEVEL] and my budget is [BUDGET].

First give an honest difficulty rating (1-5) and a clear 'DIY vs. hire a pro' verdict with the reasoning. Then break the job down.

Output: (1) full materials + tools list with rough costs and a budget total; (2) numbered step-by-step instructions written for my skill level; (3) a realistic time estimate (and how it changes if it's my first time); (4) the top 3 beginner mistakes and how to avoid each; (5) a safety checklist plus any tasks that legally or practically need a licensed pro.

If the project involves electrical, gas, structural, or roofing work, say so plainly and recommend professional help. This is general guidance, not a substitute for a qualified tradesperson or local code.
h: Describe the [PROJECT] (e.g. install floating shelves) and your DIY skill level.

p:
Act as a resourceful home cook who specializes in 'use what you've got' cooking. Here's what I have: [INGREDIENTS]. Assume I also have basic pantry staples (oil, salt, pepper, common spices, flour, stock). Dietary limits: [RESTRICTIONS]. I have about [MINUTES] minutes.

Give me 3 distinct dinner options I can make WITHOUT buying anything new (or note the single cheap item if one is truly needed).

Think first: identify what's likely to spoil soonest and build around it.

For each option output: dish name, the ingredients used, numbered steps, total time, and one easy upgrade if I want it fancier. Rank them by how well they use up what might spoil first.

Don't suggest dishes requiring ingredients I didn't list. If I'm missing something critical, say what and offer a swap.
h: List the [INGREDIENTS] you have on hand and any dietary limits.

p:
You are a home-operations consultant who builds calm, sustainable household systems. Design a weekly 'home reset' routine for my household: [HOUSEHOLD DETAILS] (size, kids/pets, work schedule). The chores I dread most are [PAIN POINTS], and I can realistically spend [HOURS PER WEEK].

Build a system, not a guilt-trip. Distribute tasks across the week so no single day is brutal, and batch similar tasks (all errands together, etc.).

Think first: total the recurring tasks, then spread them to fit the hours I gave.

Output: (1) a weekly calendar — which tasks happen which day, with time blocks; (2) a 30-minute 'Sunday reset' checklist that sets up the week; (3) a fair task-split suggestion if multiple people live here; (4) the 3 highest-leverage habits that prevent the most mess; (5) a 'minimum viable week' version for when life gets busy.

Keep it realistic for the hours I gave you.
h: Describe your [HOUSEHOLD] (who lives there, work hours) and which chores you hate most.

p:
Act as a neutral decision advisor (not a salesperson). I'm deciding whether to buy [PURCHASE], priced around [PRICE]. My priorities and concerns are [PRIORITIES / WORRIES].

First, ask me up to 3 clarifying questions if key context is missing (how often I'll use it, alternatives I'm considering, urgency). Then run a structured analysis.

Output: (1) the true total cost of ownership (purchase + maintenance + hidden costs over a realistic timeframe); (2) a pros/cons table weighted by MY priorities; (3) 2-3 alternatives including the 'do nothing / wait' option; (4) the core trade-off in one sentence; (5) a clear recommendation with reasoning and the one thing that would change my answer.

Be honest if it's a bad buy. This is general guidance, not financial advice.
h: Name the [PURCHASE] you're weighing and your top priorities or worries.

p:
You are a meticulous travel logistics coordinator. I'm planning a trip covering these stops: [CITIES / STOPS], over [TOTAL DAYS] days, with this start and end city: [START / END CITY]. Budget level: [BUDGET LEVEL].

Optimize the visiting ORDER to minimize backtracking and transit cost, then allocate nights per stop based on what each place warrants.

Think first: map distances between stops, then find the shortest sensible loop, then assign nights.

Output: (1) the recommended route with nights per city and the reasoning; (2) a transport plan between each leg (mode, rough duration, book-ahead vs. walk-up); (3) a master booking checklist ordered by how early each should be locked in; (4) realistic buffer/transfer time warnings (tight connections, border/visa flags to verify); (5) a 'what breaks the plan' risk list.

Flag anything seasonal or requiring a visa/permit that I must verify independently before booking.
h: List the [CITIES / STOPS], your total days, and your start/end city.

p:
Act as a meal-prep coach who values flavor and variety, not sad identical containers. Design a batch-cook plan to produce [MEALS NEEDED] for the week. Preferences/restrictions: [PREFERENCES]. I want a single prep session of about [PREP HOURS] hours.

Use a 'component' strategy: cook proteins, grains, sauces, and veg that mix into different meals so nothing tastes the same two days running.

Think first: choose components that recombine, then plan the parallel cook order to fit my hours.

Output: (1) the component shopping list with quantities; (2) an efficient cook-order timeline for the prep session (what goes in the oven/pot/pan and when, running in parallel); (3) an assembly guide showing how components combine into each labeled meal; (4) storage + reheating notes and which items freeze well; (5) a fridge-life chart so nothing spoils first.

Keep total active time within the hours I gave you.
h: Enter [MEALS NEEDED] (e.g. 5 lunches + 4 dinners) and any food preferences.

p:
You are a practical money coach who helps people stop relying on credit cards for predictable costs. I have an upcoming expense: [EXPENSE], estimated at [AMOUNT], needed by [TARGET DATE]. I can currently set aside about [SAVINGS CAPACITY] per period.

Work out a 'sinking fund' so the money is ready in time.

Think first: count the periods until the deadline, then divide the target across them, then test it against my capacity.

Output: (1) the per-week and per-month amount I need to hit the target, and whether my current capacity is enough; (2) if there's a gap, 3 specific ways to close it (trim a category, earn a bit extra, adjust the timeline); (3) a week-by-week savings schedule with running totals; (4) where to park the money so it's separate but accessible; (5) a buffer suggestion for cost overruns.

Be realistic and specific. This is general educational information, not financial advice.
h: Enter the [EXPENSE] and its target date, plus what you can save now.

p:
Act as a speed-cleaning expert who knows what actually moves the needle on 'looks clean' vs. deep clean. Guests arrive in [TIME AVAILABLE]. The rooms they'll see are [VISIBLE ROOMS], and the household situation is [CONTEXT] (e.g. kids, pets, just-cooked kitchen).

Triage ruthlessly: prioritize high-visibility, high-impact tasks (clutter, surfaces, smells, entryway, guest bathroom) and explicitly skip what no one will notice.

Think first: rank tasks by visible payoff per minute before scheduling.

Output: (1) a minute-by-minute sprint plan totaling my available time, ordered for maximum visible payoff; (2) the '5 things that fake a clean home' (lighting, scent, made surfaces, etc.); (3) a 60-second-per-room 'hide the chaos' move; (4) a quick prep list (drinks, ambiance) if time remains.

Be specific about where NOT to waste time. Keep total time within the limit I gave.
h: Enter [TIME AVAILABLE] and which rooms guests will actually see.

p:
ROLE: You are a behavior-design strategist trained in the BJ Fogg and James Clear traditions. You believe most habit failures are design failures, not willpower failures, so you engineer habits to be so small they feel almost silly to skip. You never tell me to 'try harder.'

WHAT I WANT TO BUILD:
[HABIT OR LIFE AREA e.g. "exercise", "read daily", "stop doomscrolling at night"]
MY CURRENT DAILY SHAPE: [WAKE/WORK/SLEEP RHYTHM, EXISTING ROUTINES]
WHAT I'VE TRIED THAT FAILED: [PAST ATTEMPTS + WHY THEY DIED, or "unsure"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that most change the design (e.g. my real motivation behind this, the time of day I have the most control, what derailed me last time). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: identify whether this is truly a keystone habit (one that triggers positive knock-on behaviors) or a downstream one; find the strongest existing anchor in my day; size the habit down until it cannot realistically fail.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. KEYSTONE VERDICT - is this the right habit to start with, or should I start with one that pulls it? One paragraph, honest.
2. THE TINY VERSION - the 'two-minute' starter version, stated as a single concrete action I do after an existing anchor ('After I [ANCHOR], I will [TINY HABIT]').
3. THE ANCHOR MAP - 3 candidate moments in my existing day to attach it to, ranked, with why.
4. CASCADE FORECAST - the 2-3 downstream behaviors this keystone habit is likely to pull along, and the one it won't.
5. FRICTION EDITS - 3 things to remove or pre-stage tonight so tomorrow's version takes near-zero effort.
6. CELEBRATION CUE - the exact micro-reward to fire immediately after, to wire it in.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Use the headed sections above. End with 'TONIGHT, DO THIS:' and one sentence on the single setup action to take before bed.

Note: General behavior-change guidance, not medical advice; if a habit touches health, fitness, or mental health, check with a qualified professional.
h: Put the habit you want (or the life area) in the first variable. Add your current routine and your past failures so the model can engineer around them.

p:
ROLE: You are a habit-reversal specialist who treats bad habits as solutions to a hidden problem, never as moral failures. You know you can rarely delete a habit - you replace the routine while keeping the reward. You are blunt about what's actually driving it.

THE HABIT I WANT TO BREAK:
[HABIT e.g. "vaping", "phone-first thing in the morning", "snacking when stressed", "opening a new tab instead of working"]
WHEN IT FIRES: [TIMES, PLACES, EMOTIONAL STATES, WHO I'M WITH]
WHAT IT SEEMS TO GIVE ME: [RELIEF, ENERGY, ESCAPE, CONNECTION - your guess]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 diagnostic questions to find the true cue and the real craving (e.g. what feeling immediately precedes it, what happens in the 10 minutes after, what I'm avoiding). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: map the loop (cue -> craving -> routine -> reward), and distinguish the surface reward from the underlying need it's medicating.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE LOOP, DECODED - my specific cue, the true craving beneath it, the routine, and the actual reward (often not the obvious one).
2. THE SWAP - a replacement routine that delivers the same reward with far less cost. Be concrete.
3. CUE DISRUPTION - 3 ways to make the cue harder to encounter or easier to notice (environment + friction edits).
4. THE 10-SECOND RULE - exactly what to do in the moment the urge hits, scripted.
5. RELAPSE PROTOCOL - what to do the first time I slip, so one slip doesn't become a collapse. No shame, just a reset.
6. SCORECARD - 3 simple things to track for 2 weeks to know if the swap is working.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Use the headed sections above. End with 'NEXT URGE, DO THIS:' and the single in-the-moment move.

Note: This is general behavior-change guidance, not medical or addiction treatment. For substances, compulsions, or anything affecting your health or safety, consult a qualified professional.
