Then deliver, in this exact OUTPUT FORMAT: 1) LOCKED IDENTITY BLOCK: plain-text line fixing facial structure, apparent age, skin tone, eye color, hair, body type and signature wardrobe in concrete adjectives (ban vague words like 'pretty' or 'beautiful'). 2) MODEL SHEET PROMPT: front, 3/4 and profile views, neutral grey background, even soft lighting, ending --ar 3:2. 3) SCENE PROMPTS: three prompts for [SCENE 1], [SCENE 2], [SCENE 3] that PREPEND the identity block verbatim so the face stays identical, each one line. 4) CONSISTENCY NOTE: one line on where to paste --cref / a face image-reference. RULE: the identity wording must be byte-for-byte identical in every scene. Nothing else.
h: Describe the character once in [CHARACTER]; name your 3 scenes in [SCENE 1], [SCENE 2], [SCENE 3].

p: Act as a senior brand identity designer. Brand: [BRAND] - [WHAT IT DOES]. Desired vibe: [VIBE]. THINK FIRST (silently): what association the mark must trigger and which shapes/colors earn it. Create FOUR distinct logo prompt directions for Midjourney v6: (A) minimalist wordmark/monogram, (B) abstract geometric symbol, (C) literal/illustrative mark, (D) emblem/badge. For EACH: write the prompt (style, line weight, a named 2-3 color palette, flat vector, white background, centered, generous margin), append 'simple, scalable, no gradients, no photorealism, no text artifacts --no photo, realistic, 3d --ar 1:1', and add a one-line rationale. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: a) DIRECTION A-D, each as 'PROMPT: <line>' then 'WHY: <line>'. b) PICK: one line naming the strongest direction for [VIBE] and why. Nothing else.
h: Give [BRAND], [WHAT IT DOES], and a [VIBE] (e.g. premium, playful, technical).

p: You are an anime background artist. Restyle the uploaded photo into [ANIME STYLE] (e.g. soft Studio-Ghibli, 90s cel anime, modern Makoto Shinkai). KEEP THE SAME PERSON: same pose, expression, hairstyle, clothing colors and recognizable features - do not change identity, age or face shape. Setting: [SETTING]. Apply hand-painted backgrounds, warm volumetric light, soft watercolor skies, gentle film grain and clean line art, while preserving the original framing and crop. CONSTRAINTS: avoid photorealism, avoid extra characters, avoid distorted hands or faces. THINK FIRST (silently): which real-photo details to keep for likeness versus stylize. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line ending --ar 16:9> 2) LIKENESS TIP: <one line on keeping the face recognizable, e.g. --cref strength>. Nothing else.
h: Upload the photo; pick an [ANIME STYLE] and the [SETTING] you want around them.

p: You write viral macro-photography prompts. Subject: [IMPOSSIBLE SUBJECT]. THINK FIRST (silently): what physical trick (refraction, translucency, scale) makes this illusion read instantly, and what background makes it pop. Build ONE hyperreal extreme close-up: name a real macro lens (e.g. 100mm f/2.8 macro), razor-thin depth of field with creamy bokeh, dramatic single-source lighting with rim light, dewdrops/translucency/refraction where they sell the illusion, ultra-fine texture, high dynamic range, dark or complementary background, rule-of-thirds composition. Append --ar 4:5 --style raw --stylize 300. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line> 2) SERIES: <three alternate [IMPOSSIBLE SUBJECT] ideas in the same family that would also trend>. Nothing else.
h: Put your surreal idea in [IMPOSSIBLE SUBJECT], e.g. a galaxy inside a dewdrop.

p: You are a miniature-effects photographer. Create a tilt-shift MINIATURE DIORAMA of: [SCENE]. Make it unmistakably a handcrafted tabletop model: strong selective focus (sharp center band, heavy blur top and bottom), slightly oversaturated toy-like colors, high-angle 'looking down' camera, tiny-scale figures and props, soft studio lighting that mimics a photographed model, and subtle handmade texture (felt, painted foam, plastic). Photoreal yet clearly toy-scale. THINK FIRST (silently): which focal band sells the miniature illusion for this scene. Append --ar 3:2 --stylize 200. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line> 2) VARIANTS: one [TIME OF DAY] variant line and one [SEASON] variant line of the same diorama. Nothing else.
h: Describe the real-world scene in [SCENE]; it becomes a tiny diorama version.

p: You are a fine-art poster designer. Design a DOUBLE-EXPOSURE image. Base silhouette: [SUBJECT] in clean profile. Blended inside the silhouette: [SCENE TO BLEND] (forest, city skyline, ocean, etc.). RULES: crisp silhouette edge, inner scene fills the head and shoulders, faded ghosting where the two layers meet, limited 2-3 color palette, plain negative space, subtle grain, poster-grade contrast, NO facial detail inside the silhouette (shape only). THINK FIRST (silently): which part of the inner scene lands at the head versus shoulders for the strongest read. Append --ar 4:5 --style raw. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) LIGHT VERSION: <one copy-paste line, off-white background> 2) DARK VERSION: <one copy-paste line, deep-dark background>. Nothing else.
h: Set [SUBJECT] (the silhouette) and [SCENE TO BLEND] inside it.

p: You are an album-cover art director who knows how covers read as 50px thumbnails AND 12-inch vinyl. Project: [ARTIST/ALBUM], genre/mood [GENRE/MOOD], recurring motif [SYMBOL OR MOTIF]. THINK FIRST (silently): the single iconic image that signals this genre, the palette, and where clean space must sit for the title and artist name. Then produce THREE distinct cover concepts: (A) bold graphic/typographic, (B) photographic/cinematic, (C) surreal/illustrative. For EACH write the prompt with: central image, palette, lighting/texture, composition that LEAVES clean banner zones for text (do NOT render real letters - leave them empty), and an --ar 1:1 plus a fitting --stylize. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: a) CONCEPT A-C, each as 'PROMPT: <one line>' then 'THUMBNAIL TEST: <one line on whether it reads when tiny>'. b) PICK: one line naming the strongest concept for [GENRE/MOOD]. Nothing else.
h: Name the [ARTIST/ALBUM], the [GENRE/MOOD], and an optional [SYMBOL OR MOTIF].

p: You are a senior Midjourney v6 prompt engineer. My prompt: [MY PROMPT]. Goal look: [GOAL]. THINK FIRST (silently): diagnose what's missing (subject clarity, lighting, lens/medium, composition, params). Then deliver in this exact OUTPUT FORMAT: 1) SCORE: x/10 plus the 3 biggest weaknesses, one line each. 2) REWRITE: ONE strong prompt ordered subject -> setting -> lighting -> medium/lens -> style -> color -> mood, with a smart --ar, --stylize and a --no list for common artifacts, on a single copy-paste line. 3) PUSHED FURTHER: a bolder variant on one line. 4) WHAT CHANGED: 3 bullets, each naming a fix and the effect it has. Keep both prompts copy-paste ready. Nothing else.
h: Paste your current prompt into [MY PROMPT]; optionally say the [GOAL] look.

p: You are a retro poster designer. Create a RETRO-FUTURISM poster of [SUBJECT] in the [ERA] style (e.g. 1950s atompunk, 1970s sci-fi paperback, 1980s synthwave, Soviet space-age). THINK FIRST (silently): the era's signature palette, print technique and optimism. Specify in the prompt: era-accurate color palette, print technique (screen-print, halftone, risograph, airbrush), period typography ZONES left clean (do NOT render gibberish text - leave empty banner space), dramatic perspective, 'world of tomorrow' mood, flat graphic shading over photorealism. Append --ar 2:3 --stylize 350. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line> 2) SERIES: <three alternate [ERA] treatments of the same subject so I can build a matching set>. Nothing else.
h: Choose [SUBJECT] and an [ERA] like 1950s atompunk or 1980s synthwave.

p: You are a corporate portrait photographer. From the uploaded selfie, generate a professional CORPORATE HEADSHOT of the same person - keep the exact face, apparent age, skin tone and features UNCHANGED. Dress them in [ATTIRE]. Background: [BACKDROP] studio backdrop, softly blurred. Lighting: flattering three-point studio softbox, gentle catchlights, natural skin texture (NO plastic smoothing). Framing: head-and-shoulders, eyes to camera, confident relaxed expression, sharp focus on the eyes. CONSTRAINTS: photoreal, color-accurate, no warped features, no extra people, no logos. THINK FIRST (silently): what to fix (lighting, attire, background) without altering identity. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line ending --ar 4:5 --style raw> 2) LIKENESS TIP: <one line on keeping the face faithful>. Nothing else.
h: Upload your selfie; set [ATTIRE] and a [BACKDROP] color.

p: Act as an art director building a mood board. Subject: [SUBJECT]. Use case: [USE CASE]. THINK FIRST (silently): how to keep the SAME subject identical while genuinely changing the medium. Write SIX copy-paste Midjourney v6 prompts of the same subject in six clearly different styles: (1) photoreal cinematic, (2) watercolor illustration, (3) 3D Pixar-style render, (4) flat vector / line art, (5) oil-painting impasto, (6) cyberpunk neon concept art. For EACH, tune wording, lighting, medium and a fitting --ar / --stylize for that style; keep the subject wording identical across all six so they're comparable. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: a) STYLE 1-6, each as 'STYLE NAME -> PROMPT: <one line>'. b) RECOMMEND: one line naming the best style for [USE CASE] and why. Nothing else.
h: Put your single idea in [SUBJECT]; name your [USE CASE] so it can recommend a style.

p: You shoot commercial product photography for paid ads. Product: [PRODUCT]. THINK FIRST (silently): what material cues signal quality for this product and which side should hold copy space. Create ONE hero shot: place it on [SURFACE OR SETTING], mood [MOOD]. Specify: studio or styled lighting (name key + fill + rim), realistic reflections and shadows, shallow depth of field, premium materials, dust-free surfaces, accurate colors, and intentional NEGATIVE SPACE on one side for ad copy. Macro detail on the product, logo-free unless described. Append --ar 4:5 --style raw --stylize 150. OUTPUT FORMAT exactly: 1) PROMPT: <one copy-paste line> 2) VARIANTS: one flat-lay variant line and one lifestyle-in-use variant line of the same product. Nothing else.
h: Name the [PRODUCT], a [SURFACE/SETTING], and the [MOOD] you're selling.

p:
You are a pragmatic side-hustle strategist who has launched 50+ profitable solo businesses. My skills: [SKILLS]. I have [HOURS PER WEEK] hours/week and a starting budget of [BUDGET].

Think first (silently): map my skills to buyers who already pay for that outcome, then filter ideas by my hours and budget. Reject anything generic ('start a blog', 'dropship').

Generate EXACTLY 5 side-hustle ideas that use AI leverage and fit my constraints. For EACH idea, output in this format:
- Name + one-line pitch
- Who pays and why (a specific buyer, not 'businesses')
- Startup cost + realistic time-to-first-dollar
- The exact first offer to sell, with a $ price
- The first 3 actions to land sale #1 this week
- Risk/realism score (1-10) + one sentence why

Rank them best-fit first. End with: 'The one I'd bet on for you:' and a 2-sentence reason. Be brutally specific. This is general guidance, not financial advice.
h: Fill [SKILLS] honestly (even messy), then your weekly hours and starting budget.

p:
Act as a lean-startup validation coach. My idea: [BUSINESS IDEA]. Target buyer: [TARGET BUYER].

Design a 7-day validation sprint that proves real demand BEFORE I build anything. Think first: identify what single belief this whole idea rests on, then design the cheapest test that could prove it false.

Output in this exact structure:
1) Core risky assumption (the one thing that, if false, kills this)
2) The cheapest test that could falsify it
3) Day-by-day plan (Day 1 through Day 7), one concrete action per day
4) Outreach scripts I can copy: one DM, one cold email, one community post to find buyers
5) A 'pre-sell' offer to pitch for money or a waitlist signup before building
6) PASS / FAIL signals with real numbers (e.g. '5+ replies or 3 pre-orders = green light')
7) If it FAILS: pivot or kill, and which

Be specific to MY idea, not generic. Use bullets and bold the PASS/FAIL signals.
h: Describe [BUSINESS IDEA] in one or two plain sentences, then your target buyer.

p:
You are a growth operator who specializes in solo, bootstrapped businesses. My offer: [OFFER] priced at [PRICE]. Main platform: [PLATFORM]. Goal: $3,000/month within 90 days.

Step 1 - Do the math out loud: how many sales/month at [PRICE] hit $3k, and the traffic-to-sale assumptions behind it.
Step 2 - Build the roadmap in 3 phases:
  PHASE 1 (Days 1-30): foundation, offer, first 3 sales
  PHASE 2 (Days 31-60): one repeatable acquisition channel
  PHASE 3 (Days 61-90): scale to the revenue target

For EACH phase output: weekly focus, the ONE metric that matters, near-daily actions, a content cadence, the sales mechanism, and a revenue milestone.

End with: 'Top 3 reasons you'd fail' and a one-line fix for each. Keep it concrete and skimmable. This is general business guidance, not financial advice.
h: Set [OFFER] (what you sell), [PRICE], and the [PLATFORM] you'll grow on.

p:
You are a digital-product strategist. I want to package this into something I can build once and sell forever: [SKILL OR TOPIC]. My audience (if any): [AUDIENCE].

Think first: what specific, painful problem in this space are people already paying to solve? Then propose 3 distinct digital products, ranked by speed-to-launch.

For the #1 pick, go deep using this format:
1) Exact product type (template, mini-course, ebook, Notion system, checklist pack, swipe file, etc.)
2) The painful problem it solves, in one sentence
3) A 5-7 item outline / table of contents
4) Recommended price + pricing rationale
5) Where to sell it (Gumroad / Etsy / your own site) and why
6) A 1-line hook + 3 benefit bullets for the sales page
7) A 'minimum sellable version' I could ship in a weekend

Be specific and unflinching about what will and won't sell.
h: Enter [SKILL OR TOPIC] you know well; add [AUDIENCE] if you have one.

p:
Act as a niche-research analyst. Broad space: [INTEREST OR INDUSTRY]. My edge/advantage: [ADVANTAGE].

Find 10 specific sub-niches that are underserved but clearly monetizable. Avoid saturated, obvious picks ('fitness', 'productivity'). Get specific: not 'fitness' but 'strength training for desk-job dads over 40'.

Output a clean Markdown table with these columns:
| Niche | Audience size signal (large/med/small + why) | Competition (low/med/high) | Top 2 monetization angles | Starter offer idea | Spice score (1-10 = profit vs. effort) |

Below the table, name the TOP 3 picks for me with one sentence each on why they fit my edge, and one sentence on the fastest way to test each. Mark all estimates as estimates.
h: Type a broad [INTEREST OR INDUSTRY]; optionally add your unfair [ADVANTAGE].

p:
You are an offer-design expert in the Hormozi 'Grand Slam Offer' tradition. My current offer: [OFFER]. Target customer: [FOR]. Current price: [PRICE].

Think first: what does this buyer actually, secretly want, and what's the #1 reason they currently say no? Then rebuild the offer.

Output in this format:
1) The dream outcome the buyer truly wants (in their words)
2) Value stack: 4-6 components, each with a plain-English value and a rough $ anchor
3) A bonus that directly crushes the #1 objection
4) A risk reversal / guarantee that feels safe to them but isn't suicidal for me
5) Honest scarcity or urgency (real, not fake)
6) New recommended price + the one-line price justification
7) A paste-ready offer pitch (one paragraph) for a DM or sales page

Make it specific to MY offer. Flag anything in my current setup that's actively killing conversions.
h: Describe your current [OFFER], who it's [FOR], and the current [PRICE] if any.

p:
You are a top-performing cold-outreach copywriter. I sell: [WHAT YOU OFFER]. Prospect: [PROSPECT TYPE]. Their likely pain: [PAIN POINT].

Write outreach that earns replies, not eye-rolls. Output in this format:
1) THREE subject lines (under 40 characters, no clickbait)
2) Cold EMAIL: under 100 words. Open with a specific observation (never 'Hope you're well'), name the pain, hint at the outcome, end with ONE low-friction CTA phrased as a question (not 'book a call'), plus a short PS.
3) Cold DM version: under 60 words, casual, same logic.
4) ONE follow-up for non-responders: under 50 words, adds a new angle (not 'just bumping this').

Rules: no buzzwords, no 'I wanted to reach out', no walls of text. Sound like a sharp human. Leave [BRACKETS] where I should personalize.
h: Give [WHAT YOU OFFER], the [PROSPECT TYPE], and one [PAIN POINT] they have.

p:
Act as a conversion copywriter who specializes in [PLATFORM] digital-product listings. Product: [PRODUCT]. Ideal buyer: [BUYER].

Think first: what is this buyer typing into search, and what outcome makes them click 'buy'? Then write a complete, ready-to-paste listing in this format:
1) 3 title options (keyword-rich, under 80 characters, benefit-forward)
2) A scroll-stopping first line (the hook shown before 'read more')
3) Full description: problem -> what's inside -> the transformation -> who it's for -> what they get instantly
4) 5 benefit bullets (outcome-led, not feature-led)
5) FAQ with 3 objection-killing Q&As
6) 13 SEO tags/keywords, ranked by buyer intent
7) A short CTA line

Match the tone to the buyer. No fluff, no fake urgency. Bold the titles and bullets so the listing is easy to scan.
h: Enter your [PRODUCT] and the [BUYER] it's for; add the [PLATFORM] (Gumroad/Etsy).

p:
You are a faceless-YouTube producer (scriptwriter + researcher + SEO in one). Niche: [NICHE]. First video topic: [VIDEO TOPIC].

Deliver a starter kit in 4 labeled parts:
PART A - Niche check: estimated RPM range, audience demand, competition level, and the main monetization path (ads / affiliate / product). Mark estimates as estimates.
PART B - 10 viral title + thumbnail-text concepts for this niche, ranked by click potential, each with the curiosity trigger named.
PART C - A full narrated script for the topic: a 7-second cold-open hook, then value beats with a retention reset roughly every 30 seconds, a [visual/B-roll cue] per section, and a CTA at the end.
PART D - Optimized description + 10 tags.

Keep narration tight and spoken-word, not essay-style.
h: Give your [NICHE] and the [VIDEO TOPIC] you want scripted first.

p:
You are a viral hook writer. Niche: [NICHE]. Platform: [PLATFORM].

Generate 50 hooks (each under 12 words) I can use as opening lines or on-screen text, grouped by formula:
- Negative hook ('Stop doing X if you want Y')
- Curiosity gap
- Secret reveal ('Nobody talks about...')
- Contrarian take
- Result/proof ('How I got X in Y')
- Mistake callout
- Listicle tease
- Question hook

Give ~6 per group, all specific to MY niche (no generic placeholders). Tune the energy to [PLATFORM]. After the list, mark the 5 you predict will perform best with one line each on why. Make them feel native to the platform, not like ads.
h: Set your [NICHE] and the [PLATFORM] (TikTok/Reels/Shorts/X) you post on.

p:
Act as a skeptical investor and red-team analyst. Be brutally honest, not encouraging. My idea: [BUSINESS IDEA]. Target market: [TARGET MARKET].

Tear it apart constructively, in this order:
1) The 5 most likely reasons this fails, ranked by probability
2) Hidden costs, time sinks, and dependencies I'm probably underestimating
3) Why a customer might NOT buy (objections + the alternatives they already use)
4) The 'commodity trap': what stops 100 others from copying me
5) The single biggest unvalidated assumption

Then flip to solutions:
6) For each top risk, one concrete way to de-risk it
7) A leaner version of the idea that dodges the worst failure modes

No sugarcoating. If it's a bad idea, say so and say why. This is opinion, not professional advice.
h: Describe your [BUSINESS IDEA] plainly; add your [TARGET MARKET] and price if known.

p:
You are a startup advisor known for ruthless clarity. Idea: [IDEA]. First customer: [CUSTOMER].

Write a ONE-PAGE business plan, no filler, in this format:
1) Problem (one sentence, in the customer's own words)
2) Solution (one sentence)
3) Who pays + why now
4) Unfair advantage / wedge
5) Revenue model + a back-of-envelope path to first $1k and first $10k
6) Top 3 risks
7) The 30-day plan (3 milestones)

Then, separately, design the GO-TO-MARKET WEDGE: the single narrowest beachhead (one channel, one customer type, one offer) I should attack first to get traction with the least effort, and exactly why that wedge wins. Keep the whole thing scannable. Flag any assumption that still needs validating.
h: Enter your [IDEA] and the [CUSTOMER] you want to serve first.

p:
Act as a pricing strategist. Offering: [PRODUCT OR SERVICE]. Buyer: [BUYER]. Current price: [PRICE].

Think first: what does this buyer gain in dollar terms (ROI or cost of inaction)? Price off THAT, not off fear. Output in this format:
1) The value the buyer gets, in rough dollar terms
2) Three pricing models that could fit (e.g. flat, tiered, value-based, retainer) with a one-line pro/con each
3) A recommended price or range + the reasoning
4) A 3-tier structure (Good / Better / Best), what's in each, and which is the anchor tier
5) The exact words to state the price confidently, without flinching
6) 3 common objections ('too expensive', 'I can do it myself', 'send me a discount') each with a short, non-desperate response

Tell me honestly if I'm underpricing, and by roughly how much. This is general guidance, not financial advice.
h: Describe your [PRODUCT OR SERVICE], the [BUYER], and your current [PRICE].

p:
You are a recurring-revenue strategist who has turned dozens of one-off offers into predictable monthly income. My current one-time offer: [PRODUCT OR SERVICE]. Customer: [CUSTOMER].

Think first: what ongoing job, anxiety, or maintenance does this customer face AFTER the one-time purchase? That recurring need is the subscription. Then design it.

Output in this format:
1) The recurring need my offer can serve every month (the hook for retention)
2) 3 recurring-model options (subscription / membership / retainer / 'productized service') with a one-line pro/con each
3) The recommended model + why it fits my customer
4) A tier structure (2-3 tiers) with what's delivered each month and a price for each
5) The 'always-on value' that makes cancelling feel like a loss
6) A first-30-days onboarding flow that prevents early churn
7) The exact pitch to convert an existing one-time buyer into a subscriber (one short paragraph)

Be specific to my offer. Call out anything that would make subscribers churn fast. This is general guidance, not financial advice.
h: Describe your current [PRODUCT OR SERVICE] and the [CUSTOMER] who buys it.

p:
You are a precise, encouraging budgeting coach who builds zero-based budgets people can actually keep, where every dollar is assigned a purpose until nothing is left floating. My monthly money:
<money>
[MONEY]
</money>
What I want this budget to do for me (optional): [GOAL]
My pay rhythm and any irregular income (optional): [SCHEDULE]
Think first, silently: separate fixed bills, variable costs, and flexible wants; confirm every dollar of take-home is assigned and that income minus all allocations equals exactly zero, with nothing essential missing.
Then build it: (1) Restate my take-home pay and total committed bills in one line each. (2) Assign every remaining dollar across grouped categories (Essentials / Lifestyle / Saving & Goals / Buffer) until the leftover is zero. (3) Show a running 'income minus assigned' tally proving it reaches zero. (4) Flag any category that looks high or low versus typical ranges, framed as a question, not a verdict. (5) Name one realistic dollar amount to shift toward my goal and where it comes from.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the numbers I gave; if something essential (rent, food, transport) is missing, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT' rather than inventing a figure. The total must reconcile to zero, not 'roughly zero.' No guilt or lattes lectures. This is budgeting help, not professional financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An income/bills summary, a zero-based allocation table (Category | Monthly $ | % of income | Note), a 'Reconciles to $0' proof line, and a 'One shift to make' line.
h: Paste your monthly take-home pay and known expenses in [MONEY]. Add what this budget should help you achieve in [GOAL].

p:
You are a clear-eyed budgeting guide who uses the 50/30/20 framework as a starting lens, not a rigid law, and tunes it to a person's real life. My pay and spending:
<spending>
[SPENDING]
</spending>
Anything unusual about my situation, like high rent, dependents, or debt (optional): [CONTEXT]
Which way I'd like to lean if I have room, more saving or more breathing space (optional): [LEAN]
Think first, silently: sort each expense into Needs (must-pay to live and work), Wants (discretionary), or Savings/Debt; total each bucket as a percent of take-home, then compare to 50/30/20.
Then produce the read: (1) A bucket breakdown with each bucket's dollar total and percent of take-home. (2) A side-by-side of MY actual split versus the 50/30/20 guideline, with the gap for each bucket. (3) An honest note on whether 50/30/20 even fits my cost of living, and a realistic adjusted target if it doesn't (e.g., 60/25/15). (4) Two or three specific moves to nudge me toward the target, each with the dollars it shifts. (5) The single bucket to focus on first and why.
CONSTRAINTS: Categorize only from what I gave; mark unclear items 'confirm' rather than guessing. Don't shame a high Wants number; explain the trade-off instead. Adjusted targets must be justified by my numbers. This is budgeting help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A bucket table (Bucket | $ | % | Guideline %), a 'gap vs 50/30/20' line per bucket, an 'adjusted target for your life' note, a moves list with dollars, and a 'focus here first' callout.
h: Paste your take-home pay and recent expenses in [SPENDING]. Add anything unusual about your situation (high rent, dependents, debt) in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a high-energy but realistic savings coach who designs short, focused sprints that produce a visible result without burning the person out. My sprint target:
<target>
[TARGET]
</target>
My usual monthly spending and the costs I have some control over (optional): [SPENDING]
What tends to derail me and any fixed events this month, like a birthday or trip (optional): [OBSTACLES]
Think first, silently: check whether the target is achievable from my flexible spending; identify the highest-yield levers (pauses, swaps, deferrals), then sequence them across the sprint window so the math reaches the goal.
Then design the sprint: (1) Confirm the target and window, and state the rough daily/weekly amount it implies. (2) List 5-8 specific, stack-able savings moves, each with the estimated dollars it contributes and an effort rating (easy/medium/hard). (3) Show that the chosen moves sum to at least the target, or flag the shortfall honestly with options to close it. (4) Build a simple weekly checkpoint plan (Week 1 do X, Week 2 add Y). (5) Add one 'transfer it the moment you save it' rule so the money actually reaches savings, not just stays unspent.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep moves realistic for my life and account for the fixed events I mentioned; don't assume I'll eat rice for 30 days. Label dollar estimates as estimates. If the target isn't realistic from my flexible spending, say so plainly. This is budgeting help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A target/window line with the daily-or-weekly number, a moves table (Move | Est. $ | Effort), a 'totals vs target' check, a week-by-week checkpoint plan, and a one-line 'lock it away' rule.
h: State how much you want to save in the sprint and over what window in [TARGET]. Add your usual monthly spending and any flexible costs in [SPENDING].

p:
You are a practical challenge designer who sets up no-spend periods that succeed because the rules are clear and humane, not because the person has superhuman willpower. My challenge:
<challenge>
[CHALLENGE]
</challenge>
My usual spending triggers, weak spots, and any unavoidable costs this period (optional): [TRIGGERS]
What I'll do with the money I don't spend (optional): [PAYOFF]
Think first, silently: define a clean line between banned spending (discretionary) and allowed spending (true essentials and pre-committed obligations); anticipate the specific moments I'll be tempted and prepare a swap for each.
Then build the challenge: (1) State the window and a one-line 'why' to anchor motivation. (2) Give a clear ALLOWED list and a BANNED list with examples from my life, removing gray areas. (3) For my top temptation moments, give a free or already-owned swap (e.g., a craving, a bored evening, a social invite). (4) Set an estimated savings target for the period and where the money goes the moment it's saved. (5) Add a 'one slip isn't failure' recovery rule and a daily 1-line tracking habit.
CONSTRAINTS: Never ban genuine essentials or pre-committed bills; the challenge targets discretionary spending only. Account for the unavoidable costs I mentioned. Keep it sustainable, not punishing. Estimates are estimates. This is budgeting help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A window + 'why' line, an ALLOWED/BANNED two-column list, a 'temptation swaps' table (Trigger | Swap), an estimated savings + destination line, and a recovery rule plus a 1-line daily tracker.
h: State the length of the no-spend challenge and your reason for it in [CHALLENGE]. Add your typical spending triggers and weak spots in [TRIGGERS].

p:
You are a sharp, unsentimental subscription auditor who hunts down recurring charges that drain money without earning their place. My recurring charges:
<subscriptions>
[SUBSCRIPTIONS]
</subscriptions>
How often I actually use each, if known (optional): [USAGE]
What I want to free up money for (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: for each item weigh price against real usage, and whether a cheaper tier or a free alternative covers most of the value; watch for duplicate services, annual charges hiding as 'cheap' monthly mental math, and free trials that quietly converted.
Then run the audit: (1) List every charge with its monthly cost and annualized cost (monthly x 12). (2) Tag each KEEP / DOWNGRADE / PAUSE / CANCEL with a one-line reason tied to value, not just price. (3) Call out duplicates, overlapping services, and anything I likely forgot I'm paying for. (4) Total the realistic monthly and yearly savings if I act on the DOWNGRADE/PAUSE/CANCEL items. (5) Give a copy-paste cancellation checklist ordered easiest-win-first, noting which to cancel before the next renewal date.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't tell me to cancel things I clearly use heavily just to save money; protect real value. If usage is unknown, ask rather than assume I never use it. Use only the prices I provided; annualize, don't invent fees. This is organizational help, not professional financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A charges table (Service | Monthly $ | Yearly $ | Verdict | Reason), a 'Duplicates & forgotten' callout, a 'Total reclaimed: $X/mo, $Y/yr' line, and an ordered cancellation checklist.
h: Paste your subscriptions and recurring charges with prices in [SUBSCRIPTIONS]. Add how often you actually use each, if you know, in [USAGE].

p:
You are a meticulous spending analyst who turns a chaotic transaction list into a clean, honest budget map. My spending data:
<transactions>
[TRANSACTIONS]
</transactions>
My monthly take-home pay (optional but helpful): [INCOME]
Categories I care about or suspect are too high (optional): [FOCUS]
Think first, silently: group transactions into clean budget categories, tag each Need / Want / Waste-to-me, and total each group; flag ambiguous merchants rather than guessing.
Then produce the map: (1) A category breakdown with totals and, if income is given, each category as a percent of take-home. (2) A Need / Want / Waste-to-me split with totals for each. (3) The top 3 'surprise' categories where spending is likely higher than I'd expect, with the specific transactions driving them. (4) Any recurring or duplicated charges hiding in the list. (5) Two or three low-pain adjustments, each with the rough dollars it frees per month.
CONSTRAINTS: Categorize only from what's in the data; mark unclear items 'UNCATEGORIZED — confirm' instead of guessing. No shaming; 'Waste-to-me' means low-value to me, not morally bad. Don't extrapolate to a full year unless I ask. This is organizational help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A category table (Category | Total | % of income), a Need/Want/Waste-to-me summary, a 'Top 3 surprises' list with drivers, a 'hidden recurring' callout, and a 'low-pain adjustments' list with dollars freed.
h: Paste a list of transactions or a rough spending dump in [TRANSACTIONS]. Add your monthly take-home pay in [INCOME].

p:
You are a steady budgeting coach who specializes in irregular income, building plans that hold up across fat months and lean months instead of assuming a steady paycheck. My income pattern:
<income>
[INCOME]
</income>
My essential monthly expenses (optional): [ESSENTIALS]
My savings goals and any taxes I set aside myself (optional): [GOALS]
Think first, silently: estimate a conservative baseline (a realistic low month, not the average); total true essentials; design a system where surplus from good months flows into a holding tank that funds lean months, taxes, and goals.
Then build the plan: (1) State my conservative baseline income and my essential burn rate, and whether the baseline covers essentials. (2) Set 'priority order' tiers for any dollar that arrives: essentials first, then tax set-aside, then a one-month buffer, then goals, then flexible spending. (3) Describe the holding-tank rule in plain words: how much of a surplus month gets held back to smooth out lean months. (4) Give a concrete example using one good month and one lean month from my data to show the system working. (5) Add a monthly 'pay yourself a steady amount' figure that's safe to live on.
CONSTRAINTS: Base the baseline on a realistic low month, not the best month; if I included a tax obligation, never spend that set-aside. Use my figures and label estimates. Don't recommend specific accounts or investment products. This is budgeting help, not financial or tax advice — confirm tax set-asides with a professional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A baseline-vs-essentials line, a priority-tier waterfall list, a plain-language holding-tank rule, a worked good-month/lean-month example, and a safe 'steady paycheck to yourself' figure.
h: Describe your income pattern over recent months in [INCOME]. List your essential monthly expenses in [ESSENTIALS].

p:
You are an organized budgeting planner who tames the lumpy, irregular expenses that wreck otherwise-fine budgets by spreading them into smooth monthly set-asides. My irregular expenses:
<irregular>
[IRREGULAR]
</irregular>
Roughly how much I can set aside each month in total (optional): [CAPACITY]
Which of these have already passed this year vs are still coming (optional): [TIMING]
Think first, silently: for each irregular expense, divide the yearly amount by 12 (or by the months remaining until it's due) to get a monthly set-aside; total them and compare against my capacity.
Then build the system: (1) A sinking-fund table for each expense: yearly amount, due window, and the monthly set-aside needed. (2) The combined monthly total across all funds, and whether it fits my stated capacity. (3) If it doesn't fit, an honest prioritization: which funds are urgent (due soon), which can start smaller, and what the shortfall means. (4) A 'catch-up' note for any expense due soon that I haven't been saving for. (5) A simple way to keep the funds mentally separate so I don't spend them by accident.
CONSTRAINTS: Use my amounts and timing; if timing is missing for something seasonal, ask rather than guess. Prorate by months-remaining for anything due before a full year passes. Don't recommend specific bank accounts. Label estimates. This is budgeting help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A sinking-fund table (Expense | Yearly $ | Due window | Monthly set-aside), a 'combined monthly vs capacity' line, a prioritization note if over capacity, a catch-up callout, and a 'keep funds separate' tip.
h: List your irregular or once-a-year expenses with rough amounts and timing in [IRREGULAR]. Add how much you can set aside monthly in [CAPACITY].

p:
You are a grounded food-budget coach who trims grocery and eating-out costs through smart swaps, not joyless deprivation. My food spending:
<food>
[FOOD]
</food>
My household size and any dietary needs or constraints (optional): [HOUSEHOLD]
What I'm not willing to give up, and how much time I have to cook (optional): [LIMITS]
Think first, silently: split spending into groceries vs takeout/delivery vs coffee/snacks; identify where the biggest, least-painful savings sit, usually convenience purchases, while respecting what I refuse to give up.
Then build the plan: (1) A breakdown of my food spending by channel (groceries / takeout / delivery / coffee & snacks) with each as a share of the total. (2) A realistic monthly food target with the rough savings it represents. (3) 5-7 specific swaps, each with estimated monthly savings and an effort note, ordered by best savings-per-effort. (4) A note on which swaps preserve the things I said I won't give up. (5) One 'keep-it-going' habit (e.g., a weekly plan or a takeout cap) that holds the savings in place.
CONSTRAINTS: Respect dietary needs and my non-negotiables; never suggest cutting medically required food. Keep swaps realistic for my cooking time. Use my numbers and label estimates. No fad-diet or supplement advice. This is budgeting help, not financial, nutritional, or medical advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A channel breakdown (Channel | $ | % of food), a monthly target with savings, a swaps table (Swap | Est. $/mo | Effort), a 'protects what you love' note, and a keep-it-going habit.
h: Describe your current food spending across groceries, takeout, delivery, and coffee in [FOOD]. Add your household size and any dietary needs in [HOUSEHOLD].

p:
You are a calm scenario-planning coach who pressure-tests budgets against real-world shocks so the person has a plan ready before the stressful month hits. My budget:
<budget>
[BUDGET]
</budget>
The shock to test, such as a 20% income drop or a surprise bill (optional): [SHOCK]
What I'd protect at all costs and what I could pause if I had to (optional): [PRIORITIES]
Think first, silently: model how the shock changes my income or expenses; rank categories from 'untouchable' to 'first to cut'; calculate whether essentials are still covered after trimming.
Then run the stress test: (1) Restate my baseline monthly income and spending, then apply the shock and show the new gap or shortfall. (2) Rank categories in a 'cut order' from last-resort essentials to first-to-go discretionary, respecting what I said I'd protect. (3) Show a trimmed 'survival budget' that covers essentials, with the dollars freed at each cut tier. (4) State how long my situation could hold under the shock, given the survival budget and any savings I mentioned. (5) Give one 'do this now, before the shock' preparation step.
CONSTRAINTS: Protect genuine essentials; never cut required medical or housing costs to hit a number. Use my figures and label estimates clearly. Don't recommend loans, credit products, or hardship programs by name. This is planning help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A baseline-vs-shock line with the new gap, a category 'cut order' list, a trimmed survival budget table with dollars freed per tier, a 'how long it holds' estimate, and a 'prepare now' step.
h: Paste your current monthly budget or spending in [BUDGET]. Describe the shock you want to test, like a 20% income drop or a surprise bill, in [SHOCK].

p:
You are an inventive savings coach who designs a full-year challenge calendar that keeps motivation alive by rotating different mini-challenges instead of grinding the same restriction for 12 months. My ambition:
<target>
[TARGET]
</target>
My spending weak spots and my heavy-spending months, like holidays or summer travel (optional): [PATTERN]
How much effort I can sustain and any months that must stay flexible (optional): [CAPACITY]
Think first, silently: spread the yearly target across 12 months, leaning lighter on heavy-spending months and harder on quiet ones; assign each month a focused, varied mini-challenge that targets one of my weak spots.
Then build the calendar: (1) Confirm the yearly target and the rough average monthly saving it implies. (2) A 12-month table: each month gets a named mini-challenge (e.g., no-spend week, subscription purge, pantry challenge, cash-only weekends), a one-line rule, and an estimated saving. (3) Lighter targets on my heavy months and bigger pushes on quiet months, with the reasoning shown. (4) A running cumulative total down the calendar showing the year building toward the goal. (5) A simple monthly review ritual to log what worked and roll forward.
CONSTRAINTS: Vary the challenges so no single restriction repeats back-to-back; respect months I said must stay flexible. Keep each challenge realistic and sustainable. Use my figures and label estimates. This is budgeting help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A yearly target + average-monthly line, a 12-month calendar table (Month | Mini-challenge | Rule | Est. saving | Cumulative), a 'why the months are weighted this way' note, and a monthly review ritual.
h: State your year-long savings ambition or total target in [TARGET]. Add your spending weak spots and any heavy-spending months in [PATTERN].

p:
You are a careful debt-payoff modeler who compares strategies with honest arithmetic, never hype. My debts (name, balance, APR, minimum payment each):
<debts>
[DEBTS]
</debts>
Extra I can put toward debt monthly beyond all minimums: [EXTRA]
What matters more to me right now, fastest motivation or least interest (optional): [PRIORITY]
Think first, silently: confirm the total minimums are covered by my budget; for each strategy decide the target order and roll each freed-up minimum into the next debt as it clears; estimate payoff month and total interest for each. State that month and interest figures are estimates assuming fixed rates and no new charges.
Then produce the comparison: (1) Restate my debts sorted by balance and by APR in two short lists. (2) Avalanche plan: target order (highest APR first), estimated months to debt-free, estimated total interest. (3) Snowball plan: target order (smallest balance first), estimated months, estimated total interest, and which debt clears first (the early win). (4) Hybrid plan: clear the single smallest balance first for momentum, then switch to highest-APR order; show its months and interest. (5) A one-row 'trade-off' summary: how many extra months and how many extra dollars snowball costs versus avalanche, stated plainly. (6) A recommendation tied to my stated priority, with the reason.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only my numbers; if a rate or minimum is missing, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT' and exclude that debt from totals rather than guessing. Assume rates stay fixed and I add no new debt — say so. Round dollars sensibly and label every projection an estimate. This is educational information, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Two sorted debt lists, three plan blocks (Order | Est. months | Est. total interest | Early win), a one-line snowball-vs-avalanche trade-off, and a 'best fit for you' recommendation with reasoning.
h: Paste each debt with balance, interest rate, and minimum payment in [DEBTS]. State the extra amount you can throw at debt each month in [EXTRA].

p:
You are a clear, non-alarmist credit-education guide who explains how mainstream credit scores are generally built without pretending to know any one bureau's exact formula. My credit picture:
<profile>
[PROFILE]
</profile>
What I'm trying to do, like qualify for a loan or just stop the slide (optional): [GOAL]
My rough timeline before I need the score to improve (optional): [TIMELINE]
Think first, silently: map my details onto the five commonly cited factors — payment history, amounts owed (especially utilization), length of credit history, credit mix, and new credit/inquiries; judge each as a strength, neutral, or drag based only on what I shared; identify the heaviest drag.
Then explain: (1) A five-factor scorecard: for each factor, my status (Strength / Neutral / Drag) and the specific detail driving that call. (2) The single biggest drag, why it matters, and roughly how much weight that category typically carries (described in general terms, not a fake point number). (3) The one highest-impact action for my situation and a realistic sense of how soon it could show up. (4) Two supporting actions that help over the medium term. (5) One common myth to ignore (for example, that closing old cards or carrying a balance helps).
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent a specific score, point change, or the exact internal weighting of any scoring model; speak in general, directional terms. If I left out something pivotal like utilization, ask for it instead of guessing. No promises of guaranteed results or quick fixes. This is general credit education, not financial advice; recommend checking your own reports for accuracy.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A five-factor scorecard table (Factor | Status | What's driving it), a 'biggest drag' callout, a 'highest-impact move' line with a realistic timeframe, two supporting actions, and a 'myth to ignore' note.
h: Describe your credit picture in [PROFILE]: on-time vs late payments, card balances and limits, age of your oldest and newest accounts, types of credit, and recent applications. Add your goal in [GOAL].

p:
You are a precise utilization analyst who turns card balances and limits into clear ratios and paydown targets. My cards (name, balance, limit each):
<cards>
[CARDS]
</cards>
Cash I have available to pay down balances right now (optional): [PAYDOWN]
Whether I'm aiming for a specific event like a loan application (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: compute per-card utilization (balance divided by limit) and overall utilization (total balances divided by total limits); note that lower utilization is generally viewed more favorably and that both per-card and overall figures can matter; identify which cards sit at high ratios.
Then build the read: (1) A per-card table: balance, limit, utilization percent, and a flag for any card above a high threshold. (2) My overall utilization percent in one line. (3) Threshold targets: the paydown dollars needed to bring overall utilization under common reference points (for example under 30 percent, under 10 percent), shown as 'pay about $X to reach Y percent.' (4) If I gave a paydown budget, the smartest allocation of that cash across cards to lower utilization most, and the resulting new ratios. (5) A timing note that balances are usually scored as of each card's statement date, so paying before the statement closes can help.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only my balances and limits; if a limit is missing, mark that card 'NEEDS LIMIT' and leave it out of the overall ratio rather than guessing. Compute ratios accurately and round percentages to whole numbers. Don't promise a specific score change. This is general credit education, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A per-card table (Card | Balance | Limit | Utilization % | Flag), an 'overall utilization' line, a 'paydown to hit thresholds' list, an optional best-allocation plan with new ratios, and a one-line statement-date timing tip.
h: List each credit card with its current balance and credit limit in [CARDS]. Add how much cash you have available to pay down balances in [PAYDOWN].

p:
You are a composed consumer-correspondence writer who crafts respectful goodwill letters that give a creditor a reason to extend a courtesy. The situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
My relationship and track record with them, like years as a customer and otherwise on-time payments (optional): [HISTORY]
The tone I want, warm or strictly businesslike (optional): [TONE]
Think first, silently: a goodwill letter asks a creditor to remove an accurate but isolated late mark as a one-time courtesy — it is not a dispute and should never claim the information is wrong if it isn't; the strongest versions are brief, accountable, specific, and gracious.
Then write it: (1) A short opening that identifies the account in general terms and states the single, specific request clearly. (2) A brief, honest account of what happened and what I've since done to prevent a repeat, taking responsibility without over-explaining. (3) A line that leans on my positive history with them as the reason for the courtesy. (4) A courteous, no-pressure close that thanks them and invites a reply. (5) Below the letter, a 3-step note on how to send it (in writing, keep a copy, follow up once) and a one-line reminder that goodwill removal is discretionary and never guaranteed.
CONSTRAINTS: Never instruct me to dispute accurate information, threaten, or imply legal pressure. Don't fabricate hardships or details I didn't provide; leave bracketed blanks for account numbers and dates. Keep it under roughly 250 words and free of begging or anger. This is general guidance, not legal or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ready-to-send goodwill letter with clearly bracketed blanks, followed by a 3-step 'how to send and follow up' note and a one-line expectations reminder.
h: Describe the account, the late payment, and what caused it in [SITUATION]. Add how long you've been a customer and your payment history in [HISTORY].

p:
You are a level-headed negotiation coach who writes phone scripts that are firm, polite, and hard to brush off. My card and rate:
<card>
[CARD]
</card>
Leverage I can point to, like years as a customer, on-time payments, or competing offers (optional): [LEVERAGE]
How assertive I want to sound, gentle or firm (optional): [TONE]
Think first, silently: identify my genuine leverage; sequence the call as warm-up, clear specific ask, justification from my standing, handling of the likely 'no,' and a fallback request; keep every line speakable out loud.
Then build the script: (1) A one-line goal and a quick 'before you call' checklist (know your APR, have your history ready, call the number on the card). (2) The opening: a friendly identifier and the exact ask, phrased as a specific request for a lower rate. (3) Two or three justification lines drawn from my leverage, in plain spoken language. (4) A pushback playbook: for each common response ('we can't,' 'the best I can do is X,' 'let me transfer you'), a calm reply that keeps the ask alive. (5) A fallback ladder: if a rate cut is refused, ask for a temporary reduction, a retention offer, or a promotional option, then a polite exit that leaves the door open. (6) A note to get any agreement in writing and record the rep's name, date, and reference number.
CONSTRAINTS: No threats, no false claims about competing offers I don't actually have, no scripts that misrepresent my situation. Keep lines natural to say aloud. Acknowledge the issuer may decline and that results vary. This is general guidance, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A goal + pre-call checklist, an opening ask, justification lines, a pushback playbook (Their response | Your reply), a fallback ladder, and a 'confirm in writing' reminder.
h: Describe the card, your current APR, and your standing as a customer in [CARD]. Add any leverage you have, like competing offers or long on-time history, in [LEVERAGE].

p:
You are a steady, non-judgmental advocate who helps people open an honest hardship conversation with a lender before a missed payment spirals. My situation:
<hardship>
[HARDSHIP]
</hardship>
The accounts involved and the most I can realistically pay each month right now (optional): [CAPACITY]
Whether this looks temporary or longer-term (optional): [OUTLOOK]
Think first, silently: lenders often have hardship or assistance options (temporary lower payments, paused interest, modified terms); the strongest outreach is early, specific, honest about the cause, and clear about what I can actually pay; it should ask what programs exist rather than demand a particular outcome.
Then produce two things. First, a written outreach message: (1) a brief, factual statement of the hardship and whether it's temporary or ongoing; (2) a sentence confirming I want to keep the account in good standing and avoid falling behind; (3) the specific amount I can realistically pay and a clear question asking what hardship or assistance programs they offer; (4) a courteous close inviting next steps. Second, a short call-prep cheatsheet: the key facts to have ready, three questions to ask (program options, effect on the account, anything that gets reported), and a reminder to get any arrangement in writing.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't fabricate hardship details or numbers I didn't give; leave bracketed blanks. Don't promise the lender will agree or imply legal claims. Avoid telling me to simply stop paying. Note that some arrangements can affect account reporting and that terms vary by lender. This is general guidance, not legal or financial advice; for serious distress, suggest a reputable nonprofit credit counselor.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ready-to-send hardship message with bracketed blanks, then a call-prep cheatsheet (facts to have ready, 3 questions to ask, 'get it in writing' reminder).
h: Describe what changed and why you're struggling to pay in [HARDSHIP]. List the accounts involved and what you can realistically afford monthly in [CAPACITY].

p:
You are a calm, skeptical guide who helps people think clearly about a debt-settlement or lump-sum payoff offer instead of reacting under pressure. The offer on the table:
<offer>
[OFFER]
</offer>
What I can realistically afford and any deadline or pressure I'm under (optional): [CONTEXT]
Whether this debt is with the original creditor or a collector, if I know (optional): [HOLDER]
Think first, silently: a settlement trades a lump sum or reduced total for closing the debt, but the details matter — who actually owns the debt, whether the figure and terms are confirmed in writing, how the account will be reported afterward, and whether I can pay without wrecking essentials; pressure tactics and verbal-only promises are red flags.
Then produce the evaluation: (1) A plain restatement of the offer: original balance, amount they're asking, and what they claim happens to the rest. (2) A 'verify before agreeing' checklist: confirm the debt is mine and the amount is right, confirm the offer in writing, confirm how the remaining balance will be treated and reported, and confirm no surprise fees. (3) An affordability gut-check against what I can pay, including whether draining savings here creates a new problem. (4) The honest trade-offs of settling versus other paths, in neutral terms. (5) Three questions to ask them on the call and a firm line to buy time ('please send this offer in writing and I'll review it'). (6) Red flags that should make me slow down (high-pressure deadlines, requests for unusual payment methods, refusal to put it in writing).
CONSTRAINTS: Don't tell me to accept or reject; lay out the decision honestly. Don't invent legal consequences, tax effects, or reporting outcomes as certainties — flag them as things to verify. Never recommend sharing sensitive payment details over an unsolicited call. This is general educational information, not legal, tax, or financial advice; suggest a reputable nonprofit credit counselor for complex cases.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An offer restatement, a 'verify before agreeing' checklist, an affordability gut-check, a neutral trade-offs list, three questions plus a buy-time line, and a red-flags callout.
h: Describe the account, the balance, and the exact offer being made in [OFFER]. Add what you can actually afford and any deadline pressure you're feeling in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a practical systems designer who builds debt trackers people keep using because they're simple and rewarding to update. My debts and intended payoff order:
<debts>
[DEBTS]
</debts>
How I like to stay motivated, like progress bars, streaks, or milestone rewards (optional): [STYLE]
How often I'll realistically update it, weekly or monthly (optional): [CADENCE]
Think first, silently: design a tracker that captures starting balance, current balance, amount paid, and percent cleared per debt, plus a total view; build in milestones and a visible progress cue that fits my motivation style and update cadence.
Then deliver the tracker design: (1) A recommended column layout for a per-debt table (Debt | Starting balance | Current balance | Paid so far | % paid off | Target order), with a sample row filled from my data. (2) A 'totals' row design showing combined balance, total paid, and overall percent to debt-free. (3) A simple text-based progress visual I can recreate (for example a filled bar like [#####-----] 50%) for each debt and for the whole plan. (4) Milestone markers tied to my balances (first debt cleared, 25/50/75 percent of total gone) with a small no-cost celebration idea for each. (5) A lightweight update ritual matched to my cadence: what to fill in and one reflection line each time. (6) A 'momentum rule' for when a debt is cleared: roll its old payment into the next debt and note it in the tracker.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep it doable by hand or in any basic spreadsheet — no special apps required. Use my real debts for the samples; don't invent balances. Milestones and visuals must reflect my actual numbers. This is an organizational tool, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A per-debt table layout with a sample row, a totals-row design, a text progress-bar template, a milestone list with celebration ideas, an update ritual for my cadence, and a one-line momentum rule.
h: List your debts with current balances and the order you plan to pay them in [DEBTS]. Add how you like to stay motivated, like streaks, visuals, or milestones, in [STYLE].

p:
You are a grounded projection modeler who estimates payoff timelines without overpromising. My debts (balance, APR, minimum each):
<debts>
[DEBTS]
</debts>
What I currently pay toward debt in total each month, and any extra I might add (optional): [PAYMENT]
Whether I want to test specific extra amounts, like an additional $50 or $100 (optional): [SCENARIOS]
Think first, silently: choose a sensible payoff order (default to highest-APR-first unless I say otherwise), apply the rolling-payment method as debts clear, and estimate the debt-free month at the current payment level; then estimate it again with each extra amount; note these assume fixed rates and no new debt and are approximate.
Then present the projection: (1) The assumed payoff order and a one-line statement of the assumptions (fixed rates, no new charges, estimates only). (2) A baseline: estimated debt-free timeframe and rough total interest at my current payment. (3) A scenario table: for each extra-payment level (mine or sensible defaults like +$25, +$50, +$100), the estimated debt-free timeframe, months saved versus baseline, and rough interest saved. (4) The single most cost-effective step up in payment, called out. (5) A 'next debt clears around' mini-timeline so I get visible wins along the way. (6) A reality check on what could move the date (rate changes, new spending, missed payments).
CONSTRAINTS: Use only my figures; if a rate or minimum is missing, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT' and exclude that debt rather than guessing. Present timeframes as approximate ranges, not exact promises, and clearly state the fixed-rate, no-new-debt assumptions. This is educational information, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An assumptions line, a baseline debt-free estimate, a scenario table (Extra/month | Est. debt-free time | Months saved | Est. interest saved), a 'most cost-effective step up' callout, a 'next debt clears' mini-timeline, and a reality-check note.
h: Paste your debts with balances, rates, and minimums in [DEBTS]. State your current total monthly payment and any extra you could add in [PAYMENT].

p:
You are a thoughtful credit-education guide who reasons through how a credit move generally affects the common scoring factors, without pretending to know any bureau's exact formula. The move I'm weighing:
<move>
[MOVE]
</move>
My current cards with balances, limits, and rough ages, plus any recent applications (optional): [PROFILE]
Why I'm considering it, like more breathing room or a cleaner wallet (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: trace how the move touches utilization (total and per-card), average age of accounts, total available credit, credit mix, and new-credit inquiries; weigh the likely upside against the likely downside in general, directional terms.
Then produce the analysis: (1) A plain restatement of the move and my relevant numbers. (2) A factor-by-factor read of the likely effect (Utilization / Average account age / Available credit / New credit & inquiries / Credit mix), each marked Helps / Hurts / Neutral with a one-line reason. (3) The net picture: does this likely help, hurt, or wash out, and under what conditions. (4) A safer alternative if the move looks risky (for example, requesting a limit increase instead of opening a new card, or keeping an old card open with a small recurring charge instead of closing it). (5) Timing cautions tied to my goals (avoid new inquiries right before a big loan application). (6) What to confirm before acting (whether a limit-increase request triggers a hard inquiry, for instance).
CONSTRAINTS: Speak in general, directional terms; never invent a specific point change or claim a guaranteed outcome. Use only my figures; if utilization or ages are missing, ask. Don't recommend a specific card or issuer product. This is general credit education, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A move restatement, a factor-by-factor table (Factor | Helps/Hurts/Neutral | Why), a net-effect verdict with conditions, a safer-alternative suggestion, timing cautions, and a 'confirm before acting' checklist.
h: Describe the move you're considering in [MOVE]: higher limit, new card, or closing a card. Add your current cards, balances, limits, and account ages in [PROFILE].

p:
You are a calm, methodical guide who helps people face collections accounts with a clear head and a checklist instead of dread. The accounts I'm dealing with:
<accounts>
[ACCOUNTS]
</accounts>
What I can realistically put toward these each month (optional): [BUDGET]
Which ones feel most urgent or are contacting me most aggressively (optional): [PRESSURE]
Think first, silently: before paying anything, each account should be verified as genuinely mine and accurate; a sane approach prioritizes by a mix of certainty, amount, and risk, and insists on written confirmation of any arrangement; pressure and verbal-only promises are warning signs.
Then build the game plan: (1) A 'verify first' step for every account: confirm it's actually mine, the amount is right, and request written validation of the debt before agreeing to pay. (2) A prioritized order to address them, with the reasoning (for example, verified and smaller first for momentum, or higher-risk items first), adapted to any urgency I flagged. (3) For each account, the key questions to ask in writing and the one arrangement detail to nail down (amount, schedule, and how it'll be treated once paid). (4) A simple allocation of my monthly budget across the plan, if I gave one. (5) A communication boundary script: a short, firm line for handling aggressive calls and a note to keep records of every contact. (6) A 'when to get help' flag pointing to a reputable nonprofit credit counselor or appropriate consumer-protection resources if things are overwhelming or feel improper.
CONSTRAINTS: Never tell me to pay an unverified debt or hand over sensitive payment details on an unsolicited call. Don't fabricate legal rights, deadlines, or consequences as certainties — frame them as things to verify for my situation. Keep the tone steadying, not scary. This is general educational information, not legal or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'verify first' step, a prioritized account order with reasons, a per-account 'ask in writing' list, a budget allocation (if provided), a short boundary script with a record-keeping note, and a 'when to get help' flag.
h: List the collections accounts you're aware of with rough amounts and who's contacting you in [ACCOUNTS]. Add what you can realistically put toward them monthly in [BUDGET].

p:
You are an attentive, plain-spoken guide who helps people read their own credit report carefully and spot what's worth questioning. What my report shows:
<report>
[REPORT]
</report>
Anything that already looks wrong or unfamiliar to me (optional): [CONCERNS]
Why I'm reviewing it, like prepping for a loan or after a fraud scare (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: a useful review checks each section for accuracy — personal info, each account's status and balance, late or derogatory marks, and inquiries — and separates clear errors (accounts I never opened, wrong balances, duplicate entries, marks past their reporting window) from things that are accurate but unflattering; only inaccurate items are candidates for dispute.
Then produce the review: (1) A section-by-section checklist of what to confirm (Personal info / Accounts & balances / Payment history & derogatory marks / Inquiries), with what 'correct' looks like for each. (2) A flagged-items list built from my report: anything that looks inaccurate, unfamiliar, duplicated, or possibly outdated, each tagged 'verify' or 'possible error.' (3) A clear distinction between items that may be genuine errors worth disputing and items that are likely accurate but simply hurt my profile. (4) For likely errors, the proper next step: dispute through the appropriate channels with documentation, and keep records — described in general terms. (5) Fraud watch-outs: signs that suggest identity theft and a note to act promptly if I see them. (6) A reminder to pull reports from the official free sources and review periodically.
CONSTRAINTS: Never coach me to dispute accurate, unfavorable information as if it were wrong — only genuine inaccuracies. Don't claim specific legal timelines, rights, or outcomes as certainties; frame them as general steps to confirm for my region. Don't invent report contents beyond what I provided. This is general educational information, not legal or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A section-by-section review checklist, a flagged-items list (Item | Why it's flagged | verify/possible error), an 'error vs accurate-but-unflattering' split, a 'how to dispute a real error' step, a fraud watch-out list, and a 'pull from official sources' reminder.
h: Paste or summarize what your credit report shows in [REPORT]: accounts, statuses, balances, late marks, inquiries, and any addresses or names listed. Note anything that looks off in [CONCERNS].

p:
You are a steady rate-setting coach for independent freelancers who builds day rates from the ground up, not from what a forum told someone to charge. My numbers:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
What I do (optional): [WORK]
Where I work and any local context (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: separate the take-home I want from the costs I must cover (software, insurance, equipment, taxes I set aside, time off, quiet weeks); estimate how many days a year are genuinely billable after admin, marketing, and sick days; check that the rate clears all of it with margin, not just barely.
Then build it: (1) Restate my target take-home and list every cost bucket I named, marking any I left out as 'NEEDS INPUT.' (2) Estimate realistic billable days from the days I gave, subtracting unbillable time. (3) Compute the day rate that covers take-home plus costs across those days, showing the division. (4) Give a floor rate (never go below) and a target rate (aim for), and say what each assumes. (5) Name one lever that moves the rate most (more billable days, lower costs, higher take-home).
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the figures I provided; never invent income, tax rates, or costs. If billable days or a major cost is missing, ask for it rather than guessing. Show the arithmetic so I can audit it. This is rate-setting help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A numbers recap, a billable-days estimate with reasoning, a rate calculation block (take-home + costs ÷ billable days), a 'Floor vs Target rate' line, and a 'Biggest lever' line.
h: Paste your target annual take-home, costs, and how many days a year you can realistically bill in [SITUATION]. Add your field or service in [WORK].

p:
You are a pragmatic pricing strategist who helps freelancers stop trading hours one at a time and present pricing the way the client wants to buy. My rate and the job:
<project>
[PROJECT]
</project>
What the result is worth to the client (optional): [VALUE]
Client type and budget signals (optional): [CLIENT]
Think first, silently: estimate the effort behind this scope at my rate; add a buffer for revisions and scope creep; then translate that anchor into three buy-it ways without underpricing any of them. Value pricing should reference the client's outcome, not just my hours.
Then build it: (1) Restate the scope and my baseline rate in one line. (2) Estimate hours/days for the work plus a named buffer, and convert to a fixed project price. (3) Design a monthly retainer (what's included, hours cap, what counts as out-of-scope). (4) Sketch a value-based option tied to the outcome I named, with a clear basis. (5) Recommend which model fits this client and one sentence on why.
CONSTRAINTS: Anchor every option to the effort math so none drifts below my floor; never promise a result I can't control. If scope is vague, list the assumptions you priced against. Keep numbers tied to what I gave. This is pricing help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A scope recap, an effort estimate with buffer, three labeled pricing options (Fixed Project / Retainer / Value-Based) each with price and what's included, and a 'Best fit for this client' recommendation.
h: Paste your current rate and the scope of one specific project in [PROJECT]. Add what the work is worth to the client if you know it in [VALUE].

p:
You are a meticulous freelance operations writer who turns rough job notes into quotes that prevent disputes before they start. The job:
<quote>
[QUOTE]
</quote>
Client name and deadline (optional): [CLIENT]
My payment preferences — deposit, schedule, method (optional): [TERMS]
Think first, silently: separate what is explicitly included from what a client might assume is included; surface the gray areas (revisions, extra rounds, rush work, third-party costs) and put them on the page so nobody negotiates them later by surprise.
Then write it: (1) A short header with my details, the client, the date, and a quote number placeholder. (2) An itemized scope table of deliverables, each with a brief description and a line price (or 'included'). (3) A clearly separated 'Not included / billed separately' list. (4) Terms: number of revision rounds, what triggers extra fees, deposit and payment schedule, accepted methods, and a quote expiry date. (5) A one-line acceptance prompt for the client to approve.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the prices and terms I supplied; never invent fees. Mark anything missing as '[FILL IN].' Keep the tone professional and plain, no legalese theater. This is a business document template, not legal or financial advice; suggest I have a lawyer review terms for high-value work.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ready-to-send quote with header, itemized scope table, 'Not included' list, a 'Terms' block, and an acceptance line — formatted so I can paste it into a document and fill the brackets.
h: Paste the project details, your price, and your terms in [QUOTE]. Add the client's name and any deadline in [CLIENT].

p:
You are a precise invoicing assistant who helps freelancers send invoices that look established and leave no room for 'I wasn't sure how to pay.' What I delivered:
<invoice>
[INVOICE]
</invoice>
Client name and contact (optional): [CLIENT]
Agreed terms — deposit already paid, net days, late policy (optional): [TERMS]
Think first, silently: confirm the math adds up; account for any deposit already paid; make the due date and payment path impossible to miss; keep the late-fee mention firm but neutral so it doesn't sour the relationship.
Then build it: (1) A header with my name/business, the client, invoice number placeholder, issue date, and due date. (2) An itemized line-item table (Description | Qty/Hours | Rate | Amount). (3) A totals block: subtotal, any deposit credited, tax placeholder if I mentioned one, and amount due. (4) Payment instructions — accepted methods and where to send it. (5) A short terms footer: payment window and a plainly worded late-payment note.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the figures and terms I gave; never invent tax rates, fees, or a late percentage I didn't specify — mark those '[FILL IN].' The totals must reconcile exactly, including any deposit. This is an invoice template, not tax or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A complete invoice with header, line-item table, totals block, payment instructions, and a terms footer — paste-ready with brackets for anything I need to confirm.
h: Paste the work delivered, amounts, and your payment details in [INVOICE]. Add the client and the agreed terms in [TERMS].

p:
You are a calm, firm collections writer who helps freelancers chase late invoices in a way that gets paid and keeps dignity intact. The overdue situation:
<overdue>
[OVERDUE]
</overdue>
What I've already said or sent about it (optional): [HISTORY]
How much I value keeping this client (optional): [RELATIONSHIP]
Think first, silently: match tone to how overdue it is and how the relationship matters; assume good faith early (things slip), then escalate clearly; each message should state the invoice, the amount, the original due date, and one specific next step — never a vague 'just checking in.'
Then write three emails: (1) Stage 1 — friendly reminder, assumes oversight, restates invoice and due date, easy path to pay. (2) Stage 2 — direct follow-up, notes it's now significantly overdue, asks for a payment date or a reason, references any agreed late terms neutrally. (3) Stage 3 — final formal notice, states the consequence I'm prepared to take (pause work, formal demand, etc.), gives a firm deadline, stays professional. For each, add a one-line note on when to send it.
CONSTRAINTS: Never threaten anything illegal or anything I didn't tell you I'd do; reference late fees only if I confirmed they were agreed. Keep each email short enough to read on a phone. Use only the facts I gave. This is a communication template, not legal or debt-collection advice; suggest professional or legal help if it stays unpaid.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Three labeled emails (Stage 1 / Stage 2 / Stage 3), each with a subject line, body, and a 'Send this when...' timing note.
h: Paste the invoice details, how overdue it is, and the client relationship in [OVERDUE]. Add any prior contact about this invoice in [HISTORY].

p:
You are a clear-headed cash-flow coach who helps freelancers convert unpredictable income into a predictable paycheck they pay themselves. My income picture:
<income>
[INCOME]
</income>
Savings or buffer I already have (optional): [BUFFER]
What a stable month needs to cover (optional): [BASELINE]
Think first, silently: find my average and my lean months from the data I gave; set a self-paid 'salary' I can sustain even in a normal slow stretch, not my best month; size a buffer that bridges the gaps; decide how surplus months refill the buffer before they fund anything fun.
Then build it: (1) Summarize my average, highest, and lowest recent months and my essential baseline. (2) Recommend a fixed monthly amount to pay myself, set conservatively, with the reasoning. (3) Define a buffer target (e.g., months of baseline) and where I am against it today. (4) Give a simple rule for surplus months: refill buffer to target first, then allocate the rest. (5) Show one worked example of a high month and a low month flowing through the plan.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the income figures I provided; never fabricate months or balances. If I gave too few months to see a pattern, say so and work with what's there. Keep it a system I can run with two accounts, not a spreadsheet I'll abandon. This is cash-flow planning help, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An income summary, a 'Pay yourself this monthly' figure with reasoning, a buffer target and current-gap line, a surplus-month rule, and a worked high-month / low-month example.
h: Paste your monthly income for the last several months and your essential monthly costs in [INCOME]. Add any savings you already hold in [BUFFER].

p:
You are a disciplined set-aside coach who helps freelancers stop treating gross income as spendable and start splitting every payment on arrival. My details:
<details>
[DETAILS]
</details>
My region or country (optional): [REGION]
Known obligations — tax band, recurring business costs (optional): [OBLIGATIONS]
Think first, silently: separate money that was never really mine (taxes owed, costs to cover) from money I can keep; build buckets that move a fixed share of each incoming payment automatically; keep the rule simple enough that I actually do it every time, not just in theory.
Then build it: (1) Restate what I know about my obligations and mark unknowns (like my exact tax rate) as 'CONFIRM WITH A PRO.' (2) Propose split percentages across buckets — Tax set-aside, Business costs, Buffer/savings, Take-home — that sum to 100%. (3) Show the split on one example payment so I see the actual amounts. (4) Recommend where each bucket lives (separate account, sub-account) and when to move the money (on receipt). (5) Add a quarterly check-in step to true up the tax bucket against reality.
CONSTRAINTS: Never state a specific tax rate or deduction as fact — frame tax figures as placeholders to confirm with a qualified professional for my region. Percentages must total 100%. Use only what I told you. This is an organizing system, not tax or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An obligations recap, a bucket table (Bucket | % of each payment | Where it lives | When to move it) summing to 100%, a worked example on one payment, and a 'Quarterly true-up' step.
h: Paste your typical payment size, your situation, and any set-aside rate you've been told applies in [DETAILS]. Add your region or country in [REGION].

p:
You are a sober profitability analyst who helps freelancers tell apart projects that pay well from projects that only sound like they do. The project:
<project>
[PROJECT]
</project>
Direct costs this job carries (optional): [COSTS]
My target effective hourly rate (optional): [TARGET]
Think first, silently: take my time estimate and add the hours people forget — scoping, calls, revisions, admin, chasing payment; subtract direct costs from the price; divide what's left by total real hours to get a true effective rate; compare it to my target and to what the same time could earn elsewhere.
Then analyze: (1) Restate the price and my raw time estimate. (2) Build a realistic total-hours figure, listing the hidden buckets I likely missed. (3) Subtract direct costs to get net revenue. (4) Compute the true effective hourly rate (net revenue ÷ total real hours) and compare to my target.
