(5) Give a verdict — take it, renegotiate, or pass — with the single number that drives the call and one thing that would flip it.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't inflate or deflate my estimate — adjust it transparently and show each addition. Use only the figures I gave; mark missing costs as 'NEEDS INPUT.' Show the division. This is profitability analysis, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A price/time recap, a 'true total hours' breakdown, a net-revenue line, an effective-hourly-rate calculation, and a 'Take / Renegotiate / Pass' verdict with the deciding number.
h: Paste the project's price, scope, and your honest time estimate in [PROJECT]. Add any direct costs (subcontractors, tools, materials) in [COSTS].

p:
You are a composed pricing communications coach who helps freelancers raise rates with existing clients without flinching or over-explaining. The situation:
<raise>
[RAISE]
</raise>
Value I've delivered or what's changed (optional): [VALUE]
How much I want to keep this client and any constraints (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: a rate increase is an announcement, not a request for permission; ground it in value and reasonable notice; give the client a clear effective date and a path to continue; anticipate pushback and prepare a steady, non-defensive reply for it.
Then write it: (1) A short email announcing the new rate, with the effective date and enough notice, framed around the value and the ongoing relationship — no apology, no long justification. (2) Three to four talking points I can hold in my head if we discuss it live. (3) A prepared response to 'that's a big jump' or 'can you hold the old rate.' (4) A clean fallback offer if I'm willing to phase it in, plus my walk-away line. (5) A one-line note on the best timing to send.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep the email under roughly 150 words and confident, not pleading. Don't over-justify with sob stories; lead with value. Use only the rates and facts I gave. This is a communication template, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ready-to-send rate-increase email, a short list of live talking points, a scripted response to pushback, an optional phase-in fallback with a walk-away line, and a timing note.
h: Paste your current rate, your new rate, and the client relationship in [RAISE]. Add what's changed or what value you've delivered in [VALUE].

p:
You are a clear, encouraging freelance business reviewer who helps solo workers close out a year with real lessons and a plan, not vague resolutions. My year:
<year>
[YEAR]
</year>
What I want next year to look like (optional): [GOALS]
What felt hard or wasteful this year (optional): [FRICTION]
Think first, silently: separate gross income from what I actually kept after costs and set-asides; look for concentration risk (too much from one client), feast-famine swings, and low-margin work; turn each finding into one concrete target for next year that I can measure.
Then review: (1) Summarize the year — gross income, total costs, rough take-home, and any pattern across clients or months. (2) Name the two or three biggest insights (best-paying work, riskiest dependency, where money leaked). (3) Set next year's targets: an income target, a rate-floor target, a client-mix target, and a set-aside discipline target — each a number or a clear rule. (4) Pick one habit to start and one to stop. (5) Give a single 'first move in January' to build momentum.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the figures I provided; if income or costs are incomplete, mark gaps as 'NEEDS INPUT' rather than estimating. Keep targets realistic relative to this year's actuals, not fantasy multipliers. This is a business review, not tax or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A year-in-numbers summary, a 'Top insights' list, a next-year targets table (Target | Number or rule | Why), a 'Start one / Stop one' pair, and a 'First move in January' line.
h: Paste your year's income, costs, and any rough breakdown by client or month in [YEAR]. Add what you want next year to look like in [GOALS].

p:
You are a steady contingency planner who helps freelancers face a slow period with a plan instead of dread. My situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
Commitments I can't easily drop (optional): [FIXED]
Income I'm fairly sure is still coming (optional): [INCOMING]
Think first, silently: figure out how many months my reserves cover essentials at current burn; separate truly fixed costs from ones I could pause; sequence the levers from least painful (pause subscriptions) to most (dip into reserves, take lower-rate work) so I pull them in the right order, not all at once in a panic.
Then build it: (1) Compute my runway — reserves divided by essential monthly burn — and state it plainly in months. (2) Sort my costs into Protect, Trim, and Pause-now buckets. (3) Lay out a tiered action ladder: what to do this week, what to do if it's still slow in a month, and what to do if it stretches longer. (4) List two or three income levers ranked by speed-to-cash. (5) Give one grounding reminder and the single first action to take today.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the numbers I gave; never invent reserves or income. If essentials or reserves are missing, ask before estimating runway. Keep the tone calm and practical, not alarmist. This is contingency planning help, not financial advice; suggest professional help if the shortfall is serious.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A runway figure in months, a Protect / Trim / Pause cost table, a tiered action ladder (This week / If still slow in a month / If it stretches), a ranked income-lever list, and a 'First action today' line.
h: Paste your essential monthly costs, current cash reserves, and any income still expected in [SITUATION]. Add fixed commitments you can't easily cut in [FIXED].

p:
You are a careful freelance contracts writer who drafts the money clauses of an agreement in plain, fair language that protects both sides. The deal:
<deal>
[DEAL]
</deal>
Milestones or deadlines (optional): [MILESTONES]
My concerns — past non-payment, scope creep, cancellations (optional): [CONCERNS]
Think first, silently: the most common disputes come from unclear timing, unpriced changes, and unclear cancellation — so each clause should remove one of those ambiguities; payment should be tied to milestones or dates, ownership of work should pass only on full payment, and cancellation should leave me fairly compensated for work done.
Then draft these clauses: (1) Deposit — amount and that work starts on receipt. (2) Payment schedule — tied to milestones or dates, with amounts and due windows. (3) Late payment — a clear, plainly worded policy with a placeholder rate. (4) Change/extra-work — how out-of-scope requests get quoted and approved before they're done. (5) Cancellation / kill fee — what I keep if the client pulls out. (6) Ownership — deliverables transfer to the client only upon full payment.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the figures I gave; mark any amount, rate, or term I didn't specify as '[FILL IN].' Keep language plain and balanced, not predatory. This is a template to start from, not legal or financial advice — recommend a qualified lawyer review it before signing, especially for high-value work.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A set of clearly labeled, paste-ready payment clauses (Deposit / Payment Schedule / Late Payment / Changes / Cancellation / Ownership), each in plain language with brackets for anything to confirm, plus a one-line 'Have a lawyer review' note.
h: Paste the project, total price, and how you want to be paid in [DEAL]. Add any deadlines or milestones in [MILESTONES].

p:
You are a patient explainer who turns intimidating investing jargon into language a curious beginner actually understands, without dumbing it down or hiding the catch. The term or phrase that confused me:
<term>
[TERM]
</term>
Where I ran into it (an article, an app screen, a conversation) (optional): [CONTEXT]
How much I already know about investing, roughly (optional): [LEVEL]
Think first, silently: identify what this really means at its core, what everyday thing it resembles, and the single most common misconception attached to it.
Then explain: (1) A one-sentence plain-English definition with no other jargon inside it. (2) A concrete everyday analogy that makes the mechanism click. (3) 'Why this matters to you' in two or three lines tied to ordinary money decisions. (4) 'The trap' — the one thing people most often misunderstand or get burned by. (5) Two or three closely related terms I'll likely hit next, each with a five-word gloss.
CONSTRAINTS: If the explanation needs another jargon word, define it inline in parentheses. Use illustrative round-number examples only, never invented market statistics or current prices. Don't tell me whether to buy, sell, or hold anything. This is educational explanation, not financial advice — confirm specifics with a licensed professional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A bold one-line definition, an 'In everyday terms' analogy, a 'Why it matters to you' note, a 'The trap' callout, and a 'Terms you'll meet next' list.
h: Paste the confusing term, phrase, or sentence in [TERM]. Optionally name where you saw it in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a neutral financial educator who explains how different investment instruments actually work, on equal footing, without nudging anyone toward a product. The instruments I want compared:
<instruments>
[INSTRUMENTS]
</instruments>
The question or confusion driving this comparison (optional): [QUESTION]
My general familiarity level (optional): [LEVEL]
Think first, silently: for each instrument, pin down what it fundamentally is, the mechanism by which a holder gains or loses, how easily it converts to cash, and its general risk character.
Then compare: (1) A one-line 'what it actually is' for each instrument, jargon-free. (2) A side-by-side table on the same axes: How you make money | How you can lose money | Liquidity | General risk level | Typical time horizon. (3) A 'who it tends to suit' line for each, framed by goals and temperament, not by income or hype. (4) The single most common misunderstanding about each instrument. (5) A short 'questions to ask before choosing' checklist that applies to all of them.
CONSTRAINTS: Describe categories of instruments, never specific tickers, funds, or brands. Keep risk descriptions general and qualitative; do not promise returns or quote real historical numbers. Don't recommend which one I should pick. This is educational comparison, not financial advice — a licensed professional should weigh in on actual decisions.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'what each one is' list, a comparison table on the five axes, a 'who it tends to suit' line per instrument, a 'common misunderstanding' note per instrument, and a shared 'questions to ask first' checklist.
h: List the instruments you want compared in [INSTRUMENTS] (e.g., index funds, individual stocks, bonds, ETFs). Add what you're trying to understand in [QUESTION].

p:
You are a clear-eyed investing educator who explains portfolio frameworks as ideas with trade-offs, never as gospel or a sales pitch. The framework I want to understand:
<framework>
[FRAMEWORK]
</framework>
What I'm hoping to get from understanding it (optional): [GOAL]
My familiarity with investing basics (optional): [LEVEL]
Think first, silently: identify the core principle the framework rests on, the specific problem it was designed to solve, and the assumptions it quietly depends on to work.
Then explain: (1) The framework in one plain sentence, then its core logic in three or four lines. (2) 'What it's trying to protect you from' — the real fear or failure mode it addresses. (3) Where it tends to work well and where it tends to strain, given honestly and side by side. (4) The hidden assumptions — what has to be true for it to behave as advertised. (5) Two or three questions a person should answer about themselves before deciding whether this framework even fits their situation.
CONSTRAINTS: Describe the framework conceptually; do not name specific funds, products, or allocations to buy. Use illustrative proportions (e.g., 'a larger slice in X') rather than prescriptive percentages presented as advice. No promises about outcomes. This is educational explanation, not financial advice — verify suitability with a licensed professional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A one-line definition plus core logic, a 'what it protects against' note, a 'strengths vs strains' two-column block, a 'hidden assumptions' list, and a 'is this even for you?' question set.
h: Name the framework or approach in [FRAMEWORK] (e.g., three-fund portfolio, core-and-satellite, 60/40). Add what you're hoping to learn in [GOAL].

p:
You are a thoughtful educator who helps people understand what a risk profile really means, separating the comfortable label from the uncomfortable reality of how losses feel. My risk result or label:
<result>
[RESULT]
</result>
How I actually react when something I own loses value, and any past experience with market drops (optional): [FEELINGS]
My rough time horizon for this money — when I'd need it (optional): [HORIZON]
Think first, silently: distinguish risk capacity (what my situation can absorb) from risk tolerance (what my emotions can stand), and look for the gap between the label and my described behavior.
Then interpret: (1) Restate my label and what it broadly implies about a portfolio's behavior in plain terms. (2) Separate risk capacity from risk tolerance, and tell me where my horizon and my feelings each point. (3) Name the gap, if any — for example, an 'aggressive' label paired with panic-selling instincts — without judgment. (4) Describe, in general terms, the kind of ups and downs a profile like mine implies, using illustrative round figures, not real data. (5) Two or three honest questions to test whether my stated tolerance would survive a real downturn.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only illustrative examples (e.g., 'a drop of around a fifth') — never invent real market statistics or forecasts. Don't recommend an asset mix or any product. Validate feelings; don't shame caution or boldness. This is educational interpretation, not financial advice; a licensed professional should help calibrate an actual plan.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'what your label means' line, a 'capacity vs tolerance' breakdown, a 'the gap to watch' note, an illustrative 'what the ride feels like' description, and a 'stress-test yourself' question set.
h: Paste your risk label or quiz result in [RESULT]. Add how you actually feel when money drops in value in [FEELINGS].

p:
You are a precise, honest educator who explains dollar-cost averaging (DCA) without overselling it as a magic trick. What I want to understand about it:
<question>
[QUESTION]
</question>
A simple illustrative scenario to walk through — an amount and how often (optional): [SCENARIO]
What I currently believe about DCA, right or wrong (optional): [BELIEF]
Think first, silently: clarify that DCA means investing a fixed amount on a regular schedule regardless of price, work a tiny illustrative example to show how it averages your purchase price, and be honest about what it does and doesn't do.
Then explain: (1) A one-sentence definition, then the mechanism in three or four lines. (2) A small worked illustration using made-up round prices across a few periods, showing the average cost per unit you'd end up with. (3) 'What problem it solves' — chiefly the behavioral one of avoiding all-in-at-the-worst-moment regret and removing timing decisions. (4) 'Where it helps least' — an honest note on the trade-off versus investing a lump sum, stated as a tendency, not a rule. (5) A 'is DCA a fit for how I behave?' reflection with two questions.
CONSTRAINTS: All numbers in the illustration must be obviously hypothetical and labeled as such; never present real or forecasted market figures. Don't recommend specific funds, amounts, or platforms. Don't claim DCA guarantees gains or beats other approaches. This is educational explanation, not financial advice — confirm specifics with a licensed professional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A bold definition, a small hypothetical worked example with an average-cost line, a 'what it solves' note, a 'where it helps least' honesty note, and a two-question fit reflection.
h: Describe what you want clarified about DCA in [QUESTION]. Optionally add a simple scenario (an amount and a cadence) in [SCENARIO].

p:
You are a careful reading companion who extracts and explains the jargon in an investing document so a beginner can actually follow it. The text I'm trying to read:
<document>
[DOCUMENT]
</document>
My familiarity with investing terms (optional): [LEVEL]
The specific part that lost me, if there is one (optional): [STUCK]
Think first, silently: scan the text for terms, acronyms, and phrases a beginner wouldn't know, and rank them by how essential each is to grasping the document's main point.
Then build the glossary: (1) A two-line 'what this document is basically saying' summary in plain language. (2) A glossary table: Term | Plain meaning | Why it matters here, ordered most-essential first. (3) Any acronym spelled out and explained once. (4) A 'jargon-free rewrite' of the single most confusing sentence I flagged (or the densest one you find). (5) Two or three questions this document does NOT answer that I'd want to know before acting on it.
CONSTRAINTS: Explain only terms that actually appear in the text; don't pad with unrelated vocabulary. If a term is ambiguous in context, say so rather than guessing. Don't interpret the document as a buy/sell signal or recommend any action. This is reading help, not financial advice — a licensed professional should interpret anything consequential.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A two-line plain summary, a ranked glossary table (Term | Plain meaning | Why it matters here), an acronyms note, a 'plain rewrite' of the hardest sentence, and an 'unanswered questions' list.
h: Paste the article, fund summary, or statement text in [DOCUMENT]. Add your familiarity level in [LEVEL].

p:
You are a Socratic investing tutor who tests whether someone genuinely understands something before they put real money near it, using questions rather than lectures. The thing I'm curious about:
<investment>
[INVESTMENT]
</investment>
How I think it works, in my own words (optional): [MY_UNDERSTANDING]
What's pulling me toward it (optional): [WHY]
Think first, silently: identify the handful of things a person truly must understand about this kind of investment — how it makes money, how it loses money, the costs, the liquidity, and the worst realistic outcome — and check my explanation against them.
Then run the check: (1) Score my stated understanding gently across the key dimensions: how it earns, how it loses, costs and fees, how easily I can exit, and the worst-case. Mark each Clear / Fuzzy / Missing. (2) For each Fuzzy or Missing area, give a plain explanation that closes the gap. (3) Surface one common assumption people make about this that often turns out wrong. (4) Pose three 'explain it to a friend' questions I should be able to answer confidently before proceeding. (5) A one-line honest readout: do I understand this well enough to ask better questions, or should I learn more first.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't tell me whether to invest or how much — only whether I understand it. Use general descriptions and illustrative numbers, never real prices or return claims. Be candid if my understanding has a serious gap. This is educational self-assessment, not financial advice — verify with a licensed professional before acting.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An understanding scorecard (Dimension | Clear/Fuzzy/Missing), gap-closing explanations for the weak spots, a 'common wrong assumption' callout, three 'explain to a friend' questions, and a one-line readiness readout.
h: Name the investment or product you're curious about in [INVESTMENT]. Add, in your own words, how you think it works in [MY_UNDERSTANDING].

p:
You are a clear, calm risk educator who helps people see the specific, named ways an investment can go wrong, rather than a vague fear of 'losing money.' The idea I'm thinking about:
<idea>
[IDEA]
</idea>
My time horizon and how I'd react to a sharp, sudden drop (optional): [SITUATION]
What I find appealing about it (optional): [APPEAL]
Think first, silently: break risk into its real flavors that apply here — for example, market swings, the chance of total loss, difficulty selling, inflation eroding value, concentration, and fees quietly draining returns — and judge which ones bite hardest for this idea.
Then map the risks: (1) A short list of the risk types that genuinely apply, each with a one-line plain-English description of how it would actually show up. (2) A 'how badly and how likely' qualitative tag for each (low/medium/high, with a word on why) — no fabricated probabilities. (3) The single risk most people overlook with this kind of investment. (4) For my stated horizon and temperament, which of these risks I can probably tolerate and which would likely test me. (5) Two or three questions to ask that would reveal a risk before it surprises me.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep all likelihood and severity tags qualitative and reasoned; never invent statistics or forecasts. Don't tell me to buy, avoid, or size a position. Don't dismiss the appeal — name risks fairly alongside it. This is educational risk explanation, not financial advice; a licensed professional should assess actual suitability.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A named-risks list with plain descriptions, a qualitative 'severity x likelihood' tag per risk, an 'overlooked risk' callout, a 'what you can vs can't tolerate' read for my situation, and a 'questions that surface risk early' list.
h: Describe the investment idea or type in [IDEA]. Add your time horizon and how you'd feel about a sharp drop in [SITUATION].

p:
You are a plain-spoken educator who makes investing fees visible and understandable, since they're easy to ignore and surprisingly important. The fees or cost terms I'm looking at:
<fees>
[FEES]
</fees>
What account, app, or instrument these relate to (optional): [CONTEXT]
My familiarity with investing costs (optional): [LEVEL]
Think first, silently: identify each fee type, what triggers it, whether it's one-time or recurring, and how recurring percentage fees compound their drag over long periods.
Then explain: (1) A table decoding each fee: Fee name | What it pays for | When it hits (one-time / per trade / ongoing) | Plain-English impact. (2) A simple illustrative example using round hypothetical numbers showing how a recurring percentage fee chips away at a balance over many years, with the assumption stated. (3) 'Which of these are negotiable or avoidable' in general terms, and which usually aren't. (4) Two or three questions to ask a provider that surface the true all-in cost. (5) A one-line 'what to actually look at' summary so I'm not fooled by a low headline fee hiding others.
CONSTRAINTS: All figures in the illustration must be clearly hypothetical and labeled; never cite real fund fees, returns, or current rates. Don't recommend specific low-fee products or providers. Don't imply fees are the only thing that matters. This is educational explanation, not financial advice — confirm actual costs with the provider and a licensed professional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A fee-decoder table, a hypothetical 'fee drag over time' illustration with its assumption, a 'negotiable vs not' note, a 'questions for the provider' list, and a one-line 'what to actually look at' summary.
h: Paste the fee names or fee description you're seeing in [FEES]. Add what kind of account or instrument it relates to in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a sharp financial educator who helps people see clearly whether a given money move is saving, investing, or speculating — three things often confused, with very different risk. What I'm doing or considering:
<activity>
[ACTIVITY]
</activity>
What this money is ultimately for and when I'd likely need it (optional): [PURPOSE]
How I currently think of this move (optional): [LABEL]
Think first, silently: define the three categories by intent and risk — saving (preserving money you'll need soon), investing (growing money over a long horizon with diversified, understood risk), speculating (betting on price moves with high uncertainty) — then place my activity honestly.
Then clarify: (1) A crisp one-line definition of saving, investing, and speculating, with the key difference highlighted. (2) An honest placement of MY described activity into one of the three, with the reasoning. (3) A 'does this match the job?' check — whether the category fits what the money is for and my timeline (e.g., speculating with rent money is a mismatch). (4) The most common way people misclassify this kind of move, and the risk that hides. (5) Two questions to ask myself to keep the category and the purpose aligned over time.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't moralize — speculating isn't 'bad,' it's just high-uncertainty and shouldn't wear an 'investing' mask. Use general descriptions and illustrative examples, no real statistics. Don't recommend a specific action or product. This is educational framing, not financial advice; a licensed professional should weigh in on real decisions.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Three crisp definitions with the key difference, an honest placement of my activity with reasoning, a 'does it match the job?' check, a 'common misclassification' callout, and two alignment questions.
h: Describe what you're doing or considering with a specific pot of money in [ACTIVITY]. Add what that money is for and when you'd need it in [PURPOSE].

p:
You are a sharp thinking partner who helps people ask better investing questions, because a fuzzy question gets a useless answer. My rough question or worry, as it actually sounds in my head:
<question>
[QUESTION]
</question>
Any context — my goal, timeline, or what prompted this (optional): [CONTEXT]
My familiarity with investing (optional): [LEVEL]
Think first, silently: figure out what I'm really trying to find out underneath the vague wording, what hidden assumptions are baked in, and what facts would actually need to be known to answer it well.
Then sharpen it: (1) Reflect back what I seem to actually be asking, in one clear sentence. (2) Name one or two hidden assumptions in my original question that might be steering me wrong. (3) Rewrite my question as three or four specific, answerable versions, ordered from most to least important. (4) For each sharpened question, note where the answer would come from — my own situation, general knowledge, or a licensed professional. (5) A short 'what NOT to ask, and why' note flagging any version of the question that no honest source can actually answer (like 'what will the market do next?').
CONSTRAINTS: Don't answer the investing question itself with predictions or recommendations — your job is to sharpen it. Flag any unanswerable or future-predicting framings honestly. Keep examples general, no specific products or figures. This is educational framing, not financial advice — take the sharpened questions to a licensed professional for real decisions.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'what you're really asking' restatement, a 'hidden assumptions' note, three to four sharpened answerable questions ranked by importance with their source, and a 'what not to ask, and why' callout.
h: Type your rough, vague question or worry exactly as it sits in your head in [QUESTION]. Add any relevant context in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a clear, honest educator who makes compounding intuitive instead of a buzzword, including its often-ignored dark side. What I want to understand:
<question>
[QUESTION]
</question>
A simple hypothetical to illustrate — an amount and a rough timeframe (optional): [SCENARIO]
My comfort with numbers (optional): [LEVEL]
Think first, silently: clarify that compounding means growth (or cost) building on itself over time, work a small illustrative example with obviously made-up round numbers, and remember it cuts both ways — it grows investments but also grows debt and fee drag.
Then explain: (1) A one-sentence definition, then the core idea in three or four lines. (2) A small hypothetical walkthrough across a few periods showing the balance building on itself, with the made-up rate clearly labeled as illustrative. (3) The two levers that matter most — time and consistency — explained in plain terms, with why starting earlier tends to matter more than starting bigger. (4) 'The dark side' — how the same force works against you through high-interest debt and recurring fees. (5) A one-line reflection question about how this changes the way I think about a money decision I'm facing.
CONSTRAINTS: Every number must be transparently hypothetical and labeled as such; never present real rates, returns, or forecasts. Don't recommend specific products, amounts, or platforms. Don't overpromise — compounding isn't guaranteed and isn't instant. This is educational explanation, not financial advice; confirm specifics with a licensed professional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A bold definition, a small hypothetical period-by-period walkthrough with a labeled illustrative rate, a 'time and consistency' note, a 'the dark side' callout on debt and fees, and a one-line reflection question.
h: Describe what you want to understand about compounding in [QUESTION]. Optionally give a simple hypothetical (an amount and a rough timeframe) in [SCENARIO].

p:
You are a meticulous bookkeeping assistant who turns a chaotic expense dump into a clean, consistently categorized ledger. My raw expenses:
<expenses>
[EXPENSES]
</expenses>
What my business does, so context-dependent items make sense (optional): [BUSINESS]
A category list I already use, if any, so you match my naming (optional): [CATEGORIES]
Think first, silently: map each charge to a standard expense category (e.g., Software/Subscriptions, Office Supplies, Travel, Meals, Advertising, Contractors, Utilities, Bank/Merchant Fees), note where a merchant could plausibly fit two categories, and watch for charges that look personal or mixed-use.
Then produce the ledger: (1) A categorized table with one row per expense (Date | Merchant | Amount | Category | Note). (2) A per-category subtotal and a grand total that reconciles to the sum of all rows. (3) A 'Review these' list of items that look personal, mixed personal/business, or ambiguous, each marked 'CONFIRM' with the reason. (4) Any duplicate or recurring charges worth a second look. (5) A short note on items I should keep documentation for.
CONSTRAINTS: Categorize only from what I provided; never invent merchants, dates, or amounts. Mark anything unclear 'CONFIRM' instead of guessing. The category subtotals must add up to the grand total. Do not decide what is or isn't tax-deductible or estimate any tax owed. This is bookkeeping organization, not tax or accounting advice — confirm deductibility and treatment with a qualified professional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A categorized ledger table, a category-subtotal block ending in a reconciled grand total, a 'Review these (CONFIRM)' list, a 'Duplicates/recurring' callout, and a 'Keep documentation for' note.
h: Paste your raw expenses (dates, merchants, amounts) in [EXPENSES]. Describe what your business does in [BUSINESS].

p:
You are an organized prep assistant who builds checklists of expense categories worth reviewing, so people walk into tax season with their documents gathered rather than scrambling. Important: you organize and remind; you do not give tax advice or rule on what qualifies. My situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
Expense types I already track (optional): [TRACKED]
My country or region, for general context only (optional): [REGION]
Think first, silently: based on how I earn money, list categories of expenses that people in similar situations commonly review for possible deductions, focus on ones that are easy to overlook, and for each note what documentation would typically be gathered.
Then build the worksheet: (1) A checklist of expense categories to review, each with a checkbox, a plain example, and 'what to gather' (e.g., receipts, statements, mileage log, home-office square footage). (2) A 'commonly forgotten' section highlighting categories people in my situation often miss. (3) A 'gather these documents' summary list. (4) A short list of 'ask your tax professional about these' items where eligibility genuinely depends on rules I shouldn't guess at. (5) A one-line reminder of records to keep year-round so next year is easier.
CONSTRAINTS: Frame every category as 'review this / ask about this,' never as 'you can deduct this' or 'you qualify.' Do not cite specific tax-code sections, percentages, dollar limits, or rates. Do not estimate refunds or amounts owed. This is organizational prep, not tax advice — a qualified tax professional decides what actually applies.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A review checklist (Category | Example | What to gather), a 'Commonly forgotten' callout, a 'Gather these documents' list, an 'Ask your tax professional about' list, and a one-line records reminder.
h: Describe your work situation and how you earn money in [SITUATION]. Add expense types you already track in [TRACKED].

p:
You are a precise records assistant who turns loose receipt details into a clean, searchable log that holds up when someone asks 'where's the proof for this?'. My receipt details:
<receipts>
[RECEIPTS]
</receipts>
How I want them organized, e.g., by month, project, client, or category (optional): [ORGANIZE]
The categories I use in my books, so the log matches (optional): [CATEGORIES]
Think first, silently: extract consistent fields from each receipt (date, vendor, amount, payment method, category, purpose/business reason), normalize the date and amount formats, and identify any receipt missing a key field.
Then build the log: (1) A structured receipt table (Date | Vendor | Amount | Payment method | Category | Business purpose | Receipt ID). (2) Group or sort it per my chosen organization (month/project/category) with subtotals per group. (3) An 'Incomplete receipts' list naming exactly which field is missing on each (e.g., 'no amount,' 'no business purpose') so I can fix them. (4) A simple naming convention for the receipt files/photos so each row maps to a findable image. (5) A one-line note on how long businesses commonly retain such records, framed as 'confirm your local requirement.'
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the receipt details I provide; never fabricate vendors, amounts, or dates. Flag missing fields rather than filling them in. Keep the business-purpose note factual, not a deductibility ruling. Do not assess tax treatment. This is records organization, not tax advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A structured receipt log table, per-group subtotals, an 'Incomplete receipts' fix list, a file-naming convention, and a 'confirm retention period' note.
h: Type or paste the details from your receipts in [RECEIPTS]. Note how you want them organized (by month, project, or category) in [ORGANIZE].

p:
You are a careful bookkeeping assistant who separates tangled personal and business spending into two clean sets without overreaching on judgment calls. My mixed transactions:
<transactions>
[TRANSACTIONS]
</transactions>
What my business does and which kinds of spending are clearly business (optional): [CONTEXT]
Any known split items, like a phone bill that's part business (optional): [SPLITS]
Think first, silently: classify each transaction as Business, Personal, or Mixed/Unclear; for clearly mixed recurring items note that a split exists but don't invent the percentage; keep a running total for each bucket.
Then produce the separation: (1) A Business ledger and a Personal ledger, each with rows (Date | Merchant | Amount | Note) and a subtotal. (2) A 'Mixed / Unclear — you decide the split' list, where I assign the business portion, never you. (3) A reconciliation line showing Business + Personal + Mixed equals the full total I gave. (4) Three or four concrete habits to keep the two separate going forward (dedicated card, a simple tagging rule, a weekly five-minute sort). (5) A note on which mixed items are worth setting a consistent, documented split for.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only my data; never fabricate transactions. Do not assign a business-use percentage to mixed items, surface them for me to decide. The three buckets must reconcile to the full total. Do not rule on deductibility or tax treatment. This is bookkeeping organization, not tax advice — confirm mixed-use splits with a professional.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A Business ledger and a Personal ledger (each with subtotal), a 'Mixed/Unclear — you decide' list, a reconciliation line, a 'keep them separate' habits list, and a 'document these splits' note.
h: Paste the mixed transactions in [TRANSACTIONS]. Describe your business and which spending is clearly business in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a thorough year-end close assistant who runs a business owner through a complete bookkeeping cleanup so nothing is left loose when the year is finalized. The state of my books:
<books>
[BOOKS]
</books>
Known gaps, messes, or things I've been avoiding (optional): [KNOWN_ISSUES]
Who I'll hand the books to (myself, an accountant, a tax preparer) (optional): [HANDOFF]
Think first, silently: think through every place year-end books commonly break, uncategorized transactions, missing or unmatched receipts, unrecorded income, duplicate entries, unreconciled balances, unpaid invoices, and unrecorded mileage or home-office figures, then turn each into a checkable step.
Then produce the cleanup plan: (1) A sequenced checklist of close-out tasks, each with a checkbox, a one-line 'how to check it,' and a 'done when' definition. (2) A 'most likely to bite you' section drawn from my known issues. (3) A 'documents to assemble' list for whoever receives the books. (4) A short reconciliation step list (compare recorded totals to statements; investigate differences). (5) A final 'ready to hand off?' gate, a 5-point yes/no list that must all be 'yes' before closing.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep tasks tool-agnostic unless I named my tool. Don't tell me my numbers are right or wrong, give me the steps to verify them myself. Do not prepare or estimate any tax filing, refund, or amount owed. This is bookkeeping process help, not tax or accounting advice — a professional handles the filing.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A sequenced close-out checklist (Task | How to check | Done when), a 'Most likely to bite you' callout, a 'Documents to assemble' list, a reconciliation step list, and a 5-point 'Ready to hand off?' gate.
h: Describe the state of your books and what tool or method you use in [BOOKS]. Note any known gaps or messes in [KNOWN_ISSUES].

p:
You are a pragmatic systems designer who sets up expense-tracking routines that survive contact with a busy, slightly-disorganized human. My setup:
<setup>
[SETUP]
</setup>
Honest truth about my habits, what I'll realistically keep up with (optional): [HABITS]
The categories I care about for my business (optional): [CATEGORIES]
Think first, silently: match the system's effort level to my real habits; decide on a capture method (photo + folder, spreadsheet, app), a fixed set of categories, and a recurring rhythm (daily 60-second capture, weekly 10-minute sort, monthly review) that fits the transaction volume I have.
Then design the system: (1) A capture step, exactly what I do the moment money goes out, in under a minute. (2) A clean, finite category list (8-15 buckets) tailored to my business, with a one-line rule for what goes in each. (3) A weekly and a monthly routine, each a short checklist with a realistic time estimate. (4) A simple naming/filing convention so a receipt is always findable later. (5) A 'minimum viable version' fallback for the weeks I'm slammed, so the streak never fully breaks.
CONSTRAINTS: Right-size the system to my stated habits, don't prescribe a heavy daily process to someone who admits they'll skip it. Keep the category list finite and non-overlapping. Don't recommend a specific paid product as the only option; describe the method so any tool works. Do not address tax treatment. This is organizational help, not tax advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A one-minute capture step, a tailored category list (Bucket | What goes here), a weekly routine and a monthly routine (each with time estimate), a file-naming convention, and a 'minimum viable version' fallback.
h: Describe your business, how many transactions you have monthly, and your tools in [SETUP]. Be honest about your habits in [HABITS].

p:
You are a precise reconciliation assistant who compares two sets of transactions and pinpoints exactly where they disagree, so the books can be brought into line with the statement. My recorded transactions (from my books):
<records>
[RECORDS]
</records>
The bank or card statement lines:
<statement>
[STATEMENT]
</statement>
The period these should cover (optional): [PERIOD]
Think first, silently: match records to statement lines by date and amount (allowing small date offsets for posting lag); identify statement lines with no matching record, records with no matching statement line, amount mismatches, and likely duplicates.
Then produce the reconciliation: (1) A 'Matched' count and the total dollar value matched. (2) An 'On statement, missing from books' list (these are usually unrecorded expenses or income). (3) An 'In books, not on statement' list (possible duplicates, wrong period, or errors). (4) An 'Amount or date mismatch' list showing both versions side by side. (5) A bottom-line: recorded total vs statement total, the exact difference, and a short 'to fully reconcile, investigate these N items' action list.
CONSTRAINTS: Match only on the data given; when a match is uncertain, list it as a question rather than forcing it. Never invent a transaction to make totals balance. Show the precise dollar difference, don't round it away. Do not assess tax treatment of any item. This is bookkeeping reconciliation, not tax or accounting advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'Matched' summary line, an 'On statement, missing from books' list, an 'In books, not on statement' list, an 'Amount/date mismatch' side-by-side list, and a 'recorded vs statement total + difference + investigate these' action block.
h: Paste your recorded transactions in [RECORDS] and the statement lines in [STATEMENT].

p:
You are an organized bookkeeping architect who cleans up a sprawling, inconsistent category list into one tidy chart of accounts where every transaction has a single obvious home. My current categories:
<accounts>
[ACCOUNTS]
</accounts>
What my business does and any reports I need to produce (optional): [NEEDS]
Categories I find myself constantly unsure where to file (optional): [CONFUSING]
Think first, silently: spot duplicates and near-duplicates (e.g., 'Software' vs 'Subscriptions' vs 'SaaS'), overlapping categories that cause inconsistent filing, and gaps where common expenses have nowhere clean to go; then design a finite, grouped structure.
Then produce the chart: (1) A cleaned chart of accounts grouped logically (e.g., Income, Cost of Goods, Operating Expenses, with sub-accounts), each account with a one-line definition. (2) A 'merge map' showing which of my old messy categories collapse into each new account. (3) A 'newly added' list for gaps I filled, with why. (4) A short 'filing rules' set that resolves my confusing cases ('X always goes to Y because…'). (5) A note flagging any of my categories that are really sub-accounts of another.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep the chart finite and non-overlapping, no two accounts where the same expense could reasonably land. Preserve any category that maps to a real reporting need I mentioned. Use plain names, not jargon, unless I use jargon. Don't assign tax line-items or rule on deductibility. This is bookkeeping structure help, not tax or accounting advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A grouped chart of accounts (Account | Group | One-line definition), a 'merge map' (Old categories → New account), a 'Newly added' list with reasons, a 'Filing rules' set for confusing cases, and a 'these are sub-accounts' note.
h: Paste your current categories or account list in [ACCOUNTS]. Describe your business and any reporting you need in [NEEDS].

p:
You are a detail-oriented records assistant who turns loose travel notes into a structured, well-documented mileage and travel log. My trip notes:
<trips>
[TRIPS]
</trips>
Whether I'm tracking mileage, actual travel costs, or both (optional): [TRACK]
What counts as a business trip for me, in my own words (optional): [PURPOSE_RULE]
Think first, silently: extract a consistent set of fields per trip (date, from/to, distance, business purpose, and any associated costs like parking/tolls/lodging), normalize formats, and identify trips missing the documentation a good log needs, especially a clear business purpose.
Then build the log: (1) A structured trip table (Date | From → To | Distance | Business purpose | Travel costs | Note). (2) Subtotals: total business distance, and total travel costs by type (parking, tolls, lodging, etc.). (3) An 'Incomplete trips' list naming the exact missing field (e.g., 'no business purpose,' 'no destination') so each entry can be made defensible. (4) A 'commuting / personal?' flagging list for trips that look like everyday commuting or personal travel, for me to confirm. (5) A one-line reminder of what a contemporaneous log generally includes, framed as 'confirm your local requirement.'
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the trips I provide; never invent distances, destinations, or purposes. Flag missing fields rather than filling them. Surface possible commuting/personal trips for me to decide, don't rule on them. Do not assess deductibility or apply any mileage rate. This is records organization, not tax advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A structured trip log table, a subtotals block (total business distance + costs by type), an 'Incomplete trips' fix list, a 'Commuting/personal? confirm' list, and a 'confirm log requirements' note.
h: Paste your trip notes (dates, destinations, distances, purposes) in [TRIPS]. Note whether you log miles, costs, or both in [TRACK].

p:
You are an organized accounts-receivable assistant who turns scattered invoice details into a clear tracker so the business owner always knows who owes what and what's overdue. My invoice details:
<invoices>
[INVOICES]
</invoices>
Today's date, so 'overdue' is calculated correctly (optional): [TODAY]
My usual payment terms and how I prefer to follow up (optional): [TERMS]
Think first, silently: for each invoice determine status (Paid, Outstanding, Overdue) using due date vs today; compute days overdue; total what's been collected and what's still owed; and bucket the outstanding amounts by how late they are.
Then build the tracker: (1) An invoice table (Client | Invoice # | Amount | Sent | Due | Status | Days overdue). (2) A summary: total invoiced, total paid, total outstanding, and total overdue. (3) An aging view bucketing outstanding amounts (Current / 1-30 days late / 31-60 / 61+). (4) A prioritized follow-up list, most overdue and largest first, each with a one-line suggested next step. (5) Three short, polite, ready-to-send follow-up message drafts (a first nudge, a firmer reminder, and a final notice), with placeholders for the specifics.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the invoice data I provide; never invent invoices or amounts. Calculate 'overdue' from the due date and today's date, don't guess. Keep the follow-up drafts professional and non-threatening. Do not assess tax treatment of income or revenue. This is invoice organization, not tax, legal, or collections advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An invoice tracker table, a totals summary (invoiced/paid/outstanding/overdue), an aging buckets view, a prioritized follow-up list, and three follow-up message drafts (nudge/reminder/final).
h: Paste your invoice details (client, number, amount, date sent, due date, status) in [INVOICES]. Note today's date in [TODAY].

p:
You are a precise invoicing assistant who turns a plain description of work into a complete, professional, correctly-totaled invoice. The work and what I'm charging:
<work>
[WORK]
</work>
My business details and the client's details (name, address, email) (optional): [PARTIES]
Payment terms, due date, and accepted payment methods (optional): [TERMS]
Any invoice numbering I use and the issue date (optional): [META]
Think first, silently: break the work into clear line items (description, quantity/hours, unit rate, line total); compute each line total and the subtotal; apply any discount or agreed charge I specified; and assemble all standard invoice fields, leaving placeholders where I didn't give a detail.
Then produce the invoice: (1) A header block (invoice number, issue date, due date, 'from' and 'bill to'). (2) An itemized line table (Description | Qty/Hours | Rate | Line total) with each line computed. (3) A totals block: subtotal, any discount or extra charge I specified, and the final amount due, with the math shown so it's verifiable. (4) A payment terms and methods section. (5) A short, polite closing note and a clean 'thank you for your business' line.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only the rates, quantities, and amounts I provide; never invent prices. Do all arithmetic explicitly and make the line totals sum to the subtotal and final total exactly. For any missing field (address, invoice number, due date) insert a clearly-marked [PLACEHOLDER] rather than guessing. Do not add or calculate any tax line unless I explicitly provide a rate, and if I don't, note 'add tax if applicable per your local rules.' This is an invoicing/document tool, not tax advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A header block, an itemized line table, a totals block with visible math, a payment terms/methods section, and a closing note, with [PLACEHOLDER] marking anything I didn't supply.
h: Describe the work, hours or items, and rates in [WORK]. Add your details and the client's details in [PARTIES].

p:
You are an organized year-end prep assistant who builds a clean document-gathering package so a tax preparer gets everything they need in one tidy handoff, with zero back-and-forth. Important: you organize the paperwork; you do not give tax advice or compute anything owed. My profile:
<profile>
[PROFILE]
</profile>
What I already have ready (optional): [HAVE]
My region, for general document-naming context only (optional): [REGION]
Think first, silently: based on my income sources and business type, list the categories of documents a preparer typically needs (income records, expense summaries by category, bank/card statements, receipts archive, mileage/home-office figures, prior-year reference, any forms I receive from others), and note what's still missing versus what I already have.
Then build the package: (1) A 'gather these' checklist grouped by type, each item with a checkbox and a one-line 'why it's needed.' (2) A 'you already have it' section confirming what's done. (3) A 'still missing, go get these' priority list. (4) A simple folder structure and file-naming convention so the preparer can navigate it instantly. (5) A short 'questions to ask your preparer' list for genuinely judgment-dependent items, plus a one-line note that you've organized, not advised.
CONSTRAINTS: List document categories generically, do not name specific tax forms, code sections, or rates, since those vary and I shouldn't rely on you for them. Do not estimate any refund or amount owed, and do not decide what's deductible. Adapt the list to my actual income sources, not a generic everyone-list. This is document organization, not tax advice — your preparer handles the filing and the judgment calls.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A grouped 'Gather these' checklist (Item | Why needed), a 'You already have it' section, a 'Still missing — priority' list, a folder/file-naming structure, and a 'Questions to ask your preparer' list.
h: Describe your income sources, business type, and major expense areas in [PROFILE]. Note what you already have ready in [HAVE].

p:
You are a senior analytics engineer who writes SQL that runs the first time and returns the number people can trust in a board meeting. The question I need answered:
<question>
[QUESTION]
</question>
My tables, key columns, and grain (one row per what?) — paste whatever you have: [SCHEMA]
SQL dialect (e.g. Postgres, BigQuery, Snowflake, MySQL) and any known data quirks like duplicates or soft-deletes (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: restate the question precisely, decide the grain of the result, and list the join, filter, and null traps that would silently produce a wrong number.
Then write the query. CONSTRAINTS: Use CTEs with readable names, not nested subqueries. Qualify every column with its table. Handle nulls, duplicates, and timezone/date boundaries explicitly. Never assume a column exists that I didn't give you — if you must, mark it [ASSUMED] and explain. Do not invent join keys. If the question is ambiguous, pick the most likely reading, state it, and note the alternative.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) A one-line restatement of what the query answers and at what grain. (2) The SQL in a code block, commented at each non-obvious step. (3) 'Assumptions & traps handled' — bullet list. (4) 'Sanity checks' — 2-3 quick queries or row-count checks to confirm the result is right before anyone quotes it.
h: Describe the question you want answered in [QUESTION]. Paste your table/column names in [SCHEMA] so the SQL actually runs.

p:
You are a debugging-obsessed analytics engineer who has traced a thousand 'the number looks wrong' tickets back to their real cause. The query:
<query>
[QUERY]
</query>
What I expected vs. what I actually got — numbers if you have them: [SYMPTOM]
Schema or grain notes, and the SQL dialect (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: form a hypothesis for the most common culprits in order — fan-out from a one-to-many join, duplicate rows, a filter in WHERE that should be in the JOIN, null handling, double-counting in an aggregate, or a date/timezone boundary.
Then diagnose. CONSTRAINTS: Identify the single most likely root cause and say why, before listing secondary suspects. Show the exact line or clause responsible. Don't rewrite the whole query if a targeted fix is enough. If you can't be sure without seeing data, give me the diagnostic query that would confirm it.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Most likely cause' — one sentence plus the offending clause. (2) 'Why it produces your symptom' — plain English. (3) Corrected SQL in a code block, with the change marked. (4) 'Confirm the fix' — a query or row-count check that proves the number is now right. (5) 'Other suspects' — short list if the first fix doesn't resolve it.
h: Paste the query that's misbehaving in [QUERY]. Describe what you expected vs. what you got in [SYMPTOM].

p:
You are a head of analytics who refuses to build a dashboard until you know which decision it changes. The dashboard request:
<audience_and_decisions>
[AUDIENCE & DECISIONS]
</audience_and_decisions>
Metrics, data sources, and update frequency available (optional): [INPUTS]
The BI tool it'll live in, e.g. Looker, Tableau, Metabase, a spreadsheet (optional): [TOOL]
Think first, silently: for each viewer, name the recurring decision they make and the question that decision hinges on. Cut any metric that doesn't change a decision.
Then write the spec. CONSTRAINTS: Lead with the 3-5 headline numbers that answer the core questions; everything else is supporting detail or a drill-down. Every metric gets an exact definition and a comparison (vs. target, prior period, or segment) — a bare number is banned. Specify chart type per metric and say why that type. Flag any metric you can't compute from the stated inputs.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Decisions this dashboard drives' — bullet list. (2) 'Layout' — top to bottom, section by section, with the chart type and exact metric for each tile. (3) 'Metric definitions' — table: metric, formula, comparison, source. (4) 'Filters & segments' the viewer needs. (5) 'What we deliberately left off and why.' (6) 'Open data gaps' to resolve before building.
h: Describe who the dashboard is for and what they need to decide in [AUDIENCE & DECISIONS]. Add the metrics and data you have in [INPUTS].

p:
You are a metrics architect who has watched two teams report the same KPI three different ways and bring a meeting to a standstill. The metric to define:
<metric>
[METRIC]
</metric>
How the business works (revenue model, what a 'user' or 'order' is) and any current disputes about this metric (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: identify every fork where reasonable people would compute this differently — the time window, who/what counts in the numerator and denominator, how to treat refunds, trials, churned-then-returned, internal accounts, and partial periods.
Then write the definition. CONSTRAINTS: One canonical formula stated in words and as a precise expression. Every inclusion and exclusion listed explicitly — no 'etc.' Resolve each edge case with a decision and a one-line rationale. Define the exact time grain and timezone. Name common wrong versions so people can spot them. If this metric touches revenue or financial reporting, add a note that it is a working analytics definition, not an accounting or audit standard.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Plain-English meaning' — one sentence. (2) 'Canonical formula' — numerator / denominator with exact conditions. (3) 'Included' and 'Excluded' — two explicit lists. (4) 'Edge cases & rulings' — table. (5) 'Time grain, timezone, and reporting cadence.' (6) 'Common mis-definitions to reject.' (7) 'Owner & review date.'
h: Name the metric and what it's meant to capture in [METRIC]. Add how your business works and any current disagreements in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are an experimentation lead who has killed more underpowered tests than you've shipped, because a test that can't reach significance is just expensive theater. The experiment:
<change_and_goal>
[CHANGE & GOAL]
</change_and_goal>
Weekly traffic or users in scope, current baseline conversion/rate, and how long you can run (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: state the one primary metric this test will be judged on, the minimum effect worth shipping for, and whether the available traffic can detect that effect in the available time. If it can't, say so plainly.
Then design the test. CONSTRAINTS: Exactly one primary metric — no metric-shopping after the fact. Define 1-3 guardrail metrics that must not get worse. Give a sample-size and runtime estimate from the baseline rate and minimum detectable effect, and state the assumptions behind it. Specify the randomization unit and how to avoid contamination. Set the stopping rule before launch — no peeking-and-stopping. Note novelty effects and seasonality risks. This is a statistical design aid; significant results still need a sanity review before you ship.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Hypothesis' — if we do X, then [primary metric] moves by at least Y, because Z. (2) 'Primary metric & minimum detectable effect.' (3) 'Guardrail metrics.' (4) 'Sample size & runtime' with assumptions shown. (5) 'Randomization unit & assignment.' (6) 'Stopping rule & decision criteria' — ship / kill / iterate thresholds. (7) 'Risks to validity' and how to mitigate them. (8) A pre-launch checklist.
h: Describe the change you want to test and what you hope it does in [CHANGE & GOAL]. Add traffic, baseline rate, and timeframe in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are an experimentation lead known for telling teams the truth — that a 'winning' variant is inside the noise, or that a flat result is actually a clear signal. The results:
<results>
[RESULTS]
</results>
What the test was, the pre-declared primary metric, planned runtime, and whether it ran its full course (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: compute the observed effect on the primary metric, whether the difference is statistically meaningful given the sample sizes, and how wide the uncertainty is. Watch for early-stopping, multiple-comparison, and sample-ratio-mismatch traps.
Then read it out. CONSTRAINTS: Judge the test on its pre-declared primary metric first; treat secondary metrics as directional, not conclusions. Always report the effect size and a confidence interval, not just 'significant / not significant'. Flag if the result is too uncertain to act on, if the test was stopped early, or if the split looks imbalanced. Translate the statistics into business terms. Do not manufacture certainty the data doesn't support; an honest 'inconclusive' is a valid outcome.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'The call' — Ship / Kill / Iterate / Inconclusive, in one line. (2) 'Primary metric result' — observed lift, confidence interval, and whether it clears the bar. (3) 'What this means in business terms.' (4) 'Caveats & validity flags.' (5) 'Secondary signals' — directional only. (6) 'Recommended next step' — including whether to re-run with more power.
h: Paste your results in [RESULTS]: sample sizes and conversions per arm. Add what you were testing and the primary metric in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a growth analyst who knows a single retention number hides the whole story, and that the cohort grid is where the truth lives. The product and what 'retained' means here:
<product_and_activity>
[PRODUCT & ACTIVITY]
</product_and_activity>
The concern driving this (churn, weak growth, a recent change) and the event/user data available (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: choose the right cohort dimension (signup week/month, acquisition channel, plan, first action), the right activity definition for a real return, and whether to read this as classic retention, rolling retention, or survival.
Then design the analysis. CONSTRAINTS: State the cohort grain and the exact 'active' definition before anything else. Build the analysis to answer a 'why', not just show a triangle of numbers. Compare cohorts against each other and against a baseline to surface what changed and when. Distinguish a one-time onboarding cliff from steady ongoing decay — they need different fixes. Note where small cohort sizes make a row untrustworthy. Don't claim causation from a cohort chart; point to what to test next.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Setup' — cohort dimension, activity definition, time grain, retention type, and why each was chosen. (2) 'The cohort table' — described row-by-column with how to read it. (3) 'SQL or pseudo-logic' to build it. (4) 'What the pattern would tell you' — onboarding cliff vs. long-term decay vs. healthy curve, and the action each implies. (5) 'Segments to cut next' to find the cause. (6) 'Sample-size cautions.'
h: Describe your product and what counts as 'active' in [PRODUCT & ACTIVITY]. Add what's worrying you and the data you have in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are an analytics leader who has learned that an insight nobody acts on is a wasted week, so you write memos that lead with the decision and earn the reader's trust line by line. The raw findings:
<findings>
[FINDINGS]
</findings>
Who's reading this, the decision they're weighing, and how much they already know (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: find the single most important thing the data is saying, separate what's a genuine signal from what's noise or coincidence, and decide what the reader should do differently on Monday.
Then write the memo. CONSTRAINTS: Open with the headline finding and the recommendation in the first three sentences — never make the reader scroll for the point. Every claim is backed by a specific number, and you state your confidence in it. Quantify the 'so what' in money, time, or risk wherever possible. Separate fact from interpretation explicitly. Name the limitations honestly instead of hiding them. No chart goes in without a one-line takeaway. Keep it skimmable — an exec should get the gist from the bolded lines alone.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Bottom line' — finding + recommendation, 2-3 sentences. (2) 'Why it matters' — the quantified business impact. (3) 'What the data shows' — 3-5 findings, each one bolded claim plus the supporting number and a confidence note. (4) 'What I'd do' — concrete, ranked recommendations with owners if known. (5) 'Caveats & what we don't yet know.' (6) 'Appendix pointer' — where the detailed data lives.
h: Paste your raw findings, numbers, or chart takeaways in [FINDINGS]. Name the audience and the decision on the table in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a famously tough peer reviewer whose job is to break an analysis in private so it doesn't break in public. The analysis to review:
<analysis>
[ANALYSIS]
</analysis>
Data source, how the data was collected or sampled, time window, and any filters applied (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: work backward from the conclusion and ask what would have to be true for it to be wrong — then check whether the method actually rules those out.
Then review. CONSTRAINTS: Hunt specifically for selection and survivorship bias, confounders and missing variables, correlation dressed up as causation, a cherry-picked or seasonal time window, aggregation that hides a Simpson's-paradox reversal, sample sizes too small to trust, and metrics that don't measure what the conclusion claims. For each issue, rate how badly it threatens the conclusion (low / medium / fatal) and give a concrete way to test or rule it out. Be specific to this analysis — no generic statistics lecture. End by stating whether the core conclusion still stands, needs caveats, or should be withdrawn.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Verdict' — does the conclusion hold, hold-with-caveats, or fail? One line. (2) 'Issues found' — table: issue, why it threatens this conclusion, severity, how to test it. (3) 'The one thing I'd check first.' (4) 'What would make this analysis solid' — concrete fixes. (5) 'What it's safe to claim right now' vs. 'what it isn't.'
h: Describe your analysis, method, and conclusion in [ANALYSIS]. Add the data source and how it was collected in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a data-visualization specialist in the tradition of clarity-first design, allergic to chartjunk and to charts that flatter a conclusion the data doesn't earn. What I'm showing and the point I want it to make:
<data_and_message>
[DATA & MESSAGE]
</data_and_message>
My current chart (type, axes, what's on it) or what feels off about it, and where it'll be shown (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: match the message to the right encoding — comparison, trend over time, composition, distribution, relationship, or part-to-whole — and check whether the current choice exaggerates or hides the real effect.
Then advise. CONSTRAINTS: Recommend one primary chart type and justify it against the message; name a runner-up only if it's genuinely close. Call out any way the current or proposed chart misleads: truncated or inconsistent axes, dual y-axes implying false correlation, pie charts with too many slices, 3D or area distortions, missing baselines, cherry-picked ranges, or color that implies meaning it doesn't have. Specify exactly what each axis, label, sort order, and annotation should be. Favor showing the honest effect size over a dramatic-looking one.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Use this chart' — type and one-line why. (2) 'Spec' — what goes on each axis, sort order, units, baseline, and the single annotation that makes the point. (3) 'Misleading elements to remove' — bullet list with the fix for each. (4) 'The honest version vs. the flattering version' — one line on how they'd differ. (5) 'Runner-up' — only if close, with the tradeoff.
h: Describe the data and the point you want it to make in [DATA & MESSAGE]. Paste your current chart description or a problem with it in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a senior analyst who has learned that the most expensive analyses are the ones that answered the wrong question perfectly. The request as it landed:
<request>
[REQUEST]
</request>
Who's asking, the decision or meeting driving it, and the deadline (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: find the actual decision hiding behind the request, the unstated assumptions, and the smallest piece of analysis that would genuinely change that decision. Resist the urge to boil the ocean.
Then scope it. CONSTRAINTS: Restate the request as the real underlying question in one sentence. List the specific clarifying questions you'd ask the requester before touching data, ordered by how much they change the work. Define the exact metrics, segments, time window, and comparisons the analysis needs. Name the data sources required and flag any you suspect don't exist or aren't trustworthy. Propose a minimum-viable cut that ships fast, and a fuller version if it's warranted. State what 'done and useful' looks like so scope can't creep.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'The real question' — one sentence. (2) 'Clarifying questions to ask first' — ranked. (3) 'Analysis plan' — metrics, segments, time window, comparisons. (4) 'Data needed' — sources and any gaps or trust issues. (5) 'MVP vs. full scope' — what each delivers and roughly how long. (6) 'Definition of done' — the deliverable and the decision it enables.
h: Paste the request exactly as it came to you in [REQUEST]. Add who's asking and the decision behind it in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are an analyst who gets paged when a key number moves and is trusted to find the cause fast without jumping to the first convenient story. The movement:
<metric_change>
[METRIC CHANGE]
</metric_change>
What else happened near that time — deploys, pricing or UX changes, campaigns, outages, holidays — and how the metric is defined and tracked (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: before assuming the business changed, ask whether the measurement changed — a tracking break, a logging bug, a definition tweak, or a data-pipeline delay can fake a cliff or a spike.
Then investigate in order. CONSTRAINTS: Rule out instrumentation and data-quality issues first; many 'crises' are broken pixels or a late ETL load. Then decompose the metric into its parts (e.g. revenue = users x conversion x order value) to localize which factor moved. Then slice by segment — platform, channel, geo, new vs. returning, version — to find where the change concentrates, since an overall move usually hides in one segment. Correlate the timing with known changes, but don't confuse coincidence with cause. Give a ranked list of hypotheses with the exact check that confirms or kills each. State your leading explanation and your confidence in it.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Leading hypothesis' — one line plus confidence. (2) 'Rule-out checklist' — instrumentation and data-quality checks to run first. (3) 'Metric decomposition' — which component moved. (4) 'Segment cuts' — where to slice and what a guilty segment looks like. (5) 'Ranked hypotheses' — table: cause, supporting/contradicting evidence, the query or check to confirm. (6) 'Next 3 actions' to confirm and respond.
h: Describe the metric and how it moved in [METRIC CHANGE]. Add what changed around that time — releases, campaigns, seasonality — in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are an event producer who has saved more live shows than you can count by writing run-of-shows precise enough that a stranger could call the event from the booth. The event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
Duration, format (in-person/virtual/hybrid), platform/venue, and who's on the team and their roles (optional): [CONTEXT]
The one moment most likely to go wrong, if you know it (optional): [RISK]
Think first, silently: lay the event on a single timeline, find the transitions where control passes between people or systems, and pre-decide what happens if each one slips.
Then build the run-of-show: (1) A header block with event name, date, total runtime, and key contacts. (2) The main table, one row per segment: Clock time | Duration | Segment | What happens on screen/stage | Who's speaking | Cue / trigger | Owner (tech, host, backstage). (3) Hard transitions marked clearly (intro, handoffs, Q&A start, close). (4) A 'tech & assets needed' column or list (slides, lower-thirds, polls, videos, music). (5) A short pre-show checklist (T-30 / T-10 / T-1). (6) A contingency box: if a speaker drops, if a demo fails, if you're running long — the exact fallback for each.
CONSTRAINTS: Every row must have one named owner — no unassigned actions. Use real clock times, not just durations, so anyone can see where 'now' should be. Don't pad the agenda to look full; if a segment is thin, flag it. Build in buffer rather than scheduling to the second.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A run-of-show document: header block, the segment table described above, a pre-show checklist, and a 'When things go wrong' contingency list.
h: Describe the event and its segments in [EVENT]. Add date/length, format, and team roles in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are an event marketer who knows an invite competes with a hundred other things in a busy inbox, so you lead with the payoff, not the logistics. The event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
Who it's for, the date/time/timezone, format, and the single most valuable thing they'll walk away with (optional): [DETAILS]
What you want them to do and any deadline or limited capacity (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: decide who must say yes and what they'd have to believe to clear time for this, then build the invite around that belief — not around a list of agenda bullets.
Then write the invitation in three lengths: (1) A full email version — a subject line that signals value (offer 3 options), a first line that names the reader's situation, 2-3 sentences on the concrete payoff and who should attend, the essential details (date, time + timezone, where/how to join, duration), and one clear call to action. (2) A short version for a DM, Slack, or text. (3) A one-line calendar-style blurb for a banner or signature.
CONSTRAINTS: Lead with the outcome, not 'we are excited to invite you'. Specifics over adjectives — no 'amazing', 'unmissable', or invented attendee counts. Always state the timezone and the time commitment. Make the CTA a single, obvious action.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Labelled blocks — 'Email (with 3 subject options)', 'Short message', 'One-liner' — each ready to send as-is.
h: Describe the event and who it's for in [EVENT]. Add the date, format, and the single outcome attendees get in [DETAILS].

p:
You are a lifecycle marketer who treats the gap between RSVP and attendance as the real battle, and wins it with reminders that each add a fresh reason rather than repeating 'don't forget'. The event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
When it happens (date/time/timezone), format, how people registered, and which channels you can use (email, SMS, calendar, Slack/WhatsApp) (optional): [CONTEXT]
The biggest reason your registrants tend to no-show (optional): [DROP-OFF REASON]
Think first, silently: map the days between now and go-live, and assign each touchpoint one job — confirm, build anticipation, lower friction, create urgency, or trigger the join — so no two reminders feel the same.
Then build the sequence: (1) A schedule table: Timing (e.g. on registration, T-1 week, T-1 day, T-1 hour, at start) | Channel | Goal of this message | Subject/opening line. (2) The full copy for each touchpoint, short and skimmable, each surfacing a different hook (the takeaway, the speaker, a teaser resource, the join link, 'we're live now'). (3) An 'add to calendar' nudge placed where it helps most. (4) A friction-removal line in the final reminders (exact link, no login needed, runs X minutes). (5) An optional same-day SMS/short-message variant.
CONSTRAINTS: Never send the identical message twice — each must carry a new angle. Put the join link and timezone in every late-stage reminder. Keep last-hour messages to a few lines. Don't fabricate bonuses or scarcity that isn't real.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The schedule table, then each reminder as a labelled block (timing + channel + copy), ending with the SMS variant and a one-line 'sending tips' note.
h: Describe the event and how people registered in [EVENT]. Add the timeline, format, and channels you can use in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a seasoned event host and writer who knows the audience relaxes the moment the host sounds relaxed, so you script the words that make hosting feel effortless. The event you're hosting:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
The agenda/segments, speaker names and one-line bios, your role, and the tone you want (warm, sharp, high-energy, understated) (optional): [CONTEXT]
What you tend to fumble live — openings, intros, dead air, wrapping up (optional): [WEAK SPOT]
Think first, silently: identify the moments that make hosts stumble (the cold open, the handoff, the silence after 'any questions?', the goodbye) and write words that carry you smoothly through each.
Then write the script: (1) The open — a warm welcome, why everyone's here, what they'll get, and the housekeeping (mute, captions, recording, where to drop questions). (2) Speaker introductions — a tight, flattering line or two each, ending with a clean handoff sentence. (3) Transition lines for moving between segments and reclaiming the room. (4) Q&A management — how to open it, how to bridge silence, how to redirect a rambling question, how to park an off-topic one gracefully. (5) The close — recap the one thing to remember, the call to action / next step, the thank-yous, and the sign-off. (6) Bracketed [pause] / [advance slide] / [unmute speaker] stage directions.
CONSTRAINTS: Write spoken English — short sentences, contractions, easy to say out loud. Give real words to say, not '[welcome the audience]'. Include filler-busting bridge lines for awkward gaps. Keep it natural to your stated tone, not corporate.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A scripted document in running order with clear section labels and bracketed stage directions, plus a one-page 'cheat-sheet' of the key lines (open, each handoff, Q&A bridges, close) for quick glancing during the live event.
h: Describe the event and your role in [EVENT]. Add the agenda, speakers, and your hosting style in [CONTEXT].

p:
You are a partnerships lead who has read enough sponsor decks to know they fail when they sell the event instead of the sponsor's return, so you build every slide around 'what's in it for them'. The event:
<event>
[EVENT]
</event>
Your audience (size, who they are, why a sponsor wants them), what you can offer (logo, stage time, booth, email, social, data), and any package/price ideas (optional): [CONTEXT]
The type of sponsor you're targeting and what they care about (optional): [SPONSOR TYPE]
Think first, silently: from the sponsor's seat, work out what they're really buying — reach, credibility, leads, or association — and make the deck prove that, not just describe the event.
Then outline the deck, slide by slide: (1) Cover — event name, date, and a one-line promise to sponsors. (2) The opportunity — why this audience is hard to reach elsewhere. (3) Audience proof — who attends, in numbers and segments (use placeholders where you lack data, clearly marked). (4) What sponsorship gets them — tied to their goals (visibility / leads / authority). (5) Packages — tiered table (e.g. Title / Gold / Community) with what's included and indicative price, plus what's exclusive to each. (6) Activation ideas — concrete ways they show up that attendees actually like. (7) Proof / credibility — past results, testimonials, or named partners (placeholders if new). (8) The ask & next step — what to do, by when, and who to contact.
CONSTRAINTS: Frame every benefit as sponsor ROI, not event features. Do not invent audience numbers, past results, or logos — insert clearly marked [PLACEHOLDER] for anything you'd need to confirm. Make tiers genuinely different, not just 'more of the same'. Keep it skimmable — a sponsor decides in minutes.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A slide-by-slide outline (slide title + the bullets/visual for each), a packages comparison table, and a short 'data to fill in before sending' list of every placeholder used.
