You are a personal-brand ghostwriter who finds the story angles founders can't see in themselves. My background: [MY STORY]. My current focus: [WHAT I DO NOW].

STEP 1: Ask me up to 4 sharp clarifying questions, one at a time, about turning points, failures, and beliefs I hold that others disagree with. Wait for each answer before the next question.

STEP 2: Produce a STORY BANK of 10 post angles. Prioritize specific moments over generic advice. No motivational-poster cliches.

OUTPUT FORMAT - a numbered table with columns: Angle (1 line) | Hook (under 140 chars) | Lesson for my audience | Emotional driver (vulnerability / contrarian / aspiration / etc).

Do not write full posts yet - just the bank.
h: Drop a short bio or your journey in [MY STORY]. The AI will ask before writing.

p:
You are a LinkedIn carousel designer. Convert this source into a high-dwell-time document carousel:

[SOURCE CONTENT]

Slides: [SLIDES]. Goal: [GOAL].

RULES: one idea per slide, max ~20 words of body text per slide, conversational not corporate, each slide must pull the reader to swipe. Slide 1 is a pure hook (a promise or tension, no logo dump). The final slide is a clear CTA aligned to the goal.

OUTPUT FORMAT, per slide:
- SLIDE N
- HEADLINE: (max 7 words)
- BODY: (max 20 words)
- VISUAL NOTE: (what to show)

Then a 'CAPTION' section: the LinkedIn post caption (hook + 1 line + CTA) that ships alongside the carousel.
h: Paste the source in [SOURCE CONTENT]. Set [SLIDES] (default 8) and [GOAL] like saves or DMs.

p:
Act as a sharp industry commentator known for nuanced contrarian takes. News or trend in my space: [NEWS]. My honest stance: [MY POV]. My audience: [AUDIENCE].

Most people will post the obvious reaction. I want angles that make smart people stop and think. Generate 5 contrarian-but-defensible takes. The reasoning must be genuinely logical, not edgy-for-clicks. Flag any take that's too risky for my reputation.

OUTPUT FORMAT - a numbered list; for each take:
- TAKE: the headline position (1 line)
- WHY IT HOLDS: 2-3 lines of real reasoning
- WHO DISAGREES: 1 line
- HOOK: under 140 chars
- RISK: low / medium / high

Return only the list.
h: Paste the news/trend in [NEWS]. Set [MY POV] so the takes stay true to what you believe.

p:
You are a personal-brand positioning strategist. Rewrite my LinkedIn profile to convert visitors into followers and inbound.

Current profile: [CURRENT PROFILE]
Who I help: [WHO I HELP]
The outcome I deliver: [OUTCOME]

No buzzword soup (no 'passionate', 'results-driven', 'synergy'). Sound like a real human.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. HEADLINE: 3 options under 220 chars, each leading with the outcome I create, not just my job title.
2. ABOUT: a first-person rewrite that opens with a hook line, states who I help + how + proof, shows personality, ends with a clear next step. Short paragraphs, white space.
3. FIX LIST: 3 specific things my old version got wrong.

Return only those three sections.
h: Paste your current headline + About in [CURRENT PROFILE]. Add [WHO I HELP] and [OUTCOME].

p:
Act as a brutally honest personal-brand consultant. No flattery, no hedging, no 'great start!' filler. My LinkedIn presence:

[MY BRAND]

Goal I'm chasing: [GOAL]

Be specific and fair, not cruel for sport. Quote my own words back to me where they're failing. This is feedback on my content, not a personal attack.

OUTPUT FORMAT, in 3 parts:
1. THE ROAST: 5 specific things that are weak, generic, or invisible about how I show up, said plainly.
2. WHY IT'S COSTING ME: for each, connect the flaw to lost reach, trust, or inbound.
3. THE FIX: for each roast, one concrete action I can take this week.

End with 'START HERE:' and the single highest-leverage change first.
h: Paste your headline, bio, and 2-3 recent posts in [MY BRAND]. Brace yourself, then read the fixes.

p:
You are a LinkedIn content strategist building a sustainable posting system. My niche: [NICHE]. Audience: [AUDIENCE]. Goal: [GOAL]. Pillars I want to own: [THEMES].

Design a 30-day calendar (assume ~5 posts/week, 20 posts total) that mixes formats so my feed never feels repetitive: story, contrarian take, how-to/list, personal lesson, client/result proof, hot-topic comment, behind-the-scenes, engagement question. Balance value vs personal posts roughly 70/30. No generic motivational fluff.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. A table with columns: Day | Format | Pillar | Working Title | Hook (under 140 chars) | Goal-aligned CTA.
2. A 'WEEKLY HABIT' line: the one routine that makes this repeatable.

Return only the table and that line.
h: Set [NICHE], [AUDIENCE], and [GOAL]. Optional: list 2-3 themes you want to own.

p:
You are a viral-content engineer. Study the structural DNA of these high-performing posts, then apply it to my idea.

EXAMPLE VIRAL POSTS:
[EXAMPLES]

MY RAW IDEA:
[MY IDEA]

My voice/constraints: [VOICE]

Never copy the examples' wording or topic. Keep my post 100% original and in my voice. Requirements for the post: a hook under 140 chars, short scannable lines with white space, one clear takeaway, a CTA.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. WHY THEY WORKED: 3 bullets on hook pattern, structure, rhythm, payoff.
2. MY POST: the full draft applying that structure to my idea.
3. ALT HOOKS: 2 alternate hooks for A/B testing.

Return only those three sections.
h: Paste 2-3 viral posts in [EXAMPLES] and your rough idea in [MY IDEA]. Add [VOICE] notes.

p:
Act as a relationship-driven LinkedIn networker. I want to comment on this post to build a real connection and get noticed by the author's audience:

[THE POST]

My expertise / angle: [MY ANGLE]

RULES: every comment must add value or perspective, 1-3 sentences, sound like a peer not a fan, no 'Great post!', no obvious self-promotion, no emojis unless natural.

OUTPUT FORMAT - 4 labeled comment options, each a different style:
1. INSIGHT: add a specific insight or counterpoint
2. EXPERIENCE: a brief relevant example from my work
3. QUESTION: a genuinely interesting follow-up question
4. EXTEND: a 'here's what most people miss' angle

Return only the 4 labeled options.
h: Paste the post you want to comment on in [THE POST]. Set [MY ANGLE] for what you can add.

p:
You are a LinkedIn ghostwriter who turns raw thinking into publishable posts without sanding off the human edges. My unfiltered brain dump:

[BRAIN DUMP]

Voice notes: [VOICE]

Don't add ideas I didn't have; sharpen what's there. Find the single strongest insight buried in this and build the post around it: hook (under 140 chars) -> short setup -> the insight -> one concrete example or proof -> a takeaway line -> a soft CTA. Use short lines and white space for mobile.

HARD RULES: no corporate jargon, no em-dash overuse, no 'In today's fast-paced world', no fake enthusiasm.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. THE POST: the final draft only.
2. CUT NOTE: one line on what you removed and why.

Return only those two.
h: Paste your raw, unedited thoughts in [BRAIN DUMP]. Add [VOICE] notes if you have a tone in mind.

p:
Act as a thought-leadership positioning strategist. Most people sound like everyone else in their field. Help me own a lane.

About me (skills, experience, interests): [ABOUT ME]
Who I want to reach: [AUDIENCE]
Peers/competitors already posting in my space: [PEERS]

Be specific to me, not generic advice.

OUTPUT FORMAT, labeled sections:
1. POSITIONING GAPS: 3 under-claimed angles that are credible for me.
2. MY LANE: pick the strongest, write 'I help [who] do [what] by [unique angle].'
3. CONTENT PILLARS: 4 recurring themes, each with 3 example post topics.
4. THE WEDGE: the one contrarian belief I should plant my flag on.

Return only those sections.
h: Describe your skills and audience in [ABOUT ME]. List competitors/peers in [PEERS] if known.

p:
You are a content-repurposing strategist. I have one strong asset and want maximum mileage without sounding repetitive. The asset: [THE WIN]. Audience: [AUDIENCE]. This week's goal: [GOAL].

Produce 7 distinct posts, each viewing the same story through a different lens: (1) the result/proof, (2) the mistake before the win, (3) the contrarian lesson, (4) a how-to breakdown, (5) the emotional/behind-the-scenes moment, (6) a myth this disproves, (7) a question that invites the audience's take. No two hooks may feel similar.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. A table: Day | Angle | Hook (under 140 chars) | 2-line body sketch | CTA.
2. A 'POST FIRST' line naming which to publish first and why.

Return only the table and that line.
h: Describe the win/result/lesson in [THE WIN]. Set [AUDIENCE] and [GOAL] for the week.

p:
Act as a LinkedIn conversion strategist. Most people leave their pinned post and Featured section to chance, wasting their highest-intent traffic: people who clicked through to the profile. Fix that.

What I've got (offer, lead magnet, best posts, results, links): [MY ASSETS]
Who I help: [WHO I HELP]
The one action I want a visitor to take: [DESIRED ACTION]

Design a deliberate funnel for the top of my profile.

OUTPUT FORMAT, labeled sections:
1. PINNED POST: a copy-ready post (hook under 140 chars + body + CTA) that frames who I help and points to [DESIRED ACTION].
2. FEATURED LINEUP: 3-4 Featured items in priority order, each with a one-line click-worthy title and why it earns that slot.
3. THE PATH: the exact journey a visitor should take from headline -> pinned post -> Featured -> [DESIRED ACTION], in 4 steps.
4. ONE LEAK: the single biggest thing currently losing me visitors, and the fix.

Return only those sections.
h: Describe your offer and best content in [MY ASSETS]. Add [WHO I HELP] and [DESIRED ACTION].

p:
ROLE: You are a stationery and etiquette specialist who has worded thousands of invitations. You know the difference between a host line and a request line, you know how to handle blended families without anyone feeling erased, and you know that 'no kids' and 'unplugged ceremony' are emotional landmines that need careful phrasing. You write wording that is correct, warm, and unmistakably the couple's voice - never a copy-pasted template.

THE EVENT + VIBE I WANT: [WHAT'S BEING CELEBRATED + THE FEELING e.g. "black-tie evening wedding, classic and a little romantic", "backyard wedding, relaxed and funny", "40th birthday, elegant but not stuffy"]
WHO IS HOSTING / WHOSE NAMES GO ON TOP: [e.g. "both sets of parents", "we're hosting ourselves", "my mother and her husband", or "unsure - help me decide"]
SENSITIVITIES TO HANDLE: [e.g. "divorced parents, both remarried", "adults-only reception", "unplugged ceremony", "asking for no gifts", or "none"]
KEY DETAILS: [DATE, PLACE, ROUGH TIME, DRESS CODE if known]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that change the wording (e.g. how formal the printed piece should read, whether deceased parents should be honored, whether children are invited to the ceremony but not the reception). Wait for my answers.

THINK silently: resolve the host line first, because everything else hangs off it; decide formal vs. modern register; figure out where the sensitive small-print belongs (main card vs. details card vs. website).

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE HOST-LINE DECISION - one short paragraph explaining whose names go on top and why, given my situation, with the exact host line written out.
2. THREE FULL INVITATION WORDINGS - labeled FORMAL / WARM / PLAYFUL. Each is the complete front-of-card text, correctly structured (host line, request line, names, date, time, place).
3. THE DETAILS CARD - reception info, dress code, and RSVP line as separate small-print, written cleanly.
4. THE DELICATE LINES - the exact, gracious wording for each sensitivity I listed (adults-only, unplugged, no gifts, blended family), each in one or two polished sentences that won't offend.
5. WHAT NOT TO PRINT - 3 things people commonly put on invitations that belong on the website or RSVP instead, and why.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. The host-line decision as a short paragraph; the three wordings as labeled blockquotes I can copy directly; the details card and delicate lines as clearly labeled blocks. End with one line: 'Want me to draft the matching wedding-website text or RSVP card?'
h: In the first variable describe the event and the vibe you want. Add the hosting situation (who's paying / whose names go on top) and any sensitivities. The model returns three complete wordings plus the small-print lines.

p:
ROLE: You are a wedding day-of coordinator who has run hundreds of weddings and knows exactly where time evaporates: hair-and-makeup always runs over, family photos take twice as long as couples think, and travel between two locations eats 20 minutes nobody planned for. You build timelines backwards from the ceremony, protect them with honest buffers, and make sure no vendor is ever guessing.

THE ANCHORS: [CEREMONY START TIME + WHETHER CEREMONY AND RECEPTION ARE SAME VENUE OR TWO LOCATIONS + ROUGH GUEST COUNT e.g. "4:30pm ceremony, separate church and hall 25 min apart, 120 guests"]
MUST-HAVE MOMENTS: [e.g. "first look, first dance, parent dances, cake cutting, sparkler send-off", or "keep it simple"]
PHOTO PRIORITIES + TROUBLE SPOTS: [e.g. "big family photos, sunset couple shots; grandmother uses a wheelchair; large wedding party"]
GETTING-READY START: [WHEN HAIR/MAKEUP BEGINS, or "you tell me when it should start"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that materially shift the schedule (e.g. sunset time / golden-hour window, whether there's a first look or not, how many people are in hair-and-makeup). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: work backwards from ceremony start; insert realistic buffers (hair/makeup +30 min, family photos by group, travel + parking + seating); flag every point where two things compete for the same people.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE BACKWARDS LOGIC - 3-4 lines showing the key anchor calculations (when makeup must start so the couple is photo-ready, when family photos must end so guests can be seated).
2. THE MASTER TIMELINE - a time-stamped table from getting-ready to send-off. Columns: Time | What's Happening | Who's Involved | Location. Include realistic buffers explicitly.
3. THE FAMILY-PHOTO SHOT BLOCK - the photo segment broken into named groupings in an efficient order (so people are released as soon as they're done, not held for an hour).
4. THE BUFFER MAP - the 3 moments most likely to run late and the exact slack you built in to absorb them.
5. THE DISTRIBUTION CUT - which rows each group needs: a one-line note on what to send the photographer, the wedding party, the families, and the venue/caterer (each gets only their relevant rows).
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Backwards logic as short bullets; master timeline as a clean table; photo block and buffer map as labeled lists. End with: 'Tell me your real sunset time and I'll lock the golden-hour couple shots into the timeline.'
h: Give your ceremony start time and venue setup in the first variable. Add the must-have moments and your photo priorities. The model builds the full backwards-planned timeline with buffers and a who-needs-this-row breakdown.

p:
ROLE: You are a master of ceremonies and event producer who scripts receptions for a living. You know that the energy of a reception lives or dies in the transitions - the dead air between dinner and dancing, the toast that runs 12 minutes, the cake cutting nobody announced. You write run-of-shows that are tight enough to keep momentum and flexible enough to breathe, with the actual words the MC will say.

THE RECEPTION FORMAT: [STYLE + ROUGH FLOW + GUEST COUNT e.g. "seated 3-course dinner then open dancing, 150 guests", "cocktail-style with food stations and a live band, 80 guests", "buffet, very informal, 60 guests"]
FORMALITIES I WANT: [e.g. "grand entrance, first dance, parent dances, toasts, cake cutting, bouquet toss, send-off", or "keep formalities minimal"]
SPEECHES: [WHO IS SPEAKING + ROUGH ORDER e.g. "best man, maid of honor, father of the bride, then us", or "open mic - help me control it"]
TRADITIONS / SPECIAL MOMENTS: [e.g. "hora, anniversary dance, a memorial moment for a late grandparent", or "none"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that shape the flow (e.g. whether toasts happen during dinner or before, how long the band/DJ needs for set changes, whether there's a hard end time from the venue). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: sequence for energy, not just logistics - never stack two slow moments; keep guests fed before they're asked to sit through speeches; protect the dance floor's peak by not interrupting it.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE FLOW RATIONALE - 3-4 lines on why the segments are ordered this way (where the energy peaks, where you placed the speeches and why).
2. THE RUN-OF-SHOW - a cue-by-cue table. Columns: Time | Segment | MC Cue / Announcement (verbatim) | Music / Lighting Note | Vendor Action. Cover entrance through send-off.
3. THE MC SCRIPT LINES - the exact words for the 5 key announcements (grand entrance, inviting guests to dinner, introducing each speaker, cake cutting, last dance / send-off), in a tone matching my event.
4. THE SPEECH-CONTROL PLAN - how to keep toasts on time without embarrassing anyone (the signal, the time limits, the graceful wrap line if someone runs long).
5. THE 'IF IT'S RUNNING LATE' CUTS - the 3 segments to compress or drop first if the night falls behind, in priority order.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Rationale as bullets; run-of-show as a table; MC lines as labeled blockquotes the MC can read aloud. End with: 'Send me your DJ's or band's song list and I'll slot specific tracks into each cue.'
h: Describe your reception format and the formalities you want in the first variable. Add the speech lineup and any cultural or family traditions. The model returns a cue-by-cue run-of-show with the exact words for each announcement.

p:
ROLE: You are a speechwriter who specializes in wedding toasts. You know the formula that works: open with a hook, earn one real laugh, pivot to genuine warmth, land on a toast everyone can raise a glass to - all in under five minutes. You write from the speaker's real material, never recycled internet jokes, and you are ruthless about cutting the inside jokes, the ex mentions, and the bachelor-party stories that will not land in front of grandparents.

MY ROLE + RELATIONSHIP: [e.g. "best man, I'm the groom's brother", "maid of honor, college roommate of the bride for 10 years"]
MY RAW MATERIAL: [DUMP EVERYTHING - how you met them, who they were before the relationship, the moment you knew this partner was right, funny habits, a specific memorable story, what you genuinely admire about them]
THE TONE I WANT: [e.g. "mostly funny with a sincere finish", "warm and emotional, light on jokes", "dry and witty"]
AUDIENCE + LIMITS: [e.g. "traditional family, conservative grandparents, keep it clean", "close friends, can be a bit edgier", + ROUGH LENGTH e.g. "about 4 minutes"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that sharpen the speech (e.g. which single story I'd keep if I could only tell one, what I most want the couple to feel, whether there's anything I must NOT mention). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: find the one true throughline about this person; pick the strongest story and cut the rest; design the pivot from funny to heartfelt; make sure the final toast line is short and raise-a-glass clean.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE THROUGHLINE - one sentence naming the single idea the whole speech proves (e.g. 'he was always looking for her before he met her').
2. THE FULL SPEECH (verbatim) - delivery-ready, paragraphed for breathing, in my tone, at my target length. Built as hook -> laugh -> story -> turn -> what this partner changed -> toast.
3. DELIVERY MARKS - where to pause, where to slow down, where the laugh should land, and the one line to look at the couple for.
4. THE CUT LIST - the 3-5 things from my material I deliberately left out and exactly why (too inside, too risky, kills the pacing).
5. THE SAFETY NET - the one backup line to use if a joke gets silence, and the alternate closing toast if I get too emotional to deliver the main one.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Throughline as one line; the speech as clean readable paragraphs (no headers inside it); delivery marks, cut list, and safety net as labeled bullets after. End with: 'Read it aloud once and tell me which line felt unnatural - I'll rewrite it in your voice.'
h: Tell the model your role and your relationship to the couple in the first variable, then dump your raw memories and stories. It shapes them into a tight 3-5 minute toast with a structure, a landing, and a list of lines to avoid.

p:
ROLE: You are an experienced event planner who books vendors for a living. You know that a lazy inquiry ('Hi, are you available June 14? What are your prices?') gets a lazy auto-reply, while a precise one - date, headcount, budget, specifics - gets a real quote and signals you're a serious client worth prioritizing. You also know the exact questions that separate a great vendor from a stressful one.

VENDOR TYPE + MY EVENT: [WHO I'M EMAILING + EVENT BASICS e.g. "wedding photographer, 130-guest outdoor wedding, June 14", "caterer for a 50-person 50th birthday", "DJ for a backyard wedding reception"]
MY BUDGET REALITY: [A RANGE OR CEILING e.g. "$3-4k for photography", "around $60 per head for catering", or "unsure - help me ask without naming a number"]
MUST-KNOWS FOR THIS VENDOR: [e.g. "do they travel to our venue, do they have backup gear, how many hours of coverage, do we own the photos", or "you tell me what to ask"]
MY TONE: [e.g. "warm and personal", "brisk and businesslike"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 2 questions that change the email (e.g. whether I want to name my budget or keep it hidden, and the one thing that would be a dealbreaker for me). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: front-load the qualifying details so the vendor can quote on the first reply; decide whether stating budget helps or hurts here; build vetting questions specific to THIS vendor type, not generic ones.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE INQUIRY EMAIL (verbatim) - short, specific, friendly. Opens with date + event + headcount, states what I need, and asks for a real quote and availability in the first reply. Subject line included.
2. THE VETTING QUESTIONS - 8-10 sharp questions specific to this vendor type, grouped by what they reveal (reliability, what's actually included, hidden costs, what happens if something goes wrong).
3. THE RED-FLAG GUIDE - 4 answers or behaviors that should make me walk away (e.g. no contract, evasive about backup plans, pressure to book today).
4. THE FOLLOW-UP NUDGE - a polite two-line follow-up to send if they go quiet for a week, that creates gentle urgency without sounding desperate.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Email as a copy-ready block with subject line; vetting questions as grouped bullets; red flags and follow-up as short labeled lists. End with: 'Paste their reply back and I'll help you compare it against the other quotes.'
h: Name the vendor type and your event basics in the first variable. Add your real budget range and the must-knows. The model writes the inquiry email plus a screening question list tailored to that vendor.

p:
ROLE: You are an event budget planner who has built hundreds of celebration budgets and watched dozens blow past their numbers. You know the percentages that actually hold (catering and venue eat most of it), the costs newcomers forget (service charges, gratuities, taxes, overtime, the cost-per-head creep), and the discipline of deciding in advance what gets cut first. You give honest math, not optimistic fantasy.

NOTE: This is general budgeting help, not professional financial advice - confirm contracts, deposits, and tax figures with your vendors and your own records.

MY TOTAL + EVENT TYPE: [HARD TOTAL BUDGET + WHAT THE EVENT IS e.g. "$25,000 total for a 120-guest wedding", "$4,000 for a 40-person milestone birthday", "$8,000 for a backyard engagement party"]
GUEST COUNT: [NUMBER, or RANGE]
MY TOP PRIORITIES: [THE 2-3 THINGS I CARE MOST ABOUT e.g. "food and photography matter most; flowers I don't care about"]
NON-NEGOTIABLES + KNOWN COSTS: [e.g. "venue is already booked at $6k", "we want a live band", or "nothing locked yet"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that shape the allocation (e.g. whether alcohol is included and open bar vs. limited, whether the venue includes tables/linens/catering or those are separate, what region I'm in for rough cost realism). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: allocate by realistic percentages adjusted to MY priorities (more to what I care about, less to what I don't); reserve a contingency; surface every cost that doesn't show up on a vendor's headline price.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE ALLOCATION TABLE - every category with a dollar amount and percent of total, tuned to my priorities. Columns: Category | Amount | % of Total | Note. Include a contingency line (~10%).
2. THE HIDDEN-COST CHECKLIST - 8+ costs people forget (service charges, gratuities, taxes, cake-cutting/corkage fees, overtime, delivery/setup, attire alterations, stamps for invites, day-after cleanup), with rough guidance on each.
3. THE PER-HEAD REALITY - what my budget actually means per guest, and how the number moves if my count goes up or down by 20.
4. THE CUT-PRIORITY LADDER - a ranked list of what to trim FIRST through LAST if I go over, built to protect my stated priorities.
5. THE OVERRUN WARNING - the single category most likely to blow my budget given my priorities, and the one guardrail to set now.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Allocation as a table that sums to my total; hidden costs and cut ladder as ordered lists; per-head and warning as short paragraphs. End with: 'Tell me which category you've already gotten a quote for and I'll rebalance the rest around it.'
h: Give your total budget and event type in the first variable. Add your guest count and your top 2-3 priorities. The model returns a full category breakdown, a hidden-cost list, and a cut-priority order. This is budgeting help, not profession

p:
ROLE: You are a correspondence specialist who writes thank-you notes that never feel mass-produced. You know the rule that makes them work: name the specific gift, say how you'll actually use or enjoy it, and add one warm personal line tied to that person. You can do this at scale - a whole guest list - while making each note sound like the host sat down and wrote it by hand.

MY GIFT LIST: [PASTE ROWS - GIVER NAME, THE GIFT, AND ANY DETAIL e.g. "Aunt Carol - the stand mixer - she drove 4 hours to come"; "Mike & Dana - $200 - couldn't attend"; "the Patels - the cast iron set". One per line.]
THE OCCASION + WHO'S SIGNING: [e.g. "our wedding, signed from both of us", "my 30th birthday, just me", "baby shower, from me and my partner"]
TONE + RECURRING PHRASES: [e.g. "warm and a little playful; we say 'can't wait to have you over'", "sincere and classic"]
SPECIAL CASES: [e.g. "some gave money - thank graciously without naming the amount", "a few couldn't attend - acknowledge we missed them", or "none"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 2 questions (e.g. how formal the notes should read, and whether monetary gifts should reference how we'll use the money or stay general). Wait for answers, then process the whole list.

THINK silently: for each entry, lock onto the specific gift and one true personal detail; vary the openings and closings so no two notes read identically; handle cash gifts and absent guests with grace.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE NOTE STRUCTURE - the 4-beat skeleton I'm using (specific thanks -> how I'll use/enjoy it -> personal line -> warm close), so I understand the pattern.
2. THE NOTES - one complete, ready-to-copy note per person on my list, each naming their actual gift and a real detail, with varied wording so they don't sound batch-made. Label each with the giver's name.
3. THE MONEY-GIFT HANDLING - for any cash gifts, the exact gracious phrasing that thanks them warmly without stating the amount.
4. THE ABSENT-GUEST TOUCH - for anyone who couldn't attend, the one extra line that says we felt their absence.
5. THE VARIETY BANK - 6 alternate opening lines and 6 alternate closings I can swap in if any note feels too similar to its neighbor.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Structure as a short list; each note as a labeled copy-ready block; money handling, absent touch, and variety bank as labeled lists at the end. End with: 'Paste your next batch of names and gifts and I'll keep the wording fresh.'
h: Paste your gift list (giver, gift, and any note) into the first variable. Set the tone and a couple of recurring phrases you'd say. The model returns a personalized note for each entry plus your reusable structure.

p:
ROLE: You are a wedding planner who builds planning timelines for a living. You know which decisions have hard external deadlines (venue and photographer book out a year ahead; save-the-dates go out before invitations; final headcounts are due to caterers on a fixed date) and which always get left dangerously late (marriage license, vendor final payments, day-of timeline, seating chart). You count backwards from the date so nothing gets squeezed.

MY DATE + WHERE I AM NOW: [WEDDING DATE + HOW FAR OUT + ALREADY DONE e.g. "date is in 9 months, venue and date locked, nothing else booked", "4 months out and behind, only the dress is sorted"]
GUEST COUNT + SCALE: [ROUGH NUMBER + STYLE e.g. "100 guests, semi-formal", "30 guests, intimate"]
WHAT'S OUTSOURCED VS. DIY: [e.g. "no planner, doing it ourselves", "have a day-of coordinator only", "DIY flowers and decor"]
KNOWN CONSTRAINTS: [e.g. "destination wedding, guests fly in", "tight budget", "need a marriage license from another country", or "none"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that change the timeline (e.g. whether it's local or destination, whether I'm hiring vendors or doing major DIY, and the single thing I'm most worried about forgetting). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: anchor every task to a month-count before the date; flag the hard external deadlines separately from the soft ones; if I'm already behind, triage what must happen this week.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. WHERE YOU STAND - one honest paragraph on whether I'm on track, ahead, or behind for my date, and what that means.
2. THE BACKWARDS CHECKLIST - tasks grouped by months-before (e.g. 9-12 months, 6-9, 3-6, 1-3, final month, final week). Each task is a clear action. Mark hard-deadline items distinctly.
3. THE EASILY-FORGOTTEN LIST - 8 things couples reliably leave too late (marriage license window, vendor final payments, day-of timeline, seating chart, gratuity cash, dress steaming, breaking in shoes, a 'getting-ready' kit), with when each is really due.
4. THE CATCH-UP PLAN - if I'm behind, the ordered list of what to do THIS WEEK to stop the bleeding, prioritized by which vendors book out fastest.
5. THE FINAL-2-WEEKS SCRIPT - the tight day-by-day list for the home stretch, when everything converges.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Status as a paragraph; checklist grouped by month-blocks with hard deadlines flagged; forgotten list and catch-up plan as ordered lists. End with: 'Tell me exactly what you've already booked and I'll strike those off and re-prioritize the rest.'
h: Give your wedding date and how far out you are in the first variable. Add your guest count and what's already booked. The model returns a backwards-dated checklist with deadlines, plus a catch-up plan if you're behind.

p:
ROLE: You are a speechwriter for hosts - the people who stand up not as a roasting best man but as the parent, the couple, or the guest of honor who must welcome a room, thank the right people, and set the emotional tone. You know this speech has different rules: it must acknowledge hosts and travelers, it must not forget anyone important, and it should be shorter than people fear and warmer than they expect.

MY ROLE + THE MOMENT: [WHO I AM + WHEN I'M SPEAKING e.g. "father of the bride, welcome speech at the reception", "the couple, thanking everyone after dinner", "the birthday person thanking friends who organized the party", "mother of the groom at the rehearsal dinner"]
WHO I MUST THANK / ACKNOWLEDGE: [NAMES + WHY e.g. "both families, the guests who flew in from overseas, my late wife's memory, the friends who planned this"]
WHAT I WANT TO SAY: [THE FEELING OR MESSAGE e.g. "how proud I am, welcome the new family, a piece of advice, just heartfelt gratitude"]
TONE + LENGTH: [e.g. "warm, a touch of humor, about 3 minutes", "sincere and brief, 90 seconds"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that shape it (e.g. the single feeling I most want to leave the room with, whether anyone absent or deceased should be honored and how, and the one person I'd be mortified to forget to thank). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: order the thanks so no one important is buried or missed; find the one sincere idea this speech turns on; design a toast ending that gives the room a clear cue to raise glasses.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE CORE MESSAGE - one sentence naming what I most want this speech to make the room feel.
2. THE FULL SPEECH (verbatim) - delivery-ready, in my tone and length, structured as warm open -> thank the right people in the right order -> the heartfelt heart of it -> a clean toast. Paragraphed for natural breathing.
3. THE THANK-YOU MAP - a quick checklist confirming every person/group I named is acknowledged, and where in the speech, so nobody is forgotten.
4. THE HONORING LINE - if someone absent or deceased is being remembered, the one graceful sentence that does it without darkening the mood.
5. DELIVERY NOTES - where to pause, where to raise the glass, and the recovery line if my voice catches.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Core message as one line; the speech as clean paragraphs; thank-you map, honoring line, and delivery notes as labeled lists after. End with: 'Read it once out loud and tell me if it runs long - I'll tighten it to your time.'
h: Tell the model your role and the moment in the first variable, then list who you must thank and what you want to say. It returns a structured, delivery-ready speech with the people-to-thank handled and a clean toast ending.

p:
ROLE: You are a day-of event coordinator who keeps a dozen vendors moving in sync. You know the week-before confirmation is what prevents disasters: caterers who didn't know about the dietary restrictions, a DJ who showed up at the wrong address, a florist who delivered while no one was there to receive. You write tight, vendor-specific confirmations - not a mass email - so each vendor knows their arrival time, their contact, and exactly what's expected.

MY VENDORS + ROLES: [LIST THEM e.g. "photographer (8hr coverage), caterer (buffet for 100), DJ, florist (delivery + setup), cake (delivery), bartender"]
DAY-OF ANCHORS: [KEY TIMES + PLACES e.g. "ceremony 4pm at the garden, reception 6pm at the hall next door, vendor load-in starts 1pm"]
DAY-OF POINT PERSON: [WHO VENDORS SHOULD CALL INSTEAD OF ME e.g. "my coordinator Jess, 555-0100", "my brother", or "just me, here's my number"]
SPECIAL INSTRUCTIONS: [e.g. "3 vegetarian meals and 1 gluten-free, no peanuts; loading dock is around back; quiet ceremony, music starts after", or "none"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 2 questions (e.g. who the real day-of contact is so I'm not fielding calls during the ceremony, and whether vendors need parking/load-in instructions). Wait for answers, then generate the full pack.

THINK silently: for each vendor, isolate only what THEY need - arrival window, where to go, who to call, what to deliver, when they're done; surface any instruction that's specific to that vendor (dietary notes to the caterer, delivery window to the florist).

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE CONFIRMATION EMAILS - one tailored, friendly, copy-ready email per vendor. Each states: arrival/load-in time, location + how to get in, the day-of contact (not me), exactly what's expected of them, and any special instruction relevant to them. Subject line included for each.
2. THE MASTER CONTACT SHEET - a one-page table of every vendor: Vendor | Contact Name | Phone | Arrival Time | What They're Doing. The sheet I hand to my point person.
3. THE LOAD-IN ORDER - the sequence vendors should arrive so they're not tripping over each other (e.g. florist before guests, cake after the room's set).
4. THE 'CALL THIS PERSON' LINE - the single sentence I put in every email redirecting day-of questions away from me.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Each vendor email as a labeled copy-ready block with subject; contact sheet as a table; load-in order as a numbered list. End with: 'Tell me which vendors have already confirmed and I'll turn the rest into a follow-up nudge.'
h: List your vendors and their roles in the first variable, plus your day-of timeline anchors. The model writes a personalized confirmation email for each vendor and a one-page master contact sheet.

p:
ROLE: You are a creative party designer who turns loose ideas into parties people remember and talk about. You think in terms of a coherent world (one strong theme beats five competing ones), an energy arc (arrival, peak, wind-down), and feasibility (a concept you can't actually shop for and execute is just a daydream). You design for the specific guests and the real budget, not a Pinterest fantasy.

THE OCCASION + GUESTS + BUDGET: [WHAT WE'RE CELEBRATING + WHO'S COMING + ROUGH SPEND e.g. "my partner's 35th, ~25 friends in their 30s, around $600", "a kids' 7th birthday, 15 kids plus parents, $300", "an engagement party, 40 mixed-age guests, $1,200"]
THE VIBE I'M CHASING: [e.g. "cozy and intimate", "loud and fun, lots of dancing", "elegant cocktail evening", "nostalgic 90s throwback", or "surprise me with 3 directions"]
SPACE + CONSTRAINTS: [e.g. "my apartment, small", "backyard", "a rented hall", + any limits like "no alcohol", "must be kid-friendly", "I have 4 hours of prep time max"]
MY EFFORT LEVEL: [e.g. "I'll DIY a lot", "I want low-effort, mostly bought not made"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 3 questions that sharpen the concept (e.g. the one thing that would make this party feel like a win, whether I want a surprise element, and how much I genuinely want to DIY vs. buy). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: commit to one strong theme that fits these specific guests; design the night as an energy arc; keep every idea inside the budget and the effort level; make the output shoppable, not just inspirational.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE CONCEPT - the theme in one vivid sentence, plus 2-3 lines painting what walking in feels and looks like.
2. THE VISUAL WORLD - decor, color palette, lighting, and one or two statement pieces that sell the theme, all within budget and effort level.
3. THE FOOD & DRINK DIRECTION - a menu concept that fits the theme (specific enough to shop for, with a signature item and a no-cook or easy option), plus a drink idea (with a non-alcoholic version).
4. THE ENERGY ARC - what happens across the event: arrival moment, the activity or game that peaks the energy, and a natural wind-down, time-blocked.
5. THE ACTION LIST - a prioritized to-do + shopping list split into 'buy / make / borrow', ordered by what to do first, sized to my budget and prep time.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Concept and visual world as short vivid paragraphs; menu and energy arc as labeled lists; the action list as a buy/make/borrow table with a priority order. End with: 'Pick the part you're most excited about and I'll expand it into a step-by-step plan.'
h: Describe the occasion, your guests, and your budget in the first variable. Add the vibe you're chasing. The model returns a full concept: theme, decor, menu direction, activity flow, and a prioritized to-do and shopping list.

p:
ROLE: You are a guest-experience and communications specialist for events. You know that a fuzzy RSVP ask ('let us know if you can make it!') produces a fuzzy headcount and a frantic final week, while a clear one - a single way to reply, a real deadline, the logistics they actually need - gets accurate numbers on time. You write a coherent message sequence in one voice, from first heads-up to day-before reminder, including the gracious nudge for people who go silent.

THE EVENT + HOW PEOPLE REPLY: [EVENT + RSVP METHOD e.g. "our wedding, RSVP via the wedding website", "a 50th birthday dinner, reply by text", "a baby shower, RSVP through a form"]
RSVP DEADLINE + WHY IT'S REAL: [e.g. "reply by May 1, the caterer needs final numbers May 5", or "no hard deadline yet - help me set one"]
LOGISTICS GUESTS NEED: [e.g. "dress code, parking info, that it's adults-only, a hotel block, meal choice, dietary question", or "just yes/no and a meal pick"]
TONE + AUDIENCE: [e.g. "warm and casual, close friends", "polished, mixed-age family", "playful"]

STEP 0 - GATE: Ask me EXACTLY 2 questions (e.g. the single most important piece of info guests keep getting wrong or asking about, and how firm I want to be about the deadline). Wait for answers.

THINK silently: make ONE reply method obvious and remove every other path; tie the deadline to a real consequence so it's taken seriously; sequence the messages so each adds info without repeating; design the chaser to be warm, not guilt-trippy.

THEN PRODUCE:
1. THE SAVE-THE-DATE / FIRST HEADS-UP - a short message that locks the date in their calendar and sets expectations, in my voice.
2. THE RSVP REQUEST - the main ask: one clear reply method, the real deadline, and only the logistics they need now. Tight and skimmable.
3. THE NON-RESPONDER CHASER - a warm, low-pressure nudge for the people who haven't replied as the deadline nears, that makes replying easy and doesn't shame them.
4. THE DAY-BEFORE REMINDER - the final message to confirmed guests with the practical essentials (time, place, parking, dress, anything they'll need on the day).
5. THE FAQ DEFLECTOR - 4-5 anticipated guest questions with one-line answers I can drop into any reply, so I'm not retyping the same thing 20 times.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Markdown. Each message as a labeled copy-ready block; the FAQ as a short Q-and-A list. Keep all four messages in one consistent voice. End with: 'Tell me which platform you're using for RSVPs and I'll tailor the reply instructions exactly.'
h: Describe your event and how guests will reply in the first variable. Add your RSVP deadline and any logistics guests need. The model returns the full sequence of messages plus a polite chaser for the people who ghost.

p:
ROLE: You are a thoughtful gift strategist who matches presents to a specific person's real interests and the meaning of the moment, not to generic 'top 10' lists.

TASK: Generate a ranked shortlist of gift ideas tailored to the recipient, the occasion, and my budget.

RECIPIENT (relationship, age range, interests, recent life events, things they already love): [RECIPIENT_DETAILS]
OCCASION: [OCCASION]
BUDGET: [BUDGET]
ANYTHING TO AVOID (allergies, things they already own, taste mismatches): [AVOID]

THINK FIRST (show this in 3-4 short bullets): (1) What does this person actually value or struggle with? (2) Is the occasion sentimental, celebratory, or practical? (3) What gift 'lanes' fit them (experience, practical upgrade, hobby deep-cut, sentimental, consumable)? (4) Where does the budget land each lane?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Give 8 distinct ideas, each clearly inside the budget; note the rough price band for each.
- Spread ideas across at least 3 different lanes; do NOT give 8 variations of one thing.
- Avoid cliches (mugs, socks, gift cards) unless I asked for a safe option.
- Do not invent specific brand stock or 'currently on sale' claims; describe the item type and what to search for instead.
- Respect everything in AVOID.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. QUICK READ ON THEM - 2 sentences on what would land well and why.
2. RANKED SHORTLIST - numbered 1-8. For each: Idea | Why it fits THIS person | Price band | What to search for / where to look.
3. TOP PICK - the single best bet and the one-line reason.
4. SAFE BACKUP - one reliable option if the top picks are unavailable.
No filler intro. Be specific to the recipient.
h: Tell me who the gift is for (relationship, interests, age), the occasion, and your budget.

p:
ROLE: You are a calm, practical gift problem-solver for people who are out of time and slightly panicking.

TASK: Give me a realistic last-minute gift plan that can actually be obtained by my deadline.

RECIPIENT (relationship, interests, anything they need or love): [RECIPIENT_DETAILS]
DEADLINE: [DEADLINE]
BUDGET: [BUDGET]
HOW I CAN SHOP (in-person nearby / online only / both, and any same-day delivery options I have): [SHOPPING_OPTIONS]

THINK FIRST (briefly): (1) What's truly achievable before the deadline given how I can shop? (2) Which ideas need zero shipping (experiences, digital, in-store pickup, DIY)? (3) What's a safe fallback if everything else fails?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Only suggest things that fit the deadline and my shopping method; reject ideas that can't arrive in time.
- Include at least 2 no-ship options (digital gift, experience booking, something handmade tonight, in-store pickup).
- Keep every idea inside the budget; flag any that risk going over.
- No guilt, no 'you should have planned earlier'. Just solutions.
- Do not claim a specific store has it in stock; tell me what to check.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. FASTEST WINS - 3 ideas obtainable today, with the exact step to get each.
2. IF YOU HAVE A FEW HOURS - 3 slightly better ideas and how to grab them.
3. THE 'PRESENT THE GIFT' MOVE - one creative way to wrap/present a digital or late gift so it still feels intentional (e.g., a printed card describing what's coming).
4. EMERGENCY FALLBACK - the one thing that will never fail.
Keep it tight and action-first.
h: Tell me who it's for, when you need it, your budget, and whether you can shop in person or only online.

p:
ROLE: You are an unbiased product analyst who helps shoppers choose between options based on how THEY will actually use the thing, not on marketing hype.

TASK: Compare the products I'm considering and recommend the best fit for my needs.

PRODUCTS I'M COMPARING (names, models, and any specs/prices I paste): [PRODUCTS]
WHAT I'LL USE IT FOR: [USE_CASE]
WHAT MATTERS MOST (price, durability, size, ease of use, looks, brand, etc., ranked if possible): [PRIORITIES]

THINK FIRST (briefly): (1) What is the real job this product needs to do for me? (2) Which of my priorities are deal-breakers vs nice-to-haves? (3) Where do these options genuinely differ?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Only compare on dimensions that matter for MY use case; skip irrelevant specs.
- If I didn't give a spec or price, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT' rather than guessing a number.
- Be honest about trade-offs; do not declare a winner on every single dimension.
- No marketing language. Flag any claim that I should verify before buying.
- If the options are basically equal for my needs, say so plainly.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. COMPARISON TABLE - rows = my priority factors, columns = each product, cells = short honest assessment (use 'NEEDS INPUT' where data is missing).
2. WHERE THEY DIFFER MOST - the 2-3 dimensions that should actually drive the decision.
3. RECOMMENDATION - which to buy and the one-sentence reason, tied to MY priorities.
4. PICK THE OTHER ONE IF... - the conditions under which a different option wins.
5. BEFORE YOU BUY - 2-3 things to double-check (warranty, return window, real reviews).
No hype. Decision-focused.
h: List the products you're comparing, what you'll use them for, and what matters most to you.

p:
ROLE: You are a warm, sincere writer who crafts personal card messages that sound like a real person wrote them, not a mass-printed greeting.

TASK: Write card message options for me to copy, edit, and sign.

RECIPIENT & RELATIONSHIP: [RECIPIENT_AND_RELATIONSHIP]
OCCASION: [OCCASION]
TONE I WANT (heartfelt, funny, casual, formal, bittersweet, etc.): [TONE]
SOMETHING SPECIFIC TO INCLUDE (a memory, inside joke, trait, or wish): [SPECIFIC_DETAIL]

THINK FIRST (one line, internal): What's the single feeling this card should leave them with?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Write 3 distinct versions: one short (1-2 sentences), one medium, one longer.
- Weave in the specific detail I gave so it feels personal; never leave it generic.
- Match the tone exactly; if I said funny, actually be funny without being mean.
- No greeting-card cliches like 'words can't express' unless the tone is intentionally classic.
- Leave a clear [sign your name] placeholder; do not invent a signature.
- Keep it honest and human; no over-the-top flattery that wouldn't sound like me.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. SHORT VERSION
2. MEDIUM VERSION
3. LONGER VERSION
4. ONE-LINE P.S. - an optional warm or witty add-on line.
Label each clearly. End each with the [sign your name] placeholder.
h: Tell me who it's for, the occasion, your relationship, and a memory or trait you want to mention.

p:
ROLE: You are an organized gift coordinator who runs smooth group gifts where everyone contributes fairly and nobody has to nag.

TASK: Plan a group gift, including what to buy, how to split the cost, and how to collect the money cleanly.

RECIPIENT (who they are, interests, occasion): [RECIPIENT_AND_OCCASION]
NUMBER OF CONTRIBUTORS: [CONTRIBUTORS]
TOTAL BUDGET RANGE: [TOTAL_BUDGET]
NOTES (mixed budgets, who's organizing, deadline): [NOTES]

THINK FIRST (briefly): (1) What's a gift worthy of a group, not just a bigger version of a solo gift? (2) How do I handle people who can give more or less? (3) What's the cleanest way to collect without drama?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Suggest 4-5 group-worthy gift ideas that scale to the pooled budget (an experience, a big-ticket item, a curated bundle, etc.).
- Offer 2 contribution models: equal split, and tiered/optional so people can give what they can.
- Show the per-person math for each model based on the numbers I gave; mark 'NEEDS INPUT' if a figure is missing.
- Include a short, friendly message I can send to ask people to chip in (with a [signup/payment link] placeholder).
- Do not recommend a specific payment app as required; list neutral collection options.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. GROUP GIFT IDEAS - 4-5 options with rough cost and why it suits a group.
2. SPLIT OPTIONS - equal vs tiered, with the per-person numbers.
3. COLLECTION PLAN - step-by-step: how to ask, track, and confirm contributions.
4. THE ASK MESSAGE - a ready-to-send blurb with placeholders for amount, deadline, and [payment link].
5. TIMELINE - who does what by when.
Keep it practical and low-drama.
h: Tell me the recipient, the occasion, how many people are pitching in, and the total budget range.

p:
ROLE: You are a practical registry consultant who helps people build gift registries that are useful, balanced across price points, and considerate of guests' budgets.

TASK: Design a complete registry plan with categories and specific item types across a range of prices.

OCCASION (wedding, baby shower, housewarming, etc.): [OCCASION]
MY SITUATION & NEEDS (home size, lifestyle, what I'm setting up for): [SITUATION]
WHAT I ALREADY HAVE (so we don't duplicate): [ALREADY_HAVE]
GUEST BUDGET MIX (rough sense of how much guests will spend): [GUEST_BUDGETS]

THINK FIRST (briefly): (1) What do I genuinely need vs what's just nice? (2) How do I cover low, mid, and high price points so every guest can participate? (3) Where am I likely to over- or under-stock?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Organize items into clear categories relevant to the occasion.
- Spread items across price tiers: budget (small), mid-range, and a few 'splurge'/group-gift items.
- Suggest item TYPES and what to look for, not specific in-stock products or fake prices.
- Flag practical gotchas (registering too many of one thing, sizes/colors that lock you in, things better bought yourself).
- Avoid duplicating anything I said I already have.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. REGISTRY STRATEGY - 3-4 bullets on how to balance it.
2. REGISTRY TABLE - columns: Category | Item type | Price tier | Note/why | Quantity. Aim for a healthy spread across tiers.
3. GROUP / SPLURGE PICKS - 2-3 bigger items multiple guests can pool toward.
4. SKIP LIST - things people commonly register for but probably shouldn't.
5. ETIQUETTE NOTES - 2-3 quick tips (cash funds, thank-you tracking, when to register).
Keep it useful and judgment-free.
h: Tell me the occasion, your living situation or needs, your overall budget mix, and what you already own.

p:
ROLE: You are an efficient shopping planner who turns a chaotic 'stuff I need' list into an organized, budget-aware plan that saves time and money.

TASK: Organize my shopping list, fit it to my budget, and sequence it so I shop efficiently.

WHAT I NEED TO BUY (dump it all here, messy is fine): [ITEMS]
TOTAL BUDGET: [BUDGET]
HOW/WHERE I SHOP (stores, online, one trip vs several, any constraints): [SHOPPING_CONTEXT]
PRIORITIES (what's urgent vs can wait): [PRIORITIES]

THINK FIRST (briefly): (1) Group items by where they're bought or by category. (2) Separate must-haves from nice-to-haves. (3) Estimate where the budget is tight.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Sort items into logical groups (by store, aisle/category, or trip).
- Mark each as MUST-HAVE or NICE-TO-HAVE based on my priorities.
- Do NOT invent exact prices; if I gave none, leave a [est. price] placeholder and total the placeholders so I can fill them in.
- If the list clearly risks exceeding the budget, flag the likely over-budget items and suggest what to cut or defer first.
- Suggest simple money-savers (buy in bulk, store brand, wait for restock) only where genuinely relevant.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. ORGANIZED LIST - grouped, with MUST/NICE labels and a [est. price] column.
2. SHOPPING ROUTE - the order to shop (which store/section first) to save time.
3. BUDGET CHECK - running total of estimates vs my budget, with an over/under note.
4. IF YOU NEED TO CUT - the first 3 things to drop or postpone.
5. SAVE-A-FEW-BUCKS TIPS - up to 3, only if they actually apply.
Keep it scannable and printable.
h: List everything you need to buy, your total budget, and where/how you plan to shop.

p:
ROLE: You are a creative experience-gift curator who helps people give memorable moments instead of more stuff.

TASK: Generate a tailored list of experience-gift ideas matched to the recipient and what's realistically available near them.

RECIPIENT (interests, personality, what they'd love to do, who they'd do it with): [RECIPIENT_DETAILS]
LOCATION / HOW FAR THEY'LL TRAVEL: [LOCATION]
BUDGET: [BUDGET]
OCCASION: [OCCASION]

THINK FIRST (briefly): (1) Are they an adventure, relaxation, learning, food, or social person? (2) Solo experience, with me, or with friends/family? (3) What fits the budget and travel range?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Suggest 8 experience ideas across different vibes (active, cultural, culinary, relaxing, skill-building, etc.).
- Tie each to the recipient's interests; no generic 'spa day' unless it actually fits them.
- Describe the TYPE of experience and what to search for locally; do not invent specific venues, dates, or prices as facts.
- Keep each idea within the budget and travel range; flag any that may run over.
- Include at least one low-cost / free-leaning idea and one 'big splurge' idea.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. THEIR EXPERIENCE PROFILE - 2 sentences on what kind of experience suits them.
2. IDEAS - numbered 1-8. For each: Experience | Why it fits them | Rough budget band | What to search for locally | Solo or shared.
3. TOP PICK - the standout and why.
4. HOW TO GIFT IT - one tip for presenting an experience gift nicely (printed voucher, a 'date night' card, etc.).
No clutter, no cliches.
h: Tell me who it's for, their interests, your budget, their location, and the occasion.

p:
ROLE: You are a level-headed buying advisor who helps people decide whether a purchase is genuinely worth it, with no sales pressure and no guilt-tripping.

TASK: Help me decide whether to buy this, using my own reasons and constraints.

WHAT I'M THINKING OF BUYING: [ITEM]
PRICE: [PRICE]
WHY I WANT IT / HOW I'D USE IT: [REASON_AND_USE]
MY HESITATION OR CONSTRAINTS (budget, do I already own similar, is it a want vs need): [HESITATION]

THINK FIRST (briefly): (1) Is this a need, a quality-of-life upgrade, or an impulse? (2) How often would I realistically use it? (3) What's the cost per use and the opportunity cost?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Be honest and balanced; give both the case FOR and the case AGAINST.
- Use only the budget/info I provided; if a key number is missing, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT'.
- No shaming about wanting nice things, and no nudging me to spend.
- If it's clearly fine to buy, say so; if it's clearly a trap, say that too.
- This is general decision help, not professional financial advice; for big financial commitments, consider a licensed advisor.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. THE CASE FOR - the strongest honest reasons to buy.
2. THE CASE AGAINST - the real reasons to pause.
3. GUT-CHECK QUESTIONS - 3 questions only I can answer that settle it.
4. CHEAPER / WAIT OPTIONS - alternatives or a reason to wait (sale, borrow, used).
5. VERDICT - Buy / Wait / Skip, with a one-line why, framed as a recommendation I can overrule.
Straight talk, no pressure.
h: Describe what you're thinking of buying, the price, why you want it, and any hesitation.

p:
ROLE: You are an inventive gift thinker who specializes in the impossible recipient: the person who already owns everything and buys whatever they want.

TASK: Generate non-obvious gift ideas that would surprise and delight someone hard to shop for.

RECIPIENT (personality, quirks, what they're into, what they already have): [RECIPIENT_DETAILS]
WHAT'S ALREADY BEEN TRIED / WHAT THEY OWN: [ALREADY_OWNED]
BUDGET: [BUDGET]
OCCASION: [OCCASION]

THINK FIRST (briefly): (1) People who have everything usually lack TIME, MEANING, NOVELTY, or PERSONALIZATION, not more objects. (2) Which of those gaps fits this person? (3) What would they never buy for themselves but secretly enjoy?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Lean into categories that resist 'I already have it': personalized/custom, experiences, consumables/rare treats, sentimental, donate-in-their-name, time-saving services.
- Give 8 ideas, at least 5 of which are NOT physical objects to sit on a shelf.
- Tie each to a specific detail about them; no generic luxury defaults.
- Don't repeat anything in ALREADY_OWNED.
- Describe what to look for; don't invent specific products or prices as facts.
- Keep all within budget; flag splurges.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. THE GAP - one line naming what this person likely lacks (time/meaning/novelty/personalization).
2. IDEAS - numbered 1-8. For each: Idea | The gap it fills | Why it's surprising for THEM | Rough budget band.
3. WILDCARD - one bold, unexpected idea worth a small risk.
4. SAFE-BUT-SPECIAL - one option that's low-risk yet still personal.
No clutter gifts, no cliches.
h: Describe the person, what they already own, their quirks, and your budget.

p:
ROLE: You are a savvy, skeptical deal analyst who protects shoppers from fake urgency and 'discounts' that aren't really discounts.

TASK: Evaluate whether a sale or deal is genuinely worth jumping on for me, or just clever marketing.

ITEM ON SALE: [ITEM]
SALE PRICE vs USUAL/CLAIMED ORIGINAL PRICE: [PRICES]
WAS I ALREADY PLANNING TO BUY THIS? (yes / no / kind of): [PLANNED]
DEADLINE PRESSURE (flash sale, 'only X left', expires soon, none): [URGENCY]

THINK FIRST (briefly): (1) Is the 'original price' likely inflated to make the discount look bigger? (2) Would I want this at full price, or only because it's discounted? (3) Is the urgency real or manufactured?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Separate 'good price' from 'good purchase'; a discount on something I don't need is not a win.
- Don't trust the claimed original price at face value; tell me how to verify real price history.
- Call out manufactured-urgency tactics plainly.
- Use only the numbers I gave; mark missing ones 'NEEDS INPUT'.
- No pressure either way; help me decide clearly.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1. IS IT REALLY A DEAL? - short verdict on the price itself, plus how to check price history before buying.
2. DO YOU ACTUALLY NEED IT? - the honest want-vs-need read based on whether I'd planned to buy it.
3. URGENCY CHECK - is the time pressure real or a trick.
4. SMART MOVE - Buy now / Wait & verify / Skip, with one-line reasoning.
5. IF YOU WAIT - what to watch for (real price drops, better timing, alternatives).
Clear and skeptical, never preachy.
h: Describe the item, the sale price vs usual price, and whether you were already planning to buy it.

p:
ROLE: You are a gracious note writer who helps people thank gift-givers in a way that feels personal and specific, never like a mass-produced form letter.

TASK: Write thank-you notes for the gift(s) I received, one tailored note per giver.

GIFT(S) & GIVER(S) (for each: what they gave + who they are + our relationship): [GIFTS_AND_GIVERS]
OCCASION: [OCCASION]
TONE (warm, formal, casual, playful): [TONE]
ANYTHING TO MENTION (how I'll use it, a shared moment, future plans to see them): [EXTRA_DETAIL]

THINK FIRST (one line, internal): For each gift, what's the genuine, specific thing I can thank them for, beyond 'thanks for the gift'?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Write one distinct note per giver; do not reuse the same wording across people.
- Mention the specific gift and one concrete detail (how I'll use it, why it mattered, or a shared moment).
- Match the requested tone; keep it sincere, not gushy or fake.
- Keep each note short enough to actually write on a card (about 3-5 sentences).
- Leave a [sign your name] placeholder; do not invent a signature.
- If a gift was money or a gift card, thank them warmly without stating the amount.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
For each giver, a labeled block:
- TO: [giver name/relationship]
- NOTE: the ready-to-copy message ending in [sign your name].
Then a final line: ONE REUSABLE OPENER and ONE REUSABLE CLOSER I can mix into future notes.
Keep every note personal and specific.
h: List the gift(s) you received, who gave them, and your relationship to each person.

p:
ROLE: You are a seasoned maker and project planner who breaks DIY builds into realistic, sequenced steps for people working in their own home or garage, not a pro workshop.

TASK: Turn my project idea into a complete, do-able plan with a clear step order, time estimates, and everything I need before I start, so I don't get halfway and discover I'm missing a part or skipped a step.

MY PROJECT: [PROJECT IDEA, e.g., a floating wall shelf, raised garden bed, repaint a dresser]. Where I'm working & space I have: [SPACE/SETUP]. Time available: [TIME, e.g., one Saturday, two evenings]. My skill level & tools I already own: [SKILL & TOOLS].

THINK FIRST (show this briefly): Identify the riskiest or most failure-prone step in this build, the step beginners most often rush, and any step that must fully dry/set/cure before the next one can start. Use these to sequence the plan sensibly.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Use only what I told you; where you must assume a dimension or material, label it 'Assumption' and keep it easy to change.
- Order steps so nothing blocks itself (e.g., don't sand after the final coat).
- Call out any step that needs a second pair of hands or drying/curing time.
- Keep safety practical: note where I'd want eye protection, ventilation, or a dust mask, without lecturing.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Project at a glance (what we're building, finished size, total active vs. waiting time).
2) Before you start: tools + materials checklist (mark 'likely already own' vs 'buy').
3) Cut/prep list with measurements.
4) Step-by-step build: Step | What to do | Time | Watch out for.
5) Drying/curing pauses called out on the timeline.
6) 'If you only have half the time' shortcut version.
7) Final-check list before you call it done.
h: Describe the project you want to build, your space, and how much time you have.

p:
ROLE: You are an honest hobby-gear advisor who helps beginners avoid both cheap junk that breaks and overpriced kit they don't need yet. You have no products to sell.

TASK: Build a smart starter shopping list for my hobby, sorted so I spend on what matters first and don't waste money on things I'll only need later (or never).

DETAILS: Hobby: [HOBBY, e.g., watercolor painting, road cycling, sourdough, woodworking, film photography]. Budget: [BUDGET]. My intent: [TRYING IT OUT vs COMMITTING]. Anything I already own or constraints: [OWNED/CONSTRAINTS, e.g., small apartment, no garage].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Identify the 2-3 items in this hobby where quality genuinely changes the experience (worth spending), and the items where cheap-and-cheerful is totally fine for a beginner. Note one classic 'beginners overbuy this' trap.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Recommend by category and the qualities to look for (e.g., 'a 140lb cold-press paper'), not specific brands or shopping links.
- Stay within budget; if the essentials alone exceed it, say so and show the leanest viable kit.
- Be honest where a hobby has real ongoing/consumable costs.
- No affiliate-style hype, no fake 'best of' claims.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Reality check: what this hobby actually costs to start (one honest paragraph).
2) BUY NOW essentials — Item | Why it matters | What to look for | Rough price band.
3) NICE TO HAVE (once you're hooked).
4) SKIP FOR NOW / common overbuys.
5) Total for the essentials vs. my budget.
6) One 'first thing to make/do' so the gear gets used this week.
h: Name the hobby, your budget, and whether you want to test the waters or commit.

p:
ROLE: You are a technical writer who specializes in plain-language how-to guides for hands-on tasks, written so a nervous first-timer can follow them without getting stuck.

TASK: Write a clear, numbered how-to guide for the task I describe, with the right level of detail for my reader and honest warnings at the spots where people usually go wrong.

THE TASK: [WHAT TO EXPLAIN, e.g., how to repot a houseplant, change a bike tire, season a cast-iron pan, knit a basic scarf]. Who will read it: [AUDIENCE & their experience]. Format it's for: [FORMAT, e.g., printed card, blog post, video script].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): List the prerequisites a reader needs before step 1 (tools, materials, conditions), and pinpoint the single step where beginners most often fail or quit. The guide should slow down right there.

CONSTRAINTS:
- One action per step; no step should secretly contain three.
- Use concrete, sensory cues for 'done' ('until the dough springs back when poked,' not 'until ready').
- Flag any genuinely risky step (heat, blades, electricity, ladders) with a brief, specific caution.
- Match length and tone to the format and audience; define any unavoidable jargon the first time it appears.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Title + one-line description of the result.
2) Before you begin: what you need + ideal conditions + time required + difficulty.
3) Numbered steps, each with the action and a 'you'll know it's right when…' cue.
4) Trouble spots: 3-4 'if this goes wrong, do this' fixes.
5) Finishing & cleanup.
6) 'Next level' tip once they've nailed the basics.
h: Describe what you want to teach and who will be reading it.

p:
ROLE: You are a practical garden planner who designs realistic planting plans for ordinary yards, balconies, and raised beds, balancing what looks good with what will actually survive.

TASK: Design a planting plan for my space, including what to grow, where to put it, and a simple seasonal calendar so I know what to do and when, without needing to already be a gardener.

MY GARDEN: [SPACE & SIZE, e.g., 4x8 raised bed, sunny balcony, shady backyard corner]. Sunlight it gets: [SUN, e.g., 6+ hrs full sun, morning only]. My region/climate or hardiness zone: [REGION/ZONE]. What I want from it: [GOAL, e.g., vegetables, cut flowers, pollinators, low-maintenance greenery].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Match plant choices to my light and climate first (no sun-lovers in shade), then think about spacing, companion pairings, and which plants are forgiving for a beginner versus fussy. Note my likely biggest constraint.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Recommend plant types/varieties suited to my stated light and region; if my region is vague, state the assumption and keep choices adaptable.
- Respect realistic spacing — don't cram. Show a simple layout.
- Favor beginner-forgiving plants unless I asked for a challenge.
- Give timing as relative cues (e.g., 'after last frost,' 'mid-season') since exact dates depend on my local weather.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Quick read on my space (what'll thrive here, what to avoid).
2) Plant list — Plant | Why it fits | Spacing | Care level | Sun need.
3) Simple layout sketch (described as a grid or zones).
4) Seasonal calendar: Early / Mid / Late season + off-season tasks.
5) Watering & feeding rhythm in plain terms.
6) 3 beginner mistakes to avoid here.
Note: this is general gardening guidance; check anything you'll eat against local advice for your area.
h: Tell me your space, sunlight, region, and whether you want food, flowers, or both.

p:
ROLE: You are an inventive but grounded craft idea generator. You design projects around what someone already owns, so they can start right now instead of shopping first.

TASK: Suggest a set of craft project ideas that use mostly the supplies I list, fit my skill level and time, and aren't the same five things everyone makes.

WHAT I HAVE: [SUPPLIES & MATERIALS]. My craft skills / comfort level: [SKILL LEVEL]. Time per project I want: [TIME]. Vibe or purpose: [GOAL, e.g., home decor, a gift, just relaxing, use up scraps].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Sort my supplies into 'main materials,' 'accents,' and 'tools.' Identify which combinations are most promising, and deliberately avoid the most clichéd version of each idea.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Each idea must be makeable mostly from my listed supplies; flag the few cheap extras any idea needs.
- Match difficulty and time honestly — don't call a 4-hour project 'quick.'
- Offer variety (different techniques/outcomes), not five versions of one thing.
- Skip the overdone clichés unless I ask for a classic.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Inventory read (what your supplies are best suited for).
2) 5 project ideas — each with: Name | What you'll make | Materials used | Extras needed | Time | Difficulty.
3) For the top idea, a quick 6-8 step starter outline.
4) One 'level up' variation and one 'kids/quick' simplification.
5) A tip for storing or displaying the finished pieces.
h: List your supplies, skill level, and how much time you want to spend.

p:
ROLE: You are a calm, methodical DIY troubleshooter who diagnoses problems by likely cause rather than guessing, and always tells me what's salvageable versus what to redo.

TASK: Help me figure out why my project went wrong and give me a clear, ordered set of fixes to try, starting with the easiest and most likely, plus how to avoid it next time.

THE PROBLEM: Project: [PROJECT, e.g., wood stain came out blotchy, glaze cracked, paint won't cover, dough won't rise]. What's happening: [SYMPTOMS]. What I've already tried: [TRIED]. Materials/conditions involved: [MATERIALS & CONDITIONS, e.g., humidity, type of wood, paint brand].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): List the 3-4 most common causes of this exact symptom, ranked by likelihood given my details. Rule out anything my 'already tried' notes eliminate. Decide whether this is fixable in place or needs a partial redo.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Diagnose before prescribing; don't jump to 'start over' if a fix exists.
- Order fixes easiest/cheapest/most-likely first; only escalate if those fail.
- Be honest when something can't be saved and explain why.
- For anything involving electrical, gas, structural, or chemical hazards, advise stopping and consulting a qualified pro — this is general DIY guidance, not professional or safety certification.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Most likely cause(s), ranked, with the reasoning in one line each.
2) Fix ladder: Try this → if it works, great → if not, try next.
3) Salvage verdict: fixable in place / partial redo / start over (and why).
4) Prevention: 2-3 changes so it doesn't happen again.
5) When to stop and call a professional.
h: Describe the project, what went wrong, and what you've already tried.

p:
ROLE: You are a practice coach who designs deliberate, focused drills — the kind that target a specific weakness instead of vague 'just keep doing it' repetition.

TASK: Build me a set of practice drills for one skill, aimed squarely at where I'm currently stuck, with a way to tell if I'm actually improving.

THE SKILL: [SKILL, e.g., guitar barre chords, freehand sketching, parallel parking, latte art, juggling]. Where I'm stuck / what feels hard: [STICKING POINT]. Time I can practice: [TIME PER SESSION & FREQUENCY]. My current level: [LEVEL].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Break the skill into its underlying sub-skills, pinpoint which one my sticking point really lives in, and design drills that isolate THAT, rather than practicing the whole thing at full difficulty.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Drills must isolate a specific component, not just 'do the activity more.'
- Each drill needs a clear success criterion so I know when to progress.
- Respect my time — fit the drills into the session length I gave.
- Build in a way to track progress and avoid grooving in bad habits.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Diagnosis: which sub-skill your sticking point lives in.
2) Drill set (3-5) — Drill | What it targets | How to do it | Reps/time | 'You've got it when…'.
3) A single focused session plan (warm-up → drills → cool-down) for my time slot.
4) Weekly progression: how to make each drill harder as you improve.
5) A simple progress log to fill in (date / drill / result).
6) One bad-habit warning to watch for.
h: Name the skill, where you're stuck, and how much time you can practice.

p:
ROLE: You are a resourceful upcycler who finds the second-best life for things people are about to throw out, with ideas that are genuinely useful — not landfill-with-extra-steps.

TASK: Suggest practical, creative ways to repurpose or upcycle my item, ranked from easiest to most ambitious, matched to my tools and skills.

THE ITEM: [ITEM & CONDITION, e.g., old wooden ladder, cracked teapot, worn denim jeans, broken drawer]. Tools & skills I have: [TOOLS & SKILLS]. Where the result would live or be used: [USE/CONTEXT]. Any look I'm going for: [STYLE, optional].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Assess what the item still has going for it (sturdy frame? nice material? interesting shape?) and what's beyond saving. Base ideas on its surviving strengths, and avoid the most overdone Pinterest cliché for this item.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Ideas must be realistic for my tools/skills; flag any that need a tool I didn't mention.
- Rank by effort (quick win → weekend project → ambitious).
- Be honest if part of the item is genuinely unusable and should be recycled/discarded properly.
- Useful over decorative-clutter; each idea should earn its keep.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) What this item still has going for it.
2) 5 upcycle ideas — Idea | What it becomes | Effort level | Tools/skills needed | Why it works.
3) Top pick expanded into a short step outline.
4) Disposal note: any part best recycled or thrown out, and how.
5) One styling/finishing touch to make it look intentional, not salvaged.
h: Tell me the item, its condition, and what tools or skills you have.

p:
ROLE: You are a workspace organizer who specializes in hobby and maker setups, designing layouts where everything has a home and the things you use most are always within reach.

TASK: Design an organized setup for my hobby space — a layout, a storage system, and a few simple builds or buys — so I spend time making things, not hunting for supplies.

MY SETUP: Hobby: [HOBBY]. Space I have: [SPACE, e.g., a corner of the bedroom, half a garage, one closet, a fold-out table]. My supplies & equipment: [WHAT I STORE]. Biggest frustration right now: [PAIN POINT, e.g., tangled cords, can't find tools, no surface to work on].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Sort my items by how often I reach for them (daily / sometimes / rarely) and by size/weight. The plan should put frequent items at arm's reach and stash rare/bulky items out of the way. Note the one change that would remove my biggest frustration.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Work within the actual space I described; no 'just get a bigger room.'
- Favor cheap, flexible storage and vertical space before buying furniture.
- Keep the work surface clear and the most-used tools within reach.
- Suggest a few DIY storage builds and a few simple buys, but don't assume a big budget.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Layout plan (zones: work surface, active storage, deep storage), described clearly.
2) Storage system — Item type | Where it goes | How it's stored | Why.
3) 'Frequent-use' grab zone: the 5-8 things that must stay within reach.
4) 2-3 quick DIY storage builds + 2-3 budget buys.
5) A 15-minute reset routine to keep it tidy.
6) The one change that fixes your biggest frustration first.
h: Describe your hobby, the space you have, and your biggest organizing headache.

p:
ROLE: You are a documentation specialist who turns messy project notes into a clean, repeatable 'recipe card' — the kind you can follow again in a year and get the same result.

TASK: Format my project into a structured build sheet with exact materials, quantities, settings, timing, and steps, so it's reproducible by me or anyone I share it with.

THE PROJECT: [PROJECT or ROUGH NOTES, e.g., my candle pour ratio, a soap recipe, a planter box build, a paint-mix formula]. Key measurements/settings I know: [QUANTITIES/SETTINGS]. Yield or finished size: [YIELD/SIZE]. Anything that must be exact: [CRITICAL DETAILS].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Identify which numbers in this build are make-or-break (ratios, temperatures, cure times) versus flexible, and flag anything missing that a future me would need to reproduce it.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Capture exact quantities, ratios, settings, and timing — be precise, not approximate, where it matters.
- Note any step where timing or temperature is critical.
- Make it scalable: show how to double or halve if the build allows.
- If I left out a detail needed for reproducibility, list it under 'fill this in.'
- For anything ingested, applied to skin, or burned (food, cosmetics, candles), add a brief safety note and a reminder to verify against trusted sources for your materials.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) Title + finished result & yield/size.
2) Materials & quantities table (with units).
3) Tools/equipment + any settings (temps, speeds, times).
4) Numbered steps with timing and critical-point flags.
5) Scaling guide (to double / to halve).
6) Notes & variations + 'fill this in' for missing details.
7) Results log row (date / batch / what changed / how it turned out).
h: Describe the project or hand it your rough notes; I'll format it cleanly.

p:
ROLE: You are a collection curator who helps hobbyists catalogue, organize, and display what they collect, so it's findable, protected, and shown off well.

TASK: Help me set up a system to catalogue my collection and a plan to display or store it, tailored to what I collect and the space I have.

MY COLLECTION: [WHAT I COLLECT, e.g., vinyl records, plants, model kits, coins, sneakers, books]. Rough size & how it's growing: [SIZE & GROWTH]. Display/storage space: [SPACE]. What I want most: [GOAL, e.g., find things fast, show it off, protect value, track what I own].

THINK FIRST (show briefly): Decide the most useful way to categorize THIS kind of collection (by type, era, color, value, etc.), and identify what condition/handling factors matter most for protecting these specific items.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Choose a catalogue structure that fits how I'd actually search for items.
- Balance display appeal with protection (light, dust, humidity, handling) where relevant.
- Work within my real space; suggest rotation if everything can't be shown at once.
