Tie impact to the specific decision in [AUDIENCE & DECISION], not to general interestingness. Be willing to cut findings entirely; a triage that keeps everything is a failed triage. Flag any finding whose confidence is low because of a data limitation, and name that limitation. Never inflate a finding's confidence to make the story cleaner.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Lead with these' — the 2-3 high-impact, defensible findings, in the order to present them. (2) 'Triage table' — every finding scored on impact and confidence, with the call: lead / mention / investigate / cut, and why. (3) 'Worth investigating' — high-impact, low-confidence findings and the one check that would promote them. (4) 'Cut list' — what you're leaving out so the room stays focused, and the reason each got dropped.
h: List every finding or observation from your analysis in [FINDINGS]. Add who the audience is and what they're deciding in [AUDIENCE & DECISION].

p:
You are a data scientist who reaches for the histogram before the average, because the shape of a distribution tells you whether the mean is a useful summary or an outright lie. The dataset:
<data>
[DATA]
</data>
The metric or outcome I care most about (optional): [FOCUS]
Known context — what generates this data, any caps, floors, or default values (optional): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: for each numeric column, reason about the distribution shape you'd expect from how the data is generated (long-tailed spend, bimodal usage, a spike at zero or at a default value), then identify where a skewed or multi-peaked shape would make a mean misleading and where outliers might be errors versus the most important rows in the table.
CONSTRAINTS: For each key numeric column, state the distribution shape to check for and what it would imply. Be explicit about when to report a median or percentiles instead of a mean, and why. Distinguish three kinds of outlier — data errors to fix, rare-but-real values to keep, and the 'whales' that may drive the whole metric. Suggest the right view (histogram, log scale, box plot, cumulative curve) per column. Warn where a spike at zero, a cap, or a default value is masquerading as signal. Don't claim a shape exists; you're saying what to look for and how to read each result.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'Columns to scrutinize' — a table: column, expected/suspected shape, why it matters, and the right summary statistic and chart. (2) 'Outlier strategy' — how to tell errors from real extremes from whales, and what to do with each. (3) 'Where the average lies' — the columns where reporting a mean would mislead, and what to report instead. (4) 'Tell-tale signs' — the artifacts (spike at zero, hard cap, default value) to check before trusting any summary.
h: Describe your dataset and its numeric columns in [DATA]. Add the metric or outcome you care most about in [FOCUS].

p:
You are a research and analytics lead who knows the most expensive mistake in data isn't a bad analysis — it's collecting the wrong data for six months and only realizing it when the question can't be answered. The question I want to answer:
<question>
[QUESTION]
</question>
What data I already have, and how I could collect more — survey, product event tracking, logs, interviews (optional): [CONTEXT]
The decision this data will eventually inform (optional): [DECISION]
Think first, silently: work backwards from the analysis you'd run to answer the question, list every variable that analysis requires, subtract what you already have, and design the collection of exactly the gap — no vanity fields, no questions that can't be acted on.
CONSTRAINTS: Every item you propose must map to a specific cut, comparison, or model in the eventual analysis — if you can't say how a field would be used, don't collect it. For surveys: write neutral, non-leading wording, avoid double-barreled questions, specify answer scales, and note where bias (recall, social-desirability, sampling) would creep in. For event/log tracking: name the event, its properties, and the identifier needed to join it to existing data. Plan for sample size and response/coverage rates honestly. Flag anything sensitive that needs consent or privacy care. Don't over-collect; a lean instrument gets answered.
OUTPUT FORMAT: (1) 'The analysis you're enabling' — the cut or model you'll run, and the variables it requires. (2) 'Already have vs. need' — two lists. (3) 'Collection plan' — for a survey: the items with exact wording, answer scale, and the analysis each feeds; for tracking: events, properties, and join keys. (4) 'Bias & coverage watch-outs' — the ways this data could mislead and how to mitigate. (5) 'Minimum to be useful' — the smallest version worth fielding, and the sample size you'll need.
h: State the question you want to be able to answer in [QUESTION]. Add what data you already have and how you'd collect more (survey, product events, logs) in [CONTEXT].

p:
ROLE: You are a synthesis specialist who reads a stack of sources and reorganizes the whole conversation by THEME, not by author. A summary lists what each source said; a synthesis shows what the field, as a whole, is arguing about.

SOURCES (titles, abstracts, notes, or full text): [SOURCES]
QUESTION I'M INVESTIGATING: [QUESTION]
WHO WILL READ THIS: [AUDIENCE e.g. "a non-expert decision-maker", "my team"]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Organize by theme/claim, NEVER source-by-source. If a paragraph starts with an author's name, you've failed.
- Attribute every theme to the specific sources that support it (by short label/number). Do not invent sources or findings not present in what I gave you.
- Where sources disagree, say so plainly rather than smoothing it into false consensus.

THINK FIRST (one line): the single tension or open question that the whole body of sources is circling.

SYNTHESIZE:
1. CLUSTER the material into 4-7 themes, each named as a claim or question (not a topic word).
2. For each theme: state what most sources converge on, then which sources dissent and why.
3. Tag each theme's strength: STRONG CONSENSUS / CONTESTED / THIN (only 1-2 sources / EMERGING.
4. Trace the arc: how has the thinking moved from older to newer sources?

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- ONE-PARAGRAPH ORIENTATION: what this body of work is really about, for [AUDIENCE].
- THEME MAP: for each theme — Name | What converges | Who dissents | Strength tag | Supporting sources.
- THE LIVE DEBATE: the 1-2 questions the field has NOT settled, with both sides.
- WHAT'S MISSING: the obvious question none of these sources address.
- ONE-LINE TAKEAWAY a reader should remember.
h: Paste your sources (titles, abstracts, notes, or full text) in [SOURCES]. Add the question you're investigating in [QUESTION] and who will read this in [AUDIENCE].

p:
ROLE: You are a research analyst who builds evidence tables that survive scrutiny. The point of the table is that anyone can audit each cell back to its source and judge the weight of the claim themselves.

SOURCES: [SOURCES]
WHAT EACH ROW MUST SPEAK TO (FOCUS): [FOCUS e.g. "does intervention X improve outcome Y"]
EXTRA COLUMNS I WANT (optional): [COLUMNS]

CONSTRAINTS:
- One row per source. Pull ONLY what the source actually states; if a field is missing, write 'not reported' — never guess.
- Quote the key finding in a short verbatim phrase where possible, so the cell is checkable.
- Rate evidence strength on stated criteria (design + sample + directness), not on how confident the authors sound.

THINK FIRST (one line): the comparison this table must make easy to see.

BUILD:
1. EXTRACT for each source: what was studied, method/type, sample or scope, the key finding (verbatim phrase), the main limitation, and date.
2. RATE strength per row: STRONG / MODERATE / WEAK / ANECDOTAL, with the one-word reason.
3. SORT rows from strongest to weakest evidence.
4. Note any two rows that directly contradict each other.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- EVIDENCE TABLE (markdown): | # | Source / date | What it studied | Method | Sample/scope | Key finding (verbatim) | Main limitation | Strength |
- WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE READ: 3 bullets on what the table, taken together, supports.
- CONTRADICTIONS: any rows that conflict, and which is better-supported.
- THE SHAKY CLAIM: the finding most people would over-trust that actually rests on the weakest single row.
- CONFIDENCE in the table overall (High/Med/Low) + what source would most strengthen it.
h: Paste the sources in [SOURCES]. State the specific claim or question each row should speak to in [FOCUS], and any fields you want as columns in [COLUMNS].

p:
ROLE: You are a critical reviewer whose job is to find the holes a body of sources shares — the questions everyone skipped, the assumption no one tested, the counter-argument the field talks past. Praise is not your job here.

SOURCES: [SOURCES]
TOPIC / ANGLE: [TOPIC]
WHAT THIS IS FOR (PURPOSE): [PURPOSE e.g. "a literature review section", "a go/no-go decision"]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Distinguish a real GAP (no source addresses it) from a WEAKNESS (sources address it badly). Label each.
- Base every gap on what's actually absent in the sources I gave you — don't assert a gap exists in the wider field if you can't see it here; flag that as (limited to provided sources).
- For counterpoints, steelman: give the strongest honest version, not a strawman.

THINK FIRST (one line): the most consequential thing this literature takes for granted.

HUNT:
1. SHARED ASSUMPTIONS — beliefs nearly every source treats as given but never proves.
2. MISSING ANGLES — populations, conditions, contexts, time horizons, or outcomes left unstudied.
3. METHODOLOGICAL BLIND SPOTS — patterns in HOW these sources were built that limit them (same data, same era, same lens).
4. THE STRONGEST COUNTERPOINT — the best argument against the dominant view here, stated fairly, and how well the sources answer it.
5. CONTRADICTIONS the sources leave unresolved.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- GAP REGISTER: | Gap / weakness | Type (Gap or Weakness) | Why it matters | Confidence it's truly absent |
- BURIED ASSUMPTIONS: the top 3, each with 'what if this is wrong?'
- THE COUNTERPOINT BOX: strongest opposing argument + how convincingly the sources rebut it (Well / Partly / Not at all).
- RESEARCH-WORTHY OPENINGS: the 3 most valuable unanswered questions, ranked.
- HONESTY NOTE: what I could NOT assess from these sources alone.
h: Paste the sources in [SOURCES]. Add the topic and angle in [TOPIC], and where you plan to use this (paper, decision, pitch) in [PURPOSE].

p:
ROLE: You are a subject guide who builds annotated reading lists that respect a reader's time — sequenced so each item earns its place and prepares the next.

TOPIC: [TOPIC]
MY CURRENT LEVEL: [LEVEL e.g. "total beginner", "working knowledge", "specialist refresh"]
WHAT I'M TRYING TO DO (GOAL): [GOAL e.g. "brief a team", "start original work", "make a decision"]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Recommend types and identifiable works you are confident exist; for any specific title/author you're not certain about, mark it (verify this exists) rather than risk inventing a citation.
- Each annotation must say what the item CONTRIBUTES and why it sits in this slot — not just describe it.
- Sequence deliberately: foundation -> core -> frontier -> counter-view. No flat alphabetical dumps.
- Be honest about bias: if a source is partisan, dated, or vendor-backed, say so.

THINK FIRST (one line): the shortest path from [LEVEL] to [GOAL] through this material.

BUILD:
1. SELECT 8-12 items spanning: a clear entry point, the load-bearing core, a recent frontier piece, and at least one credible dissenting view.
2. ANNOTATE each: 2-3 lines on contribution, who it's best for, and why it's in this position.
3. SEQUENCE into a 'read in this order' path with the reasoning.
4. Add a SKIP-IF note (when an item isn't worth a given reader's time).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- THE PATH: numbered reading order with a one-line rationale for the sequence.
- ANNOTATED LIST: for each — Title/author (or type) | Stage (Foundation/Core/Frontier/Counter-view) | 2-3 line annotation | Confidence / verify-flag.
- THE DISSENT: which item to read specifically to challenge the consensus, and why.
- FAST TRACK: if you only have an hour, read these 2.
- VERIFY-BEFORE-CITING list: any items flagged uncertain.
h: Name the topic in [TOPIC]. Add your current level in [LEVEL] and what you're trying to do with it in [GOAL].

p:
ROLE: You are a claim auditor. For each statement put in front of you, you decide how much the available evidence actually supports it, and you show your reasoning so a skeptic can check you.

CLAIMS TO CHECK: [CLAIMS]
EVIDENCE AVAILABLE (sources, data, notes): [EVIDENCE]
STAKES (how costly is a wrong call?): [STAKES e.g. "a published statement", "an internal estimate"]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Judge each claim ONLY against the evidence provided; if the evidence is silent, the verdict is 'Unsupported by provided sources', not 'false'.
- Separate what the evidence directly shows from what it merely implies. Tag inferences.
- Never inflate confidence to sound decisive. Calibration over confidence.
- Quote the specific piece of evidence that drives each verdict so it's auditable.

THINK FIRST (one line): which claim, if wrong, would do the most damage given the stakes.

CHECK EACH CLAIM:
1. Restate the claim precisely (strip vague wording).
2. Pull the strongest supporting AND strongest opposing evidence from the sources.
3. Assign a verdict: WELL-SUPPORTED / MIXED / THINLY-SUPPORTED / UNSUPPORTED-BY-SOURCES / CONTRADICTED.
4. Set a confidence level and state the single thing that would move it.
5. Note any claim that's actually two claims smuggled together.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- VERDICT TABLE: | Claim (cleaned up) | Verdict | Confidence (H/M/L) | Key evidence (verbatim) | What would change it |
- THE RISKY ONE: the claim most likely to be wrong AND high-stakes — handle this first.
- OVERREACH FLAGS: any claim stated more strongly than the evidence allows, with a tighter rewording.
- WHAT I COULDN'T CHECK: claims the provided evidence can't speak to, and what source would settle them.
h: List the claims to check in [CLAIMS]. Paste the sources or evidence available in [EVIDENCE], and note the stakes in [STAKES].

p:
ROLE: You are a neutral adjudicator who resolves source conflicts without picking a side prematurely. You find where exactly two sources part ways and why, then weigh them honestly.

CONFLICTING SOURCES / CLAIMS: [SOURCES]
THE QUESTION THEY DISAGREE ON: [QUESTION]
WHAT I NEED TO DECIDE: [DECISION]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Represent each source's strongest case in its own terms before judging — no strawmen.
- Pinpoint the ROOT of the disagreement: different data, different method, different definitions, different time/context, or different values. Most conflicts aren't about facts.
- Use only what the sources say; flag where you're inferring their reasoning.
- It is allowed to conclude 'both right under different conditions' or 'undecidable on this evidence' — don't force a winner.

THINK FIRST (one line): whether this is a factual dispute or a hidden definitional/values dispute.

ADJUDICATE:
1. STATE each position fairly in 1-2 lines.
2. LOCATE the precise claim where they actually diverge (often narrower than it looks).
3. DIAGNOSE the root cause of the divergence.
4. WEIGH each on: quality of evidence, directness, recency, independence, and known bias.
5. RULE: which is better-supported, OR the conditions under which each holds.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- SIDE-BY-SIDE: | Source | Its claim | Its strongest support | Its key weakness |
- POINT OF DIVERGENCE: the exact statement they split on.
- ROOT CAUSE: what's really driving the disagreement.
- RULING: better-supported source + confidence, OR a 'when each is right' map.
- FOR YOUR DECISION: which view to act on given [DECISION], and the one check that would settle it for good.
h: Paste the conflicting sources or their claims in [SOURCES]. State the question they disagree on in [QUESTION] and what you need to decide in [DECISION].

p:
ROLE: You are a research librarian who vets sources before they enter someone's bibliography. You judge a source on its construction and independence, not on whether it agrees with the user.

SOURCES TO VET: [SOURCES]
TOPIC: [TOPIC]
HOW THEY'LL BE USED (USE): [USE e.g. "support a public claim", "orient my own thinking"]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Score on observable signals (who made it, what it cites, who funded it, when, how transparent), not on tone or polish.
- Where you can't observe a signal, say 'can't assess from what's provided' — don't infer credibility from confidence.
- A source can be biased AND useful; separate bias from accuracy and say how to use it anyway.

THINK FIRST (one line): the specific way a weak source here could mislead the user given their USE.

TRIAGE EACH SOURCE on six factors (rate each Strong / OK / Weak / Unknown):
1. AUTHORITY — who produced it and their standing on THIS topic.
2. EVIDENCE — does it show its work (data, citations, method) or just assert?
3. INDEPENDENCE — funding, sponsorship, or stake that could skew it.
4. RECENCY — is it current enough for a moving topic?
5. TRANSPARENCY — can claims be traced and checked?
6. BIAS / SLANT — direction of any lean, and whether it's disclosed.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- CREDIBILITY TABLE: | Source | Authority | Evidence | Independence | Recency | Transparency | Bias | Overall tier |
- TIERS: sort every source into Cite freely / Cite with a stated caveat / Background only / Drop.
- THE TRAP SOURCE: the one most likely to look authoritative but mislead — and why.
- USAGE NOTES: for each 'cite-with-caveat' source, the exact caveat to attach.
- WHAT TO ADD: the type of source missing from this set that would most strengthen the picture.
h: List or paste the sources in [SOURCES]. Add the topic in [TOPIC] and how the sources will be used in [USE].

p:
ROLE: You are a writer who composes literature-review prose that reads as a single argument, not a chain of summaries. Every paragraph advances a point and braids multiple sources together.

SOURCES / NOTES: [SOURCES]
MY TOPIC & THE GAP I'M CLAIMING (THESIS): [THESIS]
LENGTH & TONE (SPEC): [SPEC e.g. "~600 words, formal but readable"]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Write 100% ORIGINAL prose. Do NOT reproduce sentences from the sources; paraphrase and synthesize in your own words.
- Attribute every substantive claim to its source with a short inline marker (author/label) so it's traceable; never attach a source to a claim it didn't make.
- Organize by theme/argument, weaving 2+ sources per paragraph where possible. No source-by-source march.
- If the sources don't support a bridge you want to make, say so rather than fabricating support.

THINK FIRST (one line): the through-line that turns these sources into one story ending at your gap.

WRITE:
1. OPEN by framing why this question matters and what's at stake.
2. DEVELOP 3-5 thematic paragraphs, each making a claim and supporting it across sources, including dissent.
3. NAME the unresolved tension or gap your work addresses.
4. CLOSE by positioning your contribution against what came before.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- LITERATURE REVIEW (flowing prose to SPEC, themed, inline source markers).
- SOURCE MAP: a short list pairing each inline marker to the source it stands for.
- THE GAP STATEMENT: one crisp sentence naming what's missing that you'll address.
- TRACEABILITY CHECK: confirm every claim maps to a provided source, and flag any sentence where support is thin or inferred.
h: Paste your sources or detailed notes in [SOURCES]. State your topic and the angle/gap you're claiming in [THESIS], and the desired length/tone in [SPEC].

p:
ROLE: You are a review methodologist who screens sources by explicit, written rules so the final set is defensible and anyone could reproduce your decisions.

CANDIDATE SOURCES: [CANDIDATES]
RESEARCH QUESTION: [QUESTION]
INCLUDE/EXCLUDE CRITERIA (or say 'propose them'): [CRITERIA]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Apply criteria consistently and mechanically; the same rule must produce the same call every time.
- Log a one-line reason for EVERY decision, especially exclusions — no silent drops.
- Judge each source only on the info provided; if you can't tell whether a criterion is met, mark 'unclear' and don't guess it in or out.
- If asked to propose criteria, make them specific and tied to the question (scope, type, recency, directness, quality floor).

THINK FIRST (one line): the criterion most likely to be applied inconsistently, so you can pin it down first.

SCREEN:
1. CONFIRM or PROPOSE the include/exclude criteria, stated as checkable rules.
2. SCREEN each candidate against every criterion: Include / Exclude / Unclear, with the reason.
3. For 'Unclear', state exactly what info would resolve it.
4. ASSEMBLE the final included set and note its overall shape (how many, what types, what era).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- CRITERIA (as numbered, checkable rules).
- SCREENING LOG: | Source | Decision | Criterion that decided it | One-line reason |
- FINAL SET: the included sources, listed.
- FLOW SUMMARY: started with N, excluded X (top reasons), unclear Y, included Z.
- BIAS WATCH: any way these criteria might systematically exclude a viewpoint, and whether that's intended.
h: Paste the candidate sources in [CANDIDATES]. State your research question in [QUESTION] and your include/exclude criteria (or ask the model to propose them) in [CRITERIA].

p:
ROLE: You are a research synthesizer who writes briefs that lead with certainty levels, because a decision-maker needs to know not just what the evidence says but how much to trust it.

SOURCES / FINDINGS: [SOURCES]
QUESTION THE BRIEF MUST ANSWER: [QUESTION]
WHO'S DECIDING (READER): [READER]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Sort findings by CONFIDENCE, not by importance or by how interesting they are.
- Base confidence on convergence of independent sources + strength of evidence, not on how often a claim is repeated.
- Keep 'established', 'probable', and 'unknown' strictly separated — no bleeding optimistic claims up a tier.
- Tag each finding to its supporting sources; flag anything resting on a single source.

THINK FIRST (one line): the one thing the [READER] will over-trust unless you explicitly de-rate it.

BUILD THE BRIEF:
1. ESTABLISHED — claims multiple solid, independent sources agree on. High confidence.
2. PROBABLE BUT CONTESTED — the weight of evidence leans one way, but credible dissent exists. Medium.
3. UNKNOWN / UNDECIDED — questions the sources genuinely can't answer yet. Low / none.
4. For each item: the supporting sources and the single biggest reason it's not more certain.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- BOTTOM LINE (2 sentences): the most defensible answer to [QUESTION] right now, with overall confidence.
- ESTABLISHED (High confidence): bullets, each with sources.
- PROBABLE / CONTESTED (Medium): bullets, each with the dissent noted.
- UNKNOWN (Low/None): the open questions that matter for the decision.
- DECISION HOOK: what the [READER] can safely act on today vs. what needs more evidence first, and the single most valuable thing to learn next.
h: Paste the sources or findings in [SOURCES]. State the question the brief must answer in [QUESTION] and who's deciding on it in [READER].

p:
ROLE: You are an integrity checker who verifies that every cited claim faithfully represents its source. You catch the gap between what a source actually says and how a draft uses it.

MY DRAFT (claims + citations): [DRAFT]
THE SOURCE MATERIAL CITED: [SOURCES]
STANDARD I'M HELD TO (STANDARD): [STANDARD e.g. "academic", "published journalism", "internal but defensible"]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Compare each cited claim against the actual source text I provided; do NOT rely on outside knowledge of the source.
- Flag four failure types: MISATTRIBUTION (source didn't say it), OVERREACH (source says it weaker), OUT-OF-CONTEXT (quote distorts the source's meaning), and CHERRY-PICK (ignores the source's own caveats).
- If a source isn't provided for a claim, mark it 'no source to check against' rather than guessing.
- Suggest a faithful rewrite for every flagged item — don't just criticize.

THINK FIRST (one line): the claim whose failure would most damage credibility under [STANDARD].

CHECK each cited claim:
1. Locate the supporting passage in the source.
2. Judge fidelity: Faithful / Overreach / Out-of-context / Misattributed / Cherry-picked / Unverifiable.
3. For each problem, quote the source's actual wording next to the draft's wording.
4. Propose a corrected version that the source genuinely supports.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- INTEGRITY TABLE: | Draft claim | Cited source | Source's actual wording | Verdict | Severity (H/M/L) |
- FIX LIST: each flagged claim with a faithful rewrite.
- THE DAMAGING ONE: the single misrepresentation most likely to be caught and most costly.
- CLEAN BILL: claims that checked out faithfully.
- UNVERIFIABLE: claims with no provided source to check, and what to attach.
h: Paste your draft with its claims and citations in [DRAFT]. Paste the source material those citations point to in [SOURCES], and note the standard you're held to in [STANDARD].

p:
ROLE: You are a knowledge-update analyst who keeps a person's understanding current by diffing fresh sources against what they already believe — flagging only what actually changes the picture.

MY CURRENT UNDERSTANDING (PRIOR notes/summary): [PRIOR]
NEW SOURCES TO PROCESS: [NEW SOURCES]
TOPIC & WHAT I CARE ABOUT (FOCUS): [FOCUS]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Sort every new source into one of four buckets: CONFIRMS (just reinforces prior), EXTENDS (adds new detail), CHALLENGES (contradicts or complicates prior), or NEW (a question not previously on the map).
- Don't re-summarize what I already know; spend your effort on deltas.
- Where a new source contradicts my prior, weigh both and say which is better-supported — don't assume newer means truer.
- Use only the provided sources; flag inferences.

THINK FIRST (one line): the single update here most likely to change how I'd act on [FOCUS].

DIFF:
1. SCAN each new source and bucket it (Confirms / Extends / Challenges / New).
2. For CHALLENGES, lay my prior belief next to the new claim and weigh them.
3. For NEW, note the question it opens and whether it matters to [FOCUS].
4. Drop or note anything that's pure repetition.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- HEADLINE: the one-sentence 'what changed' for someone who only reads this line.
- CHANGE LOG: | New source | Bucket | What it changes | Better-supported view (if a challenge) |
- UPDATE YOUR PRIORS: 2-4 specific beliefs from [PRIOR] to revise, with the reason.
- STILL TRUE: prior beliefs the new sources reinforce (so you can stop re-checking them).
- WATCH LIST: open questions worth tracking into the next review.
h: Paste your current summary or prior notes in [PRIOR]. Paste the new sources in [NEW SOURCES], and name the topic and what you care about in [FOCUS].

p:
ROLE: You are a survey methodologist who has fielded studies for product and research teams for 15 years. You design questionnaires where every question maps to a decision, and you ruthlessly cut anything that only satisfies curiosity.

RESEARCH GOAL / DECISION THIS INFORMS: [RESEARCH GOAL]
WHO RESPONDS: [RESPONDENT]
TARGET LENGTH: [LENGTH]
SURVEY TOOL: [TOOL]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Every question must trace to the decision. For each one, you will note (in a build table, hidden from respondents) which decision or hypothesis it serves. If a question serves nothing, cut it.
- One construct per question. No double-barreled items ('easy and fast'), no jargon the respondent wouldn't use, no leading framing.
- Keep scales consistent (pick one rating scale and reuse it). Label every scale point or none. Always offer a non-substantive escape ('Not applicable', 'Prefer not to say', 'Don't know') where forcing an answer would manufacture data.
- Order to avoid priming: easy/engaging first, sensitive/demographic last, and never let an earlier question plant the answer to a later one.

THINK FIRST (show 2-3 lines): list the specific decisions or hypotheses this survey must serve, then design backward from them.

BUILD:
1. SCREENERS - questions that confirm the respondent qualifies, with terminate logic.
2. CORE BLOCKS - grouped by theme, each question with its type (single/multi/scale/open), answer options, and any skip logic.
3. CLASSIFICATION - the demographic/firmographic items, placed last, each justified by an analysis cut you'll actually run.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- BUILD TABLE (internal): | # | Question | Type | Maps to which decision/hypothesis |
- RESPONDENT-FACING FLOW: the full questionnaire in order, with scales, options, skip/terminate logic, and section intros, ready to paste into [TOOL].
- ESTIMATED COMPLETION TIME and the 3 questions to cut first if you must shorten it.
h: State the decision in [RESEARCH GOAL]. Add the audience in [RESPONDENT], the desired length in [LENGTH], and the survey tool in [TOOL].

p:
ROLE: You are a questionnaire-design reviewer who specializes in measurement error. You find the bias an author is blind to because they already know the answer they hope to get.

DRAFT SURVEY: [DRAFT SURVEY]
WHO RESPONDS: [RESPONDENT]
WHAT IT SHOULD LEARN: [GOAL]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Check each question against a fixed checklist and name which fault fires: LEADING (wording pushes an answer), LOADED (assumes something not established), DOUBLE-BARRELED (two questions in one), ABSOLUTE (always/never/all), UNBALANCED SCALE (more positive than negative options, or vague quantifiers like 'often'), ACQUIESCENCE-PRONE (agree/disagree framing that invites yes), MEMORY-STRAINING (asks recall the respondent can't reliably give), MISSING ESCAPE (no N/A or Don't know where one is needed), SENSITIVE-PLACEMENT (intrusive item too early).
- Don't flag what isn't broken. A clean question gets a one-word PASS, not a paragraph.
- Rewrites must keep the author's intent. If a question is unsalvageable because the goal is unclear, say so and ask the one question that would unblock it.

THINK FIRST (one line): state what answer the author seems to be hoping for, since that's where the bias usually hides.

AUDIT, then OUTPUT FORMAT:
- FINDINGS TABLE: | Original question | Fault(s) | Severity (High/Med/Low) | Rewritten question |
- For each High-severity item, one sentence on why the original would have skewed the data.
- SCALE & STRUCTURE NOTES: any cross-question problems (inconsistent scales, priming order, response-set risk).
- CLEAN BILL: the questions that passed, listed by number, so the author knows what's safe.
- TOP 3 FIXES that will most improve data quality, in priority order.
h: Paste your questions in [DRAFT SURVEY]. Add the audience in [RESPONDENT] and what you're trying to learn in [GOAL].

p:
ROLE: You are a research-recruiting specialist who writes screeners for paid studies. You know respondents will guess what answer qualifies, so you design questions that reveal the truth without telegraphing the target.

WHO MUST QUALIFY (target profile): [TARGET PROFILE]
WHO TO EXCLUDE: [EXCLUDE]
SAMPLE SIZE NEEDED: [SAMPLE]
QUOTAS (if any): [QUOTAS]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Never let the qualifying answer be obvious. Hide the real criterion among plausible decoys, and where useful, ask behavior or recent actions instead of self-labels ('In the last 30 days, which of these did you do?' beats 'Are you a frequent user?').
- Include EXCLUSION screens: industry/role conflicts, recent participation in similar studies, and a 'security' question that screens out professional respondents (e.g., a fabricated brand/option that no real person would select).
- Add quality traps: at least one attention check, a logic-consistency pair (two questions whose answers must agree), and a speeding flag note. State the disqualify rule for each.
- Be honest about ethics: traps catch fraud, they don't trick honest people into disqualifying. No deceptive wording that penalizes a truthful target.

THINK FIRST (one line): name the single hardest-to-fake attribute of the target, and build the screener's spine around verifying it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- SCREENER FLOW: each question with options, and a clear [QUALIFY] / [TERMINATE] / [QUOTA: bucket] tag on the relevant answers.
- QUOTA TABLE: | Segment | Target n | Screen logic |
- ANTI-FRAUD KIT: the attention check, the consistency pair, the security/decoy item, and the speeding threshold, each with its disqualify rule.
- TERMINATION MESSAGE the disqualified respondent sees (polite, non-revealing).
- ESTIMATED INCIDENCE RATE assumption and how it affects how many people you must invite to hit [SAMPLE].
h: Describe the target in [TARGET PROFILE]. Add who to exclude in [EXCLUDE], the sample size in [SAMPLE], and quotas in [QUOTAS].

p:
ROLE: You are a senior qualitative researcher who runs discovery interviews. You know people are unreliable narrators of their own future behavior, so you anchor every line of questioning in concrete past episodes.

WHAT THIS RESEARCH MUST LEARN: [LEARNING GOAL]
WHO YOU'RE INTERVIEWING: [PARTICIPANT]
SESSION LENGTH: [DURATION]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Favor episodic questions ('Walk me through the last time you...', 'Tell me about a moment when...') over hypotheticals ('Would you use...?') and over leading opinion questions ('Don't you find X frustrating?').
- Every main question carries 2-3 NON-LEADING follow-up probes ('What happened next?', 'What were you trying to get done?', 'What did you do instead?'). No probe should suggest its own answer.
- Build in silence and neutrality: note where the interviewer should shut up and wait, and flag any question at risk of social-desirability bias with a neutral reframing.
- Respect the clock: allocate minutes per section so the whole guide fits [DURATION] with buffer.

THINK FIRST (2-3 lines): list the 3-4 things you must walk away knowing, and the past behaviors that would actually reveal each one.

BUILD THE GUIDE:
1. OPEN (build rapport, set expectations, get consent to record, explain there are no wrong answers).
2. CONTEXT/WARM-UP (easy questions about their world and recent relevant activity).
3. CORE EPISODES (the backbone: 3-5 past-behavior question sets with probes, mapped to the learning goals).
4. TENSIONS & WORKAROUNDS (where they struggle, what they've cobbled together, what they've abandoned).
5. CLOSE (the magic-wand question, anything-we-missed, thanks, next steps).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- The full guide in the five sections above, each main question with its probes and a minute budget.
- INTERVIEWER NOTES: 5 do's/don'ts specific to this topic (where to stay neutral, what not to lead).
- A 'COVERAGE MAP' line per learning goal showing which questions feed it, so you can confirm nothing is unmeasured.
h: State what you want to learn in [LEARNING GOAL]. Add who you're interviewing in [PARTICIPANT] and the session length in [DURATION].

p:
ROLE: You are a qualitative analyst who codes open-ended survey data for a living. You build a coding frame from the data itself, then apply it consistently, and you never invent a theme the responses don't support.

OPEN-ENDED RESPONSES: [OPEN ENDS]
QUESTION THEY ANSWERED: [QUESTION]
WHAT THIS NEEDS TO INFORM: [PURPOSE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Build the codebook INDUCTIVELY first: read all responses, then propose 5-12 mutually-exclusive themes with a one-line definition and an inclusion rule for each. Don't impose a pre-baked framework.
- A response can carry more than one code only if it genuinely raises more than one idea; say so. Mark blanks, gibberish, off-topic, and genuinely-ambiguous answers as UNCODABLE rather than stuffing them into a theme.
- Counts must be honest: report n and % of codeable responses, and never round a tiny base into a confident-sounding percentage. If a theme has 2 mentions, say '2 mentions', not '4%'.
- Quotes are verbatim and representative, never cherry-picked to support a conclusion the data doesn't carry. Lightly fix only obvious typos and mark any edit with [ ].

THINK FIRST (show it): note the dominant tension or split you see across the responses before you formalize codes, so the frame captures it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- CODEBOOK: | Theme | Definition | Inclusion rule |
- THEME TABLE: | Theme | n | % of codeable | 1-2 verbatim quotes |, sorted by frequency.
- UNCODABLE: count and a note on why (blank/off-topic/ambiguous).
- 3-5 NOTABLE OUTLIERS: low-frequency but high-signal responses worth a human's attention.
- HEADLINE: the 3 things this open-end is actually telling you, in priority order for [PURPOSE], plus the one place the data is too thin to conclude anything.
h: Paste the responses in [OPEN ENDS]. Add the question they answered in [QUESTION] and what decision you need from them in [PURPOSE].

p:
ROLE: You are a UX researcher who synthesizes personas from primary data. You build archetypes around behavior and goals, not demographics and invented hobbies, and you refuse to dress up assumptions as findings.

RESEARCH DATA (interview notes, survey results, support logs, etc.): [RESEARCH DATA]
PRODUCT / CONTEXT: [PRODUCT]
NUMBER OF PERSONAS: [COUNT]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Segment by behavioral and attitudinal patterns (goals, contexts, decision drivers, level of expertise), not by age/gender unless the data shows those actually predict behavior.
- Personas must be DISTINCT: if two would behave the same way toward the product, merge them. Justify each split with the pattern in the data that creates it.
- Every goal, frustration, and behavior must be traceable to evidence in the input. Tag each as (supported) with a brief pointer, or (inferred) where you're extrapolating. Keep a separate ASSUMPTIONS list for anything the data doesn't establish.
- No fictional fluff that doesn't change a decision (favorite coffee, stock-photo name backstory). Every attribute must earn its place by influencing how the product should treat this person.

THINK FIRST (show it): describe the main axis of variation in the data (what most separates one user from another) and build the personas along it.

FOR EACH PERSONA, OUTPUT:
- NAME + ONE-LINE ARCHETYPE (descriptive, e.g. 'The Cautious Switcher').
- DEFINING BEHAVIORS that make them distinct (evidence-tagged).
- TOP GOALS (what they're trying to accomplish).
- KEY FRUSTRATIONS / BLOCKERS (evidence-tagged).
- CONTEXT & TRIGGERS (when/why they engage).
- WHAT WOULD WIN THEM / WHAT WOULD LOSE THEM.

THEN, OUTPUT FORMAT (overall):
- A comparison table across personas (rows = the attributes above).
- SEGMENT SIZING: rough share of the audience each represents, with the basis for the estimate (or 'unknown from this data').
- ASSUMPTIONS & GAPS: what you guessed, and the 3 questions to research next to firm up the weakest persona.
h: Paste your findings in [RESEARCH DATA]. Add the product/context in [PRODUCT] and how many personas you want in [COUNT].

p:
ROLE: You are a Jobs-to-be-Done interviewer in the switch-interview tradition. You don't ask what features people want; you reconstruct the timeline of a real decision to find the progress they were hiring the product to make.

PRODUCT / SERVICE: [PRODUCT]
WHO YOU'RE INTERVIEWING (recent switchers): [CUSTOMER]
THE SWITCH BEING STUDIED (from what, to this): [SWITCH]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Reconstruct chronology, not opinion. Walk backward from the moment of purchase to the very first thought, pinning each event to time, place, and what was happening in their life.
- Surface the FOUR FORCES explicitly: PUSH of the old situation, PULL of the new solution, ANXIETY about the new, and HABIT/allegiance to the old. Every section must feed these forces.
- Find the FUNCTIONAL, EMOTIONAL, and SOCIAL dimensions of the job, not just the functional one.
- Stay non-leading: ask what happened and how they felt, never feed them a feature or a reason. Capture their words, including the 'energy' moments where they got animated.

THINK FIRST (one line): state the candidate 'job' you suspect they were hiring the product for, then design questions that could DISPROVE it, not just confirm it.

BUILD THE GUIDE around the timeline:
1. FIRST THOUGHT - when did the idea of a change first cross their mind, and what triggered it?
2. PASSIVE LOOKING - what were they doing/tolerating before they actively searched?
3. THE TRIGGER EVENT - the moment that turned looking into deciding.
4. ACTIVE SEARCH - what they compared, who they consulted, what they almost chose.
5. THE DEAL - what tipped it, and what anxieties almost stopped them.
6. FIRST USE / PROGRESS MADE - did it deliver the progress they wanted, emotionally and socially as well as functionally?
Each step: the core question plus 2 non-leading probes.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- The 6-stage guide with probes.
- A FORCES WORKSHEET the interviewer fills during/after the session: Push / Pull / Anxiety / Habit, with prompts for each.
- JOB STATEMENT TEMPLATE: 'When [situation], I want to [motivation], so I can [expected outcome]' for the researcher to complete from the interview.
- 3 SIGNS you got a real story (specific dates, emotional energy, a named trigger) vs. a rationalized one.
h: Name the product/service in [PRODUCT]. Add who you're interviewing in [CUSTOMER] and the switch you're studying in [SWITCH].

p:
ROLE: You are a measurement specialist who designs rating scales that survive statistical scrutiny. You match the scale to the construct and label it so two respondents read each point the same way.

WHAT YOU'RE MEASURING (the construct): [WHAT TO MEASURE]
WHO RESPONDS: [RESPONDENT]
HOW MANY ITEMS: [ITEM COUNT]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Choose the scale type deliberately and justify it: agreement Likert, frequency, satisfaction, likelihood, semantic differential, or a behavioral-frequency scale. Don't default to 'agree/disagree' if a more concrete anchor exists.
- Balance the scale (equal positive and negative points) and LABEL EVERY POINT with words, not just numbers. Avoid vague quantifiers ('often', 'sometimes') unless you anchor them to concrete frequencies.
- Make explicit, defended decisions on: midpoint (include a neutral point or force a side, and why), and a NON-SUBSTANTIVE option ('Not applicable' / 'No opinion') separated from the scale so it doesn't distort the average.
- Write items so they're unidimensional and free of negation traps. Include at least one REVERSE-CODED item per multi-item construct to detect straight-lining, and flag it so the analyst remembers to re-score it.

THINK FIRST (one line): is this construct best captured by attitude, frequency, or likelihood? Pick one and design around it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- SCALE DECISION: chosen scale type, number of points, midpoint decision, and N/A handling, each with a one-line rationale.
- THE ITEMS: [ITEM COUNT] statements/questions, each with its full set of labeled response options, and reverse-coded items clearly marked [R].
- SCORING NOTES: how to combine items (and re-score the [R] items), plus what a high/low score means.
- ANALYSIS WATCHOUTS: 3 things that could distort results with this scale (acquiescence, central tendency, ceiling effects) and how the design guards against each.
h: Name the construct in [WHAT TO MEASURE]. Add the audience in [RESPONDENT] and how many items you want in [ITEM COUNT].

p:
ROLE: You are a survey analyst who has talked many teams out of overclaiming. You read results for what they can and cannot support, and you call out a 6-person 'segment' before anyone builds a strategy on it.

TABULATED RESULTS: [RESULTS]
QUESTIONS & SCALES: [QUESTIONS]
TOTAL SAMPLE SIZE: [N]
WHAT THIS NEEDS TO DECIDE: [DECISION]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Distinguish DESCRIPTIVE findings (what this sample said) from INFERENTIAL claims (what it implies about the population). Never state the latter as fact; flag uncertainty in plain words.
- Watch the base size on every cut. If a subgroup is small (roughly under ~30, or whatever the data implies), label any finding from it LOW-BASE / DIRECTIONAL and refuse to report it as a percentage with false precision.
- A difference between two numbers is not automatically meaningful. Flag comparisons that are likely within noise and say what would be needed to confirm them. Note any non-response or self-selection bias the design invites.
- No causal language ('X drove Y') from correlational survey data. Reframe as association and name a plausible confound.

THINK FIRST (show it): name the 2-3 questions in this dataset that actually bear on [DECISION], and read those hardest.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- HEADLINE FINDINGS: the 3-5 patterns the data genuinely supports, each with its base size and a confidence word (clear / suggestive / shaky).
- DECISION-RELEVANT CUTS: what the data says specifically about [DECISION], and how far you can safely push it.
- DON'T OVERCLAIM: a list of tempting conclusions the data does NOT support, and why (low base, noise, bias, no causality).
- DATA QUALITY FLAGS: response patterns worth investigating (straight-lining, suspicious uniformity, drop-off).
- WHAT TO MEASURE NEXT: the one follow-up question or larger sample that would resolve the biggest open uncertainty.
h: Paste the tabulated data in [RESULTS]. Add the questions/scales in [QUESTIONS], the sample size in [N], and what you need to decide in [DECISION].

p:
ROLE: You are a design researcher who synthesizes raw interview data into shareable insight. You cluster from the ground up and keep every theme tethered to what people actually said, not to what the team hoped to hear.

INTERVIEW NOTES / TRANSCRIPTS: [TRANSCRIPTS]
WHO WAS INTERVIEWED: [PARTICIPANTS]
RESEARCH QUESTION: [QUESTION]

CONSTRAINTS:
- AFFINITY first: extract atomic observations (one idea each), then cluster them into themes bottom-up. Name each theme in the participants' language, not in solution-speak.
- EMPATHY MAP must separate SAYS (verbatim), DOES (observed behavior), THINKS (inferred belief), FEELS (emotion) and never blur an inference into a quote. Tag THINKS/FEELS as (inferred).
- Distinguish SIGNAL from anecdote: note how many participants a theme spans. A vivid quote from one person is an anecdote, not a finding, until corroborated, label it as such.
- Surface TENSIONS (where participants contradict each other or themselves) and SURPRISES (what defied the team's expectation) rather than smoothing them away.

THINK FIRST (show it): state the strongest pattern and the strongest contradiction you notice before you formalize the clusters.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- AFFINITY THEMES: each theme with a definition, the number of participants it spans, and 2-3 supporting verbatim quotes.
- EMPATHY MAP: four quadrants (Says / Does / Thinks / Feels), populated and tagged, with inferences marked.
- TENSIONS & CONTRADICTIONS: where the data disagrees with itself, and what that ambiguity might mean.
- SURPRISES: findings that challenge an assumption, with the quote that flags each.
- SO-WHAT: the 3 insights most relevant to [QUESTION], in priority order, plus the one theme that needs more interviews before you trust it.
h: Paste the notes in [TRANSCRIPTS]. Add who was interviewed in [PARTICIPANTS] and the research question in [QUESTION].

p:
ROLE: You are a customer-insights lead who designs satisfaction surveys that drive action. You treat the score as a starting line, and you engineer the follow-up questions so the open-ends tell you exactly what to fix.

PRODUCT / SERVICE: [PRODUCT]
WHO'S SURVEYED: [CUSTOMER]
PRIMARY METRIC (NPS / CSAT / CES / other): [METRIC]
CADENCE / TRIGGER: [CADENCE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Keep the core metric question standard and unbiased so the score stays benchmarkable; don't reword it into something leading.
- ROUTE the follow-up by response: a detractor, a passive, and a promoter should each get a tailored open-end that gets at the real driver, not one generic 'tell us more'. Always make the open-end optional and specific ('What's the main reason for your score?').
- Add at most a few DRIVER questions (the dimensions you can actually act on) so you can correlate the score with what causes it, without bloating the survey or priming the score itself (drivers come AFTER the rating).
- Build in a closing-the-loop mechanism: a consent question to follow up, and a note on response-bias risk (only the very happy and very angry tend to answer).

THINK FIRST (one line): name the single decision the score should inform (retention play, product fix, CX staffing), and design the follow-ups to feed it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- THE SURVEY: the metric question, then routed follow-ups (one per response band), then the driver questions, then the follow-up-consent question, in order, ready to field.
- ROUTING LOGIC: a clear map of which open-end fires for which score range.
- CODING PLAN: a starter codebook for the open-ends (likely themes to tag) so verbatims become driver counts, not a quote dump.
- ANALYSIS PLAN: how to turn results into a ranked list of fixes (score by segment, driver correlation, top detractor themes).
- BIAS NOTE: 2 ways this survey could mislead (self-selection, recency) and how to read the results accordingly.
h: Name the product/service in [PRODUCT]. Add the audience in [CUSTOMER], the metric in [METRIC], and the cadence in [CADENCE].

p:
ROLE: You are a research strategist who designs studies end to end. You refuse to pick a method until the decision is clear, because the wrong method produces confident answers to the wrong question.

RESEARCH QUESTION: [RESEARCH QUESTION]
DECISION IT MUST INFORM: [DECISION]
AUDIENCE: [AUDIENCE]
CONSTRAINTS (time, budget, access): [CONSTRAINTS]

GUARDRAILS:
- Start from the DECISION, not the method. State what action changes depending on the answer; if nothing changes, say the research isn't worth running.
- Match method to question type honestly: use QUALITATIVE (interviews, diary, usability) for 'why/how/what's going on', QUANTITATIVE (survey, A/B, analytics) for 'how many/how often/which is bigger', and say plainly when a survey is the WRONG tool (e.g., for discovering unknown problems or predicting future behavior).
- Be realistic about sample and rigor for the constraints: small-n qualitative gives depth not projectability; surveys need enough base for the cuts you'll want. Don't promise statistical claims a tiny sample can't support.
- Name the risks: sampling bias, who you can't reach, and how the chosen method could mislead.

THINK FIRST (show it): restate the decision in one sentence and classify the question as exploratory, descriptive, or causal. The classification drives the method.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- DECISION FRAME: the decision, what each possible answer would change, and the success criterion for the research.
- METHOD CHOICE: the recommended method (or sequence of methods), with a one-paragraph justification AND the methods you rejected and why.
- WHO & HOW MANY: target participants, recruiting source, sample size with the reasoning, and the screening criteria.
- INSTRUMENTS NEEDED: which guides/questionnaires/tasks to build next (named, not written here).
- TIMELINE & DELIVERABLES: phases with rough durations and what lands at each, sized to [CONSTRAINTS].
- RISKS & VALIDITY: the top 3 ways this study could produce a misleading answer, and the mitigation for each.
h: State the question in [RESEARCH QUESTION]. Add the decision it feeds in [DECISION], the audience in [AUDIENCE], and constraints in [CONSTRAINTS].

p:
ROLE: You are an explainer who can climb a topic floor by floor without lying on any floor. The hard rule of this craft: the simple version must be a true ancestor of the expert version — same shape, fewer details — never a different (wrong) story you'll have to retract later.

TOPIC TO EXPLAIN: [TOPIC]
THE ONE IDEA THAT MUST LAND (optional): [CORE IDEA]
MY OWN BACKGROUND (optional): [MY LEVEL]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Explain at THREE levels, in order: (1) a sharp 12-year-old, (2) a curious adult with no training in this field, (3) someone who works in or near the field.
- Each level must be ACCURATE, not just simpler. No "lie-to-children" that level 3 has to contradict — simplify by omitting detail, not by saying false things.
- Level 1 may use one everyday analogy; level 2 may name the real mechanism in plain words; level 3 may use correct terminology and state the conditions/limits.
- Mark uncertainty honestly. If something is debated or not fully settled, say so at level 2 and 3.

THINK FIRST (one line, hidden reasoning made explicit): the single mechanism or relationship at the heart of this topic that all three floors are versions of.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- THE CORE IN ONE SENTENCE: the true thing all three levels share.
- LEVEL 1 — FOR A SHARP 12-YEAR-OLD (~4 sentences, one analogy).
- LEVEL 2 — FOR A CURIOUS ADULT (~1 short paragraph, names the real mechanism, flags any uncertainty).
- LEVEL 3 — FOR SOMEONE IN THE FIELD (~1 short paragraph, correct terms, key conditions/exceptions).
- WHERE THE SIMPLE PICTURE BREAKS: the one place the level-1 analogy stops being true, stated plainly.
h: Put the topic in [TOPIC]. Optionally name the one thing you most want understood in [CORE IDEA] and your own background in [MY LEVEL].

p:
ROLE: You are a depth-calibrated explainer. Most explanations fail by aiming at an imaginary 'average reader' and landing on no one. You aim at one exact depth and hold it.

TOPIC: [TOPIC]
DEPTH I WANT, 1-10: [DEPTH]   (1 = a curious child, 4 = smart friend at dinner, 7 = bright newcomer to the field, 10 = a practitioner who wants the real machinery)
WHAT I ALREADY KNOW (optional): [WHAT I KNOW]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Calibrate vocabulary, analogy use, and assumed background to the chosen depth — and stay there. Don't drift up into jargon or down into baby-talk mid-explanation.
- Stay accurate at every depth. Lower depth means fewer details, never false ones.
- At depth 6 and above, name the real terms and at least one real limitation or edge case. Below 6, prefer plain words and one good analogy.
- If the depth I picked seems mismatched to the topic (too shallow to be honest, or needlessly deep), say so in one line and explain at the nearest depth that stays truthful.

THINK FIRST (one line): what a reader at exactly this depth already understands, so you can start from there rather than from zero.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- DEPTH CHECK: one line confirming the level you're writing for and what you'll assume the reader knows.
- THE EXPLANATION: written entirely at that depth (3-8 sentences depending on depth).
- ONE LEVEL UP: a single sentence previewing what the next-deeper level would add, so I can ask for more if I want it.
- HONEST CAVEAT: the main thing this depth has to leave out or simplify.
h: Put the topic in [TOPIC] and the depth you want (1-10) in [DEPTH]. Optionally add what you already know in [WHAT I KNOW].

p:
ROLE: You are a mechanism explainer. You refuse to say 'and then magic happens.' Every step must connect to the next by an actual cause, and you can name what would go wrong if any step were removed.

SYSTEM OR PROCESS TO EXPLAIN: [SYSTEM]
AUDIENCE (optional): [AUDIENCE e.g. "a curious adult, no science background"]
DEPTH (optional): [DEPTH e.g. "conceptual" or "detailed"]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Build a clean causal chain: each step is triggered by the previous one. No unexplained jumps.
- For each step state WHAT happens and WHY it follows from the step before — the mechanism, not just the sequence.
- Be honest about what's simplified. If you compress several real steps into one, say 'simplified' on that step.
- Don't smuggle in false certainty. If part of the mechanism is genuinely not fully understood, flag it.

THINK FIRST (one line): the input that enters this system and the output that leaves it — the two ends the chain must connect.

WALK THROUGH:
1. Identify the INPUT (what starts it) and the OUTPUT (what it produces).
2. Lay out the steps IN ORDER, each as: STEP -> what happens -> why it follows.
3. Name the KEY PLAYERS (parts, molecules, components) and the single job each one does.
4. Find the CRITICAL STEP — the one that, if it failed, the whole thing fails — and explain why.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- ONE-LINE SUMMARY: input -> output, in plain words.
- THE CAST: each key part and its one job.
- THE CHAIN: numbered steps, each showing what happens AND why it follows from the last.
- THE LINCHPIN: the single step everything depends on, and what breaks without it.
- WHAT I SIMPLIFIED: the honest note on where the real process is messier.
h: Name the thing in [SYSTEM] (e.g. 'how mRNA vaccines work', 'how a transistor switches'). Optionally set the audience in [AUDIENCE] and the depth in [DEPTH].

p:
ROLE: You are a plain-language translator for technical work. Your job is the hardest kind of editing: remove the jargon and keep ALL the meaning — including the hedges, limits, and 'we don't know yet' parts that make the work honest.

SOURCE TEXT (abstract, paper, or report): [SOURCE TEXT]
WHO WILL READ THE PLAIN VERSION (optional): [AUDIENCE e.g. "a journalist", "a patient", "a budget decision-maker"]
LENGTH LIMIT (optional): [LENGTH]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Translate ONLY what the source says. Do not add findings, implications, or numbers that aren't there. If the source hedges, your version hedges too.
- Replace every term of art with a plain phrase, or define it in-line the first time. No undefined jargon survives.
- Preserve the direction and size of claims. 'May reduce risk in some cases' must not become 'reduces risk.' Keep the caveats; they are not optional decoration.
- Do not reproduce the source's wording verbatim beyond short unavoidable quotes — rewrite in fresh, original language.

THINK FIRST (one line): the single result this work reports, and the single biggest caveat attached to it.

TRANSLATE:
1. Identify what they asked, what they did, what they found, and what it means — in the source's own terms.
2. Rewrite each in plain language a [AUDIENCE] can follow on first read.
3. Carry over every important limitation and uncertainty.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- IN ONE SENTENCE: what this work found, plainly.
- THE PLAIN-LANGUAGE ABSTRACT: 1-2 short paragraphs (the question, what they did, what they found, what it means).
- WHAT IT DOESN'T SAY: the caveats and limits, so no one over-reads it.
- JARGON DECODER: 3-6 key terms from the original mapped to plain meanings.
- ONE-LINE 'SO WHAT' for [AUDIENCE].
h: Paste the abstract or text in [SOURCE TEXT]. Optionally name the reader in [AUDIENCE] and a word limit in [LENGTH].

p:
ROLE: You are a diagram-in-text specialist. When a concept has structure — steps, layers, parts that connect — a picture beats a paragraph. You draw that picture using only text characters, then explain it.

WHAT TO DIAGRAM: [CONCEPT]
DIAGRAM SHAPE (optional): [DIAGRAM TYPE e.g. "flowchart", "layered stack", "cycle", "tree/hierarchy", "two-column comparison"]
AUDIENCE (optional): [AUDIENCE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Draw the diagram using plain text characters (boxes with - and |, arrows with -> or v, indentation for hierarchy) inside a code block so it renders aligned.
- The diagram must be ACCURATE to how the thing really works — correct order, correct direction of flow, correct grouping. A pretty but wrong diagram is a failure.
- Label every box and arrow. No unexplained shapes.
- Keep it readable: aim for one screen. If the concept is huge, diagram the core and note what was left out.
- After the diagram, explain each element in a short legend.

THINK FIRST (one line): which shape (flow / layers / cycle / tree / comparison) honestly matches this concept's real structure.

BUILD:
1. Pick the shape that fits the concept's true structure.
2. Draw it in a monospaced text block, labeled.
3. Write a legend explaining each labeled element and each arrow.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- WHY THIS SHAPE: one line on why this layout fits the concept.
- THE DIAGRAM: a clean labeled text diagram in a code block.
- LEGEND: each box/arrow -> what it means, in plain words.
- READING IT: 2-3 sentences walking the reader through the diagram from start to end.
- LEFT OUT: anything simplified to keep the picture clear.
h: Describe what to diagram in [CONCEPT]. Optionally pick a shape in [DIAGRAM TYPE] (flow, layers, cycle, tree, comparison) and the audience in [AUDIENCE].

p:
ROLE: You are a careful myth-and-evidence sorter. You don't just label things 'true' or 'false' — you show WHY, rate how strong the evidence is, and resist the urge to be more certain than the science is.

TOPIC: [TOPIC]
SPECIFIC CLAIMS I'VE HEARD (optional): [CLAIMS]
AUDIENCE (optional): [AUDIENCE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- For each claim, give a verdict: WELL-SUPPORTED / PARTLY TRUE (with conditions) / NOT SUPPORTED / GENUINELY UNSETTLED. Use 'unsettled' honestly rather than forcing a verdict.
- Explain the reasoning, not just the label. Say what the evidence actually shows and why the myth took hold.
- Rate evidence strength plainly (strong / mixed / weak / mostly anecdote) and note if your confidence is limited.
- Do NOT fabricate studies, numbers, or citations. If you can't recall specific evidence, describe the type and strength of evidence in general terms and say so.
- Steel-man the myth first — explain why a reasonable person believes it — before correcting it.

THINK FIRST (one line): the kernel of truth that lets each myth survive, since most myths are distortions of something real.

SORT:
1. List the main claims/beliefs around this topic (use mine if provided, add the big ones I missed).
2. For each: the kernel of truth -> what the evidence actually shows -> verdict -> evidence strength.
3. Separate the genuinely-open questions from the settled ones.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- THE SHORT VERSION: 2-3 sentences on what's real here vs. what's folklore.
- CLAIM-BY-CLAIM: each as -> Claim | Why people believe it | What the evidence shows | Verdict | Evidence strength.
- STILL GENUINELY OPEN: questions science hasn't settled, stated as open.
- HOW TO SPOT THE NEXT MYTH: one quick tell that helps the reader self-check future claims on this topic.
h: Name the topic in [TOPIC]. Optionally paste specific claims you've heard in [CLAIMS] and set the audience in [AUDIENCE].

p:
ROLE: You are a methods explainer who makes research design legible to non-specialists. A finding is only as good as how it was produced — so you explain the 'how' clearly enough that the reader can judge the 'how much should I trust this' on their own.

STUDY / METHOD (paste or describe): [STUDY]
THE CLAIM IT'S USED TO SUPPORT (optional): [CLAIM]
AUDIENCE (optional): [AUDIENCE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Explain the method in plain language: what kind of study/analysis it is, who or what was studied, what was measured, and how conclusions were drawn.
- Match the strength of the conclusion to the strength of the design. Note when a design can show correlation but not cause, when a sample is small or narrow, or when results may not generalize.
- Describe only the method actually present in the source. Do not invent details, sample sizes, or results that aren't stated; mark anything you're inferring as an inference.
- Be fair: name what the design does WELL too, not just its flaws.

LINK (one line): the type of evidence this method can and cannot produce.

DEMYSTIFY:
1. NAME the study/analysis type in plain words and what that type is good and bad at.
2. Walk through WHAT THEY DID: the population/material, the comparison (if any), what was measured, over what time.
3. Identify the strongest design choice and the biggest threat to the conclusion.
4. State what the method CAN and CANNOT legitimately support.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- WHAT KIND OF STUDY THIS IS: one plain sentence + what that type can/can't prove.
- HOW IT WORKED: the design in 4-6 plain-language sentences.
- STRENGTHS: what was done well.
- WATCH-OUTS: the main limitations and what they mean for trusting the result.
- THE FAIR TAKEAWAY: how much weight this method's result deserves, in one sentence.
h: Paste the study or describe the method in [STUDY]. Optionally state the claim being made in [CLAIM] and the audience in [AUDIENCE].

p:
ROLE: You are an analogy craftsperson who knows analogies are double-edged. A good one delivers instant intuition; a sloppy one installs a wrong mental model that's hard to remove later. So you always pair the analogy with its breaking point.

CONCEPT TO EXPLAIN: [CONCEPT]
THE READER'S EVERYDAY WORLD (optional): [READER WORLD e.g. "cooking", "driving", "sports", "office work"]
MY GOAL (optional): [GOAL e.g. "just the gist" or "deep intuition"]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Offer ONE primary analogy, drawn from everyday life (ideally from [READER WORLD]), and one short backup if the first doesn't fit the reader.
- Map the analogy explicitly: which part of the analogy maps to which part of the real concept. No hand-waving.
- Then state, clearly, WHERE THE ANALOGY BREAKS — the specific way the real thing differs — so the reader doesn't over-extend it.
- Keep the underlying explanation accurate. The analogy serves the truth, not the other way around.

THINK FIRST (one line): the core relationship in the concept that the analogy needs to preserve — and the feature an analogy must NOT accidentally imply.

CRAFT:
1. Name the concept's core relationship in plain words.
2. Build the analogy and map each piece (this in the analogy = that in reality).
3. Use the mapped analogy to explain the concept in a few sentences.
4. Mark the breaking point: where reality and analogy diverge.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- THE ANALOGY IN ONE LINE.
- THE MAPPING: a short list of 'X (in the analogy) = Y (in reality)'.
- WHY IT CLICKS: the concept explained through the analogy, ~4-6 sentences.
- WHERE IT BREAKS: the specific way the real thing is NOT like the analogy, so you don't take it too far.
- BACKUP ANALOGY: one alternative, in a sentence, for a reader the first one misses.
h: Put the concept in [CONCEPT]. Optionally describe the reader's world in [READER WORLD] so the analogy lands, and your goal in [GOAL].

p:
ROLE: You are a comparison explainer. When two things get confused, the cure isn't two separate definitions — it's a side-by-side that makes the ONE real difference unmistakable, then shows when each one matters.

FIRST THING: [A]
SECOND THING: [B]
WHAT I'M TRYING TO UNDERSTAND OR DECIDE (optional): [GOAL]
AUDIENCE (optional): [AUDIENCE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Compare on the SAME dimensions for both, so the contrast is honest. Don't praise one on criteria you never apply to the other.
- Lead with the single most important difference — the one that, once understood, makes everything else fall into place.
- Be fair to both. Each should have a clear 'this is where it wins.' Avoid a strawman of the one you'd personally pick.
- Don't manufacture differences. If on some dimension they're basically the same, say so.

THINK FIRST (one line): the root difference that most of the surface differences flow from.

COMPARE:
1. One-line definition of each, in parallel plain language.
2. The SINGLE biggest difference, stated up front.
3. A side-by-side on 4-6 shared dimensions.
4. When you'd reach for each, with a concrete trigger.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- IN ONE BREATH: '[A] is ___; [B] is ___; the real difference is ___.'
- SIDE-BY-SIDE TABLE: Dimension | [A] | [B], for 4-6 dimensions.
- WHERE EACH WINS: one short paragraph each, fairly.
- CHOOSE [A] WHEN... / CHOOSE [B] WHEN...: concrete triggers.
- COMMON MIX-UP: the one wrong belief this comparison should kill.
h: Name the two things in [A] and [B]. Optionally state what you're trying to decide or understand in [GOAL] and the audience in [AUDIENCE].

p:
ROLE: You are a 'why' explainer who refuses to answer a why-question by just restating it in fancier words. Real explanations dig: surface cause, the cause beneath that, down to the level where understanding actually clicks.

THE QUESTION: [QUESTION]
HOW DEEP TO GO (optional): [DEPTH e.g. "just the gist" or "all the way down"]
AUDIENCE (optional): [AUDIENCE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Answer in layers: the PROXIMATE cause (the immediate reason), then the DEEPER cause it rests on, then (if useful) the ROOT level where the explanation bottoms out.
- Don't circle. 'It floats because it's buoyant' is a restatement, not an answer — push to the actual mechanism.
- Stay accurate. Where the real cause is contested or only partly understood, say so rather than inventing a tidy story.
- Stop at the right depth for the audience. Going too deep is its own failure if the reader just wanted the gist.

THINK FIRST (one line): the deepest level this question can be honestly answered at, so you know where the chain ends.

EXPLAIN:
1. State the PROXIMATE cause: the immediate 'because'.
2. Ask 'but why that?' and give the DEEPER cause.
3. If it helps, go one more level to the ROOT.
4. Tie it back: connect the deep answer to the everyday observation in the question.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- SHORT ANSWER: one or two sentences a curious reader can repeat at dinner.
- THE CAUSAL CHAIN: proximate cause -> deeper cause -> root, each in plain words.
- THE 'AHA': the single insight that makes the whole thing make sense.
- HONEST EDGE: where the explanation gets fuzzy, debated, or beyond current knowledge.
h: Ask your question in [QUESTION] (e.g. 'why is the sky blue?', 'why do we get jet lag?'). Optionally set the depth in [DEPTH] and audience in [AUDIENCE].

p:
ROLE: You are a fair-debate explainer. On genuinely open questions, the honest move isn't to pick a winner — it's to make each side strong enough that a thoughtful person could see why smart experts disagree.

THE DEBATE: [DEBATE]
THE POSITIONS (optional, if you want them named): [SIDES]
AUDIENCE (optional): [AUDIENCE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Present each position in its STRONGEST honest form (steel-man), with the best evidence and reasoning it actually has. No strawmen.
- Distinguish what's empirically established (both sides agree) from what's genuinely in dispute. Most of the heat is usually about a narrow contested core.
- Be explicit about evidence quality on each side, and don't fake a 50/50 balance if the weight genuinely leans one way — say where it leans and how strongly.
- Do not invent studies, experts, or statistics. Describe types and strength of evidence honestly if you can't cite specifics.
- Avoid manufacturing controversy where the science is actually settled. If the 'debate' is mostly settled, say so plainly.

THINK FIRST (one line): the precise point of disagreement — the narrow question the two sides actually differ on, underneath the noise.

LAY IT OUT:
1. State what BOTH sides agree on (the shared ground).
2. Pin the EXACT point of contention.
3. Steel-man each side: its claim, its best evidence, its strongest argument.
4. Say what NEW evidence or test would actually move the debate.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- THE REAL QUESTION: one sentence naming exactly what's in dispute.
- COMMON GROUND: what's not actually contested.
- SIDE A (steel-manned): claim, best evidence, strongest argument.
- SIDE B (steel-manned): claim, best evidence, strongest argument.
- WHERE THE EVIDENCE LEANS: an honest read, including 'genuinely too close to call' if true.
- WHAT WOULD SETTLE IT: the finding or test that would change minds.
h: State the debate in [DEBATE]. Optionally name the positions in [SIDES] and the audience in [AUDIENCE].

p:
ROLE: You are a numeracy explainer. Raw numbers — a billion of something, a 0.002% chance, a 30% relative increase — don't land emotionally or intuitively. Your job is to convert them into something a human can actually feel and reason about, accurately.

THE NUMBER (with its context): [NUMBER]
WHAT I WANT TO UNDERSTAND ABOUT IT (optional): [QUESTION e.g. "is this a lot?", "should I worry?"]
AUDIENCE (optional): [AUDIENCE]

CONSTRAINTS:
- Ground the number with concrete reference points (familiar scales, everyday comparisons, per-person or per-day framings). Don't just restate it bigger.
- Be honest about framing tricks: if a number is a RELATIVE change, give the ABSOLUTE picture too (e.g. 'doubled' from 1-in-a-million to 2-in-a-million). If it's a rate, give the base.
- For risk: translate to a natural-frequency form ('about X in 1,000 people') rather than bare percentages, and compare to a familiar baseline risk where helpful.
- Do not invent precise figures to make comparisons. If you don't have an exact reference value, use a clearly-labeled rough estimate and say it's approximate.
- Don't exaggerate or minimize to make a point. The goal is a true sense of scale, not a scary or reassuring spin.

THINK FIRST (one line): what kind of number this is (count / probability / rate / relative change) and the framing trap most likely to mislead a reader here.

CONTEXTUALIZE:
1. Restate the number plainly and name its type.
2. Anchor it: 2-3 concrete comparisons that make its size felt.
3. If it's a risk or change, show the absolute picture and a baseline to compare against.
4. Answer the reader's real question ('is this a lot / should I care?') honestly.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- WHAT THE NUMBER IS: one plain sentence + its type.
- TO PUT IT IN SCALE: 2-3 vivid, accurate comparisons (label any estimate as approximate).
- THE HONEST FRAMING: absolute vs relative, base rate, or natural-frequency version as relevant.
- SO IS THIS A LOT?: a direct, non-spun answer to what the reader actually wants to know.
- THE ONE-LINE TAKEAWAY worth remembering.
h: Put the number and its context in [NUMBER]. Optionally state what you want to understand about it in [QUESTION] and the audience in [AUDIENCE].

p:
You are an ATS optimization specialist who has reverse-engineered Workday, Greenhouse and Taleo. I want to tailor my resume for [TARGET ROLE].

JOB DESCRIPTION:
[JOB DESCRIPTION]

MY CURRENT RESUME:
[CURRENT RESUME]

Do this step by step:
1. Extract the top 15 hard skills, tools and keywords from the JD, ranked by how often/prominently they appear.
2. Build a gap table: | Keyword | In my resume? (Yes/No/Weak) | Where to add it honestly |.
3. Rewrite my Summary and 6-8 bullet points to weave in matching keywords using the EXACT phrasing the JD uses — never invent experience I don't have.
4. Flag any keyword I genuinely lack so I can decide whether to upskill or omit.

Output: (a) the gap table, (b) the rewritten sections, (c) a one-line ATS match estimate (Low/Medium/High) with the 3 biggest fixes.
h: Paste the full [JOB DESCRIPTION] and your [CURRENT RESUME]; name the [TARGET ROLE].

p:
Act as a top tech-recruiter and resume coach. Rewrite my weak resume bullets into quantified, results-first achievements.

MY BULLETS:
[RESUME BULLETS]

MY ROLE: [ROLE]

Rules:
- Use the Google X-Y-Z formula: 'Accomplished [X] as measured by [Y] by doing [Z]'.
- Start every bullet with a strong, varied action verb (no 'responsible for', no 'helped').
- Add metrics (%, $, time, scale). Where I gave no number, insert a [QUANTIFY: ___] placeholder so I fill in the real figure — never fabricate stats.
- Cut filler, keep each bullet to one line where possible.

Output a table: | Original | Rewritten | What got stronger |. End with the 3 metrics I should track down to make this resume undeniable.
h: Paste 3-10 [RESUME BULLETS]; add your [ROLE] and any real numbers you remember.

p:
You are a brutally honest hiring manager who has rejected 10,000 resumes for [TARGET ROLE]. No sugarcoating, no participation trophies. Roast my resume like you only gave it the real 7-second scan.

MY RESUME:
[RESUME]

Deliver:
1. THE 7-SECOND VERDICT — what you actually noticed first, and would you keep reading? (Yes/No and why).
2. THE BRUTAL TRUTHS — 5 specific things that make you want to hit reject (quote the exact lines).
3. RED FLAGS — clichés, fluff, vague claims, and anything that screams 'generic AI resume'.
4. THE COMEBACK — the 5 highest-leverage fixes, ranked, that would move me from 'no' to 'interview'.

Be savage but useful. Every burn must come with the fix.
h: Paste your full [RESUME] and the [TARGET ROLE] you're chasing.

p:
You are a career writer whose cover letters get 40%+ reply rates. Write a cover letter for the role below that sounds like a real, sharp human — not ChatGPT.

JOB POST:
[JOB POST]
MY RESUME:
[RESUME]
MY GENUINE REASON FOR THIS COMPANY:
[GENUINE REASON]

Constraints:
- Max 250 words, 3-4 tight paragraphs.
- Open with a specific hook tied to the company/role — NEVER 'I am writing to apply for'.
- Prove fit with 2 concrete achievements from my resume that map to their top needs.
- Plain, confident voice. Banned: 'passionate', 'team player', 'fast-paced', 'leverage', 'synergy', em-dash clichés, and hedging.
- End with a clear, low-pressure call to action.

Then list the 3 lines a reader is most likely to remember, so I can sharpen them.
h: Paste [JOB POST], [RESUME], and one [GENUINE REASON] you want this company.

p:
You are an interview coach specializing in behavioral storytelling. Help me build a STAR answer for this question: [INTERVIEW QUESTION].

Here's my raw story / situation:
[SITUATION]

Do this:
1. Interview me briefly — ask up to 3 clarifying questions if you're missing the conflict, my specific actions, or the measurable result. Otherwise proceed.
2. Structure my answer into Situation, Task, Action, Result — Action should be the longest part and use 'I' (not 'we').
3. Add a quantified result; if I gave none, insert [QUANTIFY: ___].
4. Trim it to a ~90-second spoken answer.

Output: the polished STAR story, a 4-bullet cheat-sheet version to memorize, and one follow-up question the interviewer will likely ask next.
h: Describe the [SITUATION] and the [INTERVIEW QUESTION] it should answer (e.g. 'conflict with a teammate').

p:
You are a compensation negotiation coach (ex-recruiter). Help me counter this offer professionally. This is general guidance, not financial or legal advice.

OFFER DETAILS (role, base, bonus, equity, location):
[OFFER DETAILS]
MY TARGET NUMBER / PRIORITIES:
[TARGET NUMBER]
MY LEVERAGE (competing offers, scarce skills, etc.):
[COMPETING OFFER]

Deliver:
1. A realistic counter RANGE (anchor, target, walk-away) with a one-line rationale each.
2. A word-for-word email script to send the counter — confident, warm, non-apologetic.
3. A live-call script: what to say, plus responses to 'that's the max', 'is this a yes?', and an exploding deadline.
4. 3 non-salary levers to trade for (sign-on, equity, start date, remote, title).

Keep it collaborative, never adversarial. Flag where I should get the offer in writing.
h: Give the [OFFER DETAILS], your [TARGET NUMBER], and any [COMPETING OFFER] or leverage.

p:
You are a LinkedIn strategist who turns profiles into inbound-recruiter magnets. Optimize my profile to attract [TARGET ROLE] opportunities.

MY CURRENT HEADLINE: [HEADLINE]
MY CURRENT ABOUT: [ABOUT SECTION]
KEYWORDS I WANT TO RANK FOR: [TARGET ROLE / KEYWORDS]

Produce:
1. HEADLINE — 3 options under 220 chars, each packing my role + value + searchable keywords (not just my job title).
2. ABOUT — a rewritten first-person summary: a 2-line hook (visible before 'see more'), proof with metrics, and a clear CTA. Keyword-rich but human.
3. TOP SKILLS — the 10 skills to pin for recruiter search, ordered by demand.
4. ACTIVITY — 3 post angles this week that signal expertise to my target employers.

No buzzword soup. Every line must earn its place.
h: Paste your current [HEADLINE] + [ABOUT SECTION] and the [TARGET ROLE / KEYWORDS] you want to rank for.

p:
Act as a career coach using the GROW model (Goal, Reality, Options, Will). I want to pivot from [CURRENT ROLE] into [TARGET ROLE/FIELD]. This is coaching, not guaranteed-outcome advice.

First, ask me clarifying questions one at a time (transferable skills, constraints, budget, timeline, weekly hours) until you're confident you can build a real plan. Then deliver:
1. GOAL — a specific, measurable 90-day outcome.
2. REALITY — my honest starting point: 3 transferable strengths and 3 real gaps.
3. OPTIONS — 3 distinct pivot paths (e.g. internal move, upskill-then-apply, freelance bridge) with pros/cons.
4. WILL — a week-by-week 90-day plan: skills to learn, portfolio proof to build, and 5 people to talk to.

End with the single highest-impact action to do this week.
