- My voice in 3 words: [VOICE]

THINK FIRST (silently): the click was for the freebie, so deliver it instantly and visibly — friction here kills trust. Then give one reason to expect (and want) the next email, without pitching anything yet.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- The download link is impossible to miss — top of the email, as a clear standalone line, not buried in a paragraph.
- Add one quick-start tip so the magnet actually gets used (an unused freebie builds no trust).
- Set the relationship: what they'll hear from me, how often, why it's worth keeping me out of spam.
- One light next-step nudge (reply, read this, whitelist) — no sales pitch in this email.
- Keep it under 130 words, warm, in [VOICE].

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 (the most deliverability-safe options that clearly say 'your thing is here').
2) PREVIEW TEXT — one line.
3) DELIVERY EMAIL — body with the prominent access link and the quick-start tip.
4) WHITELIST LINE — one optional sentence asking them to move me to the primary inbox, phrased as benefit to them.
h: Describe the freebie and how they get it first; then who downloaded it and what it leads toward.

p:
ROLE: You are a story-led newsletter writer. You know the most-forwarded issues are the ones that feel like a letter from a smart friend, not a broadcast — and that the lesson is what makes a story worth the read.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The raw moment / story (messy is fine): <moment>[MOMENT]</moment>
- Who reads my newsletter: [AUDIENCE]
- The one lesson they should take away: [LESSON]
- The soft CTA this naturally leads to (reply, read, try, check out): [SOFT CTA]

THINK FIRST (silently): find the exact second the story turns — the surprise, the mistake, the decision. Build the issue around that turn, and let [LESSON] emerge from it rather than being bolted on at the end.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Open mid-scene or on a line of tension — never 'I want to tell you a story about the time...'.
- Stay true to my account: no invented dialogue, numbers, or outcomes.
- The lesson must feel earned by the story and useful to the reader, not a generic moral.
- The [SOFT CTA] connects organically to the lesson; if it can't, tell me and suggest a better-fit CTA. No hard pitch.
- Mobile-formatted, generous white space, under 320 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 (open-loop / emotional / plain-curious).
2) PREVIEW TEXT — one line that deepens the loop.
3) NEWSLETTER — the full story issue, line-broken for reading on a phone.
4) CTA TIE-IN — the exact sentence that bridges from the lesson to the soft CTA, written two ways so I can pick the lighter touch.
h: Paste the raw moment first; then your audience, the lesson, and the soft action it leads to.

p:
ROLE: You are a curator-editor. In a roundup, the links are commodities — your selection and your one-line framing are the actual value. You make people trust your taste.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The links + my rough notes on each: <links>[LINKS + NOTES]</links>
- Who reads this: [AUDIENCE]
- The lens / taste I curate through (what makes something 'worth it' to me): [CURATION LENS]
- The format I want (5-7 picks, a top pick, themed sections): [FORMAT]

THINK FIRST (silently): a roundup is not a bookmark dump. For each pick, answer 'why should THIS reader spend time on this?' in one sharp line. Find a loose through-line that gives the issue a spine.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Every pick gets: a clear title/source, ONE-line reason it earns the click (framed for [AUDIENCE]), and the link as a clean standalone element.
- Lead with a short editorial intro (2-4 lines) that names the week's loose theme or your mood — your voice is the differentiator.
- Rank or flag a single 'if you only click one' top pick.
- Use only the links and facts I gave you; do not invent sources, summaries, or claims about what an article says.
- Keep each blurb tight — no copy-pasted meta descriptions, no 'this is a great read about'.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINES — 3 (top-pick-tease / theme / number-of-picks).
2) PREVIEW TEXT — one line.
3) ROUNDUP — editorial intro, the picks (with the top pick flagged), optional themed sections.
4) SIGN-OFF — one warm closing line plus a single reply-prompt to spark responses.
h: Paste your links with rough notes first; then your audience and the lens you curate through.

p:
ROLE: You are a no-flattery email editor. You diagnose the specific reasons an email underperforms, then rewrite it — and you can defend every change.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The email / issue I already wrote: <email>[EMAIL TO IMPROVE]</email>
- Who it's going to: [AUDIENCE]
- The result I wanted from it (opens / clicks / replies / one action): [GOAL]
- Any context on how it performed, if known: [PERFORMANCE — optional]

THINK FIRST (silently): audit against the chain that has to hold for the email to work — subject earns the open, preview supports it, first line earns the read, body has one clear point, CTA is singular and obvious, and nothing trips spam. Find the weakest link first.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Score the email on 6 axes (subject, opener/hook, structure & scannability, single-CTA clarity, voice/specificity, deliverability/spam risk), each 1-5, with a one-line reason — be honest, not kind.
- For each weak axis, name the exact problem, then fix it in the rewrite.
- Do not change my meaning, my facts, or my voice's intent; sharpen, don't replace with generic 'marketing' tone.
- Preserve any real numbers or claims I made; never add invented ones.
- The rewrite must aim squarely at [GOAL] with one primary CTA.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SCORECARD — the 6 axes, each scored 1-5 with a one-line reason.
2) TOP 3 FIXES — the highest-leverage changes, in priority order.
3) REWRITE — new subject line options (3), preview text, and the rewritten email body.
4) CHANGE LOG — bullet list of what I changed and why, so I can accept or reject each.
h: Paste the full email you want improved first; then your audience and the result you wanted from it.

p:
ROLE: You are a wire-service-trained PR writer. You know editors skim the headline and first two lines, then bin anything that reads like an ad. Your release must survive that skim by leading with actual news, not adjectives.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What we're announcing and why it's newsworthy now: <news>[ANNOUNCEMENT + WHY NOW]</news>
- Organization, people, and any verifiable facts (numbers, dates, partners): [WHO + FACTS]
- Target outlet type and audience: [OUTLET / AUDIENCE]
- Spokesperson name + title for the quote: [SPOKESPERSON]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): find the single most surprising or consequential fact and make THAT the headline. Strip every claim you can't back up. Decide what a reporter would actually ask after reading the first paragraph, and answer it in the second.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Inverted pyramid: most newsworthy fact first, supporting detail after, background last.
- No hype words (revolutionary, game-changing, world-class, leading) unless tied to a specific, checkable fact.
- Exactly two quotes max, and they must say something a press release normally can't — opinion, stakes, or a human angle — never restate the facts.
- Use only the facts I provide; mark anything you'd need me to confirm as [VERIFY].
- Write original copy; never paste or paraphrase another company's release.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) HEADLINE + SUBHEAD (headline under 12 words, subhead adds the second-most-important fact).
2) DATELINE + LEAD (CITY, Date — one sentence covering who/what/why-now).
3) BODY (3-4 short paragraphs, inverted pyramid, with the two quotes placed for impact).
4) BOILERPLATE ('About [Org]' — 3-4 sentences).
5) MEDIA CONTACT block + ### end mark.
6) EDITOR'S NOTE — a 2-line summary of the angle, and any [VERIFY] items I must confirm before sending.
h: Describe what you're announcing and why it matters now first; then who's involved and the verifiable facts.

p:
ROLE: You are a media-relations strategist who has watched hundreds of pitches die in inboxes. You know the only pitches that work prove you read the reporter's work and hand them a story they can already picture writing.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The journalist, their outlet/beat, and a recent piece of theirs: <journalist>[JOURNALIST + RECENT PIECE]</journalist>
- My story angle and why it fits THEIR beat: [ANGLE]
- What I can offer (data, exclusive, interview, demo): [ASSETS]
- Any timing or embargo: [TIMING]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): connect my angle to the journalist's recent work in one honest, specific link — not flattery. Decide the single most intriguing hook and lead with it. Ask: would a reporter forward this to an editor, or delete it?

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Subject line under 9 words, intriguing, no clickbait, no ALL CAPS.
- First sentence is about THEM and their work, not me. One genuine, specific reference — never generic 'love your work'.
- Body under 150 words. One clear angle, one clear ask, one obvious next step.
- No attachments-dump; offer assets as links/on-request. State the embargo plainly if there is one.
- Original writing only; do not quote their article verbatim beyond a short fair reference.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) SUBJECT LINE (plus 2 alternates).
2) PITCH EMAIL (greeting, the THEM-first opener, the angle, the offer, the ask, sign-off).
3) ONE-LINE FOLLOW-UP (to send 3-4 days later if no reply).
4) WHY THIS WORKS — 2 bullets on the specific psychological levers used, so I can adapt the template myself.
h: Name the journalist and a recent piece of theirs first; then your story angle and what you can offer.

p:
ROLE: You are a brand-messaging editor who writes the boilerplate that appears at the bottom of every press release and partner page. You know it must be accurate, unbloated, and identical everywhere — the one paragraph the whole company copies and pastes for years.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What the organization does, for whom, and the facts worth standing behind: <facts>[WHAT WE DO + FACTS]</facts>
- The audience who reads boilerplate (press, partners, investors): [AUDIENCE]
- Desired tone in 2-3 words: [TONE]
- Any one differentiator we own: [DIFFERENTIATOR]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): separate what's true and provable from what's aspirational fluff. Find the one sentence that would still be true and meaningful in two years. Cut every word that any competitor could also claim.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Third person, present tense, factual. No superlatives without a fact behind them.
- Every claim must be something I can defend if a journalist checks it; flag unverifiable ones as [VERIFY].
- The one-liner must work as a standalone descriptor in a tweet or an email signature.
- Avoid clichés (mission-driven, cutting-edge, passionate) unless made concrete.
- Original copy only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) LONG BOILERPLATE (3-4 sentences, ~70 words) — the standard release closer.
2) MEDIUM (2 sentences, ~35 words) — for bios and partner pages.
3) ONE-LINER (under 20 words) — for signatures and intros.
4) BANNED-WORD NOTE — any hype words I should keep out, plus [VERIFY] flags on anything I must confirm.
h: List what your organization actually does and the few facts worth standing behind first; then audience and tone.

p:
ROLE: You are a speechwriter-turned-PR-lead who writes quotes leaders are proud to say out loud. You know a quote that merely restates the press release is dead weight — the quotable ones carry opinion, stakes, or a vivid line a reporter can't resist.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The executive (name, title) and the topic/announcement: <exec>[EXECUTIVE + TOPIC]</exec>
- The one idea I want this leader known for here: [KEY MESSAGE]
- Their natural speaking style (plain, bold, warm, technical): [VOICE]
- The audience reading the quote: [AUDIENCE]

QUIET PLAN (do not print): for each quote pick a different job — one stakes-setting, one human/why-it-matters, one forward-looking, one that lands a memorable line. Make sure each sounds like a person talking, not a brochure.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Each quote 1-2 sentences, sayable aloud in one breath, no jargon a normal reader would stumble on.
- Must add something the facts can't: a viewpoint, a stake, a human note. Never restate the announcement.
- Stay within what this leader can truthfully claim; mark any aggressive claim as [CHECK WITH LEADER].
- No empty phrases ('we're excited to', 'thrilled to announce') unless immediately earned by a real reason.
- Original lines only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) FOUR QUOTES — each labeled with its job (stakes / human / forward-looking / memorable line), attributed to [Name, Title].
2) BEST PICK — which one to lead with and why, in one sentence.
3) DELIVERY NOTE — for each, a one-line tip on when to use it (release vs. interview vs. social).
4) FLAGS — any [CHECK WITH LEADER] items.
h: Name the executive and the announcement or topic first; then the one idea you want associated with them.

p:
ROLE: You are a crisis-communications advisor. In the first hour, your job is not to explain everything — it's to show the organization is aware, taking it seriously, and acting, without admitting things you can't yet verify or speculating into deeper trouble.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The situation, factually, and who is affected: <situation>[WHAT HAPPENED + WHO'S AFFECTED]</situation>
- What we currently KNOW for certain: [CONFIRMED FACTS]
- What we do NOT yet know: [UNKNOWNS]
- The action we're genuinely taking right now: [ACTION UNDERWAY]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): separate confirmed fact from speculation ruthlessly. Lead with people, not the brand. Decide what we can honestly commit to without admitting liability or guessing at cause. Identify what an angry stakeholder most needs to hear first.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Acknowledge, show empathy where people are affected, state what's being done, commit to an update — in that order.
- Never speculate on cause, blame, or numbers that aren't confirmed. Use 'we are working to understand' rather than guesses.
- No defensiveness, no minimizing, no legal admissions; flag anything legally sensitive as [RUN PAST LEGAL].
- Plain, human language — no corporate fog. Short sentences.
- Original wording only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) HOLDING STATEMENT — SHORT (social/first response, under 60 words).
2) HOLDING STATEMENT — STANDARD (press/website, 3-4 sentences).
3) HOLDING STATEMENT — EXTENDED (adds the commitment to update and where to find it).
4) DO-NOT-SAY LIST — phrases to avoid that could imply blame or liability.
5) NEXT-UPDATE PLAN — what to confirm before the follow-up, and the [RUN PAST LEGAL] flags.
h: Describe the situation factually and who's affected first; then what you do and don't yet know.

p:
ROLE: You are a media-training coach who has prepped executives for friendly profiles and hostile investigations alike. You know the questions that sink people aren't the obvious ones — they're the follow-ups and the sideways traps. You prepare for those.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The interview topic and the outlet/journalist if known: <topic>[INTERVIEW TOPIC + OUTLET]</topic>
- The 1-2 messages we MUST land: [KEY MESSAGES]
- Sensitive areas we'd rather not go deep on (and why): [SENSITIVE AREAS]
- The spokesperson and their comfort level: [SPOKESPERSON]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): map the questions on a spectrum from softball to ambush. For each sensitive area, predict the precise follow-up that exposes it. Design honest bridges — never evasions — back to the key message. Spot where 'no comment' would look worse than a straight answer.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Group questions: expected, skeptical/challenging, and curveball/off-topic.
- For each, give a concise model answer that is honest, on-message, and quote-safe (assume every word is on the record).
- Provide real bridge phrases that pivot without dodging; flag where dodging would backfire.
- Never script a lie or a misleading non-answer; where we genuinely can't comment, give an honest reason.
- Original content only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) THE ONE MESSAGE — the single line to return to repeatedly.
2) EXPECTED QUESTIONS — Q + model answer (5-7).
3) HARD / SKEPTICAL QUESTIONS — Q + model answer + the trap to avoid (5-7).
4) CURVEBALLS — 3 off-script questions + how to stay composed.
5) BRIDGE TOOLKIT — 5 reusable pivot phrases.
6) RED LINES — topics to decline, with the honest one-liner for declining each.
h: Describe the topic and what the interview is about first; then any sensitive areas you'd rather not discuss.

p:
ROLE: You are a PR operations lead who builds press pages reporters actually thank you for. You know a good press kit answers every question a journalist would otherwise email you at 5pm on deadline — accurately, in one scannable place.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The organization and what we want press to understand about us: <org>[ORG + WHAT WE WANT UNDERSTOOD]</org>
- Key people for bios (names, titles, one fact each): [KEY PEOPLE]
- Assets we have or can produce (logos, photos, data, footage): [ASSETS]
- Press contact details: [CONTACT]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): imagine a reporter writing about us in 30 minutes — what do they need to not get wrong? Sequence the kit so the most-needed items (correct name, fast facts, contact) are first. Flag every claim that needs verification.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Provide an outline AND the ready-to-paste copy for each text section.
- Fast facts must be checkable; mark anything I must confirm as [VERIFY].
- Bios in third person, factual, 2-3 sentences each, no fluff.
- Spell out exact name spelling, pronunciation if tricky, and approved logo/usage notes.
- Original copy only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) PRESS KIT OUTLINE — ordered list of sections.
2) BOILERPLATE ('About') copy.
3) FAST FACTS — bulleted, checkable, with [VERIFY] flags.
4) LEADERSHIP BIOS — one block per named person.
5) ASSET INVENTORY — what's available + usage/permission note for each.
6) MEDIA CONTACT block.
7) MISSING PIECES — what I still need to supply to make this complete.
h: Describe the organization and what you want press to understand first; then your key people and available assets.

p:
ROLE: You are a communications director who has announced hard things publicly. You know audiences forgive bad news but never forgive spin — so you write with candor, ownership, and respect for the people affected, while still standing on the organization's side of the story honestly.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The difficult news and exactly who it affects: <news>[BAD NEWS + WHO'S AFFECTED]</news>
- The honest reason behind the decision: [REASON]
- What we're doing to support those affected or fix it: [REMEDY / SUPPORT]
- The audience whose trust matters most here: [AUDIENCE]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): name the news plainly in the first line — burying it reads as cowardice. Decide what we genuinely own versus what we can fairly contextualize. Center the people affected before the business rationale. Strip every euphemism.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Lead with the actual news, stated clearly. No euphemisms ('rightsizing', 'strategic realignment') in place of plain words.
- Take appropriate ownership without over-admitting liability; flag legally sensitive lines as [RUN PAST LEGAL].
- Show concrete support/action, not vague 'we care' language.
- One honest, human executive quote — no defensiveness.
- Original copy; do not lift another company's statement.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) HEADLINE + SUBHEAD (clear, not soft-pedaled).
2) DATELINE + LEAD (states the news directly).
3) BODY (the reason, the people-first support, the path forward — 3-4 short paragraphs with one human quote).
4) BOILERPLATE.
5) MEDIA CONTACT + ### end mark.
6) TONE CHECK — 2 bullets confirming it reads accountable, not spun, plus [RUN PAST LEGAL] flags.
h: State the bad news plainly and who it affects first; then the reason and what you're doing about it.

p:
ROLE: You are a launch-PR specialist. You know 'we built a thing' is not news — 'this changes something for these people, and here's proof' is. Your release gives a reporter a story their editor will approve, with a hook that survives the pitch meeting.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What we're launching and the single strongest reason it's newsworthy: <launch>[LAUNCH + STRONGEST HOOK]</launch>
- Key facts (numbers, dates, customers, investors): [FACTS]
- Who it's for and the problem it solves: [AUDIENCE + PROBLEM]
- Spokesperson for the quote: [SPOKESPERSON]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): pressure-test the hook — would a reporter pitch this, or yawn? Find the angle that connects the launch to a trend, a problem, or a number bigger than the product itself. Cut feature-listing; lead with consequence.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Headline names the news AND the hook, not the product alone.
- Lead with why-it-matters; features come second, as evidence.
- Every number must be real and checkable; mark unconfirmed ones [VERIFY].
- No hype adjectives without a fact behind them; one quote that adds stakes or vision, not a feature recap.
- Original copy only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) THREE HEADLINE OPTIONS — ranked, each leading with a different hook.
2) DATELINE + LEAD.
3) BODY (3-4 paragraphs: hook → evidence → quote → availability/next step).
4) BOILERPLATE + MEDIA CONTACT + ### end mark.
5) PITCH ANGLE — the one-line story I'd pitch a reporter, plus [VERIFY] flags.
h: Describe what you're launching and the single best reason it's news first; then key facts and the spokesperson.

p:
ROLE: You are a bylined-content editor who places executive op-eds in real publications. You know editors reject thinly veiled ads instantly — they accept timely, opinionated arguments backed by genuine, lived expertise. You find that argument and pitch it.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The executive and the expertise they've genuinely earned: <exec>[EXECUTIVE + REAL EXPERTISE]</exec>
- The timely issue or debate they have a real opinion on: [TIMELY ISSUE]
- Their actual stance (even if mildly contrarian): [STANCE]
- Target publication / audience: [PUBLICATION]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): find the argument only this person can credibly make from experience — the one a generalist couldn't. Make sure the stance takes an actual position, not a both-sides shrug. Tie it to why-now so an editor sees the timeliness.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- The angle must be an argument with a clear position, grounded in the executive's real expertise — not a product pitch.
- The pitch to the editor is under 150 words and leads with the argument and its timeliness.
- The outline must promise a piece that gives value to readers even if they never become customers.
- No fabricated credentials or stats; flag claims needing support as [VERIFY/SOURCE].
- Original writing only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) THE ARGUMENT — one sentence: the position only this person can credibly hold.
2) EDITOR PITCH EMAIL — subject + under-150-word body.
3) OP-ED OUTLINE — hook, the stance, 3 supporting points (each tied to real experience), the 'so what' for readers, the close.
4) HEADLINE OPTIONS — 3.
5) CREDIBILITY CHECK — what makes the executive the right author, plus [VERIFY/SOURCE] flags.
h: Name the executive and their genuine expertise first; then the timely issue they have a real opinion on.

p:
ROLE: You are a press-office lead who fields 'do you have a comment?' calls. You know each reply is a fork: engage and you may fuel the story, decline badly and you look guilty, correct clumsily and you amplify the false claim. You pick the right move and land it in two sentences.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- What we're being asked to react to (the news/rumor/claim, verbatim if possible): <ask>[WHAT WE'RE REACTING TO]</ask>
- Our actual position on it: [OUR POSITION]
- The outcome we want (correct it / decline / acknowledge / redirect): [GOAL]
- The outlet and deadline pressure: [OUTLET + DEADLINE]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): decide whether engaging helps or feeds the story. If correcting a falsehood, state the truth without repeating the false framing. If declining, give a reason that doesn't read as a hide. Anticipate the follow-up your statement invites.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Provide 3 calibrated versions matched to different strategies (firm engage / brief acknowledge / dignified decline).
- Keep each to 1-2 sentences, quote-safe, with no phrase that could be clipped out of context to mean the opposite.
- When correcting, never restate the false claim as if it were ours; flag legally sensitive lines as [RUN PAST LEGAL].
- No defensiveness, no sarcasm, no off-the-cuff jab at a competitor.
- Original wording only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) RECOMMENDED MOVE — engage / acknowledge / decline, in one line with why.
2) THREE STATEMENT VERSIONS — labeled by strategy, each attributed to [Spokesperson, Title].
3) THE FOLLOW-UP IT INVITES — the likely next question and a one-line ready answer.
4) RISK NOTE — how each version could be misread, plus [RUN PAST LEGAL] flags.
h: Paste or describe what you're being asked to react to first; then your actual position and what you want to achieve.

p:
ROLE: You are a PR campaign strategist. You know a single mass email to 200 reporters gets ignored, while the same news, sliced into different angles for different beats and released in the right order, can build momentum. You design that rollout.

CONTEXT INPUTS
- The announcement and the goal of the push (awareness, credibility, leads): <push>[ANNOUNCEMENT + GOAL]</push>
- The audiences/markets I most want to reach: [TARGET AUDIENCES]
- What makes the news relevant to different beats: [ANGLES AVAILABLE]
- Timing, exclusivity options, embargo: [TIMING]

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): decide whether to offer an exclusive to one tier-1 outlet first, then broaden — or go wide. For each outlet TYPE, find the angle that fits that beat specifically. Sequence so early coverage feeds later coverage.

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- Describe outlet TYPES and beats to target (trade, regional, national, vertical, podcasts/newsletters) — do not invent specific publication names or reporter contacts.
- Give a distinct, beat-appropriate angle per tier — never the same pitch reused.
- Specify the rollout ORDER and the logic (exclusive-first vs. simultaneous), with embargo handling.
- Keep every angle truthful to the actual news; no inflated claims.
- Original strategy and copy only.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) ROLLOUT STRATEGY — exclusive-first or wide, and why, in 2-3 lines.
2) TIERED PLAN — a table-style list: outlet type/beat → tailored angle → pitch order → timing.
3) THREE SAMPLE SUBJECT LINES — one per tier.
4) SEQUENCING NOTE — how early coverage is used to win later coverage.
5) PREP CHECKLIST — what I must have ready (assets, spokesperson, embargo terms) before pitching begins.
h: Describe the announcement and your goal first; then the audiences you most want to reach.

p:
You are a YouTube scriptwriter who has studied audience-retention graphs for years and writes for the channel niche [NICHE]. Write a full script for a video about [TOPIC], targeting roughly [LENGTH] minutes for [AUDIENCE].

THINK FIRST (show this as a short plan, then write the script):
- The single promise this video makes in the first 15 seconds.
- The open loop you plant in the hook and pay off at the end.
- 2-3 're-hook' moments where you'll re-state stakes to fight the mid-video drop-off.

CONSTRAINTS: the hook is 30 seconds max and contains zero throat-clearing ('hey guys, welcome back'); never tell the viewer what's coming unless it raises a question they now need answered; every section ends with a reason to keep watching; write spoken-word, contraction-friendly language, not essay prose; mark [B-ROLL] and [ON-SCREEN TEXT] cues inline; no fabricated statistics - if a stat would strengthen a point, write [INSERT VERIFIED STAT].

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
**Working title (3 options, under 60 chars):** ...
**Thumbnail text idea:** 3-5 punchy words.
**Hook plan & open loop:** 2 lines.
## SCRIPT
**[0:00 - HOOK]** spoken lines + [B-ROLL]/[ON-SCREEN TEXT] cues
**[INTRO / promise]** ...
**[BODY - Section 1..N]** each with a re-hook note where relevant
**[CLIMAX / payoff of the open loop]** ...
**[OUTRO + CTA]** one clear next action
**Pinned-comment idea:** one line that sparks replies.
h: Paste your video topic, then your channel niche and target length. The AI maps the hook and retention beats before writing.

p:
You are a story structure expert fluent in Campbell's monomyth and Vogler's writer's adaptation. Take this premise and architect it as a Hero's Journey: [PREMISE]. The protagonist is [PROTAGONIST] and the tone should be [TONE].

WORK IN TWO PHASES.

PHASE 1 - ARC MAP (output first): for each of the 12 stages (Ordinary World, Call to Adventure, Refusal, Meeting the Mentor, Crossing the Threshold, Tests/Allies/Enemies, Approach, Ordeal, Reward, The Road Back, Resurrection, Return with the Elixir) give one sentence stating what concretely happens in THIS story. State the protagonist's external goal and the internal lie they believe at the start.

PHASE 2 - BEAT SHEET: expand the map into a scene-by-scene outline. Each beat = a scene with a goal, a conflict, and a change in the character's state (what they want vs what they get).

CONSTRAINTS: every stage must earn its place - if a stage is weak for this premise, say so and suggest a fix rather than padding; the internal lie must be challenged at the Ordeal and resolved at the Resurrection; no generic fantasy filler - tie every beat to the specific premise; show cause-and-effect ('therefore/but', never 'and then').

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
**Logline:** one sentence.
**External goal / Internal lie:** two lines.
**Arc map table:** | Stage | What happens here |
**Beat sheet:** numbered scenes - each: *Scene goal* / *Conflict* / *Turn (state change)*.
**Theme statement:** the one true thing this story argues.
h: Describe your story premise or main character. The AI plots the arc first, then beats.

p:
You are a short-form video strategist who lives in the first three seconds. For a video about [VIDEO_IDEA] on [PLATFORM], aimed at [AUDIENCE], generate 10 hooks that stop the scroll.

THINK FIRST (one line): what does this audience secretly fear, want, or doubt that the video can speak to instantly?

CONSTRAINTS: use 10 DIFFERENT angles - e.g. contrarian claim, specific number, callout ('if you do X, stop'), curiosity gap, before/after, mistake exposed, mini-story cold-open, question, pattern interrupt, stakes/warning; each hook is one or two spoken lines (under ~12 words for the opening line); no clickbait the video can't deliver; written to be SAID out loud, not read; include the literal on-screen-text version where it differs from the spoken line.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown table):
| # | Angle | Spoken hook | On-screen text | Why it stops the scroll |
|---|---|---|---|---|
(10 rows)

Then:
**Top 3 picks** with a one-line reason each.
**First 3 seconds of script** for the #1 hook (spoken lines + visual cue).
h: Give your video idea and platform (Reels/Shorts/TikTok). Get 10 hooks ranked by stopping power.

p:
You are a podcast producer who scripts episodes that keep listeners through the whole runtime. Build the episode flow for a [FORMAT] show (solo / co-host / interview) about [TOPIC], roughly [LENGTH] minutes, for [AUDIENCE].

THINK FIRST (short plan): the cold-open hook (the single most provocative line or question to open on), the through-line that connects all segments, and the one takeaway a listener should remember tomorrow.

CONSTRAINTS: write a conversational run-of-show, not a word-for-word monologue - give talking points and bridge lines hosts can riff on; mark natural [AD BREAK] slots after a curiosity peak, never mid-thought; write real spoken transitions between segments ('which brings me to...'); for interview format, supply escalating questions that build, not a flat list; no filler segments to pad time.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
**Episode title (3 options) + one-line description (for podcast apps).**
**Cold open (write the exact opening lines).**
## RUN OF SHOW
| Segment | Time | Talking points / questions | Transition out |
|---|---|---|---|
(include intro, 3-5 segments, [AD BREAK] markers, and outro)
**Sign-off script:** exact closing lines + CTA (subscribe / review / next-episode tease).
**Show notes:** 5 bullets + 3 timestamps to fill in.
h: Provide the episode topic and your show format (solo / co-host / interview). Get a full run-of-show.

p:
You are a direct-response ad scriptwriter who has sold products on camera for years. Write a [LENGTH]-second video ad for [PRODUCT], targeting [AUDIENCE] whose core pain is [PAIN].

THINK FIRST (one line each): which framework fits best (PAS / AIDA / Before-After-Bridge) and why; the single emotion this ad should trigger; the one action the viewer must take at the end.

CONSTRAINTS: the first line is a pattern-interrupt hook tied directly to the pain - no logo intros; agitate the real cost of the problem (time, money, embarrassment) before mentioning the product; one offer, one CTA - never two; spoken language with short punchy lines; mark [VISUAL] cues and rough timestamps; make zero claims that sound like fabricated proof - use [INSERT REAL PROOF: testimonial/demo/result]; keep it within the spoken word-count budget for the chosen length (~2.5 words/second).

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
**Framework chosen:** + one-line reason.
**Hook line (3 variations).**
## SCRIPT (timestamped)
| Time | Spoken line | [VISUAL] |
|---|---|---|
**CTA line (exact words).**
**Two alt hooks to A/B test** + which metric tells you the winner.
h: Name the product and the audience's main pain. Pick a framework or let the AI choose.

p:
You are a screenwriter fluent in Blake Snyder's Save the Cat beat structure. Build a 15-beat sheet for [PREMISE], in the [GENRE] genre, formatted as a [FORMAT] (feature film ~110 pages, or TV pilot ~55 pages).

THINK FIRST: state the logline, the hero's flaw/want/need, and the theme the story will test.

Then write all 15 beats: Opening Image, Theme Stated, Set-Up, Catalyst, Debate, Break into Two, B Story, Fun and Games, Midpoint, Bad Guys Close In, All Is Lost, Dark Night of the Soul, Break into Three, Finale, Final Image.

CONSTRAINTS: give each beat its approximate page range scaled to the chosen format; describe what specifically happens in THIS story, not the textbook definition; the Midpoint must be a true false-victory or false-defeat that raises stakes; 'Theme Stated' and 'Final Image' must mirror each other to show change; flag any beat that the premise makes hard to fill, with a suggested fix; keep it grounded in the genre's conventions.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
**Logline / Flaw-Want-Need / Theme.**
| # | Beat | Page range | What happens in this story | Emotional turn |
|---|---|---|---|---|
(15 rows)
**Opening vs Final image:** two lines showing the transformation.
**One-line pitch** you could say in an elevator.
h: Give your premise, genre, and format (feature or TV pilot). Get all 15 beats placed by page.

p:
You are a character writer obsessed with people who feel real on the page. Build a character bible for [CHARACTER] in the world of [STORY_WORLD], then prove it with dialogue.

PHASE 1 - CHARACTER BIBLE: define the core wound (the past event that shaped them), the conscious want vs the unconscious need, the lie they believe, their contradiction (a trait that fights another trait), speech signature (vocabulary, rhythm, tics, what they never say), and how they behave under pressure vs at ease.

PHASE 2 - PROOF SCENE: write a short scene (one page) where this character faces [SITUATION]. The dialogue alone should reveal the wound and the contradiction WITHOUT narration explaining it.

CONSTRAINTS: no walking-cliche traits (the 'tough but caring' default) unless complicated by a contradiction; the speech signature must be specific enough that you could identify the character from one unattributed line; subtext over on-the-nose statements - characters rarely say exactly what they mean; the scene must have a goal, an obstacle, and a shift.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
**Character Bible:** labeled sections (Wound, Want vs Need, The Lie, Contradiction, Speech Signature, Under Pressure).
**Voice test:** 3 unattributed lines that could only be this character.
## PROOF SCENE
proper screenplay-style scene (slugline, action, dialogue) for the situation.
h: Describe your character and the story world. The AI builds the bible, then a test scene.

p:
You are a brand storyteller trained in Donald Miller's StoryBrand SB7 framework. Build the story for [BUSINESS], whose ideal customer is [CUSTOMER] wanting [DESIRED_OUTCOME].

KEY RULE: the CUSTOMER is the hero, the brand is the GUIDE - never the other way around.

Work through all 7 elements: (1) A Character (what the customer wants), (2) Has a Problem (external problem, internal frustration, and the philosophical 'this is just wrong' angle), (3) Meets a Guide (your brand's empathy + authority), (4) Who Gives a Plan (3-4 simple steps), (5) Calls them to Action (direct + transitional CTA), (6) That helps avoid Failure (the stakes), and (7) Ends in Success (the transformed life).

CONSTRAINTS: name the INTERNAL problem, not just the external one - that's where the emotion lives; the plan must be simple enough to feel doable; don't make the brand the hero or list features - sell the transformation; stakes must be real but not fear-mongering.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
**SB7 breakdown:** the 7 elements, each in 1-2 lines.
**Brand one-liner:** 'We help [customer] who [problem] by [solution] so they can [success].'
**Homepage wireframe copy:** Header / Stakes / Value stack / Guide / Plan / CTA - one block each.
**Elevator pitch:** 20-second spoken version.
h: Describe your business and your ideal customer. The AI casts the customer as hero, you as guide.

p:
You are a story editor who has saved hundreds of broken drafts. Diagnose this story and prescribe fixes. Story: [STORY_SUMMARY_OR_DRAFT]. What feels off to me: [PROBLEM_DESCRIPTION].

DIAGNOSE BEFORE PRESCRIBING - check the usual failure points: unclear protagonist goal, low or unclear stakes, a passive hero (things happen TO them vs them driving), a sagging/episodic middle, weak cause-and-effect ('and then' instead of 'therefore/but'), missing internal arc, an unearned or flat ending, and theme that doesn't connect to plot.

CONSTRAINTS: quote or reference the specific moment that's broken before suggesting a fix; prioritize by impact (a fix to the spine beats a line edit); do NOT rewrite the whole story - give targeted surgical changes the author can make themselves; if the premise is fundamentally strong, say so and protect it; offer at least one option per major problem, not a single dictate.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
**Diagnosis:** the 3 most damaging problems, ranked, each with the symptom you noticed.
**Prescription table:** | Problem | Where it shows | Specific fix (with an option B) |
**The spine, restated:** the story in one cause-and-effect sentence after fixes.
**Ending check:** is the current ending earned? If not, the one change that earns it.
**Keep these:** 2-3 things working that the author should NOT touch.
h: Paste your story summary or draft and say what feels off. Get a diagnosis, not a rewrite.

p:
You are a documentary and explainer-video scriptwriter who turns dry subjects into watchable stories. Write a voiceover-driven script about [SUBJECT], whose goal is to [GOAL] (educate / persuade / sell), for [AUDIENCE], around [LENGTH] minutes.

THINK FIRST: find the narrative spine - even an explainer needs a question, tension, and a resolution. State the opening question/tension, the 'aha' turn, and the closing message in one line each.

CONSTRAINTS: open on intrigue or a vivid concrete image, never a dictionary definition; write VO to be SPOKEN - read it aloud in your head and keep sentences breathable; pair every VO block with a [VISUAL] cue (footage, motion graphic, on-screen text); control pacing - mark [PAUSE] and [MUSIC SWELL] at emotional beats; build to ONE message, don't list ten facts; no invented statistics - use [INSERT VERIFIED FIGURE] where data belongs.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
**Narrative spine:** question / turn / message (3 lines).
**Title + one-line synopsis.**
## SCRIPT
| Time | Voiceover (spoken) | [VISUAL] | Pacing note |
|---|---|---|---|
(cover open, build, turn, payoff, close)
**Estimated runtime** based on word count (~2.5 words/sec).
**Closing line (exact words)** + CTA if the goal needs one.
h: Give your subject and goal (educate / persuade / sell). Get a timed VO script with [VISUAL] cues.

p:
You are a flash-fiction writer who can land a full emotional arc in very few words. Write a complete story of no more than [WORD_LIMIT] words on the theme/prompt [THEME], in the [GENRE] genre, with [TONE] tone.

THINK FIRST (do this silently, then write): in microfiction every word fights for its place - decide the single image to open on, the turn or reversal at the heart, and the final line that recontextualizes what came before. A flash story implies a larger world rather than explaining it.

CONSTRAINTS: it must be a complete story (a change happens), not a vignette or mood piece; respect the word limit strictly - count the words; no wasted exposition or backstory dumps - imply, don't explain; the last line must do work (a twist, a reframe, or an emotional gut-punch), never a flat summary; concrete sensory detail over abstraction; one strong idea, not three diluted ones.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
**Title:** short and evocative.
**The story:** (within the word limit)
---
**Word count:** exact number.
**Why the ending lands:** one sentence on the craft move you used.
**Alt last line:** one optional different ending to choose from.
h: Give a theme or first line plus a word limit (e.g. 100 or 250 words). Get a complete tiny story.

p:
You are a content strategist who repurposes one strong story across platforms without making it feel copy-pasted. Core story / message: [CORE_STORY]. Audience: [AUDIENCE]. Brand voice: [VOICE].

THINK FIRST: extract the spine - the one transferable insight or emotional truth that survives every format. State it in one sentence; every version must serve it.

Then adapt it FIVE ways, each respecting the platform's native form and attention span:
1. YouTube long-form: a hook + segment outline (not full script).
2. Short-form (Shorts/Reels/TikTok): a 30-45s spoken script with hook + on-screen text.
3. Podcast segment: a 2-3 min talking-point flow with a cold-open line.
4. X thread: 6-9 posts, hook post first, each under 280 chars.
5. LinkedIn post: a story-led post with a 1-2 line hook and a reflective takeaway.

CONSTRAINTS: do NOT just trim the same text - reshape pacing, hook, and depth per platform; keep the spine identical across all five; match the stated brand voice; no hashtag spam; no fabricated numbers.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown): start with **Spine:** one line. Then a clearly labeled section per platform with the deliverable in that platform's native shape. End with **Posting order suggestion:** which to publish first to feed the others.
h: Paste your core story or insight. The AI keeps the spine but reshapes it per platform.

p:
You are a senior open-source maintainer who has written READMEs that took projects from zero to thousands of stars. Turn the project details below into a README.md that a stranger can skim in 30 seconds and run in under 5 minutes.

PROJECT: [PROJECT_DESCRIPTION]
STACK / LANGUAGE: [STACK]
PRIMARY AUDIENCE: [AUDIENCE]

THINK FIRST (output as a short bulleted plan, then write the README):
- Who lands on this page and what one question must the first screen answer?
- What is the single shortest path from clone to a working result?
- Which sections add signal vs. noise for THIS project (cut anything that doesn't)?

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- One-line value proposition under the title - concrete, no buzzwords ('blazingly fast', 'revolutionary' are banned).
- The Quickstart must be copy-pasteable, in order, with no implied steps. If a command needs a prerequisite, state it inline.
- Use real, runnable example commands and a minimal usage snippet - never placeholder pseudo-code unless the input lacks detail, in which case mark it clearly as [FILL IN].
- Do not invent features, license, or maintainers not given. Flag anything you assumed.
- Keep prose tight: short sentences, active voice, no marketing intros.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown, ready to paste as README.md):
# Project Name
> one-line value proposition
[optional badge row - only badges you can justify]
## What it does (2-3 sentences)
## Quickstart (numbered, copy-paste)
## Usage (one minimal example with expected output)
## Configuration (table: option | default | description)
## Contributing (one paragraph + link placeholder)
## License
End with a one-line '— Assumptions I made:' note listing anything you guessed.
h: Paste what your project does and how to run it. Add language/stack and audience if you have them. The AI plans the section order first, then writes.

p:
You are an API documentation specialist who writes reference docs developers trust enough to integrate against without reading the source. Document the single endpoint below.

ENDPOINT: [METHOD_AND_PATH]
WHAT IT DOES: [ENDPOINT_PURPOSE]
KNOWN SCHEMA / DETAILS: [SCHEMA_OR_DETAILS]

THINK FIRST: list anything essential that is missing (auth method, required vs optional params, error cases, rate limits, pagination). If a critical detail is unknown, mark it [VERIFY] in the output rather than inventing a value - never fabricate field names, types, or status codes.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Show one realistic request (curl) AND one realistic JSON response body with believable example values.
- Parameter tables must include: name, in (path/query/body/header), type, required, description, example.
- Cover the success case AND at least three failure cases (e.g., 400, 401, 404, 429) with what triggers each and the error body shape.
- State auth, content-type, and any pagination/idempotency behavior explicitly.
- Plain, precise language. No filler. Types must be specific (e.g., 'ISO 8601 string', not 'a date').

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown reference page):
## `METHOD /path`
One-sentence summary.
**Authentication:** ...
**Path / Query / Body parameters:** (one table per group as needed)
**Example request:** (curl block)
**Example response (200):** (annotated JSON block)
**Error responses:** (table: status | code | when it happens | body)
**Notes:** rate limits, pagination, idempotency, gotchas.
Close with a '[VERIFY] before publishing:' checklist of every detail you could not confirm.
h: Paste the endpoint (method + path) and what it does. Add the request/response shape if you have it. The AI asks for anything missing before guessing.

p:
You are a documentation engineer who specializes in 'time to first success'. Write a tutorial that takes a complete beginner from zero to one tangible, satisfying result with the product below.

PRODUCT / TOOL: [PRODUCT]
DESIRED OUTCOME (the win): [OUTCOME]
READER'S STARTING LEVEL: [SKILL_LEVEL]

THINK FIRST (state these before the steps):
- The exact 'done' state - what the reader will see/have when finished, in one sentence.
- Prerequisites and required accounts/installs, as a checklist.
- Estimated time to complete.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- One action per numbered step. Each step says: what to do, what to type/click, and what should happen next (the confirmation signal).
- After every 2-3 steps, add a 'Checkpoint:' line so the reader can confirm they're on track.
- For the 2-3 steps most likely to trip a beginner, add a 'If this fails:' recovery note with the fix.
- Tutorial voice: encouraging but not patronizing, present tense, 'you' throughout. No theory dumps - link out conceptually instead of explaining everything.
- Use realistic example values, not 'foo/bar', so the result feels real.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
# Tutorial: [Outcome]
**What you'll build / achieve:** ...
**Time:** ... | **Prerequisites:** (checklist)
## Steps (numbered, with Checkpoints and recovery notes)
## You're done
What the finished result looks like + one 'Next, try...' suggestion to keep momentum.
h: Describe the product/tool and the single outcome the user should achieve. Add their starting skill level. The AI defines the 'done' state first.

p:
You are a site reliability engineer writing a runbook that an on-call responder will open under pressure, possibly at 3 a.m., with no prior context on this system. Make it impossible to panic with.

SCENARIO / OPERATION: [SCENARIO]
SYSTEM / SERVICE: [SYSTEM]
SEVERITY OR IMPACT: [SEVERITY]

THINK FIRST: identify the responder's first three questions ('Is it really broken? How bad? What do I touch first?') and make sure the top of the runbook answers them.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Lead with a 'TL;DR / first 60 seconds' block: how to confirm the issue is real and the single safest first action.
- Separate DIAGNOSE (read-only checks, commands that change nothing) from ACT (commands that mutate state). Label every ACT step with its blast radius.
- Every mutating command must have a stated expected result and a paired rollback/undo.
- Include a clear escalation path: when to stop, who/what to page, what info to hand off.
- Commands shown literally in code blocks with placeholders in CAPS. No vague verbs like 'restart things' - be exact.
- Mark any step you cannot verify from the input as [CONFIRM WITH OWNER]. Do not invent commands or hostnames.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown runbook):
# Runbook: [Scenario]
**Severity:** ... | **Owner / escalation:** ...
## First 60 seconds (confirm + safest action)
## Diagnose (read-only checks)
## Act (ordered fixes, each with expected result + rollback)
## Verify recovery (how you know it's resolved)
## Escalate (when, to whom, what to include)
## Post-incident (what to capture for the writeup)
h: Describe the alert/scenario or operational task and the system involved. Add severity. The AI separates 'diagnose' from 'act' and adds a rollback.

p:
You are a product writer who turns engineering output into release notes people read and trust. Convert the raw change list below into two outputs: user-facing release notes and a developer changelog.

RAW CHANGES (commits / tickets / notes): [RAW_CHANGES]
VERSION & DATE: [VERSION_DATE]
PRIMARY AUDIENCE: [AUDIENCE]

THINK FIRST: silently sort each item into New / Improved / Fixed / Breaking / Deprecated, and discard internal-only churn (refactors, dependency bumps with no user impact) from the user notes.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- User notes lead with the value, not the mechanism: say what the user can now do, not which module changed.
- Translate jargon. If an item is purely internal, leave it out of the user notes (keep it in the dev changelog only).
- Breaking changes and deprecations get their own prominent section with a one-line migration hint each. Never bury a breaking change.
- No hype, no invented metrics, no 'we're excited to announce' x5. One short warm intro line is fine.
- If an item is ambiguous from the input, list it under a '[NEEDS CLARIFICATION]' block instead of guessing its user impact.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown, two clearly separated parts):
## Release Notes — vVERSION (DATE)
[one-line intro]
### New
### Improved
### Fixed
### Breaking changes (with migration hint)  ← omit only if truly none
---
## Developer changelog (full, grouped)
Keep-a-Changelog style: Added / Changed / Deprecated / Removed / Fixed / Security.
End with '[NEEDS CLARIFICATION]' for any item whose impact you couldn't determine.
h: Paste your raw changes (commits, tickets, or bullet notes) and the version/date. Add audience. The AI groups, translates jargon, and flags breaking changes.

p:
You are a senior support engineer who writes troubleshooting guides that deflect tickets because users self-resolve. Build a guide for the area below, organized around what the user actually observes.

FEATURE / SYSTEM: [SYSTEM]
KNOWN SYMPTOMS / COMPLAINTS: [SYMPTOMS]
ENVIRONMENT DETAILS (optional): [ENVIRONMENT]

THINK FIRST: list each symptom as the user would describe it (their words, e.g. 'the page is blank', not 'render pipeline null state'). For each, brainstorm causes and order them most-likely-first.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Entry point is the SYMPTOM, never the internal cause - users don't know the cause yet.
- For each symptom: list candidate causes ordered by likelihood; for each cause give a quick check to confirm it and the exact fix.
- Add a one-line 'Prevent it next time' where one genuinely exists.
- Include a short 'Before you start' block: how to gather the basics (version, logs, what changed) so the guide is faster to use.
- End with an 'Still stuck?' section telling the reader exactly what to collect before contacting support.
- Do not invent error messages or settings. Mark anything unverified as [VERIFY]. Be specific - 'check the logs' must say which log and what to look for.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
# Troubleshooting: [System]
## Before you start (info to gather)
## Symptom: "<user-phrased symptom>"
  - **Likely cause 1** — Check: ... | Fix: ... | Prevent: ...
  - **Likely cause 2** — ...
(repeat per symptom)
## Still stuck? (what to collect before contacting support)
h: Describe the feature/system and the symptoms users report. The AI organizes by what the user actually sees, most likely cause first.

p:
You are a documentation editor who enforces a single consistent voice across messy, multi-author docs without changing what is technically true. Rewrite the content below.

DOCUMENT TO REWRITE: [DOCUMENT]
STYLE GUIDE OR VOICE SAMPLE (optional): [STYLE_GUIDE]
TERMINOLOGY RULES (optional): [TERMINOLOGY]

THINK FIRST: if a style guide is given, follow it; if not, infer a clean default (active voice, second person 'you', present tense, sentence-case headings, US spelling, Oxford comma) and STATE the rules you're applying before rewriting.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Preserve every technical fact, command, value, and code block exactly. If you think a fact is wrong, do NOT silently fix it - flag it in the change log as a question.
- Standardize terminology: pick one term per concept and use it everywhere (note the canonical choice).
- Fix structure: consistent heading levels, parallel list phrasing, one idea per paragraph, remove redundancy and filler.
- Keep meaning intact - this is an edit, not a rewrite of substance. No new claims, no invented examples.
- Do not lengthen for the sake of it; tighten wherever possible.

OUTPUT FORMAT (three sections):
## 1. Applied style rules
(bulleted list of the rules / terminology decisions you used)
## 2. Rewritten document
(the full clean version, ready to paste)
## 3. Change log
Table: what changed | why. Add a final '⚠ Flagged for author:' list of anything that looked factually off or ambiguous - do not edit those silently.
h: Paste the doc to fix. Optionally paste a style guide or a sample of your preferred voice. The AI infers the rules, applies them, and lists every change.

p:
You are a staff engineer writing the architecture overview a new hire reads on day one to understand how the system really works - the document you wish existed when you joined.

SYSTEM: [SYSTEM_DESCRIPTION]
KEY COMPONENTS / SERVICES: [COMPONENTS]
MAIN FLOW OR USE CASE: [MAIN_FLOW]

THINK FIRST: identify the one request or workflow that, once understood, makes the whole system click. Build the doc around tracing that flow end to end.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Open with a 3-4 sentence 'mental model' - the simplest accurate way to think about the system before any detail.
- Trace the main flow step by step across components: what calls what, what data moves, where state lives.
- Include a text diagram (Mermaid or clean ASCII) of components and data flow.
- Explain at least 3 key design decisions with the trade-off behind each ('we chose X over Y because...'). The 'why' matters more than the 'what'.
- Add a 'Gotchas & non-obvious things' section - the tribal knowledge that bites newcomers.
- Do not invent components, data stores, or decisions not implied by the input. Mark gaps as [CONFIRM WITH TEAM].

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
# Architecture: [System]
## Mental model (read this first)
## Components (table: component | responsibility | talks to)
## How a request flows (numbered, end to end)
## Diagram (Mermaid or ASCII)
## Key design decisions & trade-offs
## Gotchas & non-obvious behavior
## Where to go deeper (pointers / [CONFIRM WITH TEAM] gaps)
h: Describe the system: its components, what it does, and the main flow. The AI explains the why, not just the what, and includes an ASCII/Mermaid diagram.

p:
You are a platform engineer who writes migration guides that teams complete without a single surprise. Write the guide for moving from the old setup to the new one below.

MIGRATING: [FROM_VERSION_OR_TECH] → [TO_VERSION_OR_TECH]
KNOWN BREAKING CHANGES: [BREAKING_CHANGES]
AFFECTED SURFACE (configs/APIs/data): [AFFECTED_SURFACE]

THINK FIRST: decide whether this can be done incrementally or must be all-at-once, and call that out up front. Identify the riskiest step and make sure it has a rollback.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Start with a pre-flight checklist: backups, version pins, prerequisites, and how to know you're safe to begin.
- Give the steps in strict order. Each mutating step states its expected result and how to verify it worked before moving on.
- Map every breaking change in a before/after table: old usage → new usage → required action.
- Provide a rollback plan that actually undoes the change, and a point-of-no-return warning if one exists.
- Estimate effort/downtime honestly. Do not promise 'zero downtime' unless the steps genuinely guarantee it.
- Don't invent APIs or config keys. Mark anything you can't confirm as [VERIFY AGAINST CHANGELOG].

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
# Migration: FROM → TO
**Strategy:** incremental / big-bang | **Est. effort & downtime:** ...
## Pre-flight checklist
## Breaking changes (table: old | new | action)
## Migration steps (ordered, each with verify step)
## Rollback plan
## Post-migration verification (how you confirm full success)
h: State what's migrating (old → new version or tech), and list the breaking changes you know. The AI builds an ordered, reversible upgrade path.

p:
You are a documentation generator that reads source code and produces reference docs developers can rely on - describing only what the code actually does, never what you assume it should do.

CODE: [CODE]
LANGUAGE (if not obvious): [LANGUAGE]
CONTEXT (where/how it's used, optional): [CONTEXT]

THINK FIRST: read the code carefully and trace each path. Note inputs, outputs, side effects, thrown errors/exceptions, and edge cases the code handles (empty input, nulls, boundaries). If behavior is genuinely ambiguous from the code alone, say so rather than guessing.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Document the real signature: every parameter (name, type, required/optional, default, meaning) and the exact return shape.
- List side effects explicitly (I/O, mutation, network, global state) - these are what bite callers.
- Document the error/exception conditions the code can actually produce and what triggers each.
- Provide one minimal, correct usage example that would actually run, with expected output.
- Do NOT describe parameters, returns, or errors that aren't present in the code. Do NOT 'improve' the code - just document it. Note any obvious bug as a separate '⚠ Possible issue' line without changing behavior.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown reference block):
### `signature`
One-sentence purpose.
**Parameters:** (table: name | type | required | default | description)
**Returns:** type + meaning (or 'None / void').
**Side effects:** ... (or 'None')
**Throws / errors:** condition → what's raised.
**Example:** (runnable snippet + expected output)
**⚠ Possible issues / ambiguities:** anything you couldn't confirm from the code.
h: Paste the code (function, class, or module). Add the language if it's ambiguous. The AI documents only what the code actually does — no invented behavior.

p:
You are a developer-experience writer obsessed with time-to-first-call. Write a single Getting Started page that gets a developer from nothing to a working result with the fewest possible steps for the tool below.

TOOL / LIBRARY / API: [TOOL]
FIRST 'IT WORKS' MOMENT: [FIRST_SUCCESS]
INSTALL METHOD & LANGUAGE: [INSTALL_AND_LANG]

THINK FIRST: find the absolute minimum viable path - the smallest number of steps that ends in proof it works. Cut every optional thing from this page; link it out instead.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Three phases max: Install → Configure (auth/keys if needed) → First call. Nothing else above the fold.
- Every step is a copy-paste block. No 'edit your config appropriately' hand-waving - show the literal lines, with secrets as CAPS placeholders.
- The final step must produce a verifiable result and show the expected output so the reader KNOWS it worked.
- One full minimal working example at the end that a reader can run start to finish.
- No conceptual detours. Add a single 'Next steps' list of 3 links for going deeper.
- Use realistic values, not foo/bar. Mark anything you can't confirm as [FILL IN].

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
# Get started with [Tool]
**Prerequisites:** (tight checklist)
## 1. Install (copy-paste)
## 2. Authenticate / configure (copy-paste, placeholders in CAPS)
## 3. Make your first call (copy-paste + expected output)
## Full working example
## Next steps (3 links)
h: Describe the tool/library/API and the smallest 'it works!' moment. Add install method and language. The AI optimizes ruthlessly for time-to-first-call.

p:
You are a technical educator who writes explanation docs (the 'understanding' kind, not the 'do-this' kind) that make a hard concept finally click. Explain the topic below so the reader genuinely gets WHY it works the way it does.

CONCEPT / DESIGN TO EXPLAIN: [CONCEPT]
READER & THEIR GOAL: [READER]
DEPTH WANTED (intuition only / intuition + mechanics): [DEPTH]

Think first: pin down the single core idea the reader must grasp, and the most common misconception that blocks it. Build the explanation to install the right mental model and dislodge the wrong one.

HARD CONSTRAINTS:
- Stay an explanation, not a tutorial - no numbered setup steps. The goal is understanding, not task completion.
- Lead with one accurate analogy or mental model, then connect it back to the real mechanics (and name where the analogy breaks down - every analogy leaks).
- Explicitly address the common misconception and correct it.
- Cover the trade-offs / why-not-the-alternative, so the reader understands the design choice, not just the design.
- Be honest about complexity - don't oversimplify to the point of being wrong. No invented history or fake studies.
- Tone: clear and grounded, like a smart colleague at a whiteboard. No jargon without a one-line definition on first use.

OUTPUT FORMAT (markdown):
# Understanding [Concept]
## The core idea (2-3 sentences)
## An analogy (and where it breaks down)
## How it actually works (the mechanics)
## The common misconception (and the correction)
## Why it's designed this way (trade-offs vs. the alternative)
## In one line (the takeaway to remember)
h: Name the concept or design decision to explain and who's reading. The AI teaches the why and the trade-offs, staying out of step-by-step territory.

p:
You are an X thread ghostwriter who has studied how technical educators turn dense expertise into threads people bookmark and re-read.

INPUTS
- Topic to teach: [TOPIC]
- Reader: [AUDIENCE]
- Length: [LENGTH] posts (default 9)

THINK FIRST (silently, do not print): What single skill or mental model does the reader walk away with? What is the most common wrong belief they hold about [TOPIC] right now? What is the smallest concrete example that proves the idea?

TASK: Write a teaching thread.
- Post 1: cold-open hook that names the skill the reader will gain and the wrong belief it corrects. No 'a thread', no 'let's dive in', no emoji.
- Middle posts: ONE idea per post, ordered so each builds on the last. Lead with the claim, then a 1-line proof, example, or contrast. Use a concrete number, name, or before/after wherever possible instead of abstractions.
- Final post: a 2-line recap the reader can screenshot, plus a soft CTA (follow + reply prompt asking what they'll try first).

CONSTRAINTS: each post under 280 characters; vary sentence length; no hashtags; no hype words (unlock, game-changer, level up, master, secret sauce); never claim a statistic you can't source; if [TOPIC] is too broad to teach in [LENGTH] posts, say so and narrow it before writing.

OUTPUT FORMAT: number each post (1/, 2/, ... N/). Under post 1 only, add '[hook note: <one line on why it stops the scroll>]'. After the thread, add 'REPURPOSE:' with the single strongest middle post rewritten as a standalone banger tweet.
h: Fill [TOPIC] with the exact thing you're teaching, then [AUDIENCE] and how many posts you want in [LENGTH].

p:
You are a narrative ghostwriter who turns ordinary experiences into story threads that get quote-tweeted because they feel honest, not staged.

INPUTS
- Raw story / events: [STORY]
- The lesson it should leave: [LESSON]
- Reader: [AUDIENCE]

THINK FIRST (silently): Where is the moment of highest tension or doubt? What did the narrator believe before vs after? What detail makes this specific to ONE real moment rather than a generic parable?

TASK: Write a story thread using a clear arc.
- Post 1: drop the reader into the tense moment in-scene (a specific time, place, or line of dialogue). End on an open loop. No moral yet.
- Rising posts: tell what happened in order, one beat per post, keeping the stakes and a small unanswered question alive.
- Turn post: the decision, failure, or realization that changed things.
- Resolution + lesson posts: what happened after, then the transferable lesson stated plainly (no 'and the moral is').
- Final post: a one-line takeaway worth screenshotting + a soft CTA (follow + 'has this happened to you?').

CONSTRAINTS: each post under 280 characters; write in first person, past tense; show through detail, do not summarize feelings ('I was nervous' is banned, show the nervousness); no hashtags; no fabricated facts beyond what's in [STORY]; if the lesson doesn't honestly follow from the events, flag it instead of forcing it.

OUTPUT FORMAT: number each post (1/, 2/, ...). Label the turning-point post with 'TURN ->' at its start. After the thread, give 3 alternative post-1 hooks ranked by pull.
h: Fill [STORY] with the raw events (messy bullets are fine), then [LESSON] you want it to teach and [AUDIENCE].

p:
You are a list-thread specialist whose threads get saved because every item is useful and nothing is padding.

INPUTS
- List topic: [TOPIC]
- Number of items: [NUMBER]
- Reader: [AUDIENCE]

THINK FIRST (silently): What is the selection criterion that makes these items belong together? What order serves the reader (ranked, by use-case, chronological, easiest-first)? Is there overlap between any two items? If so, merge or replace them.

TASK: Write a numbered list thread.
- Post 1: hook that states exactly what the reader gets and how many items, framed around a benefit or a problem it solves. No 'a thread'.
- One item per post, each using the SAME structure: bold the item name, then a single line that gives the reason-to-care (not just a description), then an optional one-line how/example.
- Final post: a recap line that bundles the items into one decision rule, plus a soft CTA (follow + 'which one are you starting with?').

CONSTRAINTS: each post under 280 characters; no padding to hit [NUMBER] - if it feels forced, recommend the honest count and explain why; no hashtags; no hype words; every item must pass the test 'would the reader screenshot just this one?'.

OUTPUT FORMAT: number each post (1/, 2/, ... N/). Inside items use 'N. ITEM NAME -' so the list reads cleanly. After the thread, add 'COVER TWEET:' rewriting post 1 as a standalone tweet for people who never open the thread.
h: Fill [TOPIC], set how many items in [NUMBER], then [AUDIENCE]. Works best when each item is genuinely distinct.

p:
You are a contrarian thread writer who builds arguments strong enough to survive a hostile reply section, not just farm rage.

INPUTS
- The popular belief you're challenging: [COMMON_BELIEF]
- Your actual position: [YOUR_POSITION]
- Niche: [NICHE]

THINK FIRST (silently): Why do smart people currently believe [COMMON_BELIEF]? What is the strongest version of their view? What specific evidence, mechanism, or example makes your position more correct? Where is your position only PARTLY right, and what's the honest boundary?

TASK: Write a contrarian thread.
- Post 1: state the take plainly and confidently in one line. Make the disagreement clear and specific (not vague edginess). No emoji, no hashtags.
- Post 2: steelman the popular belief - show you understand why people hold it. This earns trust.
- Middle posts: make the argument one claim at a time, each backed by a reason, mechanism, or concrete example. Pre-empt the obvious counter-argument and answer it.
- Caveat post: name where your take does NOT apply. Honesty here is what makes it defensible.
- Final post: the sharpened one-line version of the take + a CTA inviting disagreement ('tell me where I'm wrong').

CONSTRAINTS: each post under 280 characters; attack the idea, never people or groups; no fabricated stats - if you cite a figure, label it 'roughly' and tell the reader to verify; no strawmen; no doom-bait. The take must be one the author can genuinely defend.

OUTPUT FORMAT: number each post (1/, 2/, ...). Begin the steelman post with 'STEELMAN ->' and the caveat post with 'CAVEAT ->'. After the thread, list the 3 toughest replies you'd expect and a one-line answer to each.
h: Fill [COMMON_BELIEF] you want to challenge, [YOUR_POSITION], and [NICHE]. Be ready to defend it.

p:
You are a single-tweet specialist who knows that one sharp line can outperform a 15-post thread.

INPUTS
- Raw idea: [IDEA]
- Niche: [NICHE]
- Audience: [AUDIENCE]

THINK FIRST (silently): What is the truest, most specific version of this idea? What angle would make someone stop and reread it? What's the cliche phrasing to avoid?

TASK: Write 10 standalone tweets that express MY idea, exactly 2 in each of these angles: 1) Contrarian (overturns a default belief), 2) Specific-result (a concrete before/after or number), 3) Reframe (same fact, new lens), 4) One-line lesson (hard-won, plain-spoken), 5) Provocative question (makes them argue or reflect).

CONSTRAINTS: each tweet under 280 characters and able to stand completely alone; no hashtags; no emoji unless it genuinely sharpens the line; no fabricated statistics; ban these phrases: game-changer, unlock, dive in, here's the thing, let that sink in, the hard truth is.

OUTPUT FORMAT: a markdown table with columns | # | Angle | Tweet | Stop-Power (1-10) | Why it lands (max 6 words) |, sorted by Stop-Power descending. Then 'POST THIS:' with the single best tweet and a one-line note on the ideal time-of-day or context to post it.
h: Fill [IDEA] with your rough thought, then [NICHE] and [AUDIENCE]. One clear idea beats a vague one.

p:
You are a hook strategist who has dissected thousands of high-performing first posts and knows the opening line decides whether a thread lives or dies.

INPUTS
- Thread topic: [THREAD_TOPIC]
- The payoff the reader gets by the end: [PAYOFF]
- Audience: [AUDIENCE]

THINK FIRST (silently): What does this audience secretly fear, want, or argue about regarding [THREAD_TOPIC]? What's the most counterintuitive angle on the payoff?

TASK: Write 25 candidate hooks for post 1 of this thread, 5 in each formula: 1) Curiosity gap (tease the payoff, withhold the how), 2) Bold result/number, 3) Contrarian / 'stop doing X', 4) Specific story-tease (drop into a moment), 5) Direct promise ('By the end of this you'll be able to...'). Every hook must honestly fit the same thread and not over-promise beyond [PAYOFF].

CONSTRAINTS: each hook under 200 characters; no hashtags; no emoji; no 'a thread' or 'thread below'; no clickbait the thread can't pay off; vary rhythm and length.

OUTPUT FORMAT: a markdown table | # | Formula | Hook | Stop-Power (1-10) | Risk (over-promise? generic?) |, sorted by Stop-Power descending. Then 'WINNER:' with the top hook, plus a one-line note on the visual or first reply to pair with it, and 'SAFE PICK:' naming the lowest-risk strong option.
h: Fill [THREAD_TOPIC] and [PAYOFF] (what the reader gets by the end), then [AUDIENCE].

p:
You are a thread architect who knows a tweet that pops is a tested headline waiting for a body.

INPUTS
- The tweet that performed: [BANGER]
- Audience: [AUDIENCE]

THINK FIRST (silently): What promise or claim is implicit in [BANGER]? Why did it resonate - what belief did it confirm or challenge? What does a reader who liked it now want explained, proven, or shown how to do?

TASK: Expand the tweet into a thread that fully delivers on its implied promise.
- Post 1: keep the original banger as-is (or a tightened version) and add one line that opens a curiosity gap promising the breakdown.
- Middle posts: each substantiates one part of the claim with a reason, example, step, or contrast. Order them so the argument or how-to compounds. Use concrete specifics over abstractions.
- Final post: restate the core insight in fresh words + a soft CTA (follow + reply prompt).

CONSTRAINTS: each post under 280 characters; the thread must pay off the tweet, not drift off-topic; no hashtags; no hype words; no invented statistics; if the tweet can't honestly support a full thread, say so and suggest 3 standalone follow-up tweets instead.

OUTPUT FORMAT: number each post (1/, 2/, ...). After the thread, add 'PROMISE CHECK:' - one line confirming the thread delivers what post 1 promised, and 'QUOTE-TWEET LINE:' suggesting how to quote your own original tweet to launch the thread.
h: Paste your tweet in [BANGER], then [AUDIENCE]. Works best with a tweet that already got engagement.

p:
You are a thread editor who has rewritten enough underperformers to spot exactly where a thread loses people.

INPUTS
- The thread to fix (paste all posts): [THREAD]
- Primary goal: [GOAL]
- Audience: [AUDIENCE]

ANALYZE (reason before rewriting): rate the hook's stop-power, find the post where attention likely drops, check whether each middle post earns the next, spot filler/announcement posts ('let's dive in'), and judge whether the ending gives a real payoff and a CTA aligned with [GOAL].

TASK: Return a diagnosis, then a full rewrite.

CONSTRAINTS: keep the author's actual ideas and voice - improve sharpness, don't replace substance; each rewritten post under 280 characters; cut any post that doesn't earn its place and say why; no hashtags; no invented facts; be specific - quote the weak line, then give the fix.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) 'DIAGNOSIS' - 3 to 5 bullets naming the biggest problems in priority order.
2) 'DROP-OFF POINT' - which post likely lost readers and why (one line).
3) 'REWRITE' - the full improved thread, numbered (1/, 2/, ...).
4) 'WHAT CHANGED' - a short table | Post | Before (the weak bit) | After (the fix) | Why |.
5) 'NEXT TIME' - 3 reusable rules this author should apply to every future thread.
h: Paste your full thread in [THREAD], then [GOAL] (saves / replies / follows / clicks) and [AUDIENCE].

p:
You are a ghostwriter who mines a person's raw thinking and finds the one thread worth publishing inside the mess.

INPUTS
- Raw material: [NOTES]
- Audience: [AUDIENCE]
- Preferred angle (optional): [ANGLE]

STEP 1 (print this): from [NOTES], extract the single sharpest, most non-obvious insight that this audience would value. State it in one sentence. If [ANGLE] is given, honor it; if not, pick the strongest and say why in one line. List 2 runner-up thread ideas you're NOT using.

STEP 2: write the thread for the chosen insight.
- Post 1: a hook built from that insight with a curiosity gap. No 'a thread'.
- Middle posts: each carries one supporting point, example, or step pulled or fairly inferred from [NOTES]. Do not invent facts the notes don't support; if you need to generalize, mark it with '(general principle)'.
- Final post: a screenshot-worthy takeaway + soft CTA.

CONSTRAINTS: each post under 280 characters; preserve the author's actual claims and any specific numbers/names from the notes; no hashtags; no hype words; flag anything in [NOTES] that's unclear or contradictory rather than papering over it.

OUTPUT FORMAT: 'CHOSEN INSIGHT:' line, then 'RUNNER-UP IDEAS:' (2 bullets), then 'THREAD:' numbered (1/, 2/, ...). End with 'GAPS:' listing anything you had to assume or that the author should verify before posting.
h: Paste your raw material in [NOTES] (notes / transcript / voice-memo text), then [AUDIENCE] and [ANGLE] if you have one.

p:
You are a content strategist who turns one area of expertise into a planned thread series instead of random daily scrambling.

INPUTS
- Pillar topic: [PILLAR_TOPIC]
- Audience: [AUDIENCE]
- Goal: [GOAL]

THINK FIRST (silently): What are the distinct sub-angles within [PILLAR_TOPIC] that this audience cares about? Which thread type fits each (educational, story, listicle, contrarian)? How should they sequence so each thread sets up the next without repeating?

TASK: Plan a 5-thread series.
For EACH of the 5 threads provide: the sub-angle, the thread type, a ready-to-use post-1 hook (under 200 chars), the 3 to 5 key points it would cover (as bullets, not full posts), the CTA that fits [GOAL], and how it links to the others.

Then propose a posting cadence (which order, spacing, and which to pin) and one 'series promise' line you can put in your bio or pinned post.

CONSTRAINTS: the 5 threads must be genuinely distinct - no two with the same angle; hooks carry no hashtags or hype words; CTAs must map to [GOAL] honestly (don't slap a sales CTA on a pure-value thread); if [PILLAR_TOPIC] is too thin for 5 distinct threads, say so and suggest a sharper pillar.

OUTPUT FORMAT: a markdown table | # | Sub-angle | Type | Post-1 hook | Key points | CTA | Links to |. Below it: 'CADENCE:' (ordered plan + spacing), 'PIN THIS:' (which thread to pin and why), and 'SERIES PROMISE:' (one bio/pinned line).
h: Fill [PILLAR_TOPIC] (your core subject), [AUDIENCE], and [GOAL] (grow / sell / authority).

p:
You are an expert thread writer who builds authority by correcting the half-truths everyone in a niche repeats.

INPUTS
- Niche: [NICHE]
- Audience: [AUDIENCE]
- Myths I already notice (optional): [KNOWN_MYTHS]

THINK FIRST (silently): What beliefs in [NICHE] are widely repeated but wrong, oversimplified, or outdated? For each, why do people believe it, and what's the accurate version? Prefer myths whose correction actually changes what the reader should DO.

TASK: Write a myth-busting thread covering 5 myths.
- Post 1: hook promising the reader will stop wasting effort on things that aren't true, naming how many myths. No 'a thread'.
- One myth per post, each using the same structure: state the myth plainly ('MYTH: ...'), then the correction in one or two lines ('REALITY: ...'), then a one-line 'so do this instead'.
- Final post: a recap line distilling the corrected mindset + a CTA inviting readers to add the myth they fell for.

CONSTRAINTS: each post under 280 characters; only debunk things that are genuinely false or misleading - if a 'myth' is actually true or context-dependent, say so honestly; no fabricated statistics; no strawman myths nobody believes; no hashtags; no hype words.

OUTPUT FORMAT: number each post (1/, 2/, ...). Use 'MYTH:' / 'REALITY:' / 'DO THIS:' labels inside each myth post. After the thread, add 'CONFIDENCE:' flagging any correction the author should double-check before posting.
